noti_0a_p5_0.text: NOTICE: When handling an electronic part that has an ESD sensitive sticker (Figure 1), the service technician should follow these guidelines to reduce any possible electrostatic charge build-up on the service technicians's body and the electronic part in the dealership:

proc_0a_p5_0.text:
ESD Sensitive Parts Handling Guidelines
1. Do not open package until it is time to install the part.
2. Avoid touching electrical terminals of the part.
3. Before removing the part from its package, ground the package to a known good ground on the car.
4. Always touch a known good ground before handling the part. This should be repeated while handling the part and more frequently after sliding across the seat, sitting down from a standing position or walking a distance.
steps: Do not open package until it is time to install the part.
Avoid touching electrical terminals of the part.
Before removing the part from its package, ground the package to a known good ground on the car.

Always touch a known good ground before handling the part. This should be repeated while handling the part and more frequently after sliding across the seat, sitting down from a standing position or walking a distance.

fig_0a_p5_0.text: Figure 1 Electrostatic Discharge Sensitive Parts Label

para_0a_p5_0.text:
The body number plate identifies the identification numbers and model year, build date code, car division, series, style, body assembly plant, plant number, paint color codes, type of paint, trim combination, modular seat code, and roof option. The I.D. plate is located on the cowl top panel.

The vehicle identification number is on the left upper instrument panel (Figure 2). The sequential unit number is prefixed by letters and numbers which are explained in Figure 3.

The model series and body styles for the current year are listed in Figure 3.

fig_0a_p6_0.text:
Figure 3 Vehicle Identification Number
JG1: World Manufacturer Identifier (JG1 = Chevrolet, JG2 = Pontiac)
MR: Car Line / Series (R = Metro, Sprint or Firefly)
2: Body Style (2 = 2 door hatch back, 6 = 4 door hatch back)
1: Restraint Type (1 = Manual belts)
5: Engine Type (5 = G10)
Check Digit: Check Digit
K (Model Year): Model Year ("H" = 1987, "J" = 1988, "K" = 1989, "L" = 1990, "M" = 1991)
K (Assembly Plant): Assembly Plant (K = Kosai, Japan)
700001: Sequential Number

para_0a_p6_0.text:
The Vehicle Identification Chart (Figure 3) provides detailed VIN code information by liter and by the Engine Code letter located on the vehicle indentification plate.

The stick-on labels attached to the engine, or laser etching or stampings of the engine block, indicate the engine unit number or date code.

All engines and transaxles are stamped with a partial VIN. The stamping contains nine positions.

The first position is the GM car division identification.
  1 = Chevrolet
  2 = Pontiac
  3 = Oldsmobile
  4 = Buick
  6 = Cadillac

The second position is the model year.
  K = 1989

The third position is the vehicle assembly plant code.

Fourth through ninth position is the assembly plant sequential number of the car.

Information on the tire size, vehicle capacity weight and recommended tire inflation pressure is listed on the Tire Placard that is located on the edge of the left door lock pillar. The tire pressure specifications are also shown in Section OB.

GENERAL VEHICLE LIFTING AND JACKING

On front-wheel drive vehicles, the centerline-of-gravity is further forward than on rear-wheel drive vehicles. Therefore, whenever removing major components from the rear of the vehicle, while supported on a hoist, it is mandatory to support the vehicle in a manner to prevent the possibility of the vehicle tipping forward (Figures 4 through 7).

caut_0a_p6_0.text: CAUTION: Failure to follow the procedures outlined may result in unsatisfactory car performance, or a durability failure which may result in loss of control of car. Lifting should only be done at the positions indicated in figures to prevent possible damage to the car. If the vehicles to be jacked up only at the front or rear end, be sure to block the wheels in order to ensure safety. After the vehicle is jacked up, be sure to support it on stands. It is extremely dangerous to do any work on the vehicle raised on jack alone.

fig_0a_p6_1.text:
Figure 4 Front Support Location
1: FRONT LEFT TIRE
2: FRONT FENDER LEFT PANEL
3: EMBOSSED-MARK
note: SUPPORT LOCATION: FORWARD OF EMBOSSED-MARK; 110 mm (4.3 in.) from Bolt; WHEN USING FRAME CONTROL HOIST

This vehicle is primarily dimensioned in the metric system. Most fasteners are metric and are close in dimension to well-known customary fasteners in the inch system. It is important that replacement fasteners
be of the correct nominal diameter, thread pitch and strength.

para_0a_p7_0.text:
Original equipment metric (except "beauty" bolts, those bolts exposed on bumpers and cross-recess head screws) are identified by a number marking which indicates the strength of the material in the fastener.

General Motors Engineering Standards, along with other North American Industries, have adopted a portion of the standard metric fastener sizes defined by ISO (International Standards Organization). This was done to reduce the number of fastener sizes used and yet retain the best strength qualities in each thread size. For example, the customary 1/4-20 and 1/4-28 screws are replaced by the metric M6.0 X1 screw which has nearly the same diameter and 25.4 threads per inch. The thread pitch is in between the customary coarse and fine thread pitches. Metric and customary thread notation differ slightly. The difference is shown in Figure 8.

para_0a_p7_1.text:
Most commonly used metric fastener strength property classes are 4T, 7T and radial line with the class identification embossed on the head of each bolt. Some metric nuts will be marked with punch mark strength identification on the nut face. Figure 11 shows the different strength markings.

When replacing metric fasteners, be careful to use bolts and nuts of the same strength or greater than the original fasteners (the same number marking or higher). It is likewise important to select replacement fasteners of the correct size.

Correct replacement bolts and nuts are available through the parts division. Many metric fasteners available in the aftermarket parts channels were designed to metric standards of countries other than the United States, and may be of lower strength, may not have the same numbered head marking system, and may be of a different thread pitch.

The metric fasteners used on GM products are not designed to new, international standards thay may not yet be manufactured by some non-domestic bolt and nut suppliers. In general, except for special applications, the common sizes and pitches are shown in Figure 11.

noti_0a_p7_0.text: NOTICE: The designations of the standard fasteners used on this car are of coarse screw thread up to M8 and fine screw thread beyond M10. As can be seen in the tables, the designation to pitch relations are somewhat different between coarse screw thread and fine screw thread. Therefore, when replacing fasteners, the pitch should be confirmed carefully even if the replacement fasteners is the same metric fastener.

para_0a_p7_2.text:
Figure 13 shows how to determine the model and serial number of an automatic transaxle.

Figure 13 shows how to determine the model and serial number of a manual transaxle.

para_0a_p7_3.text:
A prevailing torque nut is designed to develop an interference between the nut and bolt threads. This is
most often accomplished by distortion of the top of an all metal nut or by using a nylon patch on the threads in the middle of the hex flat. A nylon insert may also be used as a method of interference between nut and bolt threads (Figure 16).

para_0a_p8_0.text: A prevailing torque bolt is designed to develop an interference between bolt and thread nuts, or the threads of a tapped hole. This is accomplished by distorting some of the threads or by using a nylon patch or adhesive (Figure 16).

proc_0a_p8_0.text:
Recommendations for Reuse
1. Clean, unrusted prevailing torque nuts and bolts may be reused as follows:
  a. Clean dirt and other foreign material off nuts or bolts.
  b. Inspect nuts or bolts to ensure there are no cracks, elongation, or other signs of abuse or overtightening. (If there is any doubt, replace with a new prevailing torque fastener of equal or greater strength).
  c. Assemble parts and hand start nuts or bolts.
  d. Observe that before fastener seats, it develops torque per the chart in Figure 10. (If there is any doubt, replace with a new prevailing torque fastener of equal or greater strength).
  e. Tighten fastener to torque specified in appropriate section of this manual.
2. Bolts and nuts which are rusty or damaged should be replaced with new parts of equal or greater strength.

  c. Assemble parts and hand start nuts or bolts.
  d. Observe that before fastener seats, it develops torque per the chart in Figure 10. (If there is any doubt, replace with a new prevailing torque fastener of equal or greater strength).

2. Bolts and nuts which are rusty or damaged should be replaced with new parts of equal or greater strength.

para_0a_p9_0.text: Bolt and nut strength identification markings. Customary (inch) bolts use radial line marks on the bolt head corresponding to grade (Grade 2, 5, 7, 8). Metric bolts use class numbers or marks (4.6, 4.8, 5.8, 8.8, 9.8, 10.9) or numeric marks (4, 6, 7, 8). Increasing numbers represent increasing strength. Nuts also carry strength identification markings.

tbl_0b_p14_0.text:
Scheduled Maintenance Service: 0B-1
Owner Inspections and Services: 0B-5
Recommended Fluids and Lubricants: 0B-7
Maintenance Items: 0B-7
Approximate Fluid Capacities: 0B-7
Tire Pressure Specifications: 0B-7

para_0b_p14_0.text:
The maintenance instructions contained in the Maintenance Schedule are based on the assumption that the car will be used as designed:
• To carry passengers and cargo within the limitation indicated on the Tire Placard located on the driver's door or door lock pillar,
• On reasonable road surfaces within legal operating limits,
• On unleaded gasoline.

Fig. 1

Follow Schedule I if the car is operated under one or more of the following conditions:
• When most trips are less than 4 miles (6 km).
• Operating when outside temperatures remain below freezing and when most trips are less than 10 miles (16 km).
• When most trips include extended idling and/or frequent low-speed operation as in stop-and-go traffic.
• Operating in dusty areas.

Schedule I should also be followed if the car is used in delivery service, police, taxi or other commercial applications.

Fig. 2

Follow Schedule II ONLY if none of the driving conditions specified in Schedule I apply.

Refer to Figs. 1 and 2 for the schedules of time and/or mileage intervals. The following text and illustrations (Figs. 3 and 4) give the details of the required maintenance services.

impo_0b_p14_0.text: IMPORTANT: ALWAYS USE SG QUALITY ENERGY CONSERVING II OILS OF PROPER VISCOSITY

para_0b_p14_1.text:
The "SG" designation may be shown alone or in combination with other designations such as "SG/CC," "SG/CD," "SF, SG, CC," etc.

Engine oil viscosity (thickness) has an effect on fuel economy and cold-weather operation (starting and oil flow). Lower viscosity engine oils can provide better fuel economy and cold-weather performance; however, higher temperature weather conditions require higher viscosity engine oils for satisfactory lubrication.

caut_0b_p14_0.text: CAUTION: Using oils of any viscosity other than those viscosities recommended could result in engine damage.

para_0b_p14_2.text:
When choosing an oil, consider the range of temperatures the vehicle will be operated in before the next oil change. Then, select the recommended oil viscosity from the chart (Fig. 3).

Lubricate transaxle shift linkage. Lubricate parking brake cable guides, underbody contact points and linkage at every other oil change.

Do not lubricate the parking brake cables. Lubrication destroys the plastic coating on the cables.

Check torque of mounting bolts and/or nuts. Tighten TBI mounting bolts or nuts to 12 lbs. ft. (17 N·m). Tighten MPFI/SFI throttle body mounting bolts or nuts to 20 lbs. ft. (27 N·m).

Check tires for abnormal wear or damage. To equalize wear and obtain maximum tire life, rotate as shown in Fig. 4. If irregular or premature wear exists, check wheel alignment. Also, check for damaged wheels.

While the tires and wheels are removed, perform the brake system inspection specified at Brake System Inspection that appears after the heading "Each Time Oil Is Changed."

Inspect the water pump belt tension and adjust as necessary.

fig_0b_p17_1.text:
Fig. 4 Tire Rotation, Front-Wheel-Drive Cars
LF: Left Front
RF: Right Front
LR: Left Rear
RR: Right Rear

proc_0b_p17_0.text:
Cooling System Service
1. Inspect the cooling system every 15,000 miles (25 000 km).
2. Drain, flush and refill system with new coolant every 30,000 miles (50 000 km) or 24 months. See Recommended Fluids and Lubricants, or Section 6B.
steps: Inspect the cooling system every 15,000 miles (25 000 km).
Drain, flush and refill system with new coolant every 30,000 miles (50 000 km) or 24 months. See Recommended Fluids and Lubricants, or Section 6B.

proc_0b_p17_1.text:
Transaxle Service – Manual Transaxle Fluid
1. Check the gear oil level in the manual transaxle every time the engine oil is changed. Before checking gear oil level, check the transaxle case for leaks. If leak is found, repair it.
2. Replace the gear oil every 12,000 miles (20 000 km) or every 12 months under severe conditions.
steps: Check the gear oil level in the manual transaxle every time the engine oil is changed. Before checking gear oil level, check the transaxle case for leaks. If leak is found, repair it.
Replace the gear oil every 12,000 miles (20 000 km) or every 12 months under severe conditions.

proc_0b_p17_2.text:
Transaxle Service – Automatic Transaxle Fluid
1. Inspect automatic transaxle fluid every time the oil is changed.
2. Replace oil inlet hose every 45 months or 45,000 miles (75 000 km).
3. Change fluid every 15,000 miles (25 000 km) for Schedule I conditions or every 100,000 miles (160 000 km) for Schedule II conditions.
steps: Inspect automatic transaxle fluid every time the oil is changed.
Replace oil inlet hose every 45 months or 45,000 miles (75 000 km).
Change fluid every 15,000 miles (25 000 km) for Schedule I conditions or every 100,000 miles (160 000 km) for Schedule II conditions.

para_0b_p17_0.text: Replace spark plugs every 30,000 miles (50 000 km). See "Maintenance Items" in this section.

proc_0b_p17_3.text:
Spark Plug Wire Inspection
1. Clean wires and inspect for burns, cracks or other damage.
2. Also check distributor cap and rotor for cracks, and their electric terminals for wear and corrosion. Replace the parts as necessary.
3. Check the wire boot fit at the distributor and/or coil, and at the spark plugs. Replace the wires as needed.
steps: Clean wires and inspect for burns, cracks or other damage.
Also check distributor cap and rotor for cracks, and their electric terminals for wear and corrosion. Replace the parts as necessary.
Check the wire boot fit at the distributor and/or coil, and at the spark plugs. Replace the wires as needed.

proc_0b_p17_4.text:
Positive Crankcase Ventilation (PCV) Inspection
1. Inspect valve for proper function. Replace valve if necessary.
2. Also, replace any worn, plugged or collapsed hoses.
steps: Inspect valve for proper function. Replace valve if necessary.
Also, replace any worn, plugged or collapsed hoses.

para_0b_p17_1.text: 1.0L (VIN 6) – Perform EGR System check. See Section 6E2 or 6E3, Chart C7.

proc_0b_p17_5.text:
Air Cleaner and PCV Filter Replacement
1. Replace the air cleaner filter every 30,000 miles (50 000 km). Replace more often under dusty conditions.
2. Replace the crankcase vent filter if so equipped.
steps: Replace the air cleaner filter every 30,000 miles (50 000 km). Replace more often under dusty conditions.
Replace the crankcase vent filter if so equipped.

para_0b_p17_2.text: Adjust timing to underhood label specifications.

proc_0b_p17_6.text:
Fuel Tank, Cap and Lines Inspection
1. Inspect fuel tank, cap and lines for damage or leaks.
2. Inspect fuel rails and injection assemblies, if so equipped.
3. Inspect fuel cap gasket for an even filler neck imprint or any damage. Replace parts as needed.
steps: Inspect fuel tank, cap and lines for damage or leaks.
Inspect fuel rails and injection assemblies, if so equipped.
Inspect fuel cap gasket for an even filler neck imprint or any damage. Replace parts as needed.

proc_0b_p17_7.text:
If equipped
1. Inspect all hoses and ducts for proper hook-up.
2. Make sure valve works properly.
steps: Inspect all hoses and ducts for proper hook-up.
Make sure valve works properly.

para_0b_p17_3.text:
Listed below are vehicle inspections and services which should be made at the frequencies indicated by either the owner or a qualified technician to help ensure proper safety, emission performance and dependability of the vehicle. Whenever repairs are necessary, have them completed at once.

Any safety related or emissions related components that could have been damaged in an accident should be inspected and all needed repairs should be performed before operating the vehicle.

para_0b_p17_4.text: Make sure the indicator points to the gear chosen.

para_0b_p19_0.text:
Inspect for interference or binding, damaged or missing parts.

Inspect all belts for cracks, fraying, wear and proper tension. Adjust or replace as needed.

When opening hood, note the operation of secondary latch. It should keep hood from opening all the way when primary latch is released. Make sure that hood closes firmly.

para_0b_p19_1.text:
Inspect belt system, including: webbing, buckles, latch plates, retractors, guide loops and anchors.

On cars with movable restraints, make sure restraints stay in the desired position.

Be sure seatbacks latch on those cars with folding seats using mechanical latches. Make sure the recliner is holding by pushing and pulling on the top of the seatback while it is reclined.

Be alert to rattles in rear of car. Make sure the spare tire, all jacking equipment, any tire inflator and any covers or doors are securely stowed at all times. Oil jack ratchet or screw mechanism after each use.

Lubricate key lock cylinder.

proc_0b_p19_0.text:
Body lubrication service
1. Lubricate all body door hinges, including the hood, fuel door and rear compartment hinges and latches, the glove and console doors, and any folding seat hardware.
2. Also lubricate the hood and door hinges, hatchback lid hinge and linkage.
steps: Lubricate all body door hinges, including the hood, fuel door and rear compartment hinges and latches, the glove and console doors, and any folding seat hardware.
Also lubricate the hood and door hinges, hatchback lid hinge and linkage.

caut_0b_p19_0.text: CAUTION: Before performing the following starter safety switch check, be sure to have enough room around the car. Then, firmly apply both the parking brake and the regular brakes. Do not use the accelerator pedal. If the engine starts, be ready to turn off the ignition promptly. Take these precautions because the car could move without warning and possibly cause personal injury or property damage.

para_0b_p19_2.text:
On automatic transaxle cars, try to start the engine in each gear. The starter should crank only in "P" (Park) or "N" (Neutral).

On manual transaxle cars, place the shift lever in "Neutral", push the clutch halfway and try to start. The starter should crank only when the clutch is fully depressed.

proc_0b_p19_1.text:
Steering column lock operation
1. While parked, try to turn key to "Lock" in each gear range. The key should turn to "Lock" only when the gear selector is in "Park" on automatic or "Reverse" on manual transaxles.
2. On all vehicles, the key should come out only in "Lock".
steps: While parked, try to turn key to "Lock" in each gear range. The key should turn to "Lock" only when the gear selector is in "Park" on automatic or "Reverse" on manual transaxles.
On all vehicles, the key should come out only in "Lock".

caut_0b_p19_1.text: CAUTION: Do not remove the ignition key when the vehicle is moving. This will lock the steering column and result in loss of ability to steer the vehicle.

caut_0b_p19_2.text: CAUTION: Before checking the holding ability of the parking brake and automatic transaxle "PARK" mechanism, park on a fairly steep hill with enough room for movement in the downhill direction. To reduce the risk of personal injury or property damage, be prepared to apply the regular brakes promptly if the car begins to move.

para_0b_p19_3.text:
To check the parking brake, with the engine running and transaxle in "Neutral", slowly remove foot pressure from the regular brake pedal (until the car is held by only the parking brake).

To check the automatic transaxle "Park" mechanism holding ability, release all brakes after shifting the transaxle to "P" (Park).

At least every spring, flush from the underbody with plain water, any corrosive materials used for ice and snow removal and dust control. Take care to thoroughly clean any areas where mud and other debris can collect. Sediment packed in closed areas of the vehicle should be loosened before being flushed.

proc_0b_p19_2.text:
Engine cooling system
1. Inspect coolant and freeze protection. If dirty or rusty, drain, flush and refill with new coolant. Keep coolant at the proper mixture for proper freeze protection, corrosion inhibitor level and best engine operating temperature.
2. Inspect hoses and replace if cracked, swollen or deteriorated. Tighten hose clamps. Clean outside of radiator and air conditioning condenser. Wash radiator filler cap and neck. To help ensure proper operation, a pressure test of both the cooling system and cap is also recommended.
steps: Inspect coolant and freeze protection. If dirty or rusty, drain, flush and refill with new coolant. Keep coolant at the proper mixture for proper freeze protection, corrosion inhibitor level and best engine operating temperature.

Inspect hoses and replace if cracked, swollen or deteriorated. Tighten hose clamps. Clean outside of radiator and air conditioning condenser. Wash radiator filler cap and neck. To help ensure proper operation, a pressure test of both the cooling system and cap is also recommended.

para_0b_p20_0.text:
Engine oil – GM Goodwrench motor oil or equivalent for API service SG Energy Conserving II of recommended viscosity.
Engine coolant – Mixture of water and good quality ethylene glycol base antifreeze conforming to GM Specification 1825M (GM Part No. 1052753).
Brake system – Delco Supreme 11 Brake Fluid (GM Part No. 1052535) or equivalent DOT-3 brake fluid.
Parking brake cable guides, underbody contact points and linkage – Chassis grease meeting requirements of GM Specification 6031M (GM Part No. 1052497).
Automatic transaxle – DEXRON® II Automatic transmission fluid (GM Part No. 1051855).
Manual transaxle – Synchromesh transmission fluid SAE 75W (GL-50) (GM Part No. 12345349).
Automatic transaxle shift linkage – Chassis grease meeting requirements of GM Specification 6031M (GM Part No. 1052497).
Manual transaxle shift linkage – Chassis grease meeting requirements of GM Specification 6031M (GM Part No. 1052497).
Clutch linkage pivot points – Chassis grease meeting requirements of GM Specification 6031M (GM Part No. 1052497).
Key lock cylinders – Lubricate with Multi-Purpose Lubricant (GM Part No. 12345120) or synthetic SAE 5W30 engine oil. (Do not use aerosols)
Chassis lubrication, Parking Brake Cables, and Wheel Bearings – Chassis grease meeting requirement of GM Specification 6031M (GM Part No. 1052497).
Hood and door hinges, or hatchback lid hinge and linkage, Rear folding seat, Fuel door hinge and Rear compartment lid hinges – Engine oil.
Windshield washer solvent – GM Optikleen washer solvent Part No. 1051515 or equivalent.
Weatherstrips – Silicone grease (GM Part No. 1052863 or equivalent).

para_1a_p21_0.text:
The base heater system is designed to provide heating, ventilation, windshield defrosting, and side window defogging. The ventilation system of this vehicle consists of a fresh air intake located at the cowl panel. Air is drawn into the vehicle through the blower case and is drawn out through ventilator outlets located near the bottom of each rear quarter panel.

The power-vent, heat, and defrost provisions of the base system are controlled within the heater and blower case assemblies. Both of these assemblies are mounted to the rear of the bulkhead, and the blower inlet is mounted to the rear of the cowl (Figure 1).

All heating system operations are actuated through the heater control assembly. There are four control levers used to operate the heating system. They are as follows (Figure 2):

proc_1a_p21_0.text:
Control Levers
1. Lever A - Mode control lever: Determines output airflow to the vehicle interior.
2. Lever B - Fresh/recirculate control lever: Used to control the amount of fresh and/or recirculated air admitted into the heating system.
3. Lever C - Temperature control lever: Used to control the amount of airflow through and/or around the heater core.
4. Lever D - Blower speed control lever (Blower switch): A five position, four speed switch used to control the amount of output airflow into the vehicle interior.
steps: Lever A - Mode control lever: Determines output airflow to the vehicle interior.
Lever B - Fresh/recirculate control lever: Used to control the amount of fresh and/or recirculated air admitted into the heating system.
Lever C - Temperature control lever: Used to control the amount of airflow through and/or around the heater core.
Lever D - Blower speed control lever (Blower switch): A five position, four speed switch used to control the amount of output airflow into the vehicle interior.

tbl_1a_p24_0.text:
CONDITION: Blower inoperative in all blower switch positions.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Fuse blown.
  - CORRECTION: Check for short, replace fuse.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty blower resistor.
  - CORRECTION: Inspect blower resistor, replace if necessary (Figure 9).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty blower motor.
  - CORRECTION: Replace blower motor (Figure 8).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty wiring or ground.
  - CORRECTION: Repair as necessary.

tbl_1a_p24_1.text:
CONDITION: Insufficient heating.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Control cables broken or binding.
  - CORRECTION: R&R control cables as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Control door (damper) broken or binding.
  - CORRECTION: R&R control door (damper) as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Heater ducts restricted or blocked.
  - CORRECTION: R&R heater ducts as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Radiator leaking or blocked.
  - CORRECTION: R&R radiator as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Heater core leaking or blocked.
  - CORRECTION: R&R Heater core as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Heater hoses leaking or blocked.
  - CORRECTION: Replace heater hoses.

para_1b_p32_0.text:
This vehicle can be provided with an optional air conditioner. This Section covers the description, service and charging procedures of the air conditioner.

The compressor performs two main functions. It compresses the low pressure refrigerant vapor from the evaporator into a high pressure, high temperature vapor. Also, the compressor pumps refrigerant and refrigerant oil through the A/C system. Complete compressor overhaul procedures can be found in Section 1D of the Service Manual. The compressor used in this vehicle is of the swash plate design.

The magnetic clutch engages and disengages the compressor when the air conditioning is on.

The condenser assembly, mounted to the right of the radiator is made up of coils which carry the refrigerant and cooling fins which provide rapid transfer of heat. The air passing through the condenser cools the high pressure refrigerant vapor causing it to condense into a liquid. Increased cooling is achieved by a condenser cooling fan mounted in front of the condenser assembly.

The receiver/dryer performs the following three functions:

A. The receiver serves as a temporary storage container for condensed-liquid refrigerant so that it is possible to supply the evaporator with the condensed-liquid refrigerant, conforming to the demand of the cooling load.

B. The dryer serves to remove the dirt and water mixed in the cycling refrigerant by means of the filter and desiccant bag.

C. The sight glass, installed atop the receiver serves to show the flowing state of the refrigerant.

The pressure switch is used to stop further rotation of the compressor by turning OFF the electric circuits to the compressor and condenser cooling fan when the cycling refrigerant has abnormally dropped or risen in pressure.

The expansion valve is a device used to convert high pressure liquid refrigerant into a low pressure state. As high pressure liquid refrigerant enters the valve from the receiver/dryer and passes through the seat and orifice, it is then expanded and ejected as a low pressure liquid. This low pressure liquid refrigerant then flows to the evaporator core where it becomes a low pressure vapor and leaves the evaporator core as such.

para_1b_p33_0.text: The evaporator is a device which cools and dehumidifies the air before it enters the vehicle. High pressure liquid refrigerant flows through the expansion valve into the low pressure area of the evaporator. The heat in the air passing through the evaporator core is lost to the cooler surface of the core, thereby cooling the air. As the process of heat loss from the air to the evaporator core surface is taking place, any moisture (humidity) in the air condenses on the outside surface of the evaporator core and is drained off as water.

para_1b_p35_0.text:
These relays operate the condenser fan and magnetic clutch. The condenser fan motor operates continuously, as long as the air conditioning (compressor), is under operation.

A device used with the engine running to operate the air conditioner only when needed and is controlled by the air conditioner amplifier.

When the evaporative temperature of the refrigerant drops to 1°C (34°F) or below, the evaporator fins will develop frost or ice, reducing the airflow, lowering the cooling capacity. The thermistor is a sensor which is used to prevent this frosting or icing of the evaporator.

The sensor, installed on the evaporator, converts the evaporator core temperature into a resistance valve which is sent to the air conditioner amplifier.

A pressure switch is used to stop the compressor operation when the cycling refrigerant is abnormally risen or dropped in pressure due to its leakage, lack, of refrigerant. The switch is installed in the high pressure line behind the receiver/dryer.

At low speeds, such as idle, operating the compressor loads the engine. To prevent stalling, the vacuum switching valve utilizes inlet manifold vacuum to increase engine idle speed. The vacuum switching valve is controlled by signals from the engine control module (ECM) and the air conditioner amplifier.

A water temperature switch is used to stop A/C operation if an engine overheat condition is detected. The switch will stop A/C operation if engine coolant temperature exceeds 108°C (226°F).

The air conditioner amplifier is used to control the operation of the vacuum switching valve, magnetic clutch and the condenser cooling fan motor based on signals received from various sensors which detect engine operating conditions. It is installed on the lower evaporator case.

With the A/C system operating, the air conditioner amplifier control functions are as follows:

para_1b_p36_0.text: The Air Conditioning Refrigerant Recovery and Recycling Station (ACR³) J 38100-B, is designed to be compatible with charging station J 23500-01 or its equivalent. The recovery and recycling operations are performed separately with the capability to recycle 136 kg. (30 lbs.) in one operation. Two 136 kg (30 lbs.) capacity reusable tanks are included with the station.

noti_1b_p36_0.text: NOTICE: The Air Conditioning Refrigerant Recovery and Recycling Station meets UL standards for moisture and contamination removal. Recovery systems which cannot meet these standards are not approved for warranty repairs. Reuse of moisture or particulate contaminated refrigerant will result in premature compressor and other component failure.

para_1b_p36_1.text: Always wear goggles and wrap a clean cloth around fittings and/or connections when doing work that involves opening the refrigerant system. If liquid refrigerant comes into contact with the skin or eyes, injury may result.

proc_1b_p36_0.text:
Figures 6 through 10
1. Before removing or replacing any of the air conditioning refrigeration lines or components, the refrigerant must be completely recovered.
2. Always use service valve and pressure gage sets during evacuation and charging procedures.
3. Do not charge while compressor system is hot.
4. Always check the A/C system for pressure with a manifold gage set to determine if refrigerant is present in the system. Performing recovery on an A/C system which is open to the atmosphere as a result of a leak would allow the recovery station to pull only air into the tank.

caut_1b_p37_0.text: CAUTION: Use authorized refillable refrigerant tanks only. Use of non-refillable tanks could cause personal injury and will void the warranty. Non-refillable tanks will affect weight platform calibration and will result in overfilled tanks. Overfilling could cause the safety valve to "pop-off" releasing a large quantity of refrigerant. If liquid refrigerant comes into contact with skin or eyes, personal injury may result.

proc_1b_p37_0.text:
Recovery Tank Installation
1. The recovery tank is supplied with 68.95-103.4 kPa (10-15 psi) of dry nitrogen to keep it clean and dry during shipment. Bleed this pressure from the tank before attaching the tank to the station by removing sealing cap and opening either valve. A new tank without the precharge of dry nitrogen indicates a leak and should be immediately returned for replacement. (See instruction manual.)
2. Place the tank on the scale platform at the rear of the station. Place the bottom lip of the tank around the outside diameter of the stand-off welded to the platform. This will center the tank automatically. Loosely fasten the strap around the tank in order to prevent the tank from tipping over.
3. Attach the red hose from the back of the station to a vacuum pump. Attach the blue hose from the back of the station to the "Liquid" part of the tank.
4. Open the "Liquid" valve on the tank. Turn on the vacuum pump. Pull a vacuum on the tank and station for approximately 10 minutes to remove all unwanted air from the tank and from the station.
5. After a vacuum has been pulled, turn off the vacuum pump and disconnect the hose from the vacuum pump. Attach the red hose to the "Vapor" port on the tank and open the "Vapor" valve.
6. Perform this procedure only when putting new tanks into service.

para_1b_p37_0.text: The weight platform should be checked for accuracy at least once a month. This calibration will ensure that the compressor will shut off when the tank is full.

caut_1b_p37_1.text: CAUTION: Use of non-refillable tanks will affect platform calibration and will result in overfilling of non-refillable tanks. Overfilling could cause the safety valve to "pop-off" releasing a large quantity of refrigerant. If liquid refrigerant comes into contact with the skin or eyes, personal injury may result.

proc_1b_p37_1.text:
Weight Platform Calibration
1. Place a known 45 lb. weight on the center of the platform. The switch should just trip or be very close to tripping with this amount of weight.
2. If the switch is tripped, the mechanism is in calibration. If the switch does not trip, place a 2 lbs. weight on top of the 45 lbs. weight. This weight should definitely trip the switch. If the switch is tripped, the mechanism must be recalibrated.
3. To recalibrate, remove 2 lbs. weight and loosen the lock nut of the trip screw. Turn the trip screw until the switch just trips. Remove the 45 lbs. weight.
4. To check, place 45 lb. weight on the platform and repeat step No. 2. Repeat step No. 3 as required. Tighten lock nut of the trip screw when recalibration is complete.

para_1b_p37_1.text:
The control panel contains the switches and monitors as follows:

• Main Power Switch--supplies electrical power to the control panel.
• Compressor Start Switch--energizes compressor.
• Compressor On Light--lights when compressor is energized.
• High Pressure Light--lights when there is pressure above 1827 kPa (265 psi) in system.
• Recovery Full Tank Light--lights when recovery tank is full.
• Recycling System Start Switch--energizes recycling pump.
• Recycling Pump On Light--lights when recycling pump is energized.

para_1b_p38_0.text:
Moisture Indicator--tells when refrigerant is wet or dry, or when filter core needs to be replaced if no color change occurs in 2 hours of recycling.
Filter Monitor--tells when filter is plugged.

Guidelines for replacing the filter drier core:
• For proper contamination removal, replace the filter drier core after recycling a maximum of 300 pounds of refrigerant.
• If the moisture indicator does not change to green after two hours of filtering time, the filter drier core is saturated with moisture and should be changed.
• If the filter drier core becomes clogged with particulates, the filter monitor needle will register in the red zone; this also means the filter core should be changed.

caut_1b_p38_0.text: CAUTION: Steps 2 and 3 are critical to avoid possible hazardous release of refrigerant. If liquid refrigerant comes into contact with the skin or eyes, injury may result.

noti_1b_p38_0.text: NOTICE: Use only authorized filter drier cores. Use of any other cores will give unsatisfactory results and will void the warranty.

proc_1b_p38_0.text:
Filter Drier Core Replacement
1. Close the liquid valve on the tank.
2. Attach the blue hose to the recovery inlet of the station.
3. Start the compressor and recover all refrigerant remaining in the station into the storage tank. The compressor will automatically shut off when recovery is complete.
4. Remove the bolts holding the filter cap to the shell. The filter drier core and bracket are attached to the filter cap.
5. Remove the tie rods from the filter cap and remove the filter drier core from the brackets.
6. Clean all internal parts.
7. Remove the filter drier core from the sealed can. Do not remove the filter from the can until ready to install it in the system.
8. If the filter cap gasket is damaged, replace it. Replacement gaskets are included with each filter drier core.
9. Center the spring on the filter cap and place the upper bracket on the spring with the center boss of the bracket fitting into the coil and the edges of the plate facing up.
10. Position the filter drier core on the upper bracket. Position the screened lower bracket on the filter drier core and install the tie rods. Tighten the tie rods until they stop.
11. Install the filter drier core assembly into the filter drier shell. Replace the bolts and tighten evenly, in the star pattern, to a torque of 20 N·m (15 lb. ft.).
12. When the core has been replaced and the filter drier reassembled, attach the blue hose to the vacuum pump and start the vacuum pump. Run the pump for approximately 10 minutes to remove all air.
13. Turn off the vacuum pump and disconnect the hose.
14. Reattach the blue hose to the "Liquid" port of the tank and open the "Liquid" valve on the tank. You are now ready to continue recycling with the station.

Remove the tie rods from the filter cap and remove the filter drier core from the brackets.
Clean all internal parts.

Install the filter drier core assembly into the filter drier shell. Replace the bolts and tighten evenly, in the star pattern, to a torque of 20 N·m (15 lb. ft.).
When the core has been replaced and the filter drier reassembled, attach the blue hose to the vacuum pump and start the vacuum pump. Run the pump for approximately 10 minutes to remove all air.
Turn off the vacuum pump and disconnect the hose.
Reattach the blue hose to the "Liquid" port of the tank and open the "Liquid" valve on the tank. You are now ready to continue recycling with the station.

para_1b_p38_1.text:
Tools Required:
  J 38100-B Refrigerant Recovery and Recycling System
  J 23500-01 Refrigerant Charging Station
  J 23575-01 Manifold Gage Set
  J 36846 Hose Adapter Kit

The refrigerant system must be discharged using an Air Conditioning Refrigerant Recovery and Recycling System J 38100-B. Following this procedure, the refrigerant system can then be evacuated and charged using air conditioning service charging station J 23500-01 or equivalent, or the manifold and gage set J 23575-01 and refrigerant.

Evacuation lines from the recovery system and the charging lines from the charging station or manifold and gage set require the use of adapters to connect to the A/C system low-side service fitting. A J 36846 hose adapter kit contains all the adapters that should be required. Refer to "Special Tools" at the end of this section.

noti_1b_p39_0.text: NOTICE: Failure to check for residual oil from the previous recovery can result in adding extra oil to the current vehicle being serviced. This will result in reduced performance and possible compressor damage. Refer to "Compressor Oil Distribution" earlier in this section.

proc_1b_p39_0.text:
Filter Drier Core Replacement
[CONTINUED FROM PREVIOUS PAGE]
1. Start vehicle and run with A/C on for 2 minutes, then attach a manifold gage set to the A/C system and attach the recovery station inlet hose to the center fitting of the manifold gage set.
2. Open both valves of the manifold gage set. Make certain the refrigerant tank "Vapor" valve and the "Liquid" valve are open.
3. Plug the station into a suitable AC power outlet and turn on the "Main Power" switch.
4. Depress the "Compressor Start" switch. The amber "Compressor On" light will come on and the compressor will start. The compressor will shut off automatically when recovery is complete. Wait 2 minutes and check for pressure rise. If a pressure rise occurs, depress the "Compressor Start" switch and allow to repeat the recovery.
5. To drain the accumulator of A/C system oil, open the "Accumulator Pressurizing" valve for 15 seconds to allow some compressor discharge pressure back into the accumulator. Open the oil drain valve slowly and drain the accumulator. When the oil stops draining, close the oil drain valve. Do not allow the accumulator to completely depressurize.
6. Although refrigerant can be filtered and recycled at any time, it is recommended to recycle when the tank is full for greatest efficiency. Two tanks are provided so you can fill one, recycle it, and use it for charging systems while filling the second tank. When the recovery tank is full, the "Trip Switch" at the bottom of the weight platform will de-energize the compressor, and the "Tank Full" light will come on. Close both tank valves, remove the tank from the platform, and replace with another refillabe tank or recycle the first tank. If the tank isn't immediately recycled, take precautions to prevent its use in A/C systems BEFORE it is recycled. Additional tanks (part #17105) are available, see the "Replacement Parts" section of your operator's manual.

proc_1b_p39_1.text:
Refrigerant Recycling
1. Plug the station into a suitable AC power outlet and turn on the main power switch.
2. Open both valves on the recovery tank.
  • For efficiency, recycle full tanks of refrigerant.
3. Turn the "Recycle Start" switch to on. The amber "Recycle On" light will come on and the refrigerant pump will start.
4. Refrigerant will be seen going through the "Moisture Indicator" at start up. If there is a sufficient supply of refrigerant, the bubbles will clear after a few seconds. When the bubbles clear from the "Moisture Indicator," the refrigerant pump is operating at maximum efficiency.
5. Allow the station to operate until the dot in the center of the "Moisture Indicator" turns green. The "Moisture Indicator Dot" should change to the shade indicated on the reference decal. Always run the recycling system a minimum of 30 minutes. If the "Moisture Indicator" starts out yellow, it could take as long as two hours to turn green, depending on the moisture content of the refrigerant.
6. Shut off the station when recycling is complete. This is not an automatic function.
steps: Plug the station into a suitable AC power outlet and turn on the main power switch.

Open both valves on the recovery tank.
  • For efficiency, recycle full tanks of refrigerant.
Turn the "Recycle Start" switch to on. The amber "Recycle On" light will come on and the refrigerant pump will start.

Refrigerant will be seen going through the "Moisture Indicator" at start up. If there is a sufficient supply of refrigerant, the bubbles will clear after a few seconds. When the bubbles clear from the "Moisture Indicator," the refrigerant pump is operating at maximum efficiency.

Allow the station to operate until the dot in the center of the "Moisture Indicator" turns green. The "Moisture Indicator Dot" should change to the shade indicated on the reference decal. Always run the recycling system a minimum of 30 minutes. If the "Moisture Indicator" starts out yellow, it could take as long as two hours to turn green, depending on the moisture content of the refrigerant.
Shut off the station when recycling is complete. This is not an automatic function.

proc_1b_p39_2.text:
Refrigerant Recycling
1. For proper contaminant removal, replace the filter drier core after recycling a maximum of 138 kg (300 pounds) of refrigerant.
2. If the moisture indicator does not change to green after two hours of filtering time, the filter drier core is saturated with moisture and should be changed.
3. If the filter drier core becomes clogged with particulates, the filter monitor needle will register in the red zone; this also means the filter core should be changed.
4. If any of the recovered refrigerant being recycled is from a system with a hermetic motor burnout, run the recycling system for a minimum of 24 hours. Check the A/C

proc_1b_p40_0.text:
Operating Hints
1. When using the recovery station in conjunction with a charging station, attach the center port hose of the manifold gage set to the inlet port of the ACR³ station. Then follow the normal operating procedures for the recovery/recycling station.
2. When using the ACR³ station in conjunction with an automatic charging station, attach the exhaust hose to the inlet of the ACR³ station. On automatic A/C service stations, a hole has been added at the rear of the cabinet for convenient access to the exhaust hose. On older style stations, you must open the front doors of the cabinet to reach the exhaust hose.

After attaching the exhaust hose to the ACR³ station, depress the "Main Power" switch on the automatic charging station and depress the "Exhaust" switch. Then follow the normal operating procedures for the ACR³ station.
3. Air is automatically vented from the recovery tank during recycling. This feature eliminates the need to "purge" hoses before recovering the refrigerant.
4. Operating the engine with the A/C off during recovery may reduce the recovery time by increasing the temperature in the accumulator area.
5. To help prevent the escape of refrigerant to the atmosphere, the ACR³ can be attached to a "Dial-A-Charge" cylinder top vent port when filling the cylinder.
6. Always check the ACR³ station for residual oil from the previous recovery. Refer to Step 5 in "Refrigerant Recovery" earlier in this section.

proc_1b_p40_1.text:
EVACUATING SYSTEM
- Whenever A/C system is exposed to atmospheric air, it must be evacuated by a vacuum pump. With manifold set attached to A/C system, it should be evacuated for approximately 15 minutes (Figure 11).
- Connect high and low charging hoses of manifold gage set as follows:
3. Attach center charging hose of manifold gage set to vacuum pump.
4. Operate vacuum pump and then open suction-side valve (LO) of manifold gage set. If there is no blockage in system, there will be an indication on high pressure gage. When this occurs, open the other valve (HI) of gage set.
5. Approx. 10 minutes later, the low pressure gage should show a vacuum lower than 760 mm Hg, providing no leakage exists.
6. Evacuation should be carried out for a minimum of 15 minutes.
7. Continue evacuation until low pressure gage indicates a vacuum of no less than 760 mm Hg, then close both valves.
8. Stop vacuum pump. Disconnect center charging hose from pump inlet. System is now ready to be charged with refrigerant.

6. Evacuation should be carried out for a minimum of 15 minutes.
7. Continue evacuation until low pressure gage indicates a vacuum of no less than 760 mm Hg, then close both valves.
8. Stop vacuum pump. Disconnect center charging hose from pump inlet. System is now ready to be charged with refrigerant.

caut_1b_p41_0.text: CAUTION: Air conditioning systems contain Refrigerant-12. This is a chemical mixture which requires special handling procedures to avoid personal injury.

para_1b_p41_0.text:
Always wear goggles and wrap a clean cloth around fittings, valves and connections when performing work that involves opening the refrigerant system. Always work in a well ventilated area and do not weld or steam clean on or near any vehicle when installing air conditioning lines or components.

If Refrigerant-12 should come in contact with any part of the body, flush the exposed area with cold water and immediately seek medical help.

All Refrigerant-12 drums are shipped with a heavy metal screw cap. The purpose of the cap is to protect the valve and safety plug from damage. It is good practice to replace the cap after each use of the drum for this same reason.

If it is necessary to transport or carry any container of Refrigerant-12 in a vehicle, do not carry it in the passenger compartment.

If the occasion arises to fill a drum of Refrigerant-12, never fill the drum completely. Space should always be allowed above the liquid for expansion.

All metal tubing lines should be free of dents or kinks to prevent loss of system capacity due to line restriction.
• The flexible hose lines should never be bent to a radius of less than four (4) times the diameter of the hose.
• The flexible hose lines should never be allowed to come within a distance of 63.5 mm (2.5 in.) of the exhaust manifold.

• Flexible hose lines should be inspected regularly for leaks or brittleness and replaced with new lines if deterioration or leaking is found.
• When disconnecting any fitting in the refrigerant system, the system must first be discharged of all refrigerant-12. However proceed very cautiously regardless of gage readings. Open very slowly, keeping face and hands away so that no injury can occur if there happens to be liquid Refrigerant-12 in the line. If pressure is noticed when fitting is loosened, allow it to bleed off.

caut_1b_p41_1.text: CAUTION: Should Refrigerant-12 strike your eye(s), consult a doctor immediately.

caut_1b_p41_2.text: CAUTION: DO NOT USE YOUR HAND TO RUB THE AFFECTED EYE(S). Instead, use quantities of fresh cold water to splash over the affected area to gradually raise its temperature above the freezing point.

caut_1b_p41_3.text: CAUTION: Obtain proper treatment as soon as possible from a doctor or eye specialist. Should the liquid R-12 touch the skin, the injury must be treated the same as skin which has been frostbitten or frozen.

proc_1b_p41_0.text:
Figures 11 thru 15
1. Make sure the evacuation process has the correct routing (Figure 12).
2. Connect manifold gage set in position. Remove slack in the hose connected to the drum refrigerent valve to purge charging line. Then open low pressure-side valve.
NOTICE: Make sure the high pressure-side valve is closed securely.
3. Start the engine and let run at fast idle (1000 ± 50 rpm) with the air conditioner on.
4. Charge A/C system with refrigerant in vapor state only. Refrigerant drum must be upright.
5. If refrigerant is found to be escaping, a "Hiss" sound may be heard between refrigerant drum and charging line. Immediately close refrigerant drum valve as well as manifold gage set low pressure valve (Figure 13).
6. Check charging line fitting at drum for looseness. Tighten as necessary and continue charging.
7. After the system has been charged to the specified amount of 500g (17.6 oz), or when the low and high pressure gages have indicated approx. 2 and 15 kg/cm² (28 and 213 psi), close the low pressure-side hand valve of the manifold gage set (Figure 14). At this time, the receiver/dry should show no bubbles in the sight glass, indicating that the system if fully charged (Figure 15).

proc_1b_p41_1.text:
REMOVING MANIFOLD GAGE SET
1. When the A/C system has been charged with the specified amount of refrigerant, remove the manifold gage set as follows:
2. Close the low-pressure-side valve of the manifold gage set. (The high-pressure-side valve is closed continuously during the process of charging.)
3. Fully tighten the handle of the refrigerant drum valve.
4. Stop the engine.
5. Using a shop rag, remove charging hoses from the service valves. This operation must be performed rapidly.
6. Put caps on the service valves of the system.

Put caps on the service valves of the system.

para_1b_p42_0.text:
Whenever a refrigerant leak is suspected in the system or when service operations performed result in disturbing lines or connections, it is advisable to test for leaks. Common sense should be used in performing any refrigerant leak test, since the need and extent of any such test will, in general, depend upon the nature of a complaint and the type of service performed on the system.

There are a number of fittings and locations throughout the air conditioning system where a liquid leak detector solution may be used to pinpoint refrigerant leaks. By merely applying the solution to the area in question with a swab, bubbles will form within seconds if there is a leak.

For restricted access areas, such as sections of the evaporator and condenser, an electronic leak detector, such as J29547 or equivalent is more practical for determining and locating leaks.

Electronic leak testers are used in areas that are difficult to test with liquid leak detectors due to poor visibility or inaccessibility.

Refrigerant Leak Detector J 29547 is a portable, battery operated model. This model provides audible signals to indicate leak detection (Figure 16).

  - CORRECTION: Check compressor.

  - CORRECTION: Check charge of refrigerant.

noti_1b_p46_0.text: NOTICE: When servicing air conditioning system, the following rules must be observed.

proc_1b_p46_0.text:
WIRING PRECAUTIONS
1. Disconnect battery negative terminal.
2. Connect wire connectors securely.
3. Use approved electrical or original clamps to reconnect air conditioner wire harness to main wire harness.
4. If harness has been disconnected or removed while servicing, install and/or connect harness in original position.
5. During installation, be careful not to pinch air conditioner wire harnesses.
6. When additional wires are required, wire with the same gauge of wire. Tape each connection.
7. Keep wire harness away from any moving or high temperature components.
8. Keep connecting fittings away from fuel lines.
9. Make sure wire harness does not come in contact with sharp edges or corners.
steps: Disconnect battery negative terminal.
Connect wire connectors securely.
Use approved electrical or original clamps to reconnect air conditioner wire harness to main wire harness.
If harness has been disconnected or removed while servicing, install and/or connect harness in original position.
During installation, be careful not to pinch air conditioner wire harnesses.
When additional wires are required, wire with the same gauge of wire. Tape each connection.
Keep wire harness away from any moving or high temperature components.
Keep connecting fittings away from fuel lines.
Make sure wire harness does not come in contact with sharp edges or corners.

proc_1b_p46_1.text:
REFRIGERANT LINES PRECAUTIONS
1. Never use heat for bending pipes. When bending a pipe, try to make its bending radius as slight as possible.
2. Keep internal parts of air conditioner free from moisture and dirt. When disconnecting any line from system, install a blind plug or cap to the fitting immediately.
3. When connecting hoses and pipes, apply a few drops of refrigerant oil to seats of flared nuts and "O" rings.
4. When tightening or loosening a fitting, use two wrenches: one for turning and the other for support.
5. Be sure of correct line installation to the receiver/dryer. Inlet fitting must be connected to condenser outlet fitting.
6. Tighten flared nuts according to Figure 17.
7. Route drain hose so that drained water does not contact vehicle components.

para_1b_p46_0.text: The following procedure can be used for quickly checking whether the A/C system has a proper charge of refrigerant. Run engine at fast idle and operate air conditioner at max cooling capacity for a few minutes. Then look at sight glass on top of the receiver/dryer. Compare what is observed with symptoms listed in checking Refrigerant Charge Chart within this section.

REFRIGERANT CHARGE CHART
CONDITION: 1) Bubbles observed in sight glass. | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: Insufficient charge of refrigerant in system. | CORRECTION: Check system for leaks with a leak tester.
CONDITION: 2) No bubbles observed in sight glass. | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: Insufficient charge of refrigerant in system. | CORRECTION: Refer to items 3 and 4.
CONDITION: 3) No temperature difference between inlet and outlet. | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: Empty or nearly empty system. | CORRECTION: Evacuate and charge system and then check it for leaks.
CONDITION: CONDITION | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT | CORRECTION: CORRECTION
CONDITION: CONDITION | CORRECTION: with a leak tester.
CONDITION: 4) Noticeable temperature difference between compressor inlet and outlet. | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: Proper or too much charge of refrigerant in system. | CORRECTION: Refer to items 5 and 6.
CONDITION: 5) When air conditioner is turned OFF, refrigerant in sight glass clears immediately and remains clear. | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: Too much charge of refrigerant in system. | CORRECTION: Discharge excess refrigerant.
CONDITION: 6) When air conditioner is turned OFF, refrigerant in sight glass produces foam and remains clear. | CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT: Proper charge of refrigerant in system. | CORRECTION: NO CORRECTION NEEDED BECAUSE CHARGE OF REFRIGERANT IS NORMAL.

para_1b_p47_0.text: The condenser fins for blockage, the condenser fittings for leakage and the condenser fins for damage. Clogged condenser fins should be washed with water and should be dried with compressed air.

noti_1b_p47_0.text: NOTICE: Be careful not to damage the condenser fins. If any condenser fins are bent, straighten them using a screwdriver or pair of pliers. If any leakage is found from the fitting or tube, repair or replace the condenser.

proc_1b_p47_0.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Slowly discharge and recover refrigerant from the high pressure-side service valve. Quick discharge causes compressor oil to escape together with refrigerant. So, care must be used to avoid any quick discharge. Refer to "Air Conditioning Refrigerant Recovery and Recycling (ACR³) Station Set Up and Refrigerant Recovery" earlier in this section.
2. Compressor delivery hose from condenser inlet fitting and receiver/dryer outlet line at fitting above the condenser cooling fan. As soon as above hose and line are disconnected, cap opened fittings so that moisture and dust do not enter the condenser.
3. Hood latch and hood lock assembly from vehicle. Refer to BUMPERS, SHEET METAL AND FRAME (SECTION 2).
4. Condenser cooling fan electrical connector.
5. Condenser assembly attaching bolts and remove the condenser, condenser cooling fan and receiver/dryer as an assembly.
6. Receiver/dryer inlet fitting from condenser assembly.
7. Receiver/dryer bracket attaching screw and receiver/dryer from condenser assembly.
8. Condenser cooling fan mounting bolts and fan from the condenser assembly.

noti_1b_p47_1.text: NOTICE: Exercise care not to damage fins of the condenser during removal.

proc_1b_p47_1.text:
Install or Connect
1. Condenser cooling fan to condenser assembly and condenser cooling fan mounting bolts to fan.
2. Receiver/dryer to condenser assembly and receiver/dryer bracket attaching screw to bracket.
3. Receiver/dryer inlet fitting to condenser assembly.
4. Receiver/dryer, condenser cooling fan and condenser as an assembly to vehicle.
5. Condenser cooling fan electrical connector.
6. Hood lock member and hood latch to vehicle.
7. Compressor delivery hose to inlet fitting and receiver/dryer outlet line at fitting above condenser cooling fan.
8. Evacuate and charge refrigerant system. See "Evacuating System" and "Charging Procedure" earlier in this section.

2. Receiver/dryer to condenser assembly and receiver/dryer bracket attaching screw to bracket.

Refer to "Air Conditioning Refrigerant Recovery and Recycling (ACR³) Station Set Up and Refrigerant Recovery" earlier in this section.

proc_1b_p48_1.text:
Inspect
1. Connect manifold gage set to A/C system (See procedure in this section).
2. Run engine at fast idle with A/C switch ON.
3. Verify that the low pressure gage shows a reading of 0.5 kg/cms - 5.0 kg/cm² (7 - 70 psi).
steps: Connect manifold gage set to A/C system (See procedure in this section).
Run engine at fast idle with A/C switch ON.
Verify that the low pressure gage shows a reading of 0.5 kg/cms - 5.0 kg/cm² (7 - 70 psi).

noti_1b_p48_2.text: NOTICE: If reading is too low (i.e., below 0.5 kg/cm², 7 psi), check the expansion valve and/or the receiver/dryer and replace as necessary. If reading is too high (i.e., above 5.0 kg/cm², 70 psi), tighten the remote bulb holder to replace the expansion valve.

proc_1b_p49_3.text:
Inspect (Expansion Valve)
1. Connect the manifold gage set to the expansion valve and its charging hose to the refrigerant drum (Figure 23).
2. Soak the remote bulb of the expansion valve in water contained in a water pan (Figure 23).
3. Close both the low and high pressure valves.
4. Open valve to the refrigerant drum.
5. Open the high pressure valve to adjust the pressure to approx. 5.0 kg/cm² (70 psi).
6. Read the pressure indicated on the low pressure gage. At the same time, use a thermometer to measure the water temperature.
7. Use the pressure temperature relationship chart to check whether the above measurements fall within the shaded acceptable range (Figure 24).

3. Close both the low and high pressure valves.
4. Open valve to the refrigerant drum.
5. Open the high pressure valve to adjust the pressure to approx. 5.0 kg/cm² (70 psi).
6. Read the pressure indicated on the low pressure gage. At the same time, use a thermometer to measure the water temperature.
7. Use the pressure temperature relationship chart to check whether the above measurements fall within the shaded acceptable range (Figure 24).

para_1b_p50_0.text: The thermistor is a temperature sensor used to measure the temperature of discharged air from the evaporator. As air temperature decreases, the resistance of the sensor increases. When air temperature becomes lower than 1°C (34°F), the signal sent from the thermistor to the air conditioner amplifier will cause the amplifier to disengage the A/C magnetic clutch. This is to prevent the evaporator from freezing.

proc_1b_p52_3.text:
Inspect
1. Check the vacuum switching valve for proper vacuum continuity using a 12V supply as shown in Figure 29.
2. Check the vacuum switching valve for short circuit condition using an ohmmeter. No continuity should exist between each terminal and the valve housing (Figure 30).
3. Check the vacuum switching valve for open circuit condition by measuring the resistance between terminals (Figure 31).
4. If any of the previous checks are not satisfactory, the vacuum switching valve must be replaced.

para_1b_p54_1.text: After finishing all repairs, make sure to carry out the performance test of the air conditioning system as follows:

proc_1b_p54_3.text:
PERFORMANCE TEST
1. Connect the high and low side charging hose of manifold gage set to the service valves.
2. Run the engine and keep the engine speed at 2,000 rpm.
3. Operate the air conditioner and set the blower switch at "HI" and the temperature lever at "COOL."
4. Keep all the windows and doors open.
5. Insert a dry bulb thermometer in the cool air outlet and place the psychrometer (dry and wet bulb thermometer) close to the inlet of evaporator (Figure 34).
6. The high pressure gage reading should be within the specified pressure range, 14.0 - 15.5 kg/cm² (200 - 200 psi).
7. The dry bulb thermometer at the air inlet should be within 25 - 35°C (77 - 95°F). Under the above conditions, operate the air conditioning system until a stabilized condition on high and low pressure gages and the thermometers have been established.
steps: 1. Connect the high and low side charging hose of manifold gage set to the service valves.
2. Run the engine and keep the engine speed at 2,000 rpm.
3. Operate the air conditioner and set the blower switch at "HI" and the temperature lever at "COOL."
4. Keep all the windows and doors open.
5. Insert a dry bulb thermometer in the cool air outlet and place the psychrometer (dry and wet bulb thermometer) close to the inlet of evaporator (Figure 34).
6. The high pressure gage reading should be within the specified pressure range, 14.0 - 15.5 kg/cm² (200 - 200 psi).

7. The dry bulb thermometer at the air inlet should be within 25 - 35°C (77 - 95°F). Under the above conditions, operate the air conditioning system until a stabilized condition on high and low pressure gages and the thermometers have been established.

proc_1b_p54_4.text:
How To Read The Standard Performance Curve
1. Read the psychrometer at the inlet and obtain the relative humidity from psychrometric chart (Figures 36 and 37).
2. Measure the dry bulb temperature at the cool air outlet, then find out the difference between the inlet and outlet temperature (Figures 36 and 37).
3. Check the relative humidity-to-temperature difference relationship using Figure 35 as an example of 17°C (62.6°F) temperature difference and 60% relative humidity. The intersection point of these measurements fall within the shaded area between the 2 lines. Therefore, cooling performance can be considered satisfactory.
steps: 1. Read the psychrometer at the inlet and obtain the relative humidity from psychrometric chart (Figures 36 and 37).
2. Measure the dry bulb temperature at the cool air outlet, then find out the difference between the inlet and outlet temperature (Figures 36 and 37).

3. Check the relative humidity-to-temperature difference relationship using Figure 35 as an example of 17°C (62.6°F) temperature difference and 60% relative humidity. The intersection point of these measurements fall within the shaded area between the 2 lines. Therefore, cooling performance can be considered satisfactory.

para_1b_p55_0.text:
Measure wet-bulb temperature and dry-bulb temperature on the psychrometer at the evaporator air inlet, follow lines at those temperatures and read the relative humidity curve at their intersection.

Example:
If dry-bulb temperature is 90°F and wet-bulb temperature is 78°F, the relative humidity is 60%.

para_1b_p56_0.text:
Measure wet-bulb temperature and dry-bulb temperature on the psychrometer at the evaporator air inlet, follow lines at those temperatures and read the relative humidity curve at their intersection.

Example:
If dry-bulb temperature is 25°C and wet-bulb temperature is 19.5°C, the relative humidity is 60%.

para_1d_p58_0.text:
When servicing the compressor, keep dirt or foreign material from getting on or into the compressor parts and system. Clean tools and a clean work area are important for proper service. The compressor connections and the outside of the compressor should be cleaned before any "On-Vehicle" repair, or before removal of the compressor. The parts must be kept clean at all times and any parts to be reassembled should be cleaned with Trichloroethane, naphtha, kerosene or equivalent solvent and dried with dry air. Use only lint free cloths to wipe parts.

The operations described below are based on bench overhaul with compressor removed from the car, except as noted. They have been prepared in order of accessibility of the components. When compressor is removed from the car for servicing, the oil remaining in the compressor should be discarded and new refrigerant oil added to the compressor.

Minor repair procedures may be done on the car without discharging the system. Major repair procedures require that the system be discharged of refrigerant.

Compressor troubles can be roughly differentiated into gas leakage, noise and insufficient pressure. Compressor gas leakages are in most cases those that develop from the shaft seal. When detecting gas leakage, always use a leak tester. If a small amount of oil seeps out from the shaft seal, there is no necessity of replacing the seal. The shaft seal has been designed to allow a small amount of oil to leak out for lubricating purpose. Thus, the shaft seal should be replaced only when a large amount of compressor oil is leaking out or when gas leakage is discovered by use of gas tester. In regard to noise and insufficient pressure, repairs should be made only after performing correct diagnosis of the trouble.

proc_1d_p59_1.text:
Compressor and Magnetic Clutch Inspection
4. Check compressor for following:
   a. High pressure gage reading is not low and low pressure gage reading is not higher than normal.
   b. Metallic sound.
   c. Leakage from shaft seal.
   If any of the above checks indicate a defect, repair the compressor.
5. Check magnetic clutch.
   a. Inspect the pressure plate and the rotor for signs of oil.
   b. Check the clutch bearings for noise and grease leakage.
   c. Using an ohmmeter, measure the resistance of the stator coil between the clutch lead wire and ground at 25°C (77°F). If the resistance is not within tolerance, replace the coil.
   d. The standard resistance has a valve of 3.2± 0.2 ohms.
steps:
4. Check compressor for following:
   a. High pressure gage reading is not low and low pressure gage reading is not higher than normal.
   b. Metallic sound.
   c. Leakage from shaft seal.
   If any of the above checks indicate a defect, repair the compressor.

5. Check magnetic clutch.
   a. Inspect the pressure plate and the rotor for signs of oil.
   b. Check the clutch bearings for noise and grease leakage.
   c. Using an ohmmeter, measure the resistance of the stator coil between the clutch lead wire and ground at 25°C (77°F). If the resistance is not within tolerance, replace the coil.
   d. The standard resistance has a valve of 3.2± 0.2 ohms.

noti_2_p74_0.text:
NOTICE: Fasteners are important attaching parts in that they could affect the performance of vital components and systems, and/or could result in major repair expense. They must be replaced with one of the same part number or with an equivalent part if replacement becomes necessary.
Do not use a replacement part of lesser quality or substitute design. Torque values must be used as specified during reassembly to ensure proper retention of these parts.

para_2_p74_0.text:
The front and rear bumpers are constructed with a full length support member secured directly to the body. The absorber extends the full length of the bumpers and is placed between the support member and the bumper covers.

Anti-corrosion materials have been applied to the interior surfaces of some metal panels to provide rust resistance. When servicing these panels, areas on which this material has been disturbed should be properly recoated with service-type anti-corrosion material.

Underbody components must be properly aligned to ensure correct suspension location. In case of collision damage, it is important that the underbody be checked thoroughly and, if necessary, realigned in order to accurately establish proper dimensions.
Refer to the Body Service Manual (Section 3) for specifications and service procedures on unitized construction bodies.
Since each part adds to the strength of the body, it is essential that proper welding, sealing, and rustproofing methods be used during service. The underbody components should be rustproofed whenever body repair operations which destroy or damage the original rustproofing are completed. When rustproofing critical underbody components, it is essential to use a good quality type of air-dry primer (such as corrosion-resistant zinc chromate). Do not use combination-type primer-surfacers.

para_3_p79_0.text: Since the problems in steering, suspension, wheels and tires involve several systems, they must all be considered when diagnosing a complaint. To avoid using the wrong symptom, always road test the car first. Proceed with the following preliminary inspections and correct any defects which are found.

proc_3_p79_0.text:
Preliminary Inspections
1. Inspect tires for proper pressure and uneven wear.
2. Raise car on a hoist and inspect front and rear suspension and rack and pinion for loose or damaged parts.
3. Spin front wheels. Inspect for out-of-round tires, out-of-balance tires, bent rims, loose and/or rough wheel bearings.
steps: Inspect tires for proper pressure and uneven wear.
Raise car on a hoist and inspect front and rear suspension and rack and pinion for loose or damaged parts.
Spin front wheels. Inspect for out-of-round tires, out-of-balance tires, bent rims, loose and/or rough wheel bearings.

tbl_3_p79_1.text:
Preliminary Inspections
Condition: Car Pulls (leads)
  - Possible Cause: 1. Mismatched or uneven tires.
  - Correction: Replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Tires not adequately inflated.
  - Correction: Adjust tire pressure.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Broken or sagging springs.
  - Correction: Replace spring.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Radial tire lateral force.
  - Correction: Replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Disturbed front end alignment.
  - Correction: Check and adjust front end alignment.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Disturbed rear wheel alignment.
  - Correction: Check and adjust rear wheel alignment.
  - Possible Cause: 7. Brake dragging in one road wheel.
  - Correction: Repair front brake.
  - Possible Cause: 8. Loose, bent or broken front or rear suspension parts.
  - Correction: Tighten or replace suspension part.

tbl_3_p80_0.text:
GENERAL DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Abnormal or Excessive Tire Wear
  - Possible Cause: 1. Sagging or broken springs.
  - Correction: Replace spring.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Tire out of balance.
  - Correction: Adjust balance or replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Disturbed front end alignment.
  - Correction: Check front end alignment.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Faulty shock absorber.
  - Correction: Replace shock absorber.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Hard driving.
  - Correction: Replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Overloaded car.
  - Correction: Replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 7. Lack of tire rotation.
  - Correction: Replace or rotate tire.
  - Possible Cause: 8. Worn or loose road wheel bearings.
  - Correction: Replace wheel bearing.
  - Possible Cause: 9. Wobbly wheel or tire.
  - Correction: Replace wheel or tire.
  - Possible Cause: 10. Tires not adequately inflated.
  - Correction: Adjust the pressure.

tbl_3_p80_1.text:
GENERAL DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Wheel Tramp
  - Possible Cause: 1. Blister or bump on tire.
  - Correction: Replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Improper shock absorber action.
  - Correction: Replace shock absorber.

tbl_3_p80_2.text:
GENERAL DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Shimmy, Shake or Vibration
  - Possible Cause: 1. Tire or wheel out of balance.
  - Correction: Balance wheels or replace tire and/or wheel.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Loose wheel bearings.
  - Correction: Replace wheel bearing.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Worn tie rod ends.
  - Correction: Replace tie rod end.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Worn lower ball joints.
  - Correction: Replace front suspension control arm.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Excessive wheel runout.
  - Correction: Repair or replace wheel and/or tire.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Blister or bump on tire.
  - Correction: Replace tire.
  - Possible Cause: 7. Excessively loaded radial runout of tire/wheel assembly.
  - Correction: Replace tire or wheel.
  - Possible Cause: 8. Disturbed front end alignment.
  - Correction: Check front end alignment.
  - Possible Cause: 9. Loose or worn steering linkage.
  - Correction: Tighten or replace steering linkage.
  - Possible Cause: 10. Loose steering gear case bolts.
  - Correction: Tighten case bolts.

tbl_3_p80_3.text:
GENERAL DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Hard Steering
  - Possible Cause: 1. Bind in tie rod end ball studs or lower ball joints.
  - Correction: Replace tie rod end or front suspension control arm.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Disturbed front end alignment.
  - Correction: Check front end alignment.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Rack and pinion adjustments.
  - Correction: Check and adjust rack and pinion torque.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Tire not adequately inflated.
  - Correction: Inflate tires to proper pressure.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Bind in steering column.
  - Correction: Repair or replace.

tbl_3_p81_0.text:
GENERAL DIAGNOSIS CHART C
Condition: Too Much Play In Steering
  - Possible Cause: 1. Wheel bearings worn.
  - Correction: Replace wheel bearing.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Rack and pinion attachments loose.
  - Correction: Tighten or repair.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Rack and pinion adjustments.
  - Correction: Check and adjust rack and pinion torque.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Worn steering shaft joints.
  - Correction: Replace joint.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Worn tie rod ends or tie rod inside ball joints.
  - Correction: Replace tie rod end or tie rod.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Worn lower ball joints.
  - Correction: Replace front suspension control arm.

tbl_3_p81_1.text:

Condition: Poor Returnability
  - Possible Cause: 1. Bind in tie rod end ball studs.
  - Correction: Replace tie rod end.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Bind in ball joints.
  - Correction: Replace ball joint.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Bind in steering column.
  - Correction: Repair or replace.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Lack of lubricant rack and pinion.
  - Correction: Check, repair or lubricate rack and pinion.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Disturbed front end alignment.
  - Correction: Check and adjust front end alignment.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Rack and pinion adjustments.
  - Correction: Check and adjust rack and pinion torque.
  - Possible Cause: 7. Tires not adequately inflated.

tbl_3_p81_2.text:

  - Possible Cause: 2. Worn back bushing.

tbl_3_p81_3.text:

  - Possible Cause: 2. Damaged shock absorbers, struts or mountings.

  - Possible Cause: 5. Loose suspension bolts or nuts.

  - Possible Cause: 7. Broken suspension springs.

  - Possible Cause: 8. Poorly lubricated or worn strut bearings.
