para_3_p84_0.text:
A wheel alignment check is in order if the following conditions are noted:
1. Left and right front tire wear is unequal.
2. Wear is uneven across the tread of any front tire.
3. Front Tire treads have scuffed appearance with "feather" edges on one side of tread ribs or blocks.

The original equipment tires have built-in tread wear indicators to show when tires need replacement. These indicators will appear as 12mm (0.47 inch) wide bands when the tire tread depth becomes 1.6mm (0.063 inch). When the indicators appear in 3 or more grooves at 6 locations, tire replacement is recommended.

para_3_p84_1.text:
Waddle is side to side movement at the front and/or rear of the car. It is caused by the steel belt not being straight within the tire. It is most noticeable at low speed, 5 to 30 mph.

It is possible to road test a car and tell on which end of the car the faulty tire is located. If the waddle tire is on the rear, the rear end of the car will shake from side to side or "waddle". From the driver's seat it feels as though someone is pushing on the side of the car.

If the faulty tire is on the front, the waddle is more visual. The front sheet metal appears to be moving back and forth and the driver feels as though he is at the pivot point in the car.

Waddle can be quickly diagnosed by using a Tire Problem Detector (TPD) and following the equipment manufacturer's recommendations.

If a TPD is not available, the more time consuming method of substituting known good tire/wheel assemblies on the problem car can be used as follows:

tbl_3_p84_0.text:
TIRE DIAGNOSIS
Irregular and/or Premature Wear: Irregular and premature wear has many causes. Some of them are: incorrect inflation pressures, lack of tire rotation, driving habits, improper alignment.
If the following conditions are noted, rotation is in order: | Conditions requiring rotation:: 1. Front tire wear is different from rear.
2. Uneven wear exists across the tread of any tire.
3. Left front and right front tire wear is unequal.
4. Left rear and right rear tire wear is unequal.
5. There is cupping, flat spotting, etc.
Irregular and/or Premature Wear: • Hard Cornering
• Under Inflation
• Lack of Rotation | Conditions requiring rotation:: • Incorrect Wheel Alignment
• Tire Construction Non-Uniformity
• Wheel Heavy Acceleration

fig_3_p84_1.text:
Figure 3-2 - Tire Wear Diagnosis
1: Hard Cornering / Under Inflation / Lack of Rotation (left tire wear pattern)
2: Incorrect Wheel Alignment / Tire Construction Non-Uniformity / Wheel Heavy Acceleration (center and right tire wear patterns)

para_3_p85_0.text: "Lead" is the deviation of the car from a straight path on a level road with no pressure on the steering wheel.

proc_3_p85_0.text:
Lead is usually caused by:
1. Incorrect alignment.
2. Uneven brake adjustment.
3. Tire construction.
steps: Incorrect alignment.
Uneven brake adjustment.
Tire construction.

para_3_p85_1.text: The way in which a tire is built can produce lead in a car. An example of this is placement of the belt. Off center belts on radial tires can cause the tire to develop a side force while rolling straight down the road. If one side of the tire is a little larger diameter then the other, the tire will tend to roll to one side. This will develop a side force which can produce car lead.

proc_3_p85_1.text:
Lead is usually caused by:
1. Part of the lead diagnosis procedure is different from the proper tire rotation pattern currently in the owner and service manuals. If a medium to high mileage tire is moved to the other side of the car, be sure to check that ride roughness has not developed.
2. Rear tires will not cause lead.
steps: Part of the lead diagnosis procedure is different from the proper tire rotation pattern currently in the owner and service manuals. If a medium to high mileage tire is moved to the other side of the car, be sure to check that ride roughness has not developed.
Rear tires will not cause lead.

para_3_p85_2.text: Wheel unbalance causes most of the highway speed vibration problems. If a vibration remains after dynamic balancing, it can be caused by three things (Figure 3-6).

proc_3_p85_2.text:
Lead is usually caused by:
1. Tire runout.
2. Wheel runout.
3. Tire stiffness variation.
steps: Tire runout.
Wheel runout.
Tire stiffness variation.

fig_3_p86_0.text:
Figure 3-5 - Lead Diagnosis
INFLATE TIRES TO RECOMMENDED PRESSURE: Step 1: Inflate tires to recommended pressure
ROAD TEST VEHICLE ON LEVEL UNCROWNED ROAD IN BOTH DIRECTIONS: Step 2: Road test vehicle on level uncrowned road in both directions
SWITCH FRONT TIRES SIDE TO SIDE AND ROAD TEST AGAIN: Step 3: Switch front tires side to side and road test again
LEADS IN SAME DIRECTION: Outcome A: Lead remains in same direction after switching tires
PUT TIRES BACK IN ORIGINAL POSITION AND CHECK ALIGNMENT: Outcome A action: Put tires back in original position and check alignment
LEAD CORRECTED IF ROUGHNESS RESULTS. REPLACE TIRES: Outcome B: Lead corrected; if roughness results, replace tires
LEAD REVERSES DIRECTION: Outcome C: Lead reverses direction after switching tires
INSTALL A KNOWN GOOD TIRE ON ONE FRONT SIDE: Outcome C action: Install a known good tire on one front side
LEAD CORRECTED REPLACE TIRE: Sub-outcome B1/C1: Lead corrected - replace tire
LEAD REMAINS INSTALL A KNOWN GOOD TIRE IN PLACE OF OTHER FRONT TIRE: Sub-outcome C2: Lead remains - install a known good tire in place of other front tire
LEAD CORRECTED REPLACE TIRE (second): Sub-outcome C2a: Lead corrected - replace tire
LEAD REMAINS KNOWN GOOD TIRES ARE NOT GOOD: Sub-outcome C2b: Lead remains - known good tires are not good

fig_3_p87_0.text:
Figure 3-6 - Vibration
1: TIRE OUT OF ROUND
2: TIRE STIFFNESS VARIATION
3: RIM BENT OR OUT OF ROUND
4: SMOOTH ROAD

para_3_p87_0.text: Suspension movement (loaded runout) on a smooth road is caused by: tire out of round, tire stiffness variation, or rim bent or out of round.

noti_3a_p88_0.text: NOTICE: When fasteners are removed, always reinstall them at the same location from which they were removed. If a fastener needs to be replaced, use the correct part number fastener for that application. If the correct part number fastener is not available, a fastener of equal size and strength (or stronger) may be used. Fasteners that are reused, and those requiring thread locking compound will be called out. The correct torque value must be used when installing fasteners that require it. If the above conditions are not followed, parts or system damage could result.

tbl_3a_p88_0.text:
GENERAL INFORMATION: 3A-1

fig_3a_p88_0.text:
Figure 3A-1 - Alignment Specifications
WHEEL TOE-IN TOP VIEW: Diagram showing Forward 'A' direction, Body Center, and toe measurement where 'B' - 'A' = Toe
POSITIVE CAMBER ANGLE: Diagram showing center line of wheel at 90° with positive camber angle illustrated
ALIGNMENT SERVICE DATA - TOE: Front 0 ± 2 mm (–0.079 ± 0.078 in); Rear 2 ± 2 mm (0.079 ± 0.079 in)
ALIGNMENT SERVICE DATA - CAMBER: Front 0 ± 1°; Rear 0 ± 1°
ALIGNMENT SERVICE DATA - CASTER: Front 3° ± 2° (16 mm 0.65 in)
ALIGNMENT SERVICE DATA - TURNING ANGLE: Inside 38°; Outside 32°
ALIGNMENT SERVICE DATA - SIDE SLIP LIMIT: Front is less than 3 mm/m (0.118 in/3 ft); Rear is 2 ± 3 mm/m (0.079 ± 0.118 in/3 ft)

para_3a_p88_0.text: Wheel alignment refers to the angular relationship between the wheels, suspension attaching parts, and the ground. The angle of the knuckle away from the vertical, the pointing in or out of the wheels, the tilt of the wheels from vertical (when viewed from the front of the vehicle) and the tilt of the suspension members from vertical (when viewed from the side of the vehicle), are involved in alignment.

para_3a_p88_1.text:
Toe is the turning in or out of the front and rear wheels. The actual amount of toe is normally only a fraction of an inch. The purpose of a toe specification is to ensure parallel rolling of the wheels. (Excessive toe-in or toe-out may increase tire wear.) Toe also serves to offset the small deflections of the wheel support system which occur when the vehicle is rolling forward. Even when the wheels are set, slightly, when the vehicle is standing still,
they tend to roll parallel on the road when the vehicle is moving.

Amount of toe can be obtained by subtracting "A" from "B" as shown in Figure 3A-1 and therefore is given in mm (in).

para_3a_p89_0.text:
Caster is the tilting of the front steering axis either forward or backward from the vertical (when viewed from the side of the vehicle). A backward tilt is positive (+) and a forward tilt is negative (–). On the short and long arm type suspension you cannot see a caster angle without a special instrument, but you can understand that if you look straight down from the top of the upper control arm to the ground you would find that the ball joints do not line up (fore and aft) when a caster angle other than 0° is present. If you had a positive caster angle the lower ball joint would be slightly ahead (toward the front of the vehicle) of the upper ball joint center line. Caster is the forward or backward tilt of the steering axis as viewed from a side elevation.

Camber is the tilting of the front and rear wheels from the vertical when viewed from the front of the vehicle. When the wheels tilt outward at the top, the camber is positive (+). When the wheels tilt inward, the camber is negative(–). The amount of tilt is measured in degrees from the vertical and this is the camber angle.

proc_3a_p89_0.text:
PRELIMINARY CHECKS PRIOR TO ADJUSTING WHEEL ALIGNMENT
1. Check all tires for proper inflation pressures and uniform tread wear.
2. Check for loose ball joints. Check tie rod ends; if excessive looseness is noted, they should be replaced before adjusting toe.
3. Check for wheel and tire assembly run-out.
4. Check car trim heights; if not within specifications, corrections should be made. The corrections must be made before adjusting toe.
5. Check for loose control arms.
6. Set steering wheel in straight state.
7. Consideration must be given to excess loads, such as tool boxes. If this excess load is normally carried in the car, it should remain in the car during alignment checks.
8. Consider the condition of the equipment being used to check alignment and follow the manufacturer's instructions.
9. Regardless of equipment used to check alignment, the car must be on a level surface.

9. Regardless of equipment used to check alignment, the car must be on a level surface.

para_3a_p89_1.text:
Caster and camber cannot be adjusted. Should camber or caster be found out of specification, locate its cause first. If it is damaged, loose, bent, dented or
worn suspension parts, they should be replaced. If it is the car body, it should be repaired to the proper specification.

To prevent incorrect reading of camber or caster, vehicle's front end must be moved up and down three times before inspection.

proc_3a_p90_0.text:
Toe Adjustment
1. Rear wheel toe is adjusted by the inside control rod bolts. Loosen right and left control rod inside nuts first and then turn right and left control rod inside bolts (cam bolts) by the same amount to align toe-in to specification.
2. After adjustment, tighten right and left inside nuts to specified torque while holding control rod inside bolt with another wrench to prevent it from turning (Figure 3A-3).
steps: Rear wheel toe is adjusted by the inside control rod bolts. Loosen right and left control rod inside nuts first and then turn right and left control rod inside bolts (cam bolts) by the same amount to align toe-in to specification.
After adjustment, tighten right and left inside nuts to specified torque while holding control rod inside bolt with another wrench to prevent it from turning (Figure 3A-3).

fig_3a_p90_0.text:
Figure 3A-3 - Rear Wheel Toe Adjustment
1: INSIDE NUT
2: INSIDE BOLT (CAM BOLT)
3: CONTROL ROD

para_3a_p90_0.text:
Camber and caster can't be adjusted. Therefore, should the camber or caster be out of specification, locate its cause first. If the cause is damaged, loose, bent, dented, or worn suspension parts, they should be replaced. If the cause lies in the vehicle body, it should be repaired to the proper specification.

When a tie rod or tie rod end is replaced, check toe and steering angle with turning radius gages.

If the steering angle is not correct, check the right and left tie rods for equal length ("A" in Figure 3A-4).

noti_3a_p90_0.text: NOTICE: If tie rod length is changed to correct steering angle, reinspect toe.

noti_3b_p91_0.text: NOTICE: When fasteners are removed, always reinstall them at the same location from which they were removed. If a fastener needs to be replaced, use the correct part number fastener for that application. If the correct part number fastener is not available, a fastener of equal size and strength (or stronger) may be used. Fasteners that are reused, and those requiring thread locking compound will be called out. The correct torque value must be used when installing fasteners that require it. If the above conditions are not followed, parts or system damage could result.

para_3b_p91_0.text:
The rack and pinion steering system consists of two main components, the rack and the pinion. When the steering wheel is turned, the motion is transmitted to the steering shaft, shaft joint and then to the pinion. Since the pinion teeth mesh with teeth on rack, the motion is further transferred to the rack and changed to linear motion. The force is then transmitted through the tie rods to the steering knuckles which turn the wheels (Figure 3B-1).

When the inner parts of the steering gear case are disassembled, they should be washed clean before reassembly. It is recommended to use GM Manual Steering Gear Lube #1052182 where grease application is indicated in the text. See Required Service Material.

REMOVAL
1) Hoist vehicle and remove front wheel (Refer to WHEEL Section 3E).
2) Remove split pin and tie rod end castle nut from steering knuckle.
3) Disconnect tie rod end from knuckle, using special tool (A).
4) For ease of adjustment after installation, make marking of tie rod end lock nut position on tie rod thread. Then loosen lock nut and remove tie rod end from tie rod.

proc_3b_p94_1.text:
INSTALLATION
1) Install tie rod end lock nut and tie rod end to tie rod. Align lock nut with mark on tie rod thread.
2) Connect tie rod end to knuckle. Tighten castle nut until holes for split pin are aligned, but only within specified torque.
3) Bend split pin as shown below.
4) Install front wheel (Refer to WHEEL Section 3E).
5) Inspect the proper toe (Refer to FRONT END ALIGNMENT Section 3A).
6) After confirming proper toe, tighten tie rod end lock nut to specified torque.
7) Tighten wheel to specified torque and lower hoist.

para_3b_p95_0.text: REMOVE AND INSTALL MANUAL RACK AND PINION ASSEMBLY (STEERING GEAR CASE)

proc_3b_p95_0.text:
REMOVAL
1) Slide driver's seat as far back as possible.
2) Pull off front part of floor mat on driver's side and remove steering shaft joint cover.
3) For ease of installation, loosen steering shaft upper joint bolt but don't remove.
4) Remove steering shaft lower joint bolt and disconnect lower joint from pinion.
5) Hoist vehicle and remove both front wheels. Refer to WHEEL (Section 3E).
6) Remove split pins and tie rod castle nuts from both knuckles.
7) Disconnect both tie rod ends from knuckles, using special tool (A).
steps: 1) Slide driver's seat as far back as possible.

3) For ease of installation, loosen steering shaft upper joint bolt but don't remove.
4) Remove steering shaft lower joint bolt and disconnect lower joint from pinion.
5) Hoist vehicle and remove both front wheels. Refer to WHEEL (Section 3E).

7) Disconnect both tie rod ends from knuckles, using special tool (A).

proc_3b_p95_1.text:
INSTALLATION
1) Mount steering gear case to body and tighten gear case mount bolts to specified torque. 25 N·m (18.0 lb. ft.)
2) Install tie rod ends to knuckles (R & L). Tighten each castle nut until holes for split pin align but within specified torque and then bend split pin as shown. 43 N·m (32.0 lb. ft.)
3) Be sure that steering wheel and brake discs (R & L) are all straightahead driving state and then insert steering lower joint into steering pinion shaft.
4) Tighten steering shaft joint bolts to specified torque (Lower side first and then upper side). 25 N·m (18.0 lb. ft.)

proc_3b_p97_1.text:
INSTALLATION
1) Install tie rod lock washer and tie rod to rack. Align straight part "A" of washer with flat part "B" of rack.
2) Tighten tie rod inside ball nut to specified torque.
3) Bend lock washer to tie rod side.
4) Position boot properly in grooves of gear case and tie rod and clamp it with wire and clip. Wire should be new and should go around twice and be tightened with its both ends twisted together. The twisted ends should be bent in the circumferential direction. After this, check to ensure that boot is free from twist and dent.

proc_3b_p98_2.text:
INSTALLATION
1) Apply grease lightly to sliding part of plunger against rack.
2) Install parts as shown.
3) After tightening rack damper screw to the tightest point, turn it back by 0 ~ 90° and check for rotation torque of pinion. Pinion torque: 1.2 N·m (0.80 lb. ft.). Also, check if rack as a whole moves smoothly.
4) After adjustment, put rack damper screw cap as deeply as possible.

1) Remove boot wires and clips.
2) Move both boots toward tie rod end.
3) Unbend bent part of tie rod lock washers and remove tie rods from right and left sides of steering rack.
4) Mark left and right tie rods accordingly.
5) Remove rack plunger and pinion assembly from gear case by performing steps 1) — 5) in Item 5 STEERING PINION REMOVAL of this section.
6) Remove rack from gear case. Direction for rack removal is as shown below.

para_3b_p102_0.text: Boots should be visually inspected for any damage and tear during every periodical inspection at specified intervals and whenever car is hoisted for some other reason.

proc_3b_p102_0.text:
STEERING RACK BOOTS
1. Hoist car.
2. Inspect each boot for tear. A torn boot allows entry of dust and water which can cause wear to steering rack and pinion to produce noise as well as rust to result in malfunction of steering system.
3. If even a small tear is noted, replace with new one.
steps: Hoist car.
Inspect each boot for tear. A torn boot allows entry of dust and water which can cause wear to steering rack and pinion to produce noise as well as rust to result in malfunction of steering system.
If even a small tear is noted, replace with new one.

para_3b_p102_1.text: Check shaft joint for wear, breakage and other damage and replace if any defect exists.

proc_3b_p102_1.text:
TIE ROD END
1) Inspect for play in ball joint.
2) Inspect for play in rack end ball joint. In either case, if found defective, replace.
steps: 1) Inspect for play in ball joint.
2) Inspect for play in rack end ball joint. In either case, if found defective, replace.

para_3b_p102_2.text: Inspect the dust seal for breakage. If even a small fissure is found, replace the dust seal with a new one. Previous to the installation of new dust seal, fill it with the specific grease furnished together with the new dust seal.

proc_3b_p102_2.text:
RACK PLUNGER
1) Inspect for wear or damage.
2) Inspect rack plunger spring for deterioration. In either case, if found defective, replace.
steps: 1) Inspect for wear or damage.
2) Inspect rack plunger spring for deterioration. In either case, if found defective, replace.

proc_3b_p103_3.text:
STEERING RACK
1. Check steering wheel for play and rattle, holding car in straight forward condition on the ground.
2. If steering wheel play is not within specification, inspect as follows and replace if found defective:
• Tie-rod end ball stud for wear (ball stud should move when more than 2 kg-cm torque is applied.)
• Lower ball joint for wear
• Steering shaft joint for wear
• Steering pinion or rack gear for wear or breakage
• Each part for looseness

para_3c_p106_0.text:
The front suspension is the strut type independent suspension. The upper end of a strut is anchored to the car body by a strut support. The strut and strut support are isolated by a rubber mount. A strut bearing is also installed a little lower to the rubber mount.

The lower end of the strut is connected to the upper end of the steering knuckle. The lower end of the knuckle is attached to the ball joint, which is attached to the suspension control arm.

Movement of the steering wheel is transmitted to the tie rod end and then to the knuckle, causing the wheel-and-tire to move. With the movement of the knuckle, the strut also rotates by means of the strut bearing and lower ball joint (Figure 1).

If complaints of hard ride are encountered, the first items to investigate are tire pressure, trim height, and Superlift shock/strut condition. If these are correct, the amount of friction in the front suspension should be checked. For additional diagnosis, refer to STEERING, SUSPENSION, WHEELS AND TIRE DIAGNOSIS (Section 3).

proc_3c_p106_0.text:
EXCESSIVE FRICTION CHECK
1. With the aid of a helper, lift up on the rear bumper and raise car as high as possible. Slowly release the bumper and allow the car to assume normal trim height.
2. Measure distance from floor to center of bumper.
3. Push down on bumper, release slowly, and allow car to assume normal trim height.
4. Measure distance from floor to center of bumper.
steps: 1. With the aid of a helper, lift up on the rear bumper and raise car as high as possible. Slowly release the bumper and allow the car to assume normal trim height.
2. Measure distance from floor to center of bumper.
3. Push down on bumper, release slowly, and allow car to assume normal trim height.
4. Measure distance from floor to center of bumper.

para_3c_p106_1.text: The difference between the two measurements should be within 12.7 mm (1/2 in.) of each other. If the difference exceeds this limit, inspect the control arms, struts, and ball joints for damage or wear.

proc_3c_p110_0.text:
Disassemble

1. Remove strut. Refer to "Strut Damper Assembly Removal" in this section.
2. Mount J34013-A Strut Compressor in J3289-20 Holding Fixture.
3. Attach strut assembly to strut compressor using both locking pins. If locking pins do not fit through holes in strut, then two 7/16" x 2" bolts with washers and nuts should be used instead (Figure 8).
4. Turn compressor forcing screw while rotating the front strut support to properly align the mounting studs and guide them through the compressor strut hood. The mounting studs must be aligned with the correct pattern in the strut hood assembly (Figure 9).
5. Continue turning compressor forcing screw until the strut spring compresses approximately 1/2".
6. Remove strut nut from top of strut assembly. Then remove the lock washer, spacer, and front strut stopper.
7. Place Guide Rod J34013-38 on damper shaft. Use guide rod to guide damper shaft through spring upper seat while decompressing the strut spring (Figure 10).
8. Remove strut assembly (Figure 11).
steps: 1. Remove strut. Refer to "Strut Damper Assembly Removal" in this section.
2. Mount J34013-A Strut Compressor in J3289-20 Holding Fixture.

3. Attach strut assembly to strut compressor using both locking pins. If locking pins do not fit through holes in strut, then two 7/16" x 2" bolts with washers and nuts should be used instead (Figure 8).

4. Turn compressor forcing screw while rotating the front strut support to properly align the mounting studs and guide them through the compressor strut hood. The mounting studs must be aligned with the correct pattern in the strut hood assembly (Figure 9).
5. Continue turning compressor forcing screw until the strut spring compresses approximately 1/2".
6. Remove strut nut from top of strut assembly. Then remove the lock washer, spacer, and front strut stopper.
7. Place Guide Rod J34013-38 on damper shaft. Use guide rod to guide damper shaft through spring upper seat while decompressing the strut spring (Figure 10).
8. Remove strut assembly (Figure 11).

6. Wheel hub with special tools J34866 and J2619-01 (Figure 17).
7. Brake disc from wheel hub by first removing four disc bolts (Figure 18).
8. Wheel studs (Figure 19).

Install or Connect
1. New wheel studs on wheel hub (Figure 21).
2. Brake disc to wheel hub. Tighten disc bolts lightly.
3. Spacer to wheel hub. Note installing directions of spacer (Figure 22).
4. Wheel hub to knuckle as follows: First while rotating hub, tap it lightly with a plastic hammer. This is to prevent hub from being installed at an angle. After confirming that hub is free from deflection, drive it in with special tool J34856 until dimension "A" noted down before removal is attained (Figure 24).
5. Pad and caliper. Tighten caliper bolts to 30 N·m (22 lb.ft.).
6. Depress brake pedal and hold it the depressed position. Tighten drive axle nut to 175 N·m (129 lb.ft.). Tighten brake disc bolts to 50 N·m (37 lb.ft.).
7. Steak drive axle nut (Figure 25).
8. Wheel.
9. Lower vehicle.

proc_3c_p119_0.text:
STRUT DAMPER
1. Strut for oil leakage. If strut is found faulty, replace it as an assembly unit because it cannot be disassembled (Figure 45).
2. Strut function check.
• Check and adjust tire pressures as specified. Bounce car body three or four times continuously by pushing front end on the side with strut to be checked. Apply the same amount of force at each push and note strut resistance both when pushed and rebounding. Also, note how many times car body rebounds before coming to stop after hands are off. Do the same for strut on the other side. Compare strut resistance and number of rebounds on the right with those on the left. They must be equal in both. With proper strut, car body should come to a stop the moment hands are off or after only one or two small rebounds. If struts are suspected, compare them with a known good car or strut.
3. For damage or deformation.
4. Bearing for wear, abnormal noise, or gripping.
5. For cracks or deformation in spring seat.
6. For deterioration of bump stopper.
7. Rebound stopper and strut mount for wear, cracks, or deformation.
Replace any parts found defective.

proc_3c_p120_1.text:
Inspect
- Each wheel disc for dents, distortion, and cracks. A disc in badly damaged condition must be replaced.
2. Wheel nuts for proper torque.
3. Wheel bearing for wear. When measuring thrust play, apply a dial gage to wheel hub center after removing wheel center cap from wheel disc.
● Thrust play limit is 0.4 mm (0.016 in.).
When measurement exceeds limit, replace bearing.
4. Wheel bearing for noise and smooth rotation by rotating wheel. If defective, replace bearing.

noti_3d_p124_0.text: NOTICE: When fasteners are removed, always reinstall them at the same location from which they were removed. If a fastener needs to be replaced, use the correct part number fastener for that application. If the correct part fastener is not available, a fastener of equal size and strength (or stronger) may be used. Fasteners that are not reused, and those requiring thread locking compound will be called out. The correct torque value must be used when installing fasteners that require it. If the above conditions are not followed, parts or system damage could result. Never attempt to heat, quench or straighten any suspension part. Replace it with a new part, or damage to the part may result.

para_3d_p126_0.text: If complaints of hard ride are encountered, the first items to investigate are tire pressure, trim height and strut condition. If these are correct, the amount of friction in the rear suspension should be checked. For additional diagnosis on this system, refer to STEERING, SUSPENSION, WHEELS AND TIRES DIAGNOSIS (Section 3).

proc_3d_p126_0.text:
SPECIAL TOOLS
1. With aid of helper, lift up on the rear bumper and raise car as high as possible. Slowly release the bumper and allow the car to resume normal trim height.
2. Measure distance from floor to center of bumper.
3. Push down on bumper, release slowly and allow car to resume normal trim height.
4. Measure distance from floor to center of bumper. The difference between the two measurements should be less than 12.7 mm (1/2 inch). If the difference exceeds this limit, inspect the control arms, struts and ball joints for damage or wear.

Remove or Disconnect
1. Hoist vehicle and remove wheel.
2. Control rod inside bolt (body center side) (Figure 9).
3. Outside (wheel side) of control rod from rear knuckle stud bolt (Figure 10).
4. Loosen rear mount nut of suspension arm, but do not remove bolt (Figure 11).
5. Loosen front nut of suspension arm (Figure 12).
6. Loosen lower mount nut of knuckle, then place jack under suspension arm to prevent it from lowering and remove lower mount nut of knuckle.
7. Raise jack placed under suspension arm enough to allow removal of lower mount bolt of knuckle (Figure 14).
8. Move brake drum/brake back plate toward outside of car body (in arrow direction) to separate lower mount of knuckle from suspension arm. Then lower jack gradually and remove coil spring (Figure 15).
9. Suspension arm.

1. Strut for oil leakage. If strut is found faulty, replace it as an assembly unit, because it cannot be disassembled (Figure 49).
2. Strut function. Check and adjust tire pressures as specified. Bounce car body three or four times continously by pushing rear end on the side with strut to be checked. Apply the same amount of force at each push and note strut resistance both when pushed and rebounding. Also, note how many times car body rebounds before coming to a stop after hands are off. Do the same for strut on the other side. Compare strut resistance and number of rebounds on the right with those on the left. They must be equal in both. With proper strut, car body should come to a stop the moment hands are off or after only one or two small rebounds. If struts are suspected, compare them with known good car or strut.
3. For damage or deformation.
4. For abnormal noise.
5. For cracks or deformation in spring seat.
6. For deterioration of bump stopper.
7. Strut mount for wear, cracks or deformation. Replace any parts found defective.

proc_3d_p138_0.text:
Inspect
1. Each wheel disc for dents, distortion and cracks. A disc in badly damaged condition must be replaced.
2. Wheel hub nuts for tightness and, as necessary, retighten to specification.
3. Wheel bearings for wear.
4. Measure wheel thrust play by applying a dial gage to spindle cap center.
5. Rear thrust play limit is 0.3 mm (0.012 in). When measurement exceeds limit, replace bearing. Refer to "Wheel Bearing Replacement" earlier in this section.
6. Wheel bearing for noise and smooth rotation by rotating wheel. If it is defective, replace wheel bearing.

para_3e_p141_0.text:
All models are equipped with metric sized (P145/80R 12) steel belted radial tires.

The tires are designed to operate satisfactorily with loads up to the full rated load capacity when inflated to the recommended inflation pressures.

Correct tire pressures and driving habits have an important influence on tire life. Heavy cornering, excessively rapid acceleration, and unnecessary sharp braking increase tire wear.

Standard equipment wheels are 4.00B x 12 inch steel wheels.

Metric tire size format is shown in Figure 3E-1. When replacement is necessary, the original equipment type tire should be used. Refer to the Tire Placard.

Most metric tire sizes do not have exact corresponding alphanumeric tire sizes. Replacement tires should be of the same size, load range and construction as those originally on the car. Use of any other size or type tire may affect ride handling, speedometer/odometer calibration, vehicle ground clearance and tire or snow chain clearance to the body and chassis.

noti_3e_p141_1.text: NOTICE: Do not mix different types of tires on the same car such as radial, bias and bias-belted tires except in emergencies, because car handling may be seriously affected and may result in loss of control.

para_3e_p141_1.text:
It is recommended that new tires be installed in pairs on the same axle. If necessary to replace only one tire, it should be paired with the tire having the most tread, to equalize braking traction.

The metric term for tire inflation pressure is the kilopascal (kPa). Tire pressures will usually be printed in both kPa and psi on the Tire Placard. Metric tire gages are available from tool suppliers. The chart, Figure 3E-2, converts commonly used inflation pressures from kPa to psi.

T: Temporary

para_3e_p142_0.text:
Wheels must be replaced if they are bent, dented, have excessive lateral or radial runout, leak air through welds, have elongated bolt holes, if lug nuts won't stay tight, or if they are heavily rusted. Wheels with greater runout than shown in Figure 3E-3 may cause objectional vibrations.

Replacement wheels must be equivalent to the original equipment wheels in load capacity, diameter, rim width, offset and mounting configuration. A wheel of improper size or type may affect wheel and bearing life, brake cooling, speedometer/odometer calibration, car ground clearance and tire clearance to the body and chassis.

1: LIMIT 1.40 mm (0.055 in.)
2: LIMIT 1.14 mm (0.045 in.)

para_3e_p142_1.text: The tire placard is located on the left door lock pillar and should be referred to for tire information. The placard lists the maximum load, tire size and cold tire pressure where applicable.

para_3e_p142_2.text: Wheel repairs that use welding, heating, or peening are not approved. All damaged wheels should be replaced.

para_3e_p143_0.text:
If a broken stud is found, see Section 3D (rear) or Section 3C (front) for Notice and Replacement procedure.

Tires and wheels are "match-mounted" at the assembly plant. This means that the radially stiffest part of the tire, or "high spot", is matched to the smallest radius or "low spot" of the wheel. This is done to provide the smoothest possible ride.

The "high spot" of the tire is originally marked by a paint dot on the outboard sidewall. This paint dot will eventually wash off of the tire.

The "low spot" of the wheel is originally marked by a paint dot on the wheel rim-flange. Properly assembled, the wheel 'rims' paint dot should be aligned with the tires' paint dot, Figure 3E-4.

Whenever a tire is dismounted from its wheel, it should be remounted so that the tire and wheel are matched. If the tire's paint dot cannot be located, a line should be scribed on the tire and wheel before dismounting to assure that it is remounted in the same position.

The pressure recommended for any model is carefully calculated to give a satisfactory ride, stability, steering, treadwear, tire life and resistance to bruises.

Tire pressure, with tires cold, (after car has set for three hours or more, or driven less than one mile) should be checked monthly or before any extended trip. Set to the specifications on the tire placard located on the left door lock pillar. It is normal for tire pressure to increase 28 kPa (4 psi) when the tires become hot during driving. Do not bleed or reduce tire pressure after driving. Bleeding reduces the "Cold Inflation Pressure".

proc_3e_p143_0.text:
Higher than recommended pressure can cause:
1. Hard ride.
2. Tire bruising or carcass damage.
3. Rapid tread wear at center of tire.
steps: 1. Hard ride.
2. Tire bruising or carcass damage.
3. Rapid tread wear at center of tire.

proc_3e_p143_1.text:
Lower than recommended pressure can cause:
1. Tire squeal on turns.
2. Hard Steering.
3. Rapid and uneven wear on the edges of the tread.
4. Tire rim bruises and rupture.
5. Tire cord breakage.
6. High tire temperatures.
7. Reduced handling.
8. Reduced fuel economy.

para_3e_p143_1.text: To equalize wear, rotate tires according to Figure 3E-6. Radial tires should be rotated at the first 12 000 km (7,500 miles) and after that, tire rotation at least every 7,500 miles is recommended. Set tire pressure.

noti_3e_p143_0.text: NOTICE: Due to their design, radial tires tend to wear faster in the shoulder area, particularly in front positions. This makes regular rotation especially necessary.

para_3e_p143_2.text:
Due to limited tire-to-body clearance on certain vehicles, tire chain usage recommendations have been published in the Owner's Manual. When chains are to be used, most current GM vehicles require SAE Class "S" tire chains. These may also be designated as 1100 Series, Type PL tire chains. These chains are specially designed to limit the "fly off" effect that occurs when the wheel rotates.

Manufacturers of tire chains have a specific chain size for each tire size to ensure proper fit when installed. Therefore, be sure to purchase the correct chains for the tires on which they are to be used. Rubber adjusters should not be used to take up slack or clearance in chains which are loose due to incorrect size. Always follow the chain manufacturers installation instructions.

Use of chains may adversely affect vehicle handling. When using chains:

proc_3e_p143_3.text:
When using chains:
1. Adjust speed to road conditions
2. Avoid sharp turns
3. Avoid locked-wheel braking
steps: Adjust speed to road conditions
Avoid sharp turns
Avoid locked-wheel braking

para_3e_p143_3.text: Install the chains on the front tires as tightly as possible, then tighten them again after driving 1/4 to 1/2 mile (0.4 to 0.8 kilometer). The use of chains on the rear tires is not recommended; the chains may contact and possibly damage the car. If you intend to use chains on the rear tires, be sure there is enough clearance.

1) Loosen wheel nuts by approximately 180° (half a rotation).
2) Hoist vehicle.

3) Wheel nuts and wheel.

noti_3e_p144_0.text: NOTICE: Never use heat to loosen a tight wheel nut. The application of heat to the wheel can shorten the life of the wheel and damage wheel bearings.

tbl_3e_p144_0.text: Wheel nuts:: Wheel nuts: | 60 N·m (44 lb ft.): 60 N·m (44 lb ft.)

para_3e_p144_1.text: Wheel nuts must be tightened in sequence and proper torque to avoid bending wheel or brake drum or disc, Figure 3E-7.

noti_3e_p144_1.text: NOTICE: Before installing wheels, remove any build-up of corrosion on the wheel mounting surface and brake drum or disc mounting surface by scraping and wire brushing. Installing wheels without good metal-to-metal contact at the mounting surfaces can cause wheel nuts to loosen, which can later allow a wheel to come off while the car is moving.

para_3e_p145_0.text:
Use a tire changing machine to mount or demount tires. Follow the equipment manufacturer's instructions. Do not use hand tools or tire irons alone to change tires as they may damage the tire beads or wheel rim.

Rim bead seats should be cleaned with a wire brush or coarse steel wool to remove lubricants, old rubber and light rust. Before mounting or demounting a tire, the bead area should be well lubricated with an approved tire lubricant.

After mounting, inflate tire to 240 kPa (35 psi) so that beads are completely seated. Install valve core and inflate to pressure recommended on tire label.

caut_3e_p145_0.text: CAUTION: Do not stand over tire when inflating. Bead may break when bead snaps over rim's safety hump and cause serious personal injury.

caut_3e_p145_1.text: CAUTION: Do not exceed 240 kPa (35 psi) pressure when inflating. If 240 kPa (35 psi) pressure will not seat beads, deflate, re-lubricate and reinflate. Over-inflation may cause the bead to break and cause serious personal injury.

para_3e_p145_1.text:
There are many different materials on the market to repair tires. Manufacturers have published detailed instructions on how and when to repair tires. These instructions can be obtained from the tire manufacturer.

Wheel runout should be measured with an accurate dial indicator. Measurements may be taken with wheel installed on or off the vehicle using an accurate mounting surface such as on a wheel balancer. Measurements may also be taken with or without the tire mounted on the wheel.

Radial runout and lateral runout should be measured on both the inboard rim flanges. With the dial indicator firmly in position, slowly rotate the wheel one revolution and record the total indicator reading. If any measurement exceeds specifications, and there is a vibration that wheel balancing will not correct, the wheel should be replaced. Disregard any indicator readings due to welds, paint runs, scratches, etc., Figure 3E-3.

caut_3e_p145_2.text: CAUTION: All models are equipped with a high pressure compact spare (T105/8013) located in the rear tailgate storage area. Replacement of this wheel must consist of an equivalent to the originally equipped wheel in load, capacity, diameter, rim width, offset and mounting configuration. A wheel of improper size may result in loss of control, personal injury, or property damage.

noti_3e_p145_0.text: NOTICE: The compact spare tire wheel should not be used with standard tires, snow tires, wheel covers, or trim rings. If such use is attempted, damage to these items or other parts of the vehicle may occur. The compact spare should only be used on vehicles which offered it as original equipment.

para_3e_p145_2.text:
Inflation pressure of the compact spare must be periodically checked and maintained at 420 kPa (60 psi). It can be mounted and dismounted from its wheel using present tire changing procedures. As with other tires, the beads should completely seat at 275 kPa (40 psi). The tire may then be safely inflated to 420 kPa (60 psi).

There are two types of wheel and tire balance, static and dynamic. Static balance, Figure 3E-8, is the equal distribution of weight around the wheel. Assemblies that are statically unbalanced cause a bouncing action called tramp. This condition will eventually cause uneven tire wear.

Dynamic balance, Figure 3E-9, is the equal distribution of weight on each side of the wheel centerline so that when the tire spins there is no tendency for the assembly to move from side to side. Assemblies that are dynamically unbalanced may cause shimmy.

Deposits of mud, etc. must be cleaned from the inside of the rim.

2: C/L OF SPINDLE
3: HEAVY SPOT WHEEL TRAMP

fig_3e_p146_1.text:

caut_3e_p146_0.text: CAUTION: Stones should be removed from the tread to avoid operator injury during spin balancing and to obtain a good balance.

para_3e_p146_0.text:
Each tire should be inspected for damage, then balanced according to the equipment manufacturer's recommendation.

Most electronic off-car balancers are more accurate than the on-car spin balancers. They are easy to use and give a dynamic (two plane) balance. Although they do not correct for drum or disc unbalance as does on-car spin balancing, this is overcome by their accuracy.

On-car balancing methods vary with equipment and tool manufacturers. Be sure to follow each manufacturer's instructions during balancing operation.

caut_3e_p146_1.text: CAUTION: The wheel spin should be limited to 35 mph (55 km/h) as indicated on the speedometer. This limit is necessary because the speedometer only indicates one-half on the actual wheel speed when one drive wheel is spinning and the other drive wheel is stopped. Unless care is taken in limiting drive wheel spin, the spinning wheel can reach excessive speeds. This can result in possible tire disintegration or differential failure, which could cause serious personal injury or extensive car damage.

para_3e_p146_1.text:
If a replacement tire cannot be obtained, there are several ways to correct tires which cause a vibration even though they are properly balanced. The best method uses in automatic machine which loads the tire and buffs small amounts of rubber from selected spots on the outer two tread rows. This method should be followed by an accurate electronic off-car wheel balance. Correction by this method is usually permanent and, if done properly, does not significantly affect the appearance or tire tread life. Tire truing with a blade-type machine is not recommended as this reduces the tread life substantially and often does not permanently correct the problem.

Another method is to dismount the tire and rotate it 180 degrees on the rim. It is important that this be done on tire assemblies which are known to be causing a vibration as it is just as likely to make good assemblies worse.

noti_3f_p147_0.text: NOTICE: When fasteners are removed, always reinstall them at the same location from which they were removed. If a fastener needs to be replaced, use the correct part number fastener for that application. If the correct part number fastener is not available, a fastener of equal size and strength (or stronger) may be used. Fasteners that are reused, and those requiring thread locking compound will be called out. The correct torque value must be used when installing fasteners that require it. If the above conditions are not followed, parts or system damage could result.

para_3f_p147_0.text: The ball and double tube type steering column includes three important features in addition to the steering function:

proc_3f_p147_0.text:
1. The column is energy absorbing, designed to compress in a front-end collision to minimize the possibility of an injury to the driver of the car.
2. The ignition switch and lock are mounted conveniently on this column.
3. With the column mounted lock, the ignition and steering operations can be locked to inhibit theft of the car.
steps: 1. The column is energy absorbing, designed to compress in a front-end collision to minimize the possibility of an injury to the driver of the car.
2. The ignition switch and lock are mounted conveniently on this column.
3. With the column mounted lock, the ignition and steering operations can be locked to inhibit theft of the car.

para_3f_p147_1.text: The turn signal lever provides for control of headlight beams (Figure 1). This column may be easily removed and installed. To insure the energy absorbing action, it is important that only the specified screws, bolts, and nuts be used as designated and that they are tightened to the specified torque (Figure 1). When the column assembly is removed from the car, special care must be taken in handling it. Use of a steering wheel puller other than the one recommended in this manual or a sharp blow on the end of the steering shaft, leaning on the assembly, or dropping the assembly could shear the plastic shear pins which maintain column length (Figure 1).

noti_3f_p149_0.text: NOTICE: Cars involved in accidents resulting in body damage or where the steering column has been impacted may also have a damaged or misaligned steering column.

proc_3f_p149_0.text:
CHECKING STEERING COLUMN FOR ACCIDENT DAMAGE
1. Check capsules on steering column bracket; all should be contacting the bottom of the slots. If not, steering column assembly should be replaced. Use thickness gage for convenience.
2. Take measurement "A" as shown. If it is shorter than the specified length, replace column assembly with new one.
3. Check steering shaft joints and shaft for any damages such as crack, breakage, malfunction or excessive play. If anything is found faulty, replace as joint assembly.
4. Check steering shaft for smooth rotation. If found defective, replace as column assembly.
5. Check steering shaft and column for bend, cracks or deformation. If found defective, replace.

REMOVAL
1) Disconnect negative battery cable.
2) Pull steering pad off and disconnect ground wire from inside of steering pad.
3) Remove steering wheel damper attaching screws and then steering wheel damper.
4) Remove steering shaft nut.
5) Scribe a line on the steering wheel and shaft to use as guide during reinstallation.
6) Remove steering wheel with special tool (A).

4) Connect ground wire to inside of steering pad and press steering pad on.
5) Connect negative battery cable.

noti_3f_p152_0.text: NOTICE: Once the steering column is removed from the car, the column is extremely susceptible to damage. Dropping the column assembly on its end could collapse the steering shaft or loosen the plastic injections which maintain column length. Leaning on the column assembly could cause the jacket to bend or deform. Any of the above damage could impair the column's collapsible design. If it is necessary to remove the steering wheel, use standard wheel puller. Under no condition should the end of the shaft be hammered upon as hammering could loosen the plastic injections which maintain column length.

proc_3f_p154_1.text:
INSTALLATION
1) Position oblong hole of steering shaft in the center of hole in column.
2) Turn ignition key to "ACC" or "ON" position and install ignition switch assembly onto column.
3) Now turn ignition key to "LOCK" position and pull it out.
4) Align hub on ignition switch assembly with oblong hole of steering shaft and rotate shaft to ensure that steering shaft is locked.
5) Tighten two new bolts until head of each bolt is broken off.
6) Turn ignition key to "ACC" or "ON" position and check to be sure that steering shaft rotates smoothly. Also check for lock operation.
7) Install steering column. Refer to STEERING COLUMN INSTALLATION.

proc_4d_p158_0.text:
REMOVAL
1. Remove wheel center cap.
2. Remove caulking, driveshaft nut (14) and washer (13).
3. Loosen wheel nuts.
4. Raise the vehicle and support it on jack stands.
5. Remove front wheel.
6. Drain transaxle oil.
7. To detach the snap ring (1) fitted on the spline of differential-side joint (inboard joint) from differential-side gear, pry the inboard joint by using a tire lever or large screwdriver (Figure 4D-3).
8. Remove ball-stud bolt and nut; then separate suspension arm from knuckle (Figure 4D-4).
9. To remove the drive axle assembly, detach inboard joint from differential-side gear and then wheel-side joint (outboard joint) from steering knuckle.

proc_4d_p158_1.text:
DISASSEMBLY
1. Remove tripod joint boot band (4).
2. Remove tripod joint housing (2) (Figure 4D-5).
3. Remove grease from the shaft and take off snap ring (3) using special tool, then pull out tripod-joint spider (15); finally, pull spider from shaft.
4. Remove boot band (6), then pull out differential-side boot from shaft (Figure 4D-5).
5. Release boot bands of wheel-side joint boot (6 and 10), then pull boot from shaft.

Do not disassemble the spider of differential-side joint. If any malcondition is found in the spider, replace the differential-side joint assembly.

proc_4d_p158_2.text:
INSPECTION
1. Check the boots for tears or deterioration. Replace them as necessary.
2. Check the snap rings and boot bands for breakage or deformation. Replace as necessary.

proc_4d_p158_3.text:
CLEANING
1. Wash the disassembled parts (except the boots and the spider of differential side joint) in degreaser. After washing, dry the parts completely with compressed air.
2. Clean the boots with a rag. DO NOT wash the boots in degreaser. Washing in degreaser causes deterioration of boot.
3. Clean the spider of the differential side joint with a clean towel. To prevent the needle bearings of the spider from being degreased, DO NOT wash the spider in degreaser.

1. Liberally apply the joint grease to the wheel side joint. Use the block joint grease in the tube included in the wheel-side boot set or wheel-side joint assembly.
2. Fit the wheel-side boot on the shaft. Fill the inside of boot with joint grease, approx. 80 grams, then fix the boot bands. (Figure 4D-8).
3. Fit the differential-side boot on the shaft.
4. Liberally apply the joint grease to the differential-side joint. Use the yellow joint grease in the tube included in the differential-side boot set or the differential-side joint assembly.
5. Install the spider of the differential-side joint on the shaft, facing its chamfered side to the wheel side joint. (Figure 4D-8).

proc_4d_p159_1.text:
INSTALLATION
1. Reverse the removal procedure.
2. Clean the front-wheel bearing seal and then apply grease to the seal.
3. Check the seal for breakage or deterioration. Replace it if necessary.
4. Apply sealant to drain plug for manual transaxle.
5. Install the wheel-side joint to the steering knuckle and then the differential-side joint to the differential-side gear. Push the differential-side joint by hand until it is positioned by the snap ring fitted to its spline. (Figure 4D-4).

proc_4d_p159_2.text:
INSTALLATION
1. Tighten each bolt and nut to the specified torque.
2. After tightening the drive shaft nut, install the split pin to the shaft.
3. Refill the transaxle.

noti_5_p161_0.text: NOTICE: All brake fasteners are important attaching parts in that they could affect the performance of vital parts and systems, and/or could result in major repair expense. They must be replaced with one of same part number or with an equivalent part if replacement becomes necessary. Do not use a replacement part of lesser quality or substitute design. Torque values must be used as specified during reassembly to assure proper retention of all parts. There is to be no welding as it may result in extensive damage and weakening of the metal.

caut_5_p161_0.text: CAUTION: When servicing wheel brake parts, do not create dust by grinding, sanding brake linings, or by cleaning wheel brake parts with a dry brush or with compressed air. Many wheel brake parts contain asbestos fibers which can become airborne if dust is created during servicing. Breathing dust containing asbestos fibers may cause serious bodily harm. A water dampened cloth or water based solution should be used to remove any dust on parts. Equipment is commercially available to perform this washing function. These wet methods will prevent asbestos fibers from becoming airborne.

para_5_p162_0.text:
When the brake pedal is depressed, hydraulic pressure is developed in the master cylinder to actuate pistons (two in front and four in rear).
The master cylinder is a tandem master cylinder. Two brake pipes are connected to the master cylinder and they make two independent circuits. One connects the front right & rear left brakes and the other connects the front left & rear right brakes.
The proportioning valve is included in these circuits between the master cylinder and rear wheels.
In this brake system, the disc brake type is used for the front wheel brake and a drum brake type (leading/trailing shoes) for the rear wheel brake.
The parking brake system is mechanical. It applies brake force to only rear wheels by means of the cable and mechanical linkage system. The same brake shoes are used for both parking and rear brakes.

para_5_p163_0.text:
This caliper has a single 48.0 mm bore and is mounted to the knuckle with two mounting bolts. Hydraulic force, created by applying force to the brake pedal, is converted by the caliper to friction. The hydraulic force acts equally against the piston and the bottom of the caliper bore to move the piston outward and to move the caliper inward resulting in a clamping action on the disc. This clamping action forces the pads against the disc, creating friction to stop the vehicle.
For details, refer to OPERATION in the next page.

noti_5_p163_0.text: NOTICE: Lubricate parts as specified. Do not use lubricated shop air on brake parts as damage to rubber components may result. If any component is removed or line disconnected, bleed the brake system. Replace pads in axle sets only. The torque values specified are for dry, unlubricated fasteners.

para_5_p164_0.text: The single piston floating caliper type brake is employed in this model. One cylinder and one piston are used for this type. (The cylinder is constructed as a monoblock with the caliper.) Fluid pressure generated in the cylinder causes the pad (2) on the piston side to press against the disc. At the same time, the floating type caliper body is moved to the right by the cylinder pressure, as shown in the figure below, which pulls pad (1) against the disc and brakes the wheel.

para_5_p164_1.text:
The disc brake has no servo assistance as in drum braking, and it is necessary to increase the working pressure of the piston and pad. For this purpose, the caliper cylinder has a large bore. Only a little change in clearance between the disc and pad has therefore a large influence on the brake pedal stroke. It is necessary to have the clearance adjusted to the minimum at all times, by means of the piston (rubber) seal.

When fluid pressure is applied to the piston, the piston moves forward. The rubber seal, which exerts considerable pressure against the piston, moves with the cylinder. However, as a part of the rubber seal has been fixed into a groove in the cylinder, the shape of the rubber seal is distorted toward the internal end of the cylinder, as shown in above Figure. When pressure is taken off of the brake pedal and fluid pressure is released from the piston, a restoring force is generated at the seal and pushes the piston back. As the pads wear away and the clearance between the disc and pads becomes larger, the piston moves a larger distance. The seal then could change in shape further, but since the end of the seal is fixed into the groove in the cylinder, the distortion is limited to the same amount as previously described. The piston moves further to cover the distance of clearance. The piston returns by the same distance and the rubber seal recovers its shape as described above and thus the clearance between the disc and pads is maintained in adjustment.

para_5_p165_0.text: The drum brake assembly has a self shoe clearance adjusting system so that drum-to-shoe clearance is maintained appropriate at all times. For details, refer to OPERATION in the next page.

noti_5_p165_0.text: NOTICE: Replace all components included in repair kits used to service this drum brake. Lubricate parts as specified.

para_5_p166_0.text:
With the general drum brake type, when the brake pedal is depressed, two pistons in the wheel cylinder force the brake shoes outward, restraining the turn of the drum. The more the brake shoes get worn, the longer the distance the pistons must move. As a result, the brake pedal travel (pedal-to-wall clearance) increases. Then the shoe clearance must be adjusted by the shoe adjusting screws. Thus, periodical adjustment is required for the drum brake type in general.
This rear brake is provided with a self-adjusting system which automatically adjusts the shoe-to-drum clearance caused by brake shoe wear.

In each rear wheel cylinder, pistons, piston cups and a piston spring (1) are installed. When the brake pedal is depressed, fluid pressure is applied to the inside of the chamber on the piston (2), (3).
Being actuated by this pressure, the piston (2) moves to the left (piston (3) moves to the right) in the following figure and presses the brake shoe against the brake drum, producing brake force.

para_5_p166_1.text: At this time, the distance the brake shoe moves is "B", that is, the distance that "A" (the end of the long hole made in the brake shoes web) moves until it contacts the lever (1) which is fitted in the long hole.

para_5_p166_2.text:
When the brake pedal is depressed, the piston and brake shoe move toward the brake drum side by the previously mentioned distance "B" and "A" until the brake shoe web contacts the lever (1). As the brake shoe gets worn and the brake shoe clearance becomes larger, the force applied to the lever (1) at the time of such a contact becomes larger. When it exceeds 10 - 12 kg (22 - 26 lbs), the "A" of the brake shoe web moves the lever (1) as much as the amount of the brake shoe lining wear toward the direction as shown with an arrow in the figure. Thus, the shoe is forced against the drum and the brake force is produced.

The distance the lever (1) moves corresponds to the amount of wear. In accordance with the lever (1) movement, the fan-shaped ratchet (2) also moves, as they are assembled as a unit. The lever (1) and ratchet (2) remain in the positions as they moved until the shoe-to-drum clearance becomes even larger.
When the brake pedal is released, the brake shoe is allowed to move back by the amount of clearance "B" by means of the return spring. In this way, the brake shoe-to-drum clearance is automatically adjusted constant every time the brake pedal is depressed.

The brake shoe-to-drum clearance "B" corresponds to 0.36 - 0.54 mm (0.014 - 0.021 in.) in terms of the brake drum diameter A — A'. And the amount adjusted by one notch of the ratchet corresponds to 0.18 mm (0.007 in.) in terms of the brake drum diameter A — A'.

The spring provided in the wheel cylinder prevents the piston from moving back more than the specified brake shoe-to-drum clearance.

para_5_p167_0.text: The master cylinder has two pistons and three piston cups. Its hydraulic pressure is produced in the primary ("a" in the below figure) and secondary ("b") chambers. The hydraulic pressure produced in the primary chamber ("a") acts on the right front wheel brake and left rear wheel brake. Also, the hydraulic pressure produced in the secondary chamber ("b") acts on the left front wheel brake and right rear wheel brake.

note_5_p167_0.text: NOTE: Replace all components included in repair kits to service this master cylinder. Lubricate rubber parts with clean, fresh brake fluid to ease assembly. Do not use lubricated shop air on brake parts as damage to rubber components may result. If any hydraulic component is removed or brake line disconnected, bleed the brake system. The torque values specified are for dry, unlubricated fasteners.

para_5_p167_1.text:
Normal operation
Depressing the brake pedal forces the primary piston "A" to move to the left in the below figure and consequently the hydraulic pressure is produced in the chamber "a". With this pressure and the return spring force, the secondary piston "B" is also pushed to the left and thus the hydraulic pressure is produced in the chamber "b".

para_5_p168_0.text: Depressing the brake pedal forces the primary piston “A” to move as described previously, but since the brake circuit connected to the chamber “a” cannot hold the pressure, no pressure is produced in the fluid immediately ahead of the piston “A”. The piston “A” keeps moving while compressing the spring and when it reaches the retainer, the piston “B” is pushed and begins to move. This causes the pressure to rise in the chamber “b” and the pressure acts on the left front wheel brake and right rear wheel brake.

para_5_p168_1.text: In this case, the leftward movement of the piston “A” has little effect on causing the fluid pressure to rise in the chamber “a” in the beginning, because the initial rise of the fluid pressure causes the piston “B” to promptly yield and move to the left. However, when the forward end of the piston “B” comes to the head of the cylinder and stops there, the leftward movement of the piston “A” becomes effective. Thus the fluid pressure is produced in the chamber “a” and it acts on the right front wheel brake and left rear wheel brake. The below figure shows secondary piston “B” at halt.

para_5_p169_0.text:
The booster is located between the master cylinder and the brake pedal. It is so designed that the force created when the brake pedal is depressed is mechanically increased combined with the engine vacuum. It provides higher brake force with less pedal effort.
The booster has a diaphragm of 155 mm effective diameter. Its operation is described in the following page.

noti_5_p169_0.text:
NOTICE: • Use all components included in repair kits to service this booster. Lubricate rubber parts, where indicated, with silicone grease provided in kits. The torque values specified are for dry, unlubricated fasteners. If any hydraulic component is removed or brake line disconnected, bleed the brake system.
• Never lubricate any hydraulic component with silicone grease.

para_5_p170_0.text:
When the brake pedal is depressed, the force is transmitted to the piston of the master cylinder through the valve operating rod, booster air valve, reaction disc and piston rod. At the same time, the force of the booster piston developed due to the pressure difference between the two chambers "A" and "B" in the above figure is added to it.
The end of the booster control valve has a double function of a vacuum valve and air valve. That is, as shown in the figure, the booster control valve closes between the "A" and "B" chambers as its outer end "C" contacts the booster piston seat and opens as "C" leaves the booster piston seat (vacuum valve function). Also it closes between the "B" chamber and outside air as its inner end "D" contacts the air valve seat and opens as "D" leaves the air valve seat (air valve function).

The valve operating rod is pushed to the right by the spring force as shown. The air valve is also enough to the right to contact the valve stopper key as shown. In this state, the vacuum valve (control valve "C") is open and the air valve (control valve "D") is closed. Thus the chambers "A" and "B" conduct and share the same negative pressure (because of no pressure difference) which allows the return spring to push the booster piston to the right.

para_5_p171_0.text: Being pushed by the operating rod, the booster air valve moves to the left as shown. Then the control valve is pushed against the booster piston seat closely by the valve spring force. Thus the vacuum valve (control valve "C") is closed to cut off between the chambers "A" and "B". At this time the air valve (control valve "D") is still closed.

Control Valve – Brake Pedal Depressed (initial)

B: CHAMBER
C: VACUUM VALVE
D: AIR VALVE
1: BOOSTER PISTON

3: BOOSTER CONTROL VALVE

para_5_p171_1.text: As the booster air valve moves further to the left, it leaves the control valve and the air valve (control valve "D") opens to allow the air flow into the chamber "B". The entry of air causes a difference in pressures between the chambers "A" and "B". When this pressure difference grows greater than the piston return spring force, the booster piston moves to the left and the booster control valve also moves to the left. The resulting air valve (control valve "D") closure stops the air flow into the chamber "B" and its pressure remains as it is. In this way, a small brake pedal depressing force is made into a strong push to the master cylinder push rod to produce high hydraulic pressure.

B: CHAMBER
C: VACUUM VALVE
D: AIR VALVE
1: BOOSTER PISTON

3: BOOSTER CONTROL VALVE

para_5_p171_2.text: When the brake pedal is released, the booster air valve returns to the right by the master cylinder piston return force and the air valve return spring force as shown. Then the vacuum valve (control valve "C") opens and causes negative pressure in the chamber "B". The result is that the master cylinder piston and booster piston return to their original positions. This is the same state as described under [When brake pedal is not depressed].

para_5_p171_3.text: Should any of the vacuum related parts in the booster be faulty, the brake force is not increased. Even then, however, the brake depressing force is transmitted to the valve operating rod, booster air valve, valve stopper key and booster piston in that order, to push the master cylinder push rod. Thus, the braking operation itself will not fail.

para_5_p172_0.text:
Brakes should be tested on dry, clean, smooth and reasonably level roadway which is not crowned. Road test brakes by making brake applications with both light and heavy pedal forces at various speeds to determine if the car stops evenly and effectively. Also drive car to see if it leads to one side or the other without brake application. If it does, check the tire pressure, front end alignment and front suspension attachments for looseness. See diagnosis chart for other causes.

With engine running at idle and the transmission in neutral, depress the brake pedal and hold a constant foot pressure on the pedal. If pedal gradually falls away with the constant pressure, the hydraulic system may be leaking. Check the master cylinder fluid levels. While a slight drop in reservoir level does result from normal lining wear, an abnormally low level indicates a leak in the system. In such a case, check the entire brake system for leakage. If even a slight evidence of leakage is noted, the cause should be corrected or defective parts should be replaced.

Improper brake fluid, mineral oil or water in the fluid may cause the brake fluid to boil or the rubber components in the hydraulic system to deteriorate. If primary piston cups are swollen, then rubber parts have deteriorated. This deterioration may also be evidenced by swollen wheel cylinder piston cups on the drum brake wheels. If deterioration of rubber is evident, disassemble all hydraulic parts and wash with alcohol. Dry these parts with compressed air before assembly to keep alcohol out of the system. Replace all rubber parts in the system, including hoses. Also, when working on the brake mechanisms, check for fluid on the linings. If excessive fluid is found, replace the linings. If master cylinder piston seals are satisfactory, check for leakage or excessive heat conditions. If condition is not found, drain fluid, flush with brake fluid, refill and bleed system. The system must be flushed if there is any doubt as to the grade of fluid in the system or if fluid has been used which contained parts that have been subjected to contaminated fluid.

BRAKE DIAGNOSIS CHART A
Condition: Not Enough Braking Force
  - Possible Cause: 1. Brake oil leakage from brake lines.
  - Correction: Locate leaking point and repair.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Brake disc or pads stained with oil.
  - Correction: Clean or replace.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Overheated brakes.
  - Correction: Determine cause and repair.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Poor contact of shoes on brake drum.
  - Correction: Repair for proper contact.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Brake shoes linings stained with oil or wet with water.
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Badly worn brake shoe linings.
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: 7. Defective wheel cylinders.
  - Correction: Repair or replace.
  - Possible Cause: 8. Malfunctioning caliper assembly.
  - Correction: Repair or replace.
  - Possible Cause: 9. Air in System.
  - Correction: Bleed System

tbl_5_p172_1.text:
BRAKE DIAGNOSIS CHART A
Condition: Brake Pull (Brakes not working in unison)
  - Possible Cause: 1. Shoe linings are wet with water or stained with oil.
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Drum-to-shoe clearance out of adjustment in some brakes. (Malfunctioning auto adjusting mechanism)
  - Correction: Check for inoperative auto adjusting mechanism.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Drum is out of round in some brakes.
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Wheel tires are inflated unequally.
  - Correction: Inflate equally.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Malfunctioning wheel cylinders.
  - Correction: Repair or replace.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Disturbed front end alignment.
  - Correction: Adjust as prescribed.
  - Possible Cause: 7. Unmatched tires on same axle.
  - Correction: Tires with approximately the same amount of tread should be used on the same axle.
  - Possible Cause: 8. Restricted brake tubes or hoses.
  - Correction: Check for soft hoses and damaged lines. Replace with new hoses and new double-walled steel brake tubing.
  - Possible Cause: 9. Malfunctioning caliper assembly.
  - Correction: Check for stuck or sluggish pistons and proper lubrication of caliper slide bushing. Caliper should slide.
  - Possible Cause: 10. Loose suspension parts.
  - Correction: Check all suspension mountings.
  - Possible Cause: 11. Loose calipers.
  - Correction: Check and torque bolts to specifications.

Condition: Noise (High Pitched Squeak Without Brake Applied)
  - Cause: 1. Front lining worn out.

tbl_5_p174_0.text:
BRAKE DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Excessive Pedal Travel (Pedal stroke too large)
  - Possible Cause: 1. Partial brake system failure.
  - Correction: Check diagonal brake systems and repair as necessary.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Insufficient fluid in master cylinder reservoirs.
  - Correction: Fill reservoirs with approved brake fluid. Check for leaks and air in brake systems. Check warning light. Bleed system if required.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Air in system. (Pedal soft/spongy).
  - Correction: Bleed system.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Rear brake system not adjusted (malfunctioning auto adjusting mechanism)
  - Correction: Repair auto adjusting mechanism. Adjust rear brakes.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Bent brake shoes.
  - Correction: Replace brake shoes.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Worn rear brake shoes.
  - Correction: Replace brake shoes.

tbl_5_p174_1.text:
BRAKE DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Dragging Brakes (A very light drag is present in all disc brakes immediately after pedal is released)
  - Possible Cause: 1. Master cylinder piston not returning correctly.
  - Correction: Repair master cylinder.
  - Possible Cause: 2. Restricted brake tubes or hoses.
  - Correction: Check for soft hoses or damaged tubes and replace with new hoses and/or new double-walled steel brake tubing.
  - Possible Cause: 3. Incorrect parking brake adjustment on rear brakes.
  - Correction: Check and adjust to correct specifications.
  - Possible Cause: 4. Weakened or broken return springs in the brake.
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: 5. Sluggish parking brake cables or linkage.
  - Correction: Repair or replace.
  - Possible Cause: 6. Wheel cylinder or caliper piston sticking.
  - Correction: Repair as necessary.

tbl_5_p175_0.text:
BRAKE DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Pedal Pulsation (Pedal pulsates when depressed for braking)
  - Cause: 1. Damaged or loose wheel bearings.
  - Correction: Replace wheel bearings.
  - Cause: 2. Distorted steering knuckle or rear axle shafts.
  - Correction: Replace knuckle or rear axle shaft.
  - Cause: 3. Excessive disc lateral runout.
  - Correction: Check per instructions. If not within specifications, replace or machine the disc.
  - Cause: 4. Parallelism not within specifications.
  - Correction: Check per instructions. If not within specifications, replace or machine the disc.
  - Cause: 5. Rear drums out of round.
  - Correction: Check runout, if not within 0.06mm (0.0024 in.), replace drums. Original maximum diameter mustn't exceed 2.0mm (0.078 in.).

tbl_5_p175_1.text:
BRAKE DIAGNOSIS CHART B
Condition: Braking Noise
  - Cause: 1. Glazed shoe linings, or foreign matter stuck to linings.
  - Correction: Repair or replace shoe lining.
  - Cause: 2. Worn or distorted shoe linings.
  - Correction: Replace shoe lining (or pad).
  - Cause: 3. Loose front wheel bearings.
  - Correction: Replace wheel bearing.
  - Cause: 4. Distorted backing plates or loose mounting bolts.
  - Correction: Replace or retighten securing bolts.

proc_5_p176_1.text:
3. EXCESSIVE PEDAL TRAVEL CHECK
1. Start engine.
2. Depress brake pedal a few times.
3. With brake pedal depressed with approximately 30 kg (66 lbs) load, measure pedal arm to wall clearance "B". It should be less than 60 mm (2.36 in.).
4. If clearance "B" is less than 60 mm (2.36 in.), the most possible cause is either rear brake shoes are worn out beyond limit or air is in the lines. Should clearance "B" remain less than 60 mm (2.36 in.) even after replacement of brake shoes and bleeding of system. Another possible but infrequent cause is booster push rod length out of adjustment or malfunction of rear brake shoe adjusters.

9. PAD LINING INSPECTION
1. Inspect pad linings periodically according to maintenance schedule and whenever wheels are removed (for tire rotation or other reason). Take a look through each end of caliper and check lining thickness of outside and inside pads.
2. If either end of either lining is worn and its thickness ("C" in figure) is less than 3 mm (0.12 in.), all pads must be replaced at the same time.

para_5_p178_2.text: It is recommended that the entire hydraulic system be thoroughly flushed with clean brake fluid whenever new parts are installed in the hydraulic system.

noti_5_p178_0.text: NOTICE: Brake fluid is extremely damaging to paint. If fluid should accidentally touch a painted surface, immediately wipe fluid from paint and clean the painted surface.

para_5_p178_3.text:
A bleeding operation is necessary to remove air whenever it is introduced to the hydraulic brake system.

The hydraulic lines of this brake system are based on the diagonal split system. When a brake pipe or hose is disconnected at the wheel, bleeding operation must be performed at both ends of the line of the removed pipe or hose. When any joint part of the master cylinder or other joint part between the master cylinder and each brake (wheel) is removed, the hydraulic brake system must be bled at all 4 wheel brakes.

noti_5_p179_0.text: NOTICE: Perform bleeding operation starting with wheel cylinder farthest from master cylinder and then at front caliper of the same brake line. Do the same on the other brake line.

2: Right brake caliper (front, sequence 2)

proc_5_p179_0.text:
Brake Bleeding Procedure
1) Fill master cylinder reservoir with brake fluid and keep at least one-half full of fluid during bleeding operation.
2) Remove bleeder plug cap. Attach a vinyl tube to the bleeder plug of wheel cylinder, and insert the other end into a container.
3) Depress brake pedal several times, and then while holding it depressed, loosen bleeder plug about one-third to one-half turn.
4) When the fluid pressure in the cylinder is almost depleted, retighten the bleeder plug.
5) Repeat this operation until there are no more air bubbles in hydraulic line.
6) When bubbles stop, depress and hold brake pedal and tighten bleeder plug.
7) Then attach bleeder plug cap.
8) After completing bleeding operation, apply fluid pressure to pipe line and check for leakage.
9) Replenish the fluid in the reservoir to specified level.
10) Check brake pedal for "sponginess". If found spongy, repeat entire procedure of bleeding.

10) Check brake pedal for "sponginess". If found spongy, repeat entire procedure of bleeding.

para_5_p180_0.text: The flexible hydraulic brake hose, which transmits hydraulic pressure from the steel brake line on the body to the rear cylinders and to the front calipers, should be inspected at least twice a year. The brake hose assembly should be checked for road hazard damage, for cracks and chafing of the outer cover, for leaks and blisters. A light and mirror may be needed for an adequate inspection. If any of the above conditions are observed on the brake hose, it will be necessary to replace it.

para_5_p180_1.text: Inspect the tube for damage, cracks, dents and corrosion. If any defect is found, replace it.

proc_5_p180_0.text:
14. BRAKE FLUID LEVEL INSPECTION
1. Be sure to use the recommended brake fluid as marked on the reservoir cap of the master cylinder or as recommended in the owner's manual.
2. Use of any other fluid is strictly prohibited.
3. Fluid level should be between MIN and MAX lines marked on reservoir.
4. When warning light lights sometimes during driving, replenish fluid to MAX line.
5. When fluid decreases quickly, inspect brake system for leakage. Correct leaking points and then refill to specified level.

noti_5_p180_0.text: NOTICE: Do not use shock absorber fluid or any other fluid which contains mineral oil. Do not use a container which has been used for mineral oil or a container which is wet from water. Mineral oil will cause swelling and distortion of rubber parts in hydraulic brake system and water mixed into brake fluid will lower fluid boiling point. Keep all fluid containers capped to prevent contamination.
