3) Install brake shoes to back plate. (Refer to steps 1 & 2 of BRAKE SHOE INSTALLATION of this section).
4) Install brake drum. (Refer to steps 1 – 7 of BRAKE DRUM INSTALLATION Figures 5-27, 5-28).
5) For proper routing and secure clamping of parking brake cable refer to Figures at left.
6) Connect cable to parking brake lever. Refer to Brake Lever Installation.
7) Upon completion of installation, adjust cable (Refer to Parking Brake Inspection and Adjustment in this section). Then check brake drum for dragging and brake system for proper performance.
8) Brake test car.

proc_5_p187_0.text:
Before disassembly, clean all around caliper with brake fluid.
1) Remove cylinder boot set ring and boot from cylinder groove.
2) Blow compressed air into cylinder through bolt hole, where flexible hose was fitted. With this air pressure, piston can be pushed out of cylinder.
steps: 1) Remove cylinder boot set ring and boot from cylinder groove.
2) Blow compressed air into cylinder through bolt hole, where flexible hose was fitted. With this air pressure, piston can be pushed out of cylinder.

caut_5_p187_0.text: CAUTION: Do not apply too highly compressed air which will cause piston to jump out of cylinder. It should be taken out gradually with moderately compressed air. Do not place your fingers in front of piston when using compressed air.

NOTICE: • Wash each part cleanly before installation in the same fluid as the one used in master cylinder reservoir.

proc_5_p187_1.text:
Before disassembly, clean all around caliper with brake fluid.
1) Piston seal: The piston seal is used to seal the piston to the cylinder and to adjust the clearance between the pads and disc. Replace seal at every overhaul. Fit piston seal into groove in cylinder, taking care not to twist it.
2) Piston and boot: Before inserting piston into cylinder, boot must be fitted in piston. Make sure to fit boot in proper direction as shown below.

proc_5_p191_0.text:
INSPECT BRAKE DISC
1. Before this inspection, brake pads must be removed (according to steps 1 – 4, PAD REMOVAL Fig. 5-20).
2. Check the disc surface for scratches in wearing parts. Scratches on the disc surface noticed at the time of the specified inspection or replacement are normal and the disc is not defective if these are not serious. But when there are deep scratches or scratches all over the surface, replace the disc. When only one side is scratched, polish and correct that side.

proc_5_p197_0.text:
1. INSPECT BRAKE DRUM
1. Inspect drum for cleanliness. Check wear of its braking surface by measuring its inside diameter.
2. Whenever brake drums are removed, they should be thoroughly cleaned and inspected for cracks, scores, deep grooves.
steps: Inspect drum for cleanliness. Check wear of its braking surface by measuring its inside diameter.
Whenever brake drums are removed, they should be thoroughly cleaned and inspected for cracks, scores, deep grooves.

tbl_5_p197_0.text:
1. INSPECT BRAKE DRUM
Item: Brake drum ID | Standard: 180 mm (7.09 in.) | Service Limit: 182 mm (7.16 in.)

para_5_p197_0.text: A cracked drum is unsafe for further service and must be replaced. Do not attempt to weld a cracked drum. Smooth up any slight scores. Heavy or extensive scoring will cause excessive brake lining wear and it will probably be necessary to resurface drum braking surface. If brake linings are slightly worn and drum is grooved, drum should be polished with fine emery cloth but should not be turned.

noti_5_p197_0.text: NOTICE: When drum is removed, visually inspect wheel cylinder for brake fluid leak. Correct leaky point, if any.

proc_5_p197_1.text:
2. INSPECT BRAKE SHOE & LINING
1. Where lining is worn out beyond service limit, replace shoe.
2. If one of brake linings is worn to service limit, all linings must be replaced at the same time.
steps: Where lining is worn out beyond service limit, replace shoe.
If one of brake linings is worn to service limit, all linings must be replaced at the same time.

tbl_5_p197_1.text:
2. INSPECT BRAKE SHOE & LINING
Brake lining: Thickness (lining + shoe rim) | Standard: 6.1 mm (0.24 in.) | Service limit: 2.8 mm (0.11 in.)

noti_5_p197_1.text: NOTICE: Never polish lining with sandpaper. If lining is polished with sandpaper, hard particles of sandpaper will be deposited in lining and may damage drum. When it is required to correct lining, replace it with a new one.

noti_5_p198_0.text: NOTICE: Clean wheel cylinder components with brake fluid.

proc_5_p202_0.text:
Inspection Master Cylinder
1. Inspect all disassembled parts for wear or damage, and replace parts if necessary.
2. Inspect master cylinder bore for scoring or corrosion. It is best to replace a corroded cylinder. Corrosion can be identified as pits or excessive roughness.
3. Rinse cylinder in clean brake fluid. Shake excess rinsing fluid from cylinder. Do not use a cloth to dry cylinder, as lint from cloth cannot be kept from cylinder bore surfaces.
steps: Inspect all disassembled parts for wear or damage, and replace parts if necessary.
Inspect master cylinder bore for scoring or corrosion. It is best to replace a corroded cylinder. Corrosion can be identified as pits or excessive roughness.
Rinse cylinder in clean brake fluid. Shake excess rinsing fluid from cylinder. Do not use a cloth to dry cylinder, as lint from cloth cannot be kept from cylinder bore surfaces.

noti_5_p202_0.text:
NOTICE: • Wash disassembled parts with brake fluid.
• Do not reuse piston cups.

noti_5_p202_1.text: NOTICE: Polishing the bore of the master cylinder with the cast aluminum body with anything abrasive is prohibited, as damage to the cylinder may occur.

noti_5_p202_2.text: NOTICE: Do not use shock absorber fluid or any other fluid which contains mineral oil. Do not use a container which has been used for mineral oil or a container which is wet from water. Mineral oil will cause swelling and distortion of rubber parts in the hydraulic brake system and water will mix with brake fluid, lowering the fluid boiling point. Keep all fluid containers capped to prevent contamination.

proc_5_p202_1.text:
Fill Reservoir
1. Fluid to fill reservoir with is indicated on reservoir cap of the car with embossed letters or in owner's manual supplied with the car.
2. Add fluid up to MAX line.
steps: Fluid to fill reservoir with is indicated on reservoir cap of the car with embossed letters or in owner's manual supplied with the car.

9) After installing, check pedal height and play.
10) Perform brake test and check each installed part for fluid leakage.

para_5_p209_0.text: There are two ways to perform this inspection; with and without a tester. Ordinarily, it is possible to roughly determine its condition without using a tester.

noti_5_p209_0.text: NOTICE: For this check, make sure that no air is in hydraulic line.

proc_5_p209_0.text:
INSPECTION WITHOUT TESTER — Check air tightness
1) Start engine.
2) Stop engine after running for 1 or 2 minutes.
3) Depress brake pedal several times with the same load as in ordinary braking and observe pedal travel. If pedal goes down deep the first time but its travel decreases as it is depressed the second and more times, air tightness is obtained.
4. 4 If pedal travel does not change, air tightness is not obtained.
steps: 1) Start engine.
2) Stop engine after running for 1 or 2 minutes.

3) Depress brake pedal several times with the same load as in ordinary braking and observe pedal travel. If pedal goes down deep the first time but its travel decreases as it is depressed the second and more times, air tightness is obtained.
4 If pedal travel does not change, air tightness is not obtained.

fig_5_p209_1.text:
Pedal travel unchanged on 1st, 2nd, 3rd depress — NO GOOD
1ST, 2ND, 3RD: All depressions at same travel — NO GOOD

noti_5_p209_1.text: NOTICE: If defective, inspect vacuum lines and sealing parts, and replace any faulty part. When this has been done, repeat the entire test!

proc_5_p209_1.text:
Check operation
1) With engine stopped, depress brake pedal several times with the same load and make sure that pedal travel does not change.
2) Start engine while depressing brake pedal. If pedal travel increases a little, operation is satisfactory. No change in pedal travel indicates malfunction.

Part: 3. Reaction disc | Inspect For: Damage or wear. | Corrective Action: Replace.
Part: 4. Diaphragm, boot, and rubber | Inspect For: Damage. | Corrective Action: Replace.
Part: 5. Piston rod and retainer | Inspect For: Damage or bend. | Corrective Action: Replace.
Part: 6. Booster No. 1 & No. 2 body | Inspect For: 1. Scratches, scores, pits, dents, or other damage affecting rolling or sealing of diaphragm or other seals.

3. Bent or nicked locking lugs.
4. Loose studs. | Corrective Action: Replace, unless easily repaired.

para_6_p214_0.text: ALL NEW GENERAL MOTORS VEHICLES ARE CERTIFIED BY THE UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY AS CONFORMING TO THE REQUIREMENTS OF THE REGULATIONS FOR THE CONTROL OF AIR POLLUTION FROM NEW MOTOR VEHICLES. THIS CERTIFICATION IS CONTINGENT ON CERTAIN ADJUSTMENTS BEING SET TO FACTORY STANDARDS. IN MOST CASES, THESE ADJUSTMENT POINTS EITHER HAVE BEEN PERMANENTLY SEALED AND/OR MADE INACCESSIBLE TO PREVENT INDISCRIMINATE OR ROUTINE ADJUSTMENT IN THE FIELD. FOR THIS REASON, THE FACTORY PROCEDURE FOR TEMPORARILY REMOVING PLUGS, CAPS, ETC., FOR PURPOSES OF SERVICING THE PRODUCT MUST BE STRICTLY FOLLOWED AND, WHEREVER PRACTICAL, RETURNED TO THE ORIGINAL INTENT OF THE DESIGN.

para_6_p214_1.text: An automobile engine is a combination of many machined, honed, polished, and lapped surfaces with tolerances that are measured in the thousandths of a millimeter (ten thousandths of an inch). Accordingly, when any internal engine parts are serviced, care and cleanliness are important. Throughout this section, it should be understood that proper cleaning and protection of machined surfaces and friction areas are part of the repair procedure. This is considered standard shop practice even if not specifically stated.

proc_6_p214_0.text:
Cleanliness and Care Guidelines
1. A liberal coating of engine oil should be applied to friction areas during assembly to protect and lubricate the surfaces on initial operation.
2. Whenever valve train components, pistons, piston rings, connecting rods, rod bearings, and crankshaft journal bearings are removed for service, they should be returned in order. At the time of installation, they should be installed in the same locations and with the same mating surfaces as when removed.
3. Battery cables should be disconnected before any major work is performed on the engine. Failure to disconnect cables may result in damage to wiring harness or other electrical parts.
4. Throughout this manual, the three cylinders of the engine are identified by numbers - No. 1, No. 2 and No. 3 as counted from crankshaft pulley side to flywheel side (Figure 1).
steps: A liberal coating of engine oil should be applied to friction areas during assembly to protect and lubricate the surfaces on initial operation.

Whenever valve train components, pistons, piston rings, connecting rods, rod bearings, and crankshaft journal bearings are removed for service, they should be returned in order. At the time of installation, they should be installed in the same locations and with the same mating surfaces as when removed.
Battery cables should be disconnected before any major work is performed on the engine. Failure to disconnect cables may result in damage to wiring harness or other electrical parts.
Throughout this manual, the three cylinders of the engine are identified by numbers - No. 1, No. 2 and No. 3 as counted from crankshaft pulley side to flywheel side (Figure 1).

noti_6_p215_0.text: NOTICE: The following information on engine service should be noted carefully, as it is important in preventing damage and in contributing to reliable engine performance.

para_6_p215_0.text:
When raising or supporting the engine for any reason, do not use a jack under the oil pan. Due to the small clearance between the oil pan and oil pump strainer, jacking against the oil pan may cause it to be bent against the oil pick-up unit.

It should be kept in mind, while working on the engine, that the 12-volt electrical system is capable of violent and damaging short circuits. When performing any work where electrical terminals could possibly be grounded, the ground cable of the battery should be disconnected at the battery.

fig_6_p215_0.text:
Figure 2 Spring Lock Connector Disconnection
A: PULL
1: CONNECTOR
2: LOCK SPRING
3: SCREWDRIVER

para_6_p215_1.text:
Any time the throttle body or air cleaner is removed, the intake opening should be covered. This will protect against accidental entrance of foreign material which could follow the intake passage into the cylinder and cause extensive damage when the engine is started.

When disconnecting connectors do not pull wiring harness but make sure to hold connector itself. Be sure to unlock the lock before disconnection with spring lock type connector. Remove spring before disconnecting. Do not deform spring (Figure 2).

noti_6_p215_1.text: NOTICE: Do not disconnect connector without unlocking. Damage to connector may result.

caut_6_p215_0.text: CAUTION: To reduce the risk of fire and personal injury, it is necesary to relieve the fuel system pressure before servicing fuel system components.

Figure 3 Hose Connections
HOSE: Hose component
PIPE: Pipe component
CLAMP: Clamp component

proc_6_p215_0.text:
Hose Connection Instructions
1. With short pipe, fit hose as far as it reaches pipe joint as shown. Clamp securely at a position 3 to 7 mm (0.12–0.27 in.) from hose end.
2. With the following type pipe, fit hose as far as its peripheral projection as shown. Clamp securely at a position 3 to 7 mm (0.12–0.27 in.) from hose end.
3. With bent pipe, fit hose as far as its bent part as shown or until pipe is about 20 to 30 mm (0.79–1.18 in.) into the hose. Clamp securely at a position 3 to 7 mm (0.12–0.27 in.) from hose end.
4. With straight pipe, fit hose until pipe is about 20 to 30 mm (0.79–1.18 in.) into the hose. Clamp securely at a position 3 to 7 mm (0.12–0.27 in.) from hose end.

tbl_6_p216_0.text:
ENGINE DIAGNOSIS
CONDITION: Hard starting (Engine Cranks OK)
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Blown fuse.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty spark plug.
  - CORRECTION: Clean and adjust plug gap or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Loose or disconnected spark plug wires or coil wire.
  - CORRECTION: Repair or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Improper ignition timing.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty ignition coil.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Maladjusted signal rotor air gap.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty crank angle sensor.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty electronic spark advance system.
  - CORRECTION: Refer to ENGINE ELECTRICAL (Section 6D).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Cracked rotor or cap in distributor.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty noise suppressor.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Dirty fuel filter.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Dirty or clogged fuel pipe.
  - CORRECTION: Clean.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Fuel pump will not work properly.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Lack of fuel in fuel tank.
  - CORRECTION: Refill.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Vacuum leak through inake manifold or throttle body gasket.
  - CORRECTION: Replace gasket.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: TBI
  - CORRECTION: Refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E12).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Incorrect spark plug tightening or faulty gasket.
  - CORRECTION: Tighten to specified torque or replace gasket.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Improper grade of engine oil or low viscosity.
  - CORRECTION: Replace with proper grade oil.

tbl_6_p216_1.text:
ENGINE DIAGNOSIS
CONDITION: Engine has no power
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Low compression
  - CORRECTION: Previously outlined.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Incorrect ignition timing.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective spark plugs.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Worn distributor terminals.
  - CORRECTION: Clean or replace. Also check rotor.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Loose or disconnected spark plug wires or coil wire.
  - CORRECTION: Connect or replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty electronic advance system.
  - CORRECTION: Refer to ENGINE ELECTRICAL (Section 6D.)
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Engine overheating.
  - CORRECTION: Refer to "Overheating Chart" of this section.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Clogged fuel pipe.
  - CORRECTION: Clean.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Dirty or clogged fuel.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.

tbl_6_p218_0.text:

  - CORRECTION: Clean or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Clogged air cleaner.
  - CORRECTION: Clean or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunction of thermostatically controlled air cleaner.
  - CORRECTION: Check and correct.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Loose connection or disconnection of vacuum hose.
  - CORRECTION: Connect securely.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Air leaking from intake manifold or throttle body gasket.
  - CORRECTION: Replace gasket.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning EGR valve.
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning high altitude switch.
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning throttle position switch (wide open switch).
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Dragging brakes.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Slipping clutch.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust or replace.

tbl_6_p218_1.text:
ENGINE DIAGNOSIS
CONDITION: Improper engine idling or engine fails to idle
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Clogged air cleaner element.
  - CORRECTION: Clean or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Leaky manifold, throttle body, or cylinder head gaskets.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective spark plug.
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Worn or disconnected coil wire.
  - CORRECTION: Connect or replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Worn distributor terminals.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Improper ignition timing.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Worn, loose connection, or disconnection of spark plug wires.
  - CORRECTION: Connect or replace.

tbl_6_p218_2.text:
ENGINE DIAGNOSIS
CONDITION: Engine hesitates (Momentary lack of response as the accelerator is depressed. Can occur at all car speeds. Usually most severe when first trying to make the car move, as from a stop sign.)
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Improper ignition timing.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective spark plug or plug gap out of adjustment.
  - CORRECTION: Replace or adjust gap.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective coil wire.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Clogged fuel filter, hose, or pipe
  - CORRECTION: Clean or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Clogged air cleaner element.
  - CORRECTION: Clean or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Loose manifold and throttle body bolts and nuts.
  - CORRECTION: Retighten.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Leaky manifold and throttle body/gaskets.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: TBI system out of order.
  - CORRECTION: Refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning throttle position switch (wide open switch).
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning high altitude switch.
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.

  - CORRECTION: Refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E12).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Improper ignition timing.
  - CORRECTION: Adjust.

  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective spark plug (excess carbon deposits, electrodes, etc.).
  - CORRECTION: Check and clean, adjust or replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning throttle position switch (wide open switch).
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning high altitude switch.
  - CORRECTION: Check and replace as necessary.

tbl_6_p221_0.text:

  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Malfunctioning EGR valve.

tbl_6_p221_1.text:

proc_6_p225_0.text:
Check the compression on all three cylinders as follows:
1. Warm up engine.
2. Stop engine after warming up.
3. Remove all spark plugs and disconnect distributor wire harness at connector.
4. Remove 15A fuse for main relay (Figure 4).
5. Install gage into spark plug hole.
6. Disengage the clutch (to lighten starting load on engine), and depress the accelerator pedal all the way to make the throttle full-open.
7. Crank the engine with the fully charged battery, and read the highest pressure on the compression gage.
8. Carry out steps 5 through 7 on each cylinder to obtain three readings.
steps: Warm up engine.
Stop engine after warming up.
Remove all spark plugs and disconnect distributor wire harness at connector.
Remove 15A fuse for main relay (Figure 4).
Install gage into spark plug hole.
Disengage the clutch (to lighten starting load on engine), and depress the accelerator pedal all the way to make the throttle full-open.
Crank the engine with the fully charged battery, and read the highest pressure on the compression gage.
Carry out steps 5 through 7 on each cylinder to obtain three readings.

proc_6_p226_0.text:
Figures 6 and 7
1. Warm up the engine to normal operating temperature.
2. With engine off, disconnect vacuum hose from gas filter. Connect three-way joint, hoses, vacuum gage, and joint between gas filter and disconnected vacuum hose (METRO) LSI. For METRO model, remove plug from intake manifold and install vacuum gage to threaded hole.
3. Run the engine at the specified idling speed (700 - 800 rpm), and read the vacuum gage. The vacuum should be within specification.
4. After checking, remove vacuum gage, joint vacuum hose. Reconnect hose to gas filter (METRO) LSI. For METRO model, remove vacuum gage, and install plug into intake manifold.

para_6a1_p228_0.text:
The engine is a water-cooled in line 3 cylinder, 4 stoke cycle gasoline unit with a direct acting type S.O.H.C. (Single overhead camshaft) valve configuration.

The single overhead camshaft is mounted over the cylinder head; it is driven from crankshaft through timing belt, and no push rods are provided in the valve train system. The camshaft opens and closes the valves directly through the hydraulic valve lash adjusters (Figure 1).

The oil pump is of a trochoid type, and mounted on the crankshaft at the crankshaft pully side.

Oil is drawn up through the oil pump strainer and passed the pump to the oil filter.

The filtered oil flows into two paths in cylinder block.

In one path, oil reaches the crankshaft journal bearings.

Oil from the crankshaft journal bearings is supplied to the connecting rod bearings by means of intersecting passages drilled in the crankshaft, and then injected from a small hole provided on the large end of connecting rod to lubricate piston, rings, and cylinder wall.

In another path, oil goes up the cylinder head and lubricates hydraulic valve lash adjusters, valves and camshaft, etc., after passing through the internal oilway of the rocker arm shafts.

There is a check valve in the path from the engine block to the cylinder head. It serves to keep the oil gallery and cylinder head filled with oil even when the engine is not running.

An oil relief valve is provided on the oil pump. This valve starts relieving oil pressure when the pressure comes over about 3.0 kg/cm² (42.7 psi, 300 kPa). Relieved oil drains back to the oil pan (Figure 2)

para_6a1_p229_0.text:
The cylinder head is made of cast aluminum alloy and has three combustion chambers arranged in-line.

The single overhead camshaft is driven by the crankshaft through the timing belt. It has six cam lobes and operates the intake and exhaust valves directly through the hydraulic valve lash adjuster.

proc_6a1_p229_0.text:
CYLINDER HEAD, VALVE TRAIN AND HYDRAULIC VALVE LASH ADJUSTER
1. When the camshaft doesn't push the valve lash adjuster, the valve lash adjuster is pushed against the cam and the body against the stem by the plunger spring force. In this state, the valve lash is kept to "O". (At "O" valve lash, the oil pressure becomes equal in the chambers "A" and "B", and the check ball closes the passage between these two chambers).
2. When the cam lobe of the camshaft starts pushing the valve lash adjuster, the valve lash adjuster and plunger are pushed downward and at the same time the body is pushed upward by the counter force from the valve stem. As a result, the chamber "B" is compressed and the pressure rises high. Then the oil in the chamber "B" leaks through the slight clearance between the body and plunger. However, as the compression time is very short, the volume hardly changes and thus the valve lash adjuster, plunger and body, as one unit, push down the valve stem to open the valve.
3. When the pushing of the camshaft against the valve lash adjuster is over, the operation as described in step 1, starts again. As the oil pressure in the chamber "B" is lower than that in "A" (for the oil in the chamber "B" under high pressure has leaked gradually in above 2.), the oil pressure in the chamber "A" pushes the check ball open to allow the oil to flow from the chamber "A" to chamber "B" till the oil pressure becomes equal between the two chambers (Figure 3).
steps: 1. When the camshaft doesn't push the valve lash adjuster, the valve lash adjuster is pushed against the cam and the body against the stem by the plunger spring force. In this state, the valve lash is kept to "O". (At "O" valve lash, the oil pressure becomes equal in the chambers "A" and "B", and the check ball closes the passage between these two chambers).

2. When the cam lobe of the camshaft starts pushing the valve lash adjuster, the valve lash adjuster and plunger are pushed downward and at the same time the body is pushed upward by the counter force from the valve stem. As a result, the chamber "B" is compressed and the pressure rises high. Then the oil in the chamber "B" leaks through the slight clearance between the body and plunger. However, as the compression time is very short, the volume hardly changes and thus the valve lash adjuster, plunger and body, as one unit, push down the valve stem to open the valve.

3. When the pushing of the camshaft against the valve lash adjuster is over, the operation as described in step 1, starts again. As the oil pressure in the chamber "B" is lower than that in "A" (for the oil in the chamber "B" under high pressure has leaked gradually in above 2.), the oil pressure in the chamber "A" pushes the check ball open to allow the oil to flow from the chamber "A" to chamber "B" till the oil pressure becomes equal between the two chambers (Figure 3).

para_6a1_p229_1.text:
The cylinder block is made of cast aluminum alloy and has 3 cylinders arranged "In Line". A cylindrical cast iron sleeve is installed in each cylinder.

A monoblock casting crankshaft is supported by 4 main bearings which are of the precision insert type. Three crank pins on the crankshaft are positioned 120° apart.

para_6a1_p229_2.text:
The piston is cast aluminum alloy, and has two compression rings and one oil ring (Metro "Base" Model has one compression ring).

Of these compression rings (top and 2nd rings), the top ring is plated with hard chromium for improvement in abrasion resistance.

The oil ring consists of two rails and one spacer. The piston pin is offset 0.5 mm (0.02 in.) towards the major thrust pressure against the cylinder wall as the piston travels its path. Pins are chromium steel and have a floating fit in the pistons. They are retained in the connecting rods by a press fit. The connecting rods are made of forged steel, and rod bearings are of precision insert type.

proc_6a1_p234_0.text:
Remove or Disconnect — Throttle Body and Intake Manifold
1. Relieve fuel pressure. Refer to ENGINE FUEL (Section 6C).
2. Negative battery cable.
3. Drain engine coolant.
4. Air cleaner assembly as previously outlined.
5. Following electric wires: Vacuum switching valve for EGR valve. Water temperature sensor. Idle speed control solenoid valve. Ground wires from intake manifold. Fuel injector. Throttle switch or throttle position sensor. Water temperature gage.
6. Fuel return and feed hoses from throttle body.
7. Water hoses from throttle body and intake manifold.
8. Following vacuum hoses: Canister purge hose from intake manifold. Canister hose from its pipe. Pressure sensor hose from intake manifold. Brake booster hose from intake manifold.
9. PCV hose from cylinder head cover.
10. Accelerator cable from throttle body.
11. Other lines and cables, as necessary.
12. Intake manifold with throttle body from cylinder head.

proc_6a1_p234_1.text:
Install or Connect — Throttle Body and Intake Manifold
1. Intake manifold gasket to cylinder head. Use a new gasket.
2. Intake manifold with throttle body to cylinder head. Install clamps (Figure 12).
3. PCV hose to cylinder head cover.
4. Vacuum hoses (Figure 4).
5. Water hoses (Figure 11).
6. Fuel return and feed hoses to throttle body (Figure 13).
7. Electrical lead wires.
8. Accelerator cable to throttle body.
9. Air cleaner assembly to throttle body as previously outlined.
10. Check to ensure that all removed parts are back in place. Reinstall any necessary parts which have not been reinstalled.
11. Refill cooling system.
12. Negative battery cable.
13. Upon completion of installation, start engine and check for fuel leaks and engine cooling water leaks.

proc_6a1_p237_0.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Negative battery cable.
2. Hoist vehicle.
3. Fender apron extension on right side. Remove clip after pushing center pin (Figure 19).
4. Water pump belt and its pulley. Loosen generator pivot bolts and its adjusting bolt and remove water pump belt.
5. Crankshaft pulley by removing four pulley bolts.
6. Timing belt outside cover.
7. Before removal of timing belt, align four timing marks by turning crankshaft (Figure 21).
8. Timing belt tensioner, tensioner plate, tensioner spring, spring damper and timing belt.
steps: 1. Negative battery cable.
2. Hoist vehicle.
3. Fender apron extension on right side. Remove clip after pushing center pin (Figure 19).
4. Water pump belt and its pulley. Loosen generator pivot bolts and its adjusting bolt and remove water pump belt.
5. Crankshaft pulley by removing four pulley bolts.
6. Timing belt outside cover.
7. Before removal of timing belt, align four timing marks by turning crankshaft (Figure 21).
8. Timing belt tensioner, tensioner plate, tensioner spring, spring damper and timing belt.

noti_6a1_p237_0.text: NOTICE: It is not necessary to loosen crankshaft timing belt pulley bolt at the center.

impo_6a1_p237_0.text: IMPORTANT: After timing belt is removed, never turn camshaft and crankshaft independently more than such an extent (Figure 23). If turned, interference may occur among pistons and valves, and parts related to pistons and valves may be damaged.

proc_6a1_p237_1.text:
Inspect
1. Timing belt for wear or crack. Replace it as necessary.
2. Tensioner for smooth rotation.
steps: Timing belt for wear or crack. Replace it as necessary.
Tensioner for smooth rotation.

proc_6a1_p237_2.text:
Install or Connect
1. Tensioner plate to tensioner. Insert lug of tensioner plate into hole of tensioner.
2. Tensioner and tensioner plate: Do not tighten tensioner bolt with wrench yet. Hand tighten only at this time. Check to ensure that plate movement in arrow direction (Figure 25) causes tensioner to move in the same direction. If no associated movement between plate and tensioner occurs, remove tensioner and plate again and reinsert plate lug into tensioner hole.
3. Check that timing mark on camshaft timing belt pulley is aligned with "V" mark on cylinder head cover. If not, align two marks by turning camshaft but be careful not to turn it more than its allowable turning range (Figure 23).
4. Check that punch mark on crankshaft timing belt pulley is aligned with arrow mark on oil pump case. If not, align two marks by turning crankshaft but be careful not to turn it more than its allowable turning range.
5. Timing belt, tensioner spring and damper. With two sets of marks aligned and tensioner plate pushed up, install timing belt on two pulleys such a way that drive side of belt is free from any slack. Then install tensioner spring and spring damper (Figure 28), hand tighten tensioner stud.
6. To take up slack of timing belt, turn crankshaft two rotations clockwise after installing it. After making sure that belt is free from slack, tighten tensioner stud first to 11 N·m (8 lb.ft.), tensioner bolt to 27 N·m (20 lb.ft.). Then confirm again that two sets of marks are aligned respectively.
7. Timing belt outside cover.
steps: 1. Tensioner plate to tensioner. Insert lug of tensioner plate into hole of tensioner.

2. Tensioner and tensioner plate: Do not tighten tensioner bolt with wrench yet. Hand tighten only at this time. Check to ensure that plate movement in arrow direction (Figure 25) causes tensioner to move in the same direction. If no associated movement between plate and tensioner occurs, remove tensioner and plate again and reinsert plate lug into tensioner hole.

3. Check that timing mark on camshaft timing belt pulley is aligned with "V" mark on cylinder head cover. If not, align two marks by turning camshaft but be careful not to turn it more than its allowable turning range (Figure 23).

4. Check that punch mark on crankshaft timing belt pulley is aligned with arrow mark on oil pump case. If not, align two marks by turning crankshaft but be careful not to turn it more than its allowable turning range.

5. Timing belt, tensioner spring and damper. With two sets of marks aligned and tensioner plate pushed up, install timing belt on two pulleys such a way that drive side of belt is free from any slack. Then install tensioner spring and spring damper (Figure 28), hand tighten tensioner stud.

6. To take up slack of timing belt, turn crankshaft two rotations clockwise after installing it. After making sure that belt is free from slack, tighten tensioner stud first to 11 N·m (8 lb.ft.), tensioner bolt to 27 N·m (20 lb.ft.). Then confirm again that two sets of marks are aligned respectively.
7. Timing belt outside cover.

impo_6a1_p237_1.text:
IMPORTANT: When installing timing belt, match arrow mark on timing belt with rotating direction of crankshaft.
In this state, No. 1 piston is at top dead center of compression stroke.

noti_6a1_p237_1.text: NOTICE: Before installing, make sure that seal is between water pump and oil pump case.

proc_6a1_p243_0.text:
Assemble
Install or Connect
1. Wash, clean, and dry all disassembled parts.
2. Apply thin coat of engine oil to inner and outer rotors, oil seal lip portion, and inside surfaces of oil pump case and plate.
3. Outer and inner rotors to pump case.
4. Rotor plate. Tighten five screws securely. After installing plate, check to be sure that gears turn smoothly by hand.
5. Guide seal to pump case and then oil level gage guide (Figure 41).
6. Two oil pump pins and oil pump gasket to cylinder block.
7. To prevent oil seal lip from being damaged or upturned when installing oil pump to crankshaft, fit oil seal guide to crankshaft, and apply engine oil to oil seal guide.
8. Oil pump to crankshaft and cylinder block: Among seven oil pump bolts, three No. 1 bolts and one No. 3 bolt are shorter than three No. 2 bolts in length. The thread of new No. 3 bolt is coated with sealant. Apply sealant to the thread of No. 3 bolt only when reusing. Install No. 1, No. 2 and No. 3 bolts in such arrangement (Figure 47), and tighten them to 11 N·m (8 lb.ft.). After installing oil pump, check to be sure that oil seal lip is not upturned, and then remove oil seal guide.
9. Rubber seal between oil pump and water pump.
10. Edge of oil pump gasket might bulge out: if it does, cut bulge off with sharp knife, making edge smooth and flush with end faces of pump case and cylinder block.
11. Timing belt guide, key, and crankshaft timing belt gear. Install timing belt guide in such a way that its concave side faces oil pump. With crankshaft locked, tighten crankshaft timing belt gear bolt to 110 N·m (81 lb.ft.) (Figure 50).
12. Timing belt, tensioner, and other parts as previously outlined.
13. Adjust water pump belt tension.
14. Refill engine oil.
15. Negative battery cable.
16. After completing installation, check oil pressure by running engine.

para_6a1_p247_1.text:
Set camshaft between two "V" blocks, and measure its runout by using a dial gage. If measured runout exceeds it limit of 0.10 mm (0.0039 in.) replace camshaft (Figure 60).

Check camshaft journals and camshaft housings, for pitting, scratches, wear or damage.

If any these conditions are found, replace camshaft or cylinder head without replacing housings.

Check clearance by using gaging plastic. The procedure as follows:

proc_6a1_p247_1.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Clean housings and camshaft journals.
2. Make sure that all valve lash adjusters are removed and install camshaft to cylinder head.
3. Place a piece of gaging plastic the full width of journal of camshaft (parallel to camshaft).
4. Install housings (Figures 72 and 73) and evenly torque housing bolts to 11 N·m (8 lb.ft.). Housing MUST be torqued to specification in order to ensure proper reading of camshaft journal clearance.
5. Remove housing, and using scale on gaging plastic envelop, measure gaging plastic width at its widest point. The standard camshaft journal clearance is 0.020 - 0.062 mm (0.0008 - 0.0024 in.) If measured camshaft journal clearance exceeds its limit of 0.12 mm (0.0047 in.), measure journal (housing) bore and outside diameter of camshaft journal. Replace camshaft or cylinder head assembly, whichever the difference from specification is greater (Figures 61, 62, and 63).
steps: 1. Clean housings and camshaft journals.
2. Make sure that all valve lash adjusters are removed and install camshaft to cylinder head.
3. Place a piece of gaging plastic the full width of journal of camshaft (parallel to camshaft).
4. Install housings (Figures 72 and 73) and evenly torque housing bolts to 11 N·m (8 lb.ft.). Housing MUST be torqued to specification in order to ensure proper reading of camshaft journal clearance.

5. Remove housing, and using scale on gaging plastic envelop, measure gaging plastic width at its widest point. The standard camshaft journal clearance is 0.020 - 0.062 mm (0.0008 - 0.0024 in.) If measured camshaft journal clearance exceeds its limit of 0.12 mm (0.0047 in.), measure journal (housing) bore and outside diameter of camshaft journal. Replace camshaft or cylinder head assembly, whichever the difference from specification is greater (Figures 61, 62, and 63).

noti_6a1_p248_0.text: NOTICE: Do not rotate camshaft while gaging plastic is installed.

noti_6a1_p251_0.text:
NOTICE: • Don't turn camshaft or start engine (i.e., valves should be operated) for about half an hour after reinstalling hydrualic valve lash adjusters and camshaft. As it takes time for valves to settle in place, operating engine within half an hour after their installation may cause interference to occur between valves and piston.
• If air is trapped in valve lash adjuster, valve may make tapping sound when engine is operated after valve lash adjuster is installed. In such a case, run engine for about half an hour at about 2,000 rpm, and then air will be purged and tapping sound will cease. Should tapping sound not cease, it is possible that valve lash adjuster is defective. Among the six of them, if defective adjuster can't be located, check as follows.

Disassemble
- For ease in servicing cylinder head, remove intake manifold with throttle body, exhaust manifold and distributor from cylinder head.
- Remove camshaft and valve lash adjusters from cylinder head.
- Using valve compressor J 8062, compress valve springs and then remove valve keepers by using forceps.
- Release valve compressor, and remove spring retainer and valve spring.
- Remove valve from combustion chamber side.
- Remove valve stem from valve guide, and valve spring seat.
- Using valve guide remover J 37968-1, drive valve guide out from combustion chamber side to valve spring side.
8. Place disassembled parts except valve stem seal and valve guide in order so that they can be installed in their original positions.

proc_6a1_p253_0.text:
Valves
1. Remove all carbon from valves.
2. Inspect each valve for wear, burn or distortion at its face and stem and, replace as necessary.
3. Measure thickness of valve head. If measured thickness exceeds limit, replace valve. The standard valve head thickness for intake valves is 1.0 mm (0.039 in.) and the limit is 0.6 mm (0.023 in.). The standard valve head thickness for exhaust valves is 1.2 mm (0.047 in.) and the limit is 0.7 mm (0.027 in.).
4. Inspect valve stem end face for pitting and wear. If pitting or wear is found, valve stem end may be resurfaced, replace valve if chamfer area is excessively worn.
5. Check each valve for radial runout with a dial gauge and "V" block. To check runout, rotate valve slowly. If measured runout exceeds limit of 0.08 mm (0.003 in.), replace valve.

A valve seat not producing a uniform contact with its valve or showing seating contact that is off the specified width must be repaired by regrinding finish by lapping.

The valve seat for both the intake and exhaust valves are machined to two different angles. The seat contact surface is cut 45° and the area above the contact surface (closest to the combustion chamber) is cut to 15°.

para_6a1_p255_2.text:
Check cylinder head for cracks in intake and exhaust ports, combustion chambers, and head surface.

Flatness of gasket surface: Using a straightedge and thickness gage, check surface at a total of six locations. If distortion exceeds the 0.05 mm (0.002 in.), correct gasket surface with a surface plate and abrasive paper of about #400 (Waterproof silicon carbide abrasive paper), place paper on and over surface plate, and rub gasket surface against paper to grind off high spots. Should this fail to reduce thickness gage readings to within the limit, replace cylinder head. Leakage of combustion gases from this gasket joint is often due to a warped gasket surface; such leakage results in reduced power output (Figures 89 and 90).

Valve Springs
1. Referring to data given in next column, check to be sure that each spring is in sound condition, free of any evidence of breakage or weakening. Remember, weakened valve springs can cause chatter, not to mention possibility of reducing power output due to gas leakage caused by decreased seating pressure (Figure 93 and 94).
2. Spring squareness: Use a square and surface plate to check each spring for squareness in terms of clearance between end of valve spring and square. Valve springs found to exhibit a larger clearance than 2.0 mm (.079 in.) must be replaced (Figure 95).

Inspect

- • Using a cylinder gage, measure the cylinder bore in thrust and axial direction at two positions (Figure 108). If any of the following measurements are out of specifications, rebore the cylinder.

2. Difference of measurements at two positions exceeds the taper limit.
3. Difference between thrust and axial measurements exceeds the out-of-round limit.

proc_6a1_p260_0.text:
Pistons
1. Piston for cracks or other damage. Damaged pistons should be replaced.
2. Piston diameter: Piston diameter should be measured at the height of 11.5 mm (0.45 in.) from the piston skirt end in the direction perpendicular to piston pin (Figure 110).
3. Piston clearance: To calculate piston clearance, measure cylinder bore diameter and piston diameter. This piston clearance is the difference between cylinder bore diameter and piston diameter. This piston clearance should be within the specification stated below. If it is out of specification, rebore the cylinder and use oversize piston. Piston clearance 0.02 -0.04 mm (0.0008 - 0.0015 in.). The cylinder bore diameters measured in thrust direction at two positions (Figure 108) should be used calculation of piston clearance.
4. Ring groove clearance: Before checking, the piston grooves must be clean, dry and free of carbon. Fit the new piston ring into groove and measure the clearance between the ring and the ring land by using thickness gage.
5. If the clearance is out of specification, replace the piston (Figure 111).

This piston clearance should be within the specification stated below. If it is out of specification, rebore the cylinder and use oversize piston. Piston clearance 0.02 -0.04 mm (0.0008 - 0.0015 in.). The cylinder bore diameters measured in thrust direction at two positions (Figure 108) should be used calculation of piston clearance.

Ring groove clearance: Before checking, the piston grooves must be clean, dry and free of carbon. Fit the new piston ring into groove and measure the clearance between the ring and the ring land by using thickness gage.
If the clearance is out of specification, replace the piston (Figure 111).

Piston Pin
1. Piston pin must be fitted into the piston bore with an easy finger push at normal room temperature.
2. Check piston pin or piston bore is badly worn or damaged, replace pin or piston, or both.

proc_6a1_p260_2.text:
Piston Rings
1. To measure the end gap, insert the piston ring into the top of cylinder bore and then measure the gap by using the thickness gage.
2. If the gap measured is out of specification, replace the ring. Decarbon and clean the top of cylinder bore, before inserting the piston ring (Figure 112).

Inspect the bearing shells for signs of pitting, burn or flaking and observe the contact pattern. Bearing shells found to be defective must be replaced.

Two kinds of rod bearing are available: standard size bearing and 0.25 mm undersize bearing. To distinguish them, 0.25 mm undersize bearing has the stamped number (US 025) on its backside as indicated in (Figure 115), but standard size has no number.

proc_6a1_p262_0.text:
Rod Bearing Clearance
1. Before checking bearing clearance, clean the bearing and the crank pin.
2. Install the bearing in the connecting rod and the bearing cap.
3. Place a piece of gaging plastic the full width of the crankpin as contacted by bearing (parallel to the crankshaft), avoiding the oil hole.
4. Install the rod bearing cap to connecting rod. When installing the cap, be sure to point the arrow mark on the cap to crankshaft pulley side, as indicatd in (Figure 116). DO NOT turn the crankshaft with gaging plastic installed.
5. Remove the cap and using the scale on the gaging plastic envelope, measure the gaging plastic width at the widest point. If the clearance exceeds the limit, use a new standard size bearing and remeasure the clearance (Figure 117).
6. If the clearance can not be brought to within the limit even by using a new standard size bearing, regrind the crankpin to the undersize and use the 0.25 mm oversize bearing.

proc_6a1_p263_1.text:
Install or Connect
1. Apply engine oil to pistons, rings, cylinder walls, connecting rod bearings, and crankpins.
2. Install a piece of hose over connecting rod threads. These guide hoses protect the crankpin
3. and threads of rod bolt from damage during installation of connecting rod and piston assembly.
3. When installing piston and connecting rod assembly into cylinder bore, point the arrow mark on piston head to crankshaft pulley side.
4. Piston and connecting rod assembly into cylinder bore. Use J8037 (Piston ring compressor) to compress the rings. Guide the connecting rod into place on the crankshaft. Using a hammer handle, tap the piston head to install the piston into the bore, hold the ring compressor firmly against the cylinder block until all piston rings have entered the cylinder bore.
5. Bearing Cap: When installing the cap to the rod, point the arrow mark on cap to crankshaft pulley side.
6. Reverse the removal procedures for the installation of remaining parts.
7. Adjust the water pump belt tension.
8. Refill engine oil.
9. Refill cooling system. Head
10. Negative battery cable.
11. Adjust accelerator cable. See DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).
12. Check ignition timing and adjust as necessary.
13. Upon completion of installation, verify that there is no fuel, water, or exhaust gas leaks.

Installing Bearing Cap
1. The upper half of bearing has an oil groove. Install this half with oil groove to cylinder (Figure 130).
2. On each main bearing cap, the arrow mark and number are embossed (Figure 131). When installing each bearing cap to cylinder block, point the arrow mark toward crankshaft pulley side and install each cap from crankshaft pulley side to flywheel side in order "1", "2", "3", and "4".

proc_6a1_p265_1.text:
Inspect
1. Check the bearings for pitting, scratches, wear, or damage.
2. If any of the above is found, replace both upper and lower halves. Never replace one half without replacing the other half.

para_6a1_p265_1.text: Check the clearance by using gaging plastic. The procedure is as follows:

proc_6a1_p265_2.text:
Main Bearing Clearance Check
- Remove bearing caps.
- Clean the bearings and main journals.
- Place a piece of gaging plastic the full width of bearing (parallel to the crankshaft) on the journal, avoiding oil hole.
- Install the bearing cap as previously outlined and torque the cap bolts to the specified torque. Bearing cap MUST be torqued to specification in order to assure proper reading.
5. Remove the cap, and using the scale on the gaging plastic envelope, measure the gaging plastic width at its widest point. If the clearance exceeds the limit, replace the bearing. Always replace both upper and lower inserts as a unit. A new standard bearing may produce proper clearance. If not, it will be necessary to regrind undersize bearing. After selecting new bearing, recheck the clearance (Figure 132).

para_6a1_p266_0.text: If the bearing is damaged, or the bearing clearance is out of specification, select a new standard bearing according to the following procedure and install it.

proc_6a1_p266_0.text:
STANDARD BEARING
1. Check the journal diameter by the method below.

The counter weights of No.1 cylinder have four stamped numbers. The three numbers can be a ("1", "2", and "3"). The first, second, third and fourth (left to right) stamped numbers indicate the journal diameters at bearing cap "1", "2", "3", and "4" respectively. For example, the first (leftmost) number "3" indicates that the journal diameter at bearing cap "1" is with 44.982 - 44.988 mm, and the second one "1" indicates that the journal diameter at cap "2" is within 44.994 - 45.000 mm (Figure 131).
2. Check the bearing cap bore diameter without bearing. On the mating surface of cylinder block, four letters are stamped. The stamped letters indicate the following cap bore diameters. The first, second, third, and fourth (left to right) stamped letters indicate the cap bore diameters of bearing caps "1", "2", "3", and "4", respectively. The first (leftmost) letter "B" indicates that the cap bore diameter of bearing cap "1" is within 49.006 - 49.012 mm, and the fourth (rightmost) letter "A" indicates that the cap bore diameter cap "4" is within 49.000 - 49.006 mm (Figure 134).
3. There are five sizes of standard bearings. To distinguish them, they are painted the following colors at the position (Figure 135). Each color indicates the following thicknesses at the center of bearing.
4. From the numbers stamped on the counter weights of No. 1 cylinder and the letters stamped on the mating surface of cylinder block, determine new standard bearing to be installed on the journal, by referring to the table (Figure 134). For example if the number stamped on the counter weight is "1" and letter stamped on the mating surface is "B", install the new standard bearing painted with "Black" to its journal.
5. Using gaging plastic, check the bearing clearance with the new standard bearing selected. If the clearance still exceeds the limit, use the next thicker bearing and recheck the clearance.
6. When replacing the crankshaft or the engine block due to any reason, select new standard bearings to be installed by referring to the numbers stamped on new crankshaft or the letters stamped on the mating surface of new engine block.

Remove or Disconnect

1. Measure end play with crankshaft set in the engine block and with the thrust bearing fitted and journal bearing caps installed.
2. Tighten bearing cap bolts to 54 N·m (40 lb.ft.).
3. Use a dial gage to read the displacement in axial (thrust) direction of the crankshaft.

proc_6a1_p270_1.text:
Out-of-Round and Taper (Uneven Wear) of Journals
1. An unevenly worn crankshaft journal shows up as a difference in diameter at a cross section or along its length (or both). This difference, if any, is to be determined from micrometer readings taken.
2. If any of the journals is badly damaged or if the amount exceeds the limit, regrind or replace the crankshaft.

Horning or Reboring Cylinders
1. When any cylinder needs reboring, all other cylinders must also be rebored at same time.
2. Select oversized piston according to amount of cylinder wear.
3. Using micrometer, measure the piston diameter.
4. Calculate the cylinder bore diameter to be rebored. D = A + B - C D : Cylinder bore diameter to be rebored. A : Piston diameter as measured. B : Piston clearance = 0.02 - 0.04 mm (0.0008 -= 0.0015 in.). C : Allowance for honing = 0.02 mm (0.0008 in.)
5. Rebore and hone cylinder to calculated dimension.
Groove

proc_6a1_p272_0.text:
Install or Connect
[CONTINUED FROM PREVIOUS PAGE]
1. Main bearings to cylinder block: Install the half with oil groove to cylinder block.
2. Thrust bearings to cylinder block between No. 2 and No. 3 cylinders. Face th oil groove sides to counterweights. (Figure 150).
3. Crankshaft to cylinder block.
4. When fitting the bearing caps to journals after setting the crankshaft in place, be sure to point the arrow mark (on each cap) to crankshaft pulley side. Fit them sequentially in order, 1, 2, 3 and 4, starting from pulley side.
5. Oil seal housing and gasket. Install new gasket. DO NOT reuse old gasket. Oil the portion of oil seal before installing. Among five housing bolts, two No. 1 bolts and one No. 2 bolt are shorter than two No. 3 bolts in length. The thread of new NO. 2 bolt is coated with sealant. Apply to the thread of No. 2 bolt only when reusing. After the oil seal housing, the gasket edges might bulge out; if so cut off the edges to flush with cylinder block and oil seal housing.
6. Oil pump: Refer to "OIL PUMP" for installation of oil pump procedure in this section.
7. Flywheel: Using a flywheel holder, hold the flywheel, and tighten the flywheel bolts to specification 61 N·m (45 lb.ft.).
8. Pistons and connecting rods as previously outlined.
9. Oil pump strainer and oil pan.
10. Cylinder head assembly to cylinder block. Before installing cylinder head assembly to cylinder block, install check valve into oil gallary in cylinder block, directing slit of valve toward top of cylinder block.

tbl_6b_p278_0.text:
GENERAL DESCRIPTION: 6B-1
DIAGNOSIS: 6B-4
Maintenance: 6B-4
Coolant System: 6B-4
Coolant Level: 6B-4
Cooling System Service: 6B-4
Cooling System Draining, Flushing, and Refill: 6B-5
ON-VEHICLE SERVICE: 6B-5
Cooling Water Pipes or Hoses: 6B-6

para_6b_p278_0.text:
The cooling system consists of the radiator, the cap, water reservoir tank, hoses, water pump, cooling fan and thermostat. The radiator is of the tube-and-fin type.

During engine warm-up (thermostat closed), the water pump discharges coolant into the water jacket chamber adjacent to No. 1 cylinder.

Coolant then flows through the cylinder block and the cylinder head. Coolant then returns to the water pump through intake manifold, heater inlet hose, heater unit, heater outlet hose, and water intake pipe.

During normal temperatures (thermostat open), coolant takes the same basic route but is now allowed to flow past the thermostat, the inlet hose and the radiator, and then back to the water pump through the outlet hose and the water intake pipe.

A pressure-vent cap is used on the radiator. The cap contains a pressure valve and vacuum valve. The pressure valve is held against its seat by a spring of pre-determined strength which protects the cooling system by relieving the pressure if the pressure in the cooling system rises by 0.9 kg/cm² (12.8 psi, 90 Kpa). The vacuum valve is held against its seat by a light spring which permits opening of the valve to relieve vacuum created in the system when it cools off and which otherwise might cause the radiator to collaspe.

Do not remove radiator cap to check engine coolant level; check coolant visually at the see-through water reservoir tank.

Coolant should be added only to the reservoir tank as necessary.

caut_6b_p278_0.text: CAUTION: As long as there is pressure in the cooling system, the temperature can be considerably higher than the boiling temperature of the solution in the radiator without causing the solution to boil. Removal of the radiator cap while engine is hot and pressure is high will cause the solution to boil instantaneously and possibly with explosive force, spewing the solution over engine, fenders and person removing cap. If the solution contains flammable anti-freeze such as alcohol (not recommended for use at any time), there is also the possibility of causing a serious fire.

para_6b_p278_1.text:
A "see-through" plastic reservoir tank is connected to the radiator by a hose. As the car is driven, the coolant is heated and expands. The portion of the coolant displaced by this expansion flows from the radiator into the reservoir tank. When the car is stopped and the coolant cools and contracts, the displaced coolant is drawn back into the radiator by vacuum.

Thus, the radiator is kept filled with coolant to the desired level at all times, resulting in increased cooling efficiency.

Coolant level should be between "FULL" and "LOW" marks on the reservoir tank.

Coolant should be added only to the reservoir tank as necessary.

The centrifugal type water pump is used in the cooling system. The pump impeller is supported by a totally sealed bearing. The water pump can not be disassembled.

para_6b_p279_0.text:
A wax pellet type thermostat is used in the coolant outlet passage to control the flow of engine coolant, to provide fast engine warm up and to regulate coolant temperatures.

A wax pellet element is hermetically contained in a metal case, and expands when heated and contracts when cooled.

When the pellet is heated and expands, the metal case pushes down the valve to open it.

As the pellet is cooled, the contraction allows a spring to close the valve.

para_6b_p280_0.text:
Thus, the valve remains closed while the coolant is cold, preventing circulation of coolant through the radiator.

At this point, coolant is allowed to circulate only throughout the engine to warm it quickly and evenly.

As the engine warms, the pellet expands and the thermostat valve opens, permitting coolant to flow through the radiator.

In the top portion of the thermostat, an air bleed valve is provided; this valve is for venting out the gas or air, if any, that is accumulated in the circuit.

There are two types of thermostats, A and B as shown in chart.

Either one is used depending on vehicle specifications. The temperature at which the valve begins to open is stamped on each thermostat. Be sure to note this stamped temperature for replacement.

tbl_6b_p280_0.text:
TORQUE SPECIFICATIONS
Thermostat: Temp. at which valve begins to open | "A": 88°C (190°F) | "B": 92°C (197°F)

para_6b_p280_1.text:

Condition: Engine overheats
  - Possible Cause: Loose or broken water pump belt
  - Correction: Adjust or replace.
  - Possible Cause: Not enough coolant
  - Correction: Check coolant level and add as necessary.
  - Possible Cause: Faulty thermostat
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: Faulty water pump
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: Dirty or bent radiator fins
  - Correction: Clean or remedy.
  - Possible Cause: Coolant leakage in Cooling System
  - Correction: Repair.
  - Possible Cause: Defective cooling fan motor or thermo switch
  - Correction: Check and replace as necessary.
  - Possible Cause: Plugged radiator
  - Correction: Check and replace radiator as necessary.
  - Possible Cause: Faulty radiator cap
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: Maladjusted ignition timing
  - Correction: Adjust.
  - Possible Cause: Dragging brakes
  - Correction: Adjust brake.
  - Possible Cause: Slipping clutch
  - Correction: Adjust or replace.

para_6b_p281_0.text:
The coolant recovery system is standard. The coolant in the radiator expands with heat, and the overflow is collected in the reservoir tank. When the system cools down, the coolant is drawn back into the radiator.

The cooling system has been filled at the factory with a quality coolant that is a 50/50 mixture of water and ethylene glycol antifreeze.

This coolant solution provides freezing protection to -36°C (-33°F).
• Maintain cooling system freeze protection at -36°C (-33°F) to ensure protection against corrosion and loss of coolant from boiling. This should be done even if freezing temperatures are not expected.
• Add ethylene glycol base coolant, when coolant has to be added because of coolant loss or to provide added protection against freezing at temperature lower than -36°C (-33°F).

noti_6b_p281_0.text: NOTICE: Alcohol or methanol base coolants or plain water alone should not be used in the cooling system at any time, as damage to the cooling system could occur.

para_6b_p281_1.text: Refer to your Owner's Manual for the correct procedures on coolant level.

caut_6b_p281_0.text: CAUTION: To help avoid the danger of being burned: do not remove the reservoir tank cap while the coolant is "boiling", and do not remove the radiator cap while the engine and radiator are still hot. Scalding fluid and steam can be blown out under pressure if either cap is taken off too soon.

para_6b_p281_2.text: The cooling system should be serviced as follows:

proc_6b_p281_0.text:
Cooling System Service
1. Check the cooling system for leaks or damage.
2. Wash radiator cap and filler neck with clean water by removing the radiator cap when engine is cold.
3. Check coolant for proper level and freeze protection.
4. Using a pressure tester, check the system and radiator cap for proper pressure holding capacity 0.9 kg/cms (12.8 psi, 90 kPa). If replacement of cap is required, use the proper cap specified for this vehicle.
5. Tighten hose clamps and inspect all hoses. Replace hoses whenever checked, swollen or otherwise deteriorated.
6. Clean frontal area of radiator core. After installing the radiator cap to the radiator, make sure that its ear is aligned with the reservoir tank hose as shown in Figure 6B-8. If not, turn the cap more to align the ear with the hose.
steps: 1. Check the cooling system for leaks or damage.
2. Wash radiator cap and filler neck with clean water by removing the radiator cap when engine is cold.
3. Check coolant for proper level and freeze protection.

4. Using a pressure tester, check the system and radiator cap for proper pressure holding capacity 0.9 kg/cms (12.8 psi, 90 kPa). If replacement of cap is required, use the proper cap specified for this vehicle.
5. Tighten hose clamps and inspect all hoses. Replace hoses whenever checked, swollen or otherwise deteriorated.

6. Clean frontal area of radiator core. After installing the radiator cap to the radiator, make sure that its ear is aligned with the reservoir tank hose as shown in Figure 6B-8. If not, turn the cap more to align the ear with the hose.

1. Remove radiator cap when engine is cool:
• Turn cap slowly to the left until it reaches a "stop" (Do not press down while turning the cap).
• Wait until pressure is relieved (indicated by a hissing sound): then press down on cap and continue to rotate to the left.

caut_6b_p282_0.text: CAUTION: To help avoid the danger of being burned, do not remove the radiator cap while the engine and radiator are still hot. Scalding fluid and steam can be blown out under pressure if the cap is taken off too soon.

proc_6b_p282_0.text:
Refer to Section 0B for correct refill intervals.
2. With radiator cap removed, run the engine until upper radiator hose is hot (this shows that the thermostat is open and the coolant is flowing through the system).
3. Stop engine and open radiator drain plug to drain coolant.
4. Close drain plug. Add water until system is filled and run engine until the upper radiator hose is hot again.
5. Repeat steps 3, and 4 several times until the drained liquid is nearly colorless.
6. Drain system and then close radiator drain plug tightly.
7. Disconnect hose from the water reservoir tank. Remove the tank and pour out any fluid. Clean the inside of the tank with soap and water. Flush it well with clean water and drain. Reinstall the tank and hose.
8. Add 50/50 mixture of a good quality enthylene glycol antifreeze and water to the radiator and the tank. Fill radiator to the base of the radiator filler neck and the reservoir tank to the "FULL" level mark. Reinstall the reservoir tank cap.
9. Run engine, with radiator cap removed, until radiator upper hose is hot.
10. With engine idling, add coolant to radiator until level reaches the bottom of filler neck. Install the radiator cap, making sure allows the ear of cap line up with the reservoir tank hose.

8. Add 50/50 mixture of a good quality enthylene glycol antifreeze and water to the radiator and the tank. Fill radiator to the base of the radiator filler neck and the reservoir tank to the "FULL" level mark. Reinstall the reservoir tank cap.

proc_6b_p283_0.text:
WATER PUMP BELT TENSION
1. Disconnect negative battery cable.
2. The belt is in proper tension when a thumb pressure (10 kg (98 N) applied to the middle point between water pump pulley and crankshaft pulley deflects it 6 to 8 mm (0.25 to 0.35 in).
3. If the tension is out of specification, adjust it. Remove the generator cover and then loosen the arm bolt, generator bolt and generator nut, and adjust the generator to slacken or tighten the belt. After adjustment, tighten bolts and nut and reinstall the generator cover.
4. Connect negative battery cable.

proc_6b_p284_2.text:
Inspect
Thermostat Inspection (continued)
1. Make sure that the air bleed valve of the thermostat is clear. Should this valve be clogged, the engine would tend to overheat.
2. Check the valve seat for some foreign matters being stuck which prevents the valve from seating tight.
3. Check the thermostatic movement of the was pellet as follows:
• Immerse the thermostat in water, and heat water gradually.
4. Check that valve starts to open at 88°C (190°F).
5. If the valve starts to open at a temperature substantially below or above, the thermostat unit should be replaced by a new one. Such a unit, if re-used, will bring about overcooling or overheating tendency.

proc_6c_p287_0.text:
When working on the fuel system there are several things to keep in mind.
1. Anytime the fuel system is being worked on, disconnect the negative battery cable except for those tests where battery voltage is required.
2. Always keep a dry chemical (Class B) fire extinguisher near the work area.
3. Always use a backup wrench when loosening or tightening a flare nut fitting.
4. Replace all fuel lines with the same fuel lines and fittings that were removed.
5. Do not replace fuel lines with fuel hose.
6. On any TBI fuel system, always relieve the line pressure before any fuel system component service is completed.
7. A small amount of fuel may be released after a fuel hose or line is disconnected. In order to reduce chance of personal injury, cover the fitting to be disconnected with a shop cloth.
8. When installing any fuel system component which uses an "O" seal, lubricate "O" with engine oil prior to installation.
9. Note that fuel hose connection varies with respect to each tye of fuel line. Be sure to connect and clamp each hose correctly (Figure 1).
10. Do not attempt any repairs on the fuel system until service procedure and figures relating to that repair are checked.
11. Adhere to all Notices and Cautions.

para_6c_p287_0.text:
The engine of this vehicle is designed to use only unleaded gasoline. Unleaded gasoline must be used for proper emission control system operation. Its use will also minimize spark plug fouling and extend engine oil life. Using leaded gasoline can damage the emission control system and could result in loss of emission warranty coverage.

This vehicle is also equipped with an evaporative emission system. The purpose of the system is to minimize the the escape of fuel vapors to the atmosphere. For information on this system, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).

para_6c_p287_1.text:
Fuel metering on this vehicle is accomplished using throttle body injection (TBI).

With throttle body injection (TBI), an injection unit is placed on the intake manifold where the carburetor is normally mounted. The throttle body injection unit is computer controlled and supplies the correct amount of fuel during all engine operating conditions. For information relative to this system, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).

caut_6c_p287_0.text:
CAUTION: • To prevent personal injury or damage to the vehicle as the result of an accidental start, disconnect the battery negative (-) cable before performing service.
• To minimize the risk of fire and personal injury, relieve the fuel system pressure before servicing the fuel system components. Refer to "Fuel Pressure Relief Procedure" in this section.
• Catch any fuel that spills when disconnecting the fuel system parts. Use a shop cloth to cover fittings while loosening them. Place the cloth in an approved container when work is complete.

proc_6c_p287_1.text:
Fuel System Pressure Relief Procedure
- Place gear shift lever in NEUTRAL position (manual transaxle equipped vehicles), or shift selector lever in the PARK position (automatic transaxle equipped vehicles). Set parking brake and block drive wheels.
2. Remove the fuel pump relay from the main fusebox (Refer to "Fuel Pump Relay Replacement" in this section).
3. Start engine, and allow it to stall. Crank engine two or three times for approximately three seconds each time to ensure fuel pressure dissipation within fuel lines.
4. Remove fuel filler cap from fuel filler neck to release fuel vapor pressure in the fuel tank. Reinstall the cap.
5. Install the fuel pump relay to the main fusebox. Refer to "Fuel Pump Relay Replacement" in this section.
6. Fuel system components are now safe for servicing.

4. Remove fuel filler cap from fuel filler neck to release fuel vapor pressure in the fuel tank. Reinstall the cap.
5. Install the fuel pump relay to the main fusebox. Refer to "Fuel Pump Relay Replacement" in this section.
6. Fuel system components are now safe for servicing.

caut_6c_p288_0.text: CAUTION: If the pressure in the fuel tank is not released prior to system service, the fuel in the tank may be forced out through the fuel hoses or lines due to this pressure during disconnection.

para_6c_p288_0.text: When repair to the fuel system has been completed, apply fuel pressure to the system and check all connections that were loosened for possible leaks. Correct as necessary. For information on pressurizing fuel system, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).

para_6c_p288_1.text:
The fuel system main components include the fuel tank, fuel pump and sending unit mounted within the fuel tank, fuel filter, 2-way check valve mounted to the top of the fuel tank, fuel filler neck and cap, fuel feed line, fuel return line, fuel breather hose, and fuel vapor line which connects to a vapor storage canister mounted in the engine compartment (Figure 2).

The vapor storage canister is used to control the emission of fuel vapors from the fuel system. For further information regarding vehicle emission control system operation, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).

caut_6c_p288_1.text: CAUTION: To reduce the risk of fire and personal injury, it is necessary to relieve the fuel system pressure before servicing fuel system components. For information on this procedure, refer to "Fuel System Pressure Relief" in this section.

para_6c_p288_2.text:
The fuel filter is located in front of the fuel tank as shown in. The filter is incorporated into the fuel feed line and is used to filter engine fuel before it reaches the throttle body injection unit.

The fuel filter is not a serviceable component, and must be replaced as an assembly (Figure 3).

para_6c_p289_0.text:
The electric fuel pump is located in the fuel tank. Pump operation is controlled through the fuel pump relay and engine control module (ECM) (Figure 4). For additional information on this system, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).

When the fuel pump is on operation, an impeller turns with the motor in the pump assembly. This causes fuel within the tank to be drawn into the fuel pump inlet port. Fuel is discharged under pressure through the pump outlet to the fuel feed line. Located in the fuel pump is a check valve to hold fuel pressure within the fuel feed line when the vehicle is not in operation (Figure 4).

proc_6c_p290_0.text:
1. Make certain that there is sufficient fuel in the tank.
2. With the engine running, inspect for leaks at all fuel feed lines and hose connections from the fuel tank to the throttle body unit. Tighten any loose connections and inspect all hoses for flattening or kinks which would restrict the flow of fuel.
3. If fuel pump is determined to be faulty, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E) for further fuel system diagnostics.

With the engine running, inspect for leaks at all fuel feed lines and hose connections from the fuel tank to the throttle body unit. Tighten any loose connections and inspect all hoses for flattening or kinks which would restrict the flow of fuel.
If fuel pump is determined to be faulty, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E) for further fuel system diagnostics.

para_6c_p290_0.text:
The fuel tank is located under the rear of the vehicle. The fuel pump and sending unit are installed in the upper part of the tank. The tank must be removed to service the fuel pump and/or sending unit (Figures 2 and 5).

The fuel tank and lines should be inspected for road damage which could cause leakage. Inspect fuel cap for correct sealing and indications of malfunctioning parts.

proc_6c_p290_1.text:
Before attempting service of any type on the fuel tank, always:
1. Remove negative battery cable.
2. Place "no smoking" signs near work area.
3. Be sure to have a fire extinguisher (Class B) close at hand.
4. Wear safety glasses.
5. Siphon or pump fuel into an approved fuel handling container.

para_6c_p290_1.text:
To help prevent refueling with leaded gasoline, the fuel filler neck of this vehicle has a built-in restrictor and deflector. The opening in the restrictor only admits the smaller gas nozzle spout, which must be fully inserted to bypass the deflector. Attempted refueling with a leaded gas nozzle will result in gasoline splashing back out of the filler neck.

The fuel filler cap used on this vehicle is of the pressure-vacuum type. A ratchet tightening device on the threaded portion of the fuel filler cap reduces the chances of incorrect installation, which would prevent sealing of the fuel vapors (Figure 6).

After the gasket on the fuel filler cap and the filler neck flange contact, the rachet produces a loud "clicking" sound, indicating the seal has been set.

A vacuum relief valve is incorporated into the fuel filler cap. If the pressure in the fuel tank becomes negative (vacuum), the relief valve opens to release the pressure created within the fuel tank. Only when vacuum becomes excessive within the tank does this occur. Normally, vacuum pressure is relieved by the 2-way check valve which is located in the fuel vapor line, mounted on the front of the fuel tank (Figure 2).

A pressure relief valve is also located in the fuel filler cap. If the pressure of fuel vapor within the tank should exceed that for which the fuel system is designed, the pressure relief valve opens, and excess pressure is vented to the atmosphere.

proc_6c_p292_1.text:
Draining Fuel Tank
1. Disconnect negative battery cable. Also, have a fire extinguisher (Class B) near work area.
2. Remove fuel filler cap.
3. Use a hand-operated pump device when possible to drain as much fuel through the filler neck hose as possible.
4. If a hand operated pump device cannot be used to complete the draining process, use a siphon at the fuel filler neck hose.
5. Reinstall removed fuel filler neck hose, fuel filler cap, and negative battery cable.

para_6c_p293_0.text:
Figures 1, 2, 10, 11, and 12

The following procedure is used prior to repairing the fuel tank.

caut_6c_p293_1.text: CAUTION: This procedure will not remove all fuel vapor. Do not attempt any repair on fuel tank where heat or flame is required, as fire or explosion could occur resulting in personal injury.

proc_6c_p293_3.text:
Figures 1, 2, 10, 11, and 12

The following procedure is used prior to repairing the fuel tank.
1. Remove all hoses, fuel pump and sending unit (See procedure in this section), and 2-way check valve from the fuel tank and drain any remaining fuel from the tank.
2. Visually inspect interior cavity of tank.
3. Move tank to flushing area (wash rack).
4. Fill tank completely with tap water, agitate vigorously, and drain.
5. Completely flush out remaining water after washing.

2. Visually inspect interior cavity of tank.
3. Move tank to flushing area (wash rack).
4. Fill tank completely with tap water, agitate vigorously, and drain.
5. Completely flush out remaining water after washing.
