tbl_6d_p297_0.text:
GENERAL INFORMATION: 6D-1
Battery: 6D1-1
Cranking System: 6D2-1
Charging System: 6D3-1
Ignition System: 6D4-1
Engine Wiring: 6D5-1
General Electrical System Diagnosis: 6D-1

para_6d_p297_0.text:
This section contains information on the maintenance, diagnosis, storing, charging, and jump starting of batteries.

This section contains the diagnosis, servicing, and specifications of the cranking circuit and starter motor.

This section contains generator diagnosis, servicing, and specifications.

This section contains the information required to service all ignition system components. These components include the distributor, ignition switch, spark plugs, and the ignition primary and secondary wiring.

This section contains engine wiring views.

The following two diagnostic charts are intended for general electrical system diagnosis. More specific diagnosis and repair procedures for electrical subsystems are located in the following subsections (Figures 1 and 2):
• 6D1 - Battery
• 6D2 - Cranking Systems
• 6D3 - Charging Systems
• 6D4 - Ignition Systems
Where a "driveability" complaint exists, or an ECM code is set, refer to Section 6E.

fig_6d_p298_0.text:
Figure 1 Electrical System General Diagnosis - 1 of 2
NO CRANKING, NO SOUND FROM SOLENOID: Starting condition being diagnosed
TURN HEADLIGHTS AND DOME LITE ON / TURN KEY TO START: Initial diagnostic step
LIGHTS DIM OR GO OUT: Branch: battery/charging issue suspected
LIGHTS STAY BRIGHT: Branch: electrical/switch issue suspected
CHECK BATTERY STATE-OF-CHARGE: Next step if lights dim or go out
TURN ON RADIO, AND HEATER: Next step if lights stay bright
EYE DARK: Battery indicator shows discharged
GREEN EYE SHOWING: Battery indicator shows charged
OPERATE OK: Radio and heater function normally
DON'T OPERATE: Radio and heater do not function
CHARGE BATTERY, CHECK FOR DRAIN, AND CHECK GENERATOR: Action if eye is dark
CHECK CRANKING VOLTAGE AT BATTERY POSTS: Action if green eye showing
CARS WITH AUTO. TRANS.: Branch for automatic transmission vehicles
CARS WITH MANUAL TRANS.: Branch for manual transmission vehicles
CHECK BULKHEAD CONNECTOR, FUSIBLE LINK AND IGNITION SWITCH CONNECTIONS: Action if radio/heater don't operate
LESS THAN 9.6 VOLTS: Low cranking voltage result
9.6 VOLTS OR MORE: Adequate cranking voltage result
CHECK VOLTAGE AT CLUTCH SWITCH TERMINAL (CLUTCH DEPRESSED, KEY IN START): Manual trans diagnostic step
TEST BATTERY. IF OK, REPAIR STARTER: Action if less than 9.6 volts
CHECK VOLTAGE FROM ENGINE BLOCK TO BATT. NEG. POST, KEY IN START POSITION, (TESTER POS. LEAD ON BLOCK): Action if 9.6 volts or more
MORE THAN 7 VOLTS ON BOTH TERMINALS: Clutch switch voltage result - adequate on both
LESS THAN 7 VOLTS ON BOTH TERMINALS: Clutch switch voltage result - low on both
LESS THAN 7 VOLTS ON ONE TERMINAL: Clutch switch voltage result - low on one
.5 VOLT OR MORE: High voltage drop on ground cable
LESS THAN .5 VOLT: Acceptable voltage drop on ground cable
CHECK CONNECTIONS AND VOLTAGE AT SOLENOID STARTER TERMINAL: Action if more than 7 volts on both terminals
CHECK CLUTCH SWITCH ADJUSTMENT AND CONNECTOR. IF OK, REPLACE SWITCH: Action if less than 7 volts on one terminal
CLEAN AND TIGHTEN GROUND CABLE CONN. OR REPLACE CABLE: Action if .5 volt or more
CHECK CRANKING VOLTAGE AT STARTER SOLENOID BATTERY TERMINAL: Action if less than .5 volt
LESS THAN 7 VOLTS: Low voltage at solenoid starter terminal
MAN. TRANS.: Manual transmission sub-branch
AUTO. TRANS.: Automatic transmission sub-branch
LESS THAN 9 VOLTS: Low voltage at solenoid battery terminal
9 VOLTS OR MORE: Adequate voltage at solenoid battery terminal
FAULTY WIRE TO STARTER: Diagnosis for manual trans low voltage
WITH KEY IN START, CHECK VOLTAGE AT IGNITION SWITCH SOLENOID TERM.: Auto trans diagnostic step
CLEAN AND TIGHTEN POSITIVE CABLE TERMINAL OR REPLACE CABLE: Action if less than 9 volts
REPLACE STARTER SOLENOID: Action if 9 volts or more
7 VOLTS OR MORE (under FAULTY WIRE): Voltage result leading to repair starter
REPAIR STARTER: Action if 7 volts or more (manual trans branch)
7 VOLTS OR MORE (ignition switch): Adequate voltage at ignition switch solenoid term
LESS THAN 7 VOLTS (ignition switch): Low voltage at ignition switch solenoid term
REPAIR WIRE FROM IGNITION SWITCH: Action if 7 volts or more at ignition switch
REPLACE IGNITION SW.: Action if less than 7 volts at ignition switch

fig_6d_p299_0.text:
Figure 2 Electrical System General Diagnosis - 2 of 2
1: CHECK: BATTERY FOR GREEN INDICATOR. VISUAL CONDITION OF BATTERY CABLES AND CONNECTIONS. IF BATTERY NEEDS CHARGING, MAKE GENERATOR AND CURRENT DRAIN CHECK, CHARGE BATTERY AND RECHECK CRANKING. IF TROUBLE HAS NOT BEEN FOUND, PROCEED.
2: REMOVE BATTERY LEAD FROM DISTRIBUTOR OR IGNITION MODULE. MAKE ALL VOLTMETER READINGS WITH KEY IN START POSITION.
3: MEASURE CRANKING VOLTAGE AT BATTERY TERMINAL POSTS.
4: 9.6 VOLTS OR MORE: MEASURE VOLTAGE FROM BATTERY NEGATIVE TERMINAL TO ENGINE BLOCK (POS. LEAD ON BLOCK).
5: LESS THAN 9.6 VOLTS: CHARGE AND LOAD TEST BATTERY.
6: .5 VOLT OR MORE: REPAIR GROUND CABLE AND CONNECTIONS.
7: LESS THAN .5 VOLT: MEASURE VOLTAGE AT SOLENOID BATTERY TERMINAL, CLEAN AND TIGHTEN CONNECTIONS AT STARTER.
8: 9 VOLTS OR MORE: REPAIR STARTER.
9: LESS THAN 9 VOLTS: CLEAN AND TIGHTEN POSITIVE CABLE CONNECTIONS. IF OK, REPLACE CABLE.

caut_6d1_p300_0.text: CAUTION: All batteries generate hydrogen gas which is extremely flammable. If ignited by a spark or flame, this gas may explode violently, causing spraying of acid, fragmentation of the battery, and possible severe personal injuries. Wear approved eye protection. Battery fluid is corrosive acid. Flush any contacted area with water immediately and thoroughly.

para_6d1_p300_0.text:
The sealed battery is standard on all vehicles. There are no vent plugs in the cover. The battery is completely sealed except for small vent holes. These vent holes allow the small amount of gas produced in the battery to escape.

The battery has three major functions in the electrical system: first, it provides a source of energy for cranking the engine; second, it acts as a voltage stabilizer for the electrical system; and third, it provides electrical energy for a limited time when electrical load exceeds generator output.

The battery specification label contains information important to the servicing of the battery. This information includes test ratings and original equipment replacement part numbers.

A battery has two ratings: (1) a reserve capacity rating at 27°C (80°F) which is the time that a fully charged battery will operate the vehicle with no generator operation; and (2) a cold rating at -18°C (0°F) which indicates the battery's cranking load capacity.

Reserve Capacity is the length of time (expressed in minutes) required for a fully charged battery to discharge to a terminal voltage of 10.5 volts. All batteries are rated at a constant temperature of 27°C (80°F) and a constant current drain of 25 amperes. This current drain is about the same as operating a vehicle with the headlights ON, other minimal electrical loading, and no generator output.

The Cold Cranking Amperage rating is the minimum amperage which must be maintained by the battery for 30 seconds while meeting a minimum voltage requirement of 7.2 volts. All batteries are tested at -18°C (0°F). This rating is a measure of the battery's cold cranking capacity.

para_6d1_p301_0.text:
The battery is not designed to last indefinitely; however, with proper care, it will provide many years of service.

If the battery tests good, but fails to perform satisfactorily in service for no apparent reason, the following are some of the more important factors that may be the cause of the problem:
1. Vehicle accessories left ON overnight.
2. Slow average driving speeds for short periods.
3. Electrical load is greater than generator output. (This can happen if aftermarket electrical accessories are added without regard to generator output.)
4. Conditions in the charging system such as electrical shorts, slipping fan belt, faulty generator, or voltage regulator.
5. Battery abuse - such as failure to maintain clean and tight battery connections or a loose battery retainer which allows the battery to slide around.
6. Extended vehicle storage.
7. Batteries being less than adequately charged when received.
8. Extended engine cranking due to mechanical engine conditions.
9. Incorrect interpretation of the charge indicator.
10. Inability to determine the correct ampere-hour charging rate for a discharged battery.
11. Continued current draw on battery through increased parasitic drain.

para_6d1_p301_1.text: The freezing point of electrolyte depends on its specific gravity. Since specific gravity depends on the state of charge, the battery should be protected against freezing by keeping it in a charged condition.

para_6d1_p301_2.text:
A built-in temperature-compensated hydrometer is incorporated into the top of the sealed battery. When observing the hydrometer, make sure that the battery has a clean top. A light may be required in poorly lit areas.

Three hydrometer indications can be observed (Figure 2):
1. GREEN DOT VISIBLE
   The battery is ready for testing.
2. DARK (DOT NOT VISIBLE)
   If there is a cranking complaint, the battery should be tested as described in the Diagnosis section. The charging and electrical systems should also be checked at this time.
3. CLEAR OR LIGHT YELLOW
   The battery fluid level has fallen below the bottom of the hydrometer. This can be caused by excessive or prolonged charging, a broken case, excessive tipping, or normal battery deterioration. When a battery is discovered in this condition and no leakage is evident, a faulty charging system that is overcharging the battery should be suspected. Therefore, the charging and electrical systems should both be checked thoroughly. If there is a cranking complaint, replace the battery.

fig_6d1_p301_0.text:
Figure 2 Built-In Hydrometer Indications
1: DARKENED INDICATOR (WITH GREEN DOT) - MAY BE JUMP STARTED
2: DARKENED INDICATOR (NO GREEN DOT) - MAY BE JUMP STARTED
3: LIGHT YELLOW OR BRIGHT INDICATOR - DO NOT JUMP START

para_6d1_p301_3.text: The battery retainer should be clean and free from corrosion before installing the battery. The retainer nuts should be tightened securely but not so tight that the upper retainer bracket is deformed. All retainer components should be in sound condition so that the battery will be held in a level position.

para_6d1_p301_4.text: The following procedure should be used for testing batteries:

proc_6d1_p301_0.text:
1. VISUAL INSPECTION
   Check for obvious damage such as a cracked or broken case that would permit electrolyte loss. If damage is noted, replace the battery, determine the cause of damage, and correct as required.
2. HYDROMETER CHECK
   a. GREEN DOT VISIBLE - Go to Step 3.
   b. DARK (DOT NOT VISIBLE) - Charge the battery as outlined under "Charging Procedure" in this section and proceed to Step 3.
3. LOAD TEST
   a. Connect a voltmeter and a battery load tester across the battery terminals.
   b. Apply a 300 ampere load for 15 seconds to remove the surface charge from the battery. Remove the load.
   c. Wait 15 seconds to let the battery recover. Apply the specified load located on the battery label or in the "Battery Usage Chart" at the end of this section. Read the voltage after 15 seconds and remove the load.
   d. If the voltage does not drop below the minimum, the battery is good and should be

para_6d1_p302_0.text: e. If the voltage drops below the minimum, the battery must be replaced (Figure 3).

proc_6d1_p302_0.text:
1. Remove the underhood lamp bulb (if equipped) and turn the ignition OFF.
2. Charge the battery to a full state of charge.
3. Disconnect the negative battery cable. Connect an ammeter capable of carrying at least 20 amperes in series between the battery terminal and the negative battery cable. With the ignition OFF and all doors closed, note the amperage draw.
4. If more than a 0.5 ampere drain is present, the vehicle's electrical system should be checked by removing fuses to determine the circuit or component that is causing the drain.
5. If less than a 0.5 ampere drain is present, remove the ammeter and connect a J 34029 High Impedance Digital Multimeter in its place. Set the selector switch to 2 on the DCA scale and turn the selector switch counterclockwise until an accurate milliampere reading is obtained. Do not open doors or operate any equipment that would draw more than 2.0 amperes or the fuse in the multimeter will blow. If the fuse blows, replace it with a 2.0 ampere fast blow fuse only. Use of any other type of fuse may cause damage to the meter. Starting with the 2,000 MA (milliampere) setting, start switching the right selector switch down the scale until an accurate amperage reading is obtained. Normal parasitic current drains on the battery are 10 MA to 75 MA. If more than a 75 MA drain is indicated on the multimeter, components such as the ignition switch, trunk lamps, tone generators, etc., should be checked to determine the component causing excessive drain on the battery.

Use of any other type of fuse may cause damage to the meter. Starting with the 2,000 MA (milliampere) setting, start switching the right selector switch down the scale until an accurate amperage reading is obtained. Normal parasitic current drains on the battery are 10 MA to 75 MA. If more than a 75 MA drain is indicated on the multimeter, components such as the ignition switch, trunk lamps, tone generators, etc., should be checked to determine the component causing excessive drain on the battery.

para_6d1_p302_1.text: When it is necessary to charge the battery, the following basic rules must be followed:

proc_6d1_p302_1.text:
Battery Charging Rules
1. Do not charge the battery if the hydrometer is CLEAR OR LIGHT YELLOW. Replace the battery if this condition is discovered.
2. If the battery feels as hot as 52°C (125°F) or hotter, or if violent gassing or spewing of electrolyte through the vent holes occurs, discontinue charging or reduce the charging rate.
3. Do not attempt to charge a battery if it seems to be frozen.
steps: Do not charge the battery if the hydrometer is CLEAR OR LIGHT YELLOW. Replace the battery if this condition is discovered.
If the battery feels as hot as 52°C (125°F) or hotter, or if violent gassing or spewing of electrolyte through the vent holes occurs, discontinue charging or reduce the charging rate.
Do not attempt to charge a battery if it seems to be frozen.

proc_6d1_p302_2.text:
CHARGING PROCEDURE
1. Batteries with hydrometers showing a GREEN DOT do not require charging unless they have just been discharged (by cranking the vehicle).
2. Make sure all charger connections are clean and tight.
3. For best results, batteries should be charged while the electrolyte and plates are at room temperature. A battery that is extremely cold may not accept current for several hours after starting the charger.
4. Charge the battery until the hydrometer shows a GREEN DOT. The battery should be checked every half-hour while charging. Tipping or shaking the battery may be necessary to make the hydrometer show the correct indication.
5. After charging, the battery should be tested as outlined in "Battery Testing" in this section.

Make sure all charger connections are clean and tight.

For best results, batteries should be charged while the electrolyte and plates are at room temperature. A battery that is extremely cold may not accept current for several hours after starting the charger.

Charge the battery until the hydrometer shows a GREEN DOT. The battery should be checked every half-hour while charging. Tipping or shaking the battery may be necessary to make the hydrometer show the correct indication.
After charging, the battery should be tested as outlined in "Battery Testing" in this section.

para_6d1_p302_2.text:
The time required to charge a battery will vary depending upon the following factors:

1. Size of Battery - A completely discharged, large heavy-duty battery requires more than twice the recharging time as is required by a completely discharged small passenger car battery.

2. Temperature - More time is required to charge a battery in cold temperatures than is required in warm temperatures. When a fast charger is connected to a cold battery, the current accepted by the battery will be very low at first, then as the
battery warms, a higher rate of current will be accepted.

para_6d1_p303_0.text:
3. Charger Capacity - A charge that can supply only five amperes will require more time than a charger that can supply 30 amperes.
4. State-of-Charge - A completely discharged battery requires more than twice as much charge as one half charged. Because the electrolyte is nearly pure water and a poor conductor in a completely discharged battery, the initial current accepted by the battery is very low. Later, as the charging current causes the electrolyte acid content to increase, the charging current will also increase.

Any battery discharged by parasitic load and left to stand in this condition for a period of time may not accept charge readily. However, if recharged long enough, most batteries will return to a usable condition.

If the battery remains in an extremely discharged condition for a prolonged period, it can become permanently damaged. This damage can be accelerated by cold temperatures. Batteries that are extremely discharged can freeze at temperatures as high as -6°C (20°F) and become permanently damaged.

To prevent damage and recharging problems, disconnect the negative battery cable from any vehicle which will not be in service for a 20 day period. If disconnecting the negative battery cable is not practical, the battery should be charged periodically (every 20-45 days).

proc_6d1_p303_0.text:
CHARGING A COMPLETELY DISCHARGED BATTERY
1. Measure the voltage at the battery terminals with an accurate voltmeter. If below 10 volts, the charge current will be very low and time will be required before the battery will accept a current in excess of a few milliamperes.
2. Set the battery charger on the high setting.
3. Some chargers feature polarity protection circuitry which prevents charging unless the charger leads are connected to the battery terminals correctly. A completely discharged battery may not have enough voltage to activate this circuitry even though the leads are connected properly. If this situation occurs, follow the specific charger manufacturer's procedures for bypassing or overriding this circuitry. Bypassing the circuitry allows the charger to activate and charge a low-voltage battery.
4. Battery chargers vary in the amount of voltage and current that they can provide. The times required for the battery to accept measurable charger current at various voltages are as follows:
VOLTAGE   HOURS
16.0 or more   Up to four hours
14.0 - 15.9   Up to eight hours
13.9 or less   Up to 16 hours
5. If the charge current is still not measurable at the end of the above charging times, the battery should be replaced.
6. If the charge current is measurable during the charging time, the battery is considered good and charging should be completed in the normal manner.
7. It is important to remember that a completely discharged battery must be recharged for a sufficient number of Ampere Hours (AH) to restore it to a usable state. As a general rule, using the Reserve Capacity (RC) Rating of the battery as the number of ampere hours of charge will usually bring the desired hydrometer indication into view. For example, if the battery is rated at 75 RC minutes, it would be completely recharged as follows:
• 10 ampere charge for 7.5 hours = 75 ampere hours
or
• 25 ampere charge for 3.0 hours = 75 ampere hours
8. Any battery recharged by this procedure should be load tested to establish serviceability - see "Diagnosis" procedures in this section.

para_6d1_p303_1.text: JUMP STARTING IN CASE OF EMERGENCY WITH AUXILIARY (BOOSTER) BATTERY

noti_6d1_p303_0.text: NOTICE: Do not push or tow the vehicle to start engine. Damage to the emission system and/or to other parts of the vehicle may result.

para_6d1_p303_2.text: Both the booster and discharged batteries should be treated carefully when using jumper cables. Follow the procedure below using caution not to generate any sparks.

caut_6d1_p303_0.text: CAUTION: Departure from these conditions or the procedure below could result in: (1) serious personal injury (especially to eyes) or property damage from such causes as battery explosion, battery acid, or electrical burns; and/or (2) damage to electronic components of either vehicle. Never expose any battery to open flame or electric spark - batteries generate a gas which is flammable and explosive. Remove rings, watches, and other jewelry. Wear approved eye protection. Do not allow battery fluid to contact eyes, skin, fabrics, or painted surfaces - battery fluid is a corrosive acid. Flush any contacted area with water immediately and thoroughly. Be careful that metal tools or jumper cables do not contact the positive battery terminal (or metal in contact with it) or any other metal on the vehicle - a short circuit will occur.

proc_6d1_p303_2.text:
Jump Starting with Auxiliary (Booster) Battery
1. Set the parking brake and place the automatic transmission (if equipped) in PARK. Turn the
2. ignition OFF. Turn OFF all electrical accessories and lights.
3. Check the built-in hydrometer. If it is CLEAR OR LIGHT YELLOW, replace the battery.
steps: 1. Set the parking brake and place the automatic transmission (if equipped) in PARK. Turn the
ignition OFF. Turn OFF all electrical accessories and lights.
Check the built-in hydrometer. If it is CLEAR OR LIGHT YELLOW, replace the battery.

noti_6d1_p304_0.text: NOTICE: When jump starting a vehicle with charging equipment, be sure that the equipment used is 12-volt and negative ground. Do not use 24-volt charging equipment. Using such equipment can cause serious damage to the vehicle's electrical system or electronic components.

proc_6d1_p304_0.text:
Jump Starting with Auxiliary (Booster) Battery
1. Attach the end of one jumper cable to the positive terminal of the booster battery and the other end of the same cable to the positive terminal of the discharged battery. Do not permit the vehicles to touch one another. If the vehicles touch, they may create a grounding effect and counteract the jumping procedure.
2. Attach one end of the negative jumper cable to the negative terminal of the booster battery and the other end to the solid engine ground (such as A/C compressor bracket). Make sure that the ground is at least 18 inches away from the discharged battery. Do not connect directly to the negative terminal of the discharged battery.
3. Start the engine of the vehicle that is providing the jump start and turn OFF all electrical accessories and lights. Start the engine of the vehicle with the discharged battery.
4. Reverse these directions exactly when removing the jumper cables. The negative cable must be disconnected from the engine that was jump started first.

Always be certain to route the replacement battery cable in exactly the same manner as the original cable.

para_6d2_p306_0.text:
The cranking circuit consists of the battery, starter motor, ignition switch, and related electrical wiring. All of these components are connected electrically (Figure 1). Only the starter motor, however, will be covered in this section.

Two types of starter motors are utilized depending upon the vehicle transmission application. Manual transmission equipped vehicles utilize conventional starter motors which consist of a frame and field assembly, an armature assembly, an overrunning clutch assembly, a starter solenoid assembly, a commutator end housing, a brush holder, and a shift lever. Automatic transmission equipped vehicles utilize a reduction type starter motor which has, in addition to the components mentioned above, a reduction gear and shock absorber assembly.

In the basic circuit, the solenoid windings are energized when the ignition switch is turned to START and the clutch start/neutral safety switch is closed. The resulting plunger and shift lever movement causes the pinion to engage the engine flywheel ring gear. This movement also causes the starter solenoid contacts to close. With the contacts closed, the starter solenoid provides a closed circuit between the battery positive terminal and the starter motor. Because the starter motor is permanently grounded to the engine block, the circuit is complete and cranking occurs as soon as the starter solenoid contacts are closed. When the engine starts, the pinion is designed to overrun and protect the armature from excessive speed until the ignition switch is released from the START position. With the ignition switch released, a return spring in the solenoid assembly forces the starter solenoid contacts open, breaks the circuit between the battery and the starter motor, and disengages the pinion. To prevent prolonged overrun, the ignition switch should be released immediately upon engine start-up.

para_6d2_p307_0.text:
Before removing any unit in the cranking circuit for repair, the following checks should be made:

Battery: To determine the condition of the battery, follow the testing procedure outlined in the Battery Section 6D1.

Wiring: Inspect for wiring damage. Inspect the connections to the starter motor, the starter solenoid, the ignition switch, the battery, and all related ground points.

Starter Solenoid and Ignition Switch: Inspect these components and determine their serviceability.

Starter Motor: If the battery, wiring, and switches are in satisfactory condition, and the engine is known to be functioning properly, remove the starter motor (See procedure in this section) and perform "Starter Assembly Performance Testing."

para_6d2_p307_1.text: 1. Starter assembly (See procedure in this section).

proc_6d2_p307_0.text:
STARTER ASSEMBLY PERFORMANCE TESTING
1. Test leads from battery to starter assembly (Figure 2).
2. • Check that the pinion extends quickly and completely to the energized position.
3. If pinion movement is satisfactory, proceed to the "Hold-In Test."
4. If pinion movement is not satisfactory, replace the starter solenoid (See "Unit Repair" procedure in this section).

proc_6d2_p308_0.text:
HOLD-IN TEST
1. Check that the pinion remains completely in the energized position.
2. If the pinion remains in the energized position proceed to the "Pinion Return Test."
3. If the pinion does not remain completely in the energized position, replace the starter solenoid (See Unit Repair procedure in this section).
steps: Check that the pinion remains completely in the energized position.
If the pinion remains in the energized position proceed to the "Pinion Return Test."
If the pinion does not remain completely in the energized position, replace the starter solenoid (See Unit Repair procedure in this section).

para_6d2_p308_1.text: Negative lead from drive housing assembly (Figure 4).

proc_6d2_p308_1.text:
PINION RETURN TEST
1. Check that the pinion returns quickly and completely to the de-energized position.
2. If the pinion returns quickly and completely to the de-energized position, proceed to the "No-Load Performance Test."
3. If the pinion does not return to the de-energized position, the cause is most likely a faulty starter solenoid return spring. Replace the starter solenoid (See "Unit Repair" procedure in this section) and retest. If, after solenoid replacement, the pinion does not return satisfactorily to the de-energized position, proceed to "Unit Repair" for further disassembly and inspection procedures.

If the pinion returns quickly and completely to the de-energized position, proceed to the "No-Load Performance Test."

If the pinion does not return to the de-energized position, the cause is most likely a faulty starter solenoid return spring. Replace the starter solenoid (See "Unit Repair" procedure in this section) and retest. If, after solenoid replacement, the pinion does not return satisfactorily to the de-energized position, proceed to "Unit Repair" for further disassembly and inspection procedures.

para_6d2_p308_2.text: Test leads from battery to starter assembly and ammeter (Figure 5).

proc_6d2_p308_2.text:
PINION RETURN TEST
1. Check that the starter motor runs smoothly and that the pinion extends quickly and completely.
2. During starter motor operation, check that the ammeter reads no more than 50 amperes

If the starter assembly performs satisfactorily and the ammeter readings are within the specified limits, the starter assembly is serviceable and should be reinstalled on the vehicle. If a cranking complaint exists, the starter should not be considered faulty and further diagnosis is necessary.
If the starter assembly does not perform satisfactorily during this test, proceed to "Unit Repair" for further disassembly and inspection procedures.

proc_6d2_p310_0.text:
Manual Transmission Equipped Vehicles – Disassemble
1. Remove the starter solenoid (See procedure in this section).
2. Remove two screws, commutator end cap, armature plate, and brake spring (Figure 11).
3. Remove two housing bolts (through bolts) and disconnect commutator end housing.
4. Using needle nose pliers, remove the brushes (Figure 12).
5. Remove the brush holder.
6. Remove the frame and field assembly, the armature, and the shift lever (Figure 13).
7. Using snap ring pliers, remove the armature snap ring, the pinion stop ring, and the overrunning clutch (Figure 14).

proc_6d2_p310_1.text:
Automatic Transmission Equipped Vehicles – Disassemble
1. Remove the starter solenoid (See procedure in this section).
2. Remove the two commutator housing bolts (Figure 15).
3. Using needle nose pliers, remove the brushes (Figure 10).
4. Remove the armature and the brush holder.
5. Remove the frame and field assembly.
6. Remove the plate (Figure 16).
7. Using snap ring pliers, remove the snap ring and the C-clip from the planetary carrier shaft (Figures 17 and 18).
8. Remove the shift lever.
9. Remove the pinion stop ring, the overrunning clutch, and the washer from the planetary carrier shaft.
10. Remove the planetary carrier shaft from the center bearing and shock absorber.
11. Remove the planetary gears and the washer from the planetary carrier shaft.
12. Remove the internal gear from the center bearing and shock absorber (Figure 19).

proc_6d2_p310_2.text:
Manual and Automatic Transmission Equipped Vehicles – Inspect
1. Check the insulation between the commutator segments and the armature core with an ohmmeter (Figure 20). If continuity exists, the armature must be replaced.
2. Check for continuity between the segments (Figure 21). If continuity does not exist at all test points, the armature must be replaced.
3. Support the armature on V-blocks and check the commutator for run-out using a J 7872 Dial Gage (Figure 22). Run-out must not exceed 0.4 mm (0.015 in.) for manual transmission equipped vehicles or 0.5 mm (0.020 in.) for automatic transmission equipped vehicles. If the commutator run-out exceeds these limits, the armature must either be replaced or corrected on a lathe.

proc_6d2_p313_0.text:
Manual and Automatic Transmission Equipped Vehicles – Inspect
4. Check the diameter of the commutator (Figure 23). The diameter should be no less than 27 mm (1.063 in.).
5. Check the commutator mica depth (Figure 24). Mica depth should be no less than 0.2 mm (0.008 in.).
6. Check the field coil for continuity. Continuity should exist between the brush and the frame and field assembly.
7. Measure the length of the brushes (Figure 25). Brush length should be no less than 10.7 mm (0.421 in.) for manual transmission equipped vehicles and no less than 9.0 mm (0.354 in.) for automatic transmission equipped vehicles. Replace as required.
8. Check the brush springs for corrosion or damage. Also check the brush holder for corrosion and the insulation on the positive plates for wear. Replace components as needed.
9. Check the overrunning clutch for free rotation in the correct direction (Figure 26) and that it locks securely in the opposite direction. Also check the pinion for abnormal wear. Replace as an assembly if required.

After press-fitting, ream the replacement bushing to obtain a 0.05 mm (0.002 in.) clearance. Lightly grease the bushing with GM #1050109. Caulk the cover if the front bushing was replaced.

para_6d3_p319_0.text: This vehicle utilizes an integral regulator charging system. The I.C. (intergrated circuit) regulator is a soild state unit that is mounted inside the generator to the rear end frame. All regulator components are enclosed in a solid mold to protect them from the heat and corrosive elements. The regulator voltage cannot be adjusted.

para_6d3_p320_0.text:
The generator rotor bearings contain enough grease to eliminate the need for periodic lubrication. Two brushes carry current through two slip rings to the field coil mounted on the rotor. Under normal operating conditions, this arrangement is capable of providing long periods of attention-free service.

Stator windings are assembled inside a laminated core that forms part of the generator drive end frame. A rectifier bridge that contains six diodes is connected to the stator windings. These diodes electrically change stator AC voltage into DC voltage. This DC voltage is then transmitted to the generator output terminal.

Two neutral diodes are utilized to smooth out voltage fluctuations caused by varying generator speeds. A capacitor or condenser, mounted in the regulator, protects the rectifier bridge and neutral diodes. This condenser also suppresses radio noise.

caut_6d3_p320_0.text: CAUTION: Never disconnect either battery terminal while the engine is running. Battery disconnection during engine operation will result in damage to electronic components.

para_6d3_p320_1.text: Check the indicator lamp for normal operation. If the indicator lamp operates normally, refer to "Undercharged Battery" in this section.

proc_6d3_p321_0.text:
Lamp On — Turn ignition switch OFF
1. Turn ignition switch OFF.
2. Check drive belt and wiring connections at generator and battery cable.
steps: Turn ignition switch OFF.
Check drive belt and wiring connections at generator and battery cable.

proc_6d3_p321_1.text:
Battery Voltage Path — Test No. 1
1. Remove rubber protector and connect a voltmeter between generator B terminal and ground. Turn ignition switch ON.
2. If Battery Voltage: Remove two-wire generator connector. Connect voltmeter between connector BLK/WHT wire (IG terminal) and ground.
3. If approximately 12 Volts: Reconnect generator connector.
4. Disconnect battery negative cable.
5. Remove nut and wire from generator B terminal.
6. Connect ammeter between disconnected wire and generator B terminal. Connect ammeter red lead to generator and black lead to wire.
7. Connect battery negative cable.
8. Turn all accessories and high-beam headlamps ON. Turn blower to HIGH.
9. Run engine as required to obtain maximum current output and record ammeter reading.
10. *If battery is fully charged, use the starter to partially discharge it before recording maximum current output.
11. If output within 10 amps of maximum rating: Refer to Generator Specifications. Check battery connections and battery condition.
12. If output NOT within 10 amps of maximum rating: Refer to Generator Specifications. Remove generator and proceed to Unit Repair.

If output NOT within 10 amps of maximum rating: Refer to Generator Specifications. Remove generator and proceed to Unit Repair.

para_6d3_p321_1.text:
If Zero Voltage at IG terminal: Repair open in BLK/WHT wire between generator connector and interior junction/fuse block or between junction/fuse block and ignition switch.

If Zero Voltage at generator B terminal: Repair open in WHT wire between generator B terminal and underhood fuse/relay center or in BLK wire between fuse/relay center and battery.

proc_6d3_p321_2.text:
Lamp Off — Test No. 2
1. Remove two-wire generator connector. Ground WHT/RED wire (L terminal) with a fused jumper.
2. If Lamp Off: Repair open in WHT/RED wire between generator L terminal and indicator lamp.
3. If Lamp On: Replace regulator.
steps: Remove two-wire generator connector. Ground WHT/RED wire (L terminal) with a fused jumper.

If Lamp On: Replace regulator.

para_6d3_p321_3.text: Lamp Off NORMAL — Generator is functioning normally.

proc_6d3_p321_3.text:
Lamp On — Test No. 3
1. Disconnect generator connector.
2. If Lamp On: Repair short to ground in WHT/RED wire between indicator lamp and generator L terminal.
3. If Lamp Off: Replace regulator.

para_6d3_p322_0.text: An undercharged battery is usually characterized by slow cranking or by a dark battery hydrometer. If this condition exists, check the following items for a cause:

proc_6d3_p322_0.text:
Undercharged Battery Diagnosis
1. Determine that the undercharged condition has not been caused by leaving accessories ON for extended periods without charging the battery.
2. Check the drive belt for proper tension. Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.
3. If a battery fault is suspected, refer to BATTERY (Section 6D1).
4. Inspect the wiring for faults. Check that all connections are clean and tight. Also check that any bulkhead connectors are tight and undamaged. Battery cable connections at battery, starter, and engine ground points should also be checked.
5. With all accessories turned OFF, connect a Voltmeter across the battery. Run the engine at a moderate speed and check the voltage. Some consideration should be given to the temperature to which the generator is exposed during this test. As the temperature of the regulator case in the generator increases, the voltage output will decrease (Figure 4). The standard setting for the voltage regulator is 14.2 - 14.8 volts at 25°C (77°F). Under normal conditions, therefore, operating voltage should always be at least 13.5 volts. If the voltage is 13.5 volts or more, proceed to Step 6. If the voltage is less than 13.5 volts, remove the generator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
6. If Steps 1 through 5 have produced satisfactory results, perform the following generator test:
steps: 1. Determine that the undercharged condition has not been caused by leaving accessories ON for extended periods without charging the battery.
2. Check the drive belt for proper tension. Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.
3. If a battery fault is suspected, refer to BATTERY (Section 6D1).

4. Inspect the wiring for faults. Check that all connections are clean and tight. Also check that any bulkhead connectors are tight and undamaged. Battery cable connections at battery, starter, and engine ground points should also be checked.

5. With all accessories turned OFF, connect a Voltmeter across the battery. Run the engine at a moderate speed and check the voltage. Some consideration should be given to the temperature to which the generator is exposed during this test. As the temperature of the regulator case in the generator increases, the voltage output will decrease (Figure 4). The standard setting for the voltage regulator is 14.2 - 14.8 volts at 25°C (77°F). Under normal conditions, therefore, operating voltage should always be at least 13.5 volts. If the voltage is 13.5 volts or more, proceed to Step 6. If the voltage is less than 13.5 volts, remove the generator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
6. If Steps 1 through 5 have produced satisfactory results, perform the following generator test:

proc_6d3_p322_1.text:
Generator Test (Step 6 sub-steps)
1. a. Disconnect the battery negative cable.
2. b. Remove the rubber protector, nut, and the generator B terminal wire.
3. c. Connect an ammeter between the generator and the disconnected wire. Connect the ammeter black lead to the wire and the ammeter red lead to the generator.
4. d. Connect a voltmeter between the generator B terminal and chassis ground.
5. e. Start and run the engine at 2000 RPM. Turn all accessories and high-beam headlights ON. Turn the blower to HIGH. Ground the generator F terminal with a screwdriver.
6. f. Adjust engine speed, as required, to obtain the maximum current output.
7. g. If the current output is within 10 amperes of the maximum rating and the voltage reading is 13.5 - 16.0 volts, the generator is not faulty and is not the cause of an undercharged battery condition. Refer to "Generator Usage" in this section. If the current output is NOT within 10 amperes of the maximum rating or the voltage reading is less than 13.5 volts, remove the generator. Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.

Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.

proc_6d3_p323_0.text:
OVERCHARGED BATTERY
1. To determine the battery's condition, refer to BATTERY (Section 6D1).
2. If an obvious overcharge condition exists, (as evidenced by the spewing of electrolyte), check the field windings for grounds and shorts. If the field winding is faulty, replace the rotor and retest the generator for proper voltage output. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
steps: 1. To determine the battery's condition, refer to BATTERY (Section 6D1).

2. If an obvious overcharge condition exists, (as evidenced by the spewing of electrolyte), check the field windings for grounds and shorts. If the field winding is faulty, replace the rotor and retest the generator for proper voltage output. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.

proc_6d3_p323_1.text:
VOLTAGE REGULATOR TEST — On-Vehicle
1. Connect a fast charger and voltmeter to the battery.
2. Turn the ignition switch to ON and slowly increase the charge rate. The indicator lamp in the vehicle will begin to dim when the voltage setting is reached. The indicator lamps should dim at 13.5 - 16.0 volts. If the test results are unsatisfactory, replace the voltage regulator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
steps: 1. Connect a fast charger and voltmeter to the battery.

2. Turn the ignition switch to ON and slowly increase the charge rate. The indicator lamp in the vehicle will begin to dim when the voltage setting is reached. The indicator lamps should dim at 13.5 - 16.0 volts. If the test results are unsatisfactory, replace the voltage regulator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.

proc_6d3_p323_2.text:
VOLTAGE REGULATOR TEST — Off-Vehicle
1. Remove the generator. Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.
2. Remove the voltage regulator from the generator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
3. Connect a voltmeter and a fast charger to a fully charged 12-volt battery.
4. Connect the voltage regulator battery terminal to the battery positive terminal.
5. Connect a non-powered test lamp across the voltage regulator rotor terminals. If the test lamp lights, proceed to Step 4. If the test lamp does not light, replace the voltage regulator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
6. Turn the fast charger ON. Observe the voltmeter. The test lamp will begin to dim as the voltage regulator voltage setting is reached. The test lamp should go out at 13.5 - 16.0 volts. If the test results are satisfactory, the voltage regulator is serviceable and should be reinstalled on the vehicle. If the test results are unsatisfactory, replace the voltage regulator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
steps: 1. Remove the generator. Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.
2. Remove the voltage regulator from the generator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
3. Connect a voltmeter and a fast charger to a fully charged 12-volt battery.
4. Connect the voltage regulator battery terminal to the battery positive terminal.

5. Connect a non-powered test lamp across the voltage regulator rotor terminals. If the test lamp lights, proceed to Step 4. If the test lamp does not light, replace the voltage regulator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.

6. Turn the fast charger ON. Observe the voltmeter. The test lamp will begin to dim as the voltage regulator voltage setting is reached. The test lamp should go out at 13.5 - 16.0 volts. If the test results are satisfactory, the voltage regulator is serviceable and should be reinstalled on the vehicle. If the test results are unsatisfactory, replace the voltage regulator. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.

para_6d3_p323_0.text: The test lamp is connected into the circuit exactly as the rotor would be when the regulator is inside the generator. The regulator interrupts the current to the test lamp when the regulator voltage setting is reached. Again, this voltage will vary slightly with temperature variations (Figure 4).

proc_6d3_p323_3.text:
To check the generator in a test stand, proceed as follows:
1. Remove the generator. Refer to On-Vehicle Service "Generator" procedure in this section.
2. Connect the generator to a fully charged 12-volt battery. Make the additional appropriate connections using a carbon pile, voltmeter, an ammeter, and 10-ohm resistor rated at six watts or more between the generator IG terminal and the battery.
3. Slowly increase generator speed and observe the voltage.
4. If the voltage is below 16 volts, proceed to Step 5. If the voltage is uncontrolled with speed and increases above 16 volts, test the voltage regulator. Refer to "Voltage Regulator Test" in
5. Operate the generator at a moderate speed and adjust the carbon pile, as required, to obtain the maximum current output. If the output is within 10 amperes of the specified rating, the generator is serviceable and can be installed on a vehicle. If the output is NOT within 10 amperes of the specified rating, proceed to Step 6. Refer to "Generator Usage" in this section.
6. Ground the generator F terminal.
7. Operate the generator at a moderate speed and adjust the carbon pile, as required, to obtain the maximum current output.
8. If the output is now within 10 amperes of the specified rating, test the voltage regulator. Refer to "Generator Usage" and "Voltage Regulator Test" in this section.
9. Check the field winding. Refer to Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.
10. If the output is NOT within 10 amperes of the specified rating, check the field winding, the neutral diodes, the rectifier bridge, and the stator. Refer to the "Generator Usage" and Unit Repair "Generator" procedure in this section.

noti_6d3_p323_0.text: NOTICE: The ground polarity of the generator and the battery must be the same.

para_6d3_p324_0.text: Noise from a generator may be caused by a loose drive pulley, loose mounting bolts, worn or dirty bearings, faulty neutral diodes, or a defective stator. A high frequency whine (or magnetic noise) that is heard at full output is acceptable and should not be considered a generator failure.

para_6d3_p324_1.text: The generator does not require periodic lubrication. All bearing assemblies are permanently lubed. At periodic intervals, check the mounting bolts for tightness and adjust the belt tension.

proc_6d3_p324_0.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Battery negative cable at battery.
2. Rubber protector, nut, and wire from generator B terminal.
3. Drive belt adjuster arm.
4. Drive belt.
5. Mounting bolt and nut.
6. Generator.

caut_6d3_p324_0.text: CAUTION: Failure to observe Step 1 may result in a serious personal injury. If a tool is shorted at the generator lead, the tool will become hot enough to cause a serious burn.

proc_6d3_p324_1.text:
Install or Connect
1. Generator with mounting bolt and nut. Tighten: Generator mounting bolt and nut to 23 N·m (17 lb.ft.).
2. Drive belt and adjuster arm. Adjust: Drive belt to obtain a 6-8 mm (0.24-0.31 in.) deflection with 10 kg (22 lb.) pressure exerted when reinstalling a used belt. Drive belt to obtain a 5-7 mm (0.20-0.27 in.) deflection with 10 kg (22 lb.) pressure exerted when installing a new belt. Tighten: Adjuster bolt to 23 N·m (17 lb.ft.).
3. Wire to generator B terminal, nut, and rubber protector. Tighten: Wire nut to 8 N·m (6 lb.ft.).
4. Battery negative cable to battery. Tighten: Cable to 12 N·m (9 lb.ft.).

proc_6d3_p325_0.text:
Figures 9 through 20
1. Hold the shaft with a hexagonal socket and remove the pulley nut with an offset box wrench. Remove the pulley.
2. Remove the generator B terminal nut and insulator.
3. Remove three screws and the rear end cover.
4. Remove two screws and the brush holder assembly.
5. Remove three screws and the voltage regulator.
6. Remove four screws, stretch the stator coil leads, and remove the rectifier.
7. Remove the rear end frame bolts and nuts.
8. Separate the rear end frame and the rotor from the drive end frame by tapping lightly on the rotor shaft with a plastic hammer.
9. Separate the rear end frame from the rotor by tapping the frame lightly with a plastic hammer.
10. Use a puller to remove the rear end bearing. Remove the bearing cover and wave breather.
11. Remove four screws and the retaining plate.
12. Drive out the drive end bearing.
13. Remove the stud bolts and the stator. (Heating the drive end frame may allow the stator to be removed more easily.)

proc_6d3_p325_1.text:
GENERATOR — Inspect
1. Check the insulation between the slip rings and the rotor core with an ohmmeter. If continuity exists, replace the rotor.
2. Check the resistance of the coil with an ohmmeter. With the ohmmeter probes on the generator slip rings, the resistance should be 2.8-3.0 ohms. If the resistance does not meet this specification, replace the rotor.
3. Check that all of the fan blades are in serviceable condition.
4. Check that all bearings are free from excessive wear, pitting, and corrosion.
5. Check the insulation between the stator coil and the rear end frame with an ohmmeter (Figure 17 - dotted probe). If continuity exists, replace the stator.
6. Check for continuity between stator neutral point A and each phase point B (Figure 17 - solid probe).
7. Using a ohmmeter adjusted to the 1000 ohm range, check for continuity between the recitifer B and E terminal. Swap the probes of the ohmmeter. The ohmmeter should measure continuity in one direction and infinity in the other direction. If the ohmmeter registers continuity or infinity in both directions, replace the regulator.
8. Check the condenser with a capacitance meter. The capacitance of the condenser should be 0.5 microfarads. If the capacitance differs from this specification by more than 0.03 microfarads, replace the condenser.
9. Check each brush for wear by measuring its length. Brush length should be 4.4-4.6 mm (0.17-0.18 in). If brush length does not meet this specification, replace the brush.

Check that all of the fan blades are in serviceable condition.
Check that all bearings are free from excessive wear, pitting, and corrosion.

Check for continuity between stator neutral point A and each phase point B (Figure 17 - solid probe).

Using a ohmmeter adjusted to the 1000 ohm range, check for continuity between the recitifer B and E terminal. Swap the probes of the ohmmeter. The ohmmeter should measure continuity in one direction and infinity in the other direction. If the ohmmeter registers continuity or infinity in both directions, replace the regulator.

Check the condenser with a capacitance meter. The capacitance of the condenser should be 0.5 microfarads. If the capacitance differs from this specification by more than 0.03 microfarads, replace the condenser.
Check each brush for wear by measuring its length. Brush length should be 4.4-4.6 mm (0.17-0.18 in). If brush length does not meet this specification, replace the brush.

proc_6d3_p325_2.text:
GENERATOR — Assemble
1. Install the stator and the stud bolts. Tighten: Stud bolts to 8 N·m (6 lb.ft.).
2. Install the drive end bearing.
3. Install the bearing retainer with four screws.
4. Install the rear end bearing, bearing cover, and wave washer.
5. Install the rotor into the drive end frame and mate the drive end frame to the rear end frame with the frame bolts and nuts. Tighten: Frame bolts and nuts to 30 N·m (22 lb.ft.).
6. Install the rectifier with four screws.
7. Install the generator B terminal insulator and nut. Tighten: Generator B terminal nut to 5 N·m (3.7 lb.ft.).
8. Install the voltage regulator with three screws.
9. Install the brush holder assembly with two screws.
10. Install the rear end cover with three screws.
11. Install the pulley and pulley nut by holding the shaft with a hexagonal socket. Tighten: Pulley nut to 113 N·m (83 lb.ft.).

GENERAL DESCRIPTION: 6D4-1

para_6d4_p330_0.text:
The ignition circuit consists of the battery, the distributor, the ignition switch, the spark plugs, and primary and secondary wiring. Two types of ignition systems are utilized depending upon vehicle application. The Metro LSi utilizes a conventional solid state ignition system with vacuum/centrifugal spark control. The Metro model utilizes an electronic spark control system which is monitored and controlled by the Electronic (Engine) Control Module (ECM).

Figure 1

The basic components of this ignition system are the ignition coil, the distributor, and the spark plugs and spark plug wiring. The distributor consists of a signal generator (signal rotor and pickup coil), ignitor, rotor, vacuum advancer, and centrifugal advancer.

When the distributor shaft rotates, a fluctuating magnetic field is generated due to changes in the air gap between the pickup coil and signal rotor. As a result, an alternating current (AC) voltage is induced in the pickup coil. This induced AC voltage peaks when a ridge on the signal rotor is adjacent to the ridge on the pickup coil. When the voltage peaks, the ignitor breaks the circuit to ground from the negative (-) side of the coil primary winding. With the circuit broken, the magnetic field in the ignition coil, which has been generated by the electrical current passing through it, collapses. The high voltage induced by the collapsing field is then forced to find a ground through the secondary coil wire, the distributor cap, the rotor, the spark plug wire, and finally across the spark plug air gap to the engine block.

Spark timing is mechanically controlled by a vacuum advance system which uses engine manifold vacuum and a centrifugal advance mechanism.

Figure 2

All spark timing changes in the distributor are performed electronically by the Electronic (Engine) Control Module (ECM). The ECM monitors information from various engine sensors, computes the desired spark timing, and signals the distributor to change the timing accordingly. No vacuum or mechanical advance mechanisms are used. For further information on this system, refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).

para_6d4_p330_1.text: Timing specifications for each engine are listed on the Vehicle Emissions Control Information label located under the hood. When using a timing light, connect an adapter between the spark plug and the spark plug wire or use an inductive-type pickup. Do not pierce the plug lead. Once the insulation of the spark plug wire has been broken, current will jump to the nearest ground and the spark plug will not fire properly. Always follow the label procedures when adjusting timing. Refer to the On-Vehicle Service "Ignition Timing" procedure later in this section.

caut_6d4_p330_0.text: CAUTION: Do not pierce the plug lead. Once the insulation of the spark plug wire has been broken, current will jump to the nearest ground and the spark plug will not fire properly.

impo_6d4_p330_0.text: IMPORTANT: Always follow the label procedures when adjusting timing.

para_6d4_p330_2.text:
The spark plug wiring used with the ignition system withstands very high temperatures and also provides an excellent insulator for the high voltages produced by both the vacuum/centrifugal and the electronic spark control ignition systems. The spark plug boot forms a tight seal on the spark plug and the boot should be twisted one-half turn before
removing. Care should also be exercised when using a timing light or other pickup equipment. Do not force anything between the boot and the wiring. Connections should be made in parallel using an adapter. Do not pull on the wire to be removed. Pull on the boot or use a tool designed for this purpose.

para_6d4_p331_0.text:
For spark plug type and gap sizes, refer to MAINTENANCE AND LUBRICATION (Section 0B). Always replace spark plugs with the correct type listed.

Normal service is assumed to be a mixture of idling, slow-speed, and high-speed driving. Occasional or intermittent highway driving is needed for good spark plug performance. The higher combustion temperatures generated during highway driving burn away any deposits of carbon or oxides that have built up from frequent idling or continual stop-and-go driving.

Spark plugs must operate within certain temperature limits if they are to provide the performance and service life expected. The spark plug selected for an engine is based on the normal service for which the engine was designed. The spark plug may not perform satisfactorily under other-than-normal operating conditions. For almost exclusive city driving, a spark plug 1 step higher in heat range might deliver longer service life than the spark plug recommended for normal operation. Conversely, a spark plug 1 step colder will perform better for heavy loads or continual high-speed driving.

Three rules to follow in selecting spark plugs for an engine in good condition are:
1. Select a spark plug with the specified heat range.
2. Should spark plug overheating occur, select a spark plug that is 1 heat range lower than the specified heat range.
3. If fouling is a problem, select a spark plug that is 1 heat range higher than the specified heat range.

para_6d4_p333_0.text:
Worn or dirty spark plugs may provide satisfactory performance at idling speed but, under more demanding operating conditions, they will frequently misfire. Misfiring spark plugs should be suspected from a number of symptons. These symptoms include: poor fuel economy, power loss, loss of speed, hard starting and generally poor engine performance. Spark plugs may misfire due to carbon fouling, an excessive air gap, a broken insulator, bridged electrodes, or a damaged spark plug wire and/or boot.

Fouled spark plugs are indicated by black carbon deposits on the electrodes. These black deposits are usually the result of slow-speed driving and short runs in which sufficient engine operating temperature is seldom achieved. Worn pistons, rings, faulty ignition, over-rich air/fuel mixture, and the incorrect spark plug heat range will also result in carbon deposits.

Carbon deposits on the spark plug insulator tip may become conductive and cause the high voltage arc to track along the tip to some point where it arcs to join the spark plug shell. This arc then ignites the air/fuel mixture later than normal which, in effect, retards the ignition timing. Heavy carbon deposits may be conductive to the extent that the arc path now becomes a shunt path to the spark plug shell. This condition prevents the high voltage from arcing and igniting the air/fuel mixture. Once arc tracking occurs, the spark plug may be permanently damaged and must be replace.

Excessive electrode wear, on low-mileage spark plugs, usually indicates that the engine is operating at speeds consistently higher than those for which the engine was designed or that the spark plug's heat range is too high. Electrode wear may also be the result of spark plug overheating caused by combustion gases leaking past the threads. Electrode wear can become excessive to the point that the high voltage no longer arcs across the electrodes.

Broken or cracked insulators are usually the result of improper installation. Broken upper insulators usually result from a poor-fitting wrench or an outside blow. Although the cracked insulator may not be evident immediately, oil or moisture will soon penetrate the crack. The crack will then cause the high voltage to ground or arc through the insulator to the spark plug shell.

para_6d4_p333_1.text:
Broken lower insulators often result from improper gapping and are usually visible immediately. When gapping a spark plug, always make the gap adjustment by bending the ground (side) electrode. Spark plugs with broken insulators must always be replaced.

Damaged spark plug wires and/or boots cause a similar condition to that of a cracked insulator. The high voltage arc flashes through the wire or boot and grounds on the spark plug shell or the engine.

para_6d4_p334_0.text:
Fouling deposits between the electrodes "ground out" the high voltage needed to fire the spark plug. The arc between the electrodes does not occur and the fuel air mixture is not ignited. This causes a power loss and exhausting of raw fuel.

A damaged spark plug boot, along with dirt and moisture, could permit the high voltage charge to short over the insulator to the spark plug shell or the engine. AC's buttress insulator design helps prevent high voltage flashover.

High voltage arcs between a fouling deposit on the insulator tip and spark plug shell. This ignites the fuel/air mixture at some point along the insulator tip, retarding the ignition timing which causes a power and fuel loss.

A crack in the spark plug insulator could cause the high voltage charge to "ground out". Here, the spark does not jump the electrode gap and the fuel air mixture is not ignited. This causes a power loss and raw fuel is exhausted.

Deposits that have formed on the insulator tip may become conductive and provide a "shunt" path to the shell. This prevents the high voltage from arcing between the electrodes. A power and fuel loss is the result.

Spark plug electrodes are worn so that the high voltage charge cannot cross the electrodes. Improper gapping of electrodes on new or "cleaned" spark plugs could cause a similar condition. Fuel remains unburned and a power loss results.

para_6d4_p335_0.text:
Spark plugs are protected by an insulating boot comprised of heat-resistant material which covers the spark plug terminal and extends downward over a portion of the spark plug insulator. These boots prevent the flash-over that causes engine misfiring.

Do not mistake corona discharge for flash-over or a shorted insulator. Corona discharge is a steady blue light appearing around the insulator, just above the shell crimp. It is the visible evidence of a high-tension field and has no effect on ignition system performance. This discharge may repel dust particles and leave a clear ring on the insulator just above the shell. This ring is sometimes mistaken as evidence that combustion gases have blown out between the shell and the insulator.

proc_6d4_p335_0.text:
1. Check for spark at each spark plug with a spark tester.
   • If no spark is detected, proceed to Step 2.
   • If spark is only detected on some spark plugs, check for a faulty distributor cap or rotor. Also check the spark plugs and the spark plug wires. Replace as needed.
2. Check for voltage at the ignition coil positive (+) terminal with a voltmeter.
   • If battery voltage is detected, proceed to Step 3.
   • If battery voltage is not detected, repair the open in the wiring between the battery and the ignition coil.
3. Check ignition coil resistance. Refer to the On-Vehicle Service "Ignition Coil" procedure later in this section.
   • If ignition coil resistance is within specification, proceed to Step 4.
   • If ignition coil resistance is not within specification, replace the ignition coil.
4. Check noise filter and condenser. Refer to item "NOISE FILTER AND CONDENSER" on page 6D4-8.
   • If both noise filter and condenser are in good condition, proceed to step 5.
   • If resistance is not about 2.2 kΩ or each condenser is conductive, replace noise filter or condenser.
5. Conventional Vacuum/Centrifugal Spark Control Ignition Systems Only
Check the continuity of the BRN/WHT wire between the ignition coil negative (-) terminal and the distributor.
   • If continuity exists, replace the pickup coil and the igniter. Refer to the "Conventional Vacuum/Centrifugal Spark Control Distributor" procedure later in this section.
   • If continuity does not exist, repair the open in the BRN/WHT wire.
6. Electronic Spark Control Ignition Systems Only Check the BRN/WHT wire between the distributor and the ECM.
   • If continuity exists, replace the generator assembly within the distributor. Refer to the "Electronic Spark Control Distributor" procedure later in this section. If the vehicle does not start after replacing the generator assembly, replace the ECM. Refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).
   • If continuity does not exist, repair the open in the BRN/WHT wire between the distributor and the ECM.

2. Check for voltage at the ignition coil positive (+) terminal with a voltmeter.
   • If battery voltage is detected, proceed to Step 3.

3. Check ignition coil resistance. Refer to the On-Vehicle Service "Ignition Coil" procedure later in this section.

4. Check noise filter and condenser. Refer to item "NOISE FILTER AND CONDENSER" on page 6D4-8.

If the vehicle does not start after replacing the generator assembly, replace the ECM. Refer to DRIVEABILITY AND EMISSIONS (Section 6E).
   • If continuity does not exist, repair the open in the BRN/WHT wire between the distributor and the ECM.

proc_6d4_p339_0.text:
If Distributor Position Was Lost:
1. Remove the number 1 spark plug.
2. Place a finger over the number 1 spark plug hole. Have an assistant crank the engine slowly until compression is felt.
3. Align the timing mark on the crankshaft pulley to the "0" position on the timing indicator.
4. Turn the rotor in the distributor housing to point at the number 1 cylinder spark plug tower on the distributor cap.
5. Install the distributor. Refer to the "Distributor" procedure earlier in this section.
6. Check the engine timing. Refer to the "Ignition Timing" procedure later in this section.

para_6d4_p339_0.text:
Start and run the engine until operating temperature is reached. Turn all electrical accessories "OFF". Check that the engine idle speed is 750 ± 50 RPM for manual transmission equipped vehicles and 850 ± 50 RPM for automatic transmission equipped vehicles. If the engine idle speed is not within specification, turn the throttle body idle speed adjusting screw until specifications have been met.

With the ignition switch "OFF", connect the pickup lead of a J34186 timing light to the number 1 spark plug. Use a jumper lead between the spark plug wire and the spark plug or use an inductive-type pickup. Do not pierce the wire or attempt to insert a probe between the spark plug wire and boot. Connect the timing light power leads according to the manufacturer's instructions.

proc_6d4_p339_1.text:
Conventional Vacuum/Centrifugal Spark Control Distributor
1. Disconnect the vacuum hose at the intake manifold gas filter. Plug the gas filter.
2. Start the engine and aim the J34186 timing light at the timing mark. The line on the pulley will line up at the timing mark. If an adjustment is necessary, loosen the distributor flange bolts. While observing the timing mark with the J34186 timing light, slowly rotate the distributor until the timing mark indicates the specified timing. Adjust the ignition timing to the specifications on the Vehicle Emissions Control Information label located under the hood.
3. Reconnect the vacuum hose to the intake manifold gas filter. Recheck the ignition timing.

◦ If the ignition timing does not advance to the specification on the Vehicle Emissions Control Identification label, check for a vacuum leak. If no leak is found, check the vacuum/centrifugal advance mechanism. Refer to the "Conventional Vacuum/Centrifugal Spark Control Distributor" procedure later in this section.
4. Tighten the distributor flange bolts to 13 N·m (120 lb.in.) and recheck the ignition timing.
5. Turn the ignition switch "OFF" and remove the J34186 timing light. Reconnect the number 1 spark plug wire, if removed.

proc_6d4_p339_2.text:
Electronic Spark Control Distributor
1. Remove the diagnostic connector cap. The diagnostic connector is located next to the ignition coil.
2. Insert a jumper wire between the diagnostic connector terminals C and D.
3. Start the engine and aim the J34186 timing light at the timing mark. The line on the pulley will line up at the timing mark. If an adjustment is necessary, loosen the distributor flange bolts. While observing the timing mark with the J34186 timing light, slowly rotate the distributor until the timing mark indicates the specified timing. Adjust the ignition timing to the specifications on the Vehicle Emissions Control Information label
4. located under the hood. Tighten the distributor flange bolts to 13 N·m (120 lb. in.) and recheck the ignition timing.
5. Remove the jumper wire from the diagnostic connector and reinstall the cap. Make sure that advanced timing is within specification.
6. Turn the ignition switch "OFF" and remove the J34186 timing light. Reconnect the number 1 spark plug wire, if removed.
steps: Remove the diagnostic connector cap. The diagnostic connector is located next to the ignition coil.
Insert a jumper wire between the diagnostic connector terminals C and D.

Start the engine and aim the J34186 timing light at the timing mark. The line on the pulley will line up at the timing mark. If an adjustment is necessary, loosen the distributor flange bolts. While observing the timing mark with the J34186 timing light, slowly rotate the distributor until the timing mark indicates the specified timing. Adjust the ignition timing to the specifications on the Vehicle Emissions Control Information label
located under the hood. Tighten the distributor flange bolts to 13 N·m (120 lb. in.) and recheck the ignition timing.
Remove the jumper wire from the diagnostic connector and reinstall the cap. Make sure that advanced timing is within specification.
Turn the ignition switch "OFF" and remove the J34186 timing light. Reconnect the number 1 spark plug wire, if removed.

para_6e_p345_0.text:
The computer command control system, which is controlled by the Electronic Control Module (ECM), is designed to maintain exhaust emission levels at federal standards while providing good driveability and fuel efficiency. The functions of the system are based on data gathered by sensors and switches located throughout the vehicle. The ECM maintains control over fuel delivery, ignition, idle air flow, the fuel pump and other system components, while monitoring the system for faulty operation with its diagnostic capabilities. See Figure 1.

It is important to review the component sections and wiring diagrams in Section "6E2" to determine which systems are controlled by the ECM.

The underhood Vehicle Emissions Control Information label (Figure 2) contains important emission specifications and setting procedures. In the upper left corner is exhaust emission information which identifies the year, the manufacturing division of the engine, the displacement of the engine in liters, the class of vehicle, and type of fuel metering. There is also an illustrated emission component and vacuum hose schematic.

This label is located in the engine compartment of every General Motors vehicle. If the label has been removed, it can be ordered from GM Service Parts Organization (GMSPO).

impo_6e_p345_0.text: IMPORTANT: ALL NEW GENERAL MOTORS VEHICLES ARE CERTIFIED BY THE UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY AS CONFORMING TO THE REQUIREMENTS OF THE REGULATIONS FOR THE CONTROL OF AIR POLLUTION FROM NEW MOTOR VEHICLES. THIS CERTIFICATION IS CONTINGENT ON CERTAIN ADJUSTMENTS BEING SET TO FACTORY STANDARDS. IN MOST CASES, THESE ADJUSTMENT POINTS EITHER HAVE BEEN PERMANENTLY SEALED AND/OR MADE INACCESSIBLE TO PREVENT INDISCRIMINATE OR ROUTINE ADJUSTMENT IN THE FIELD. FOR THIS REASON, THE FACTORY PROCEDURE FOR TEMPORARILY REMOVING PLUGS, CAPS, ETC., FOR PURPOSES OF SERVICING THE PRODUCT, MUST BE STRICTLY FOLLOWED AND, WHEREVER PRACTICABLE, RETURNED TO THE ORIGINAL INTENT OF THE DESIGN.

SYSTEMS CONTROLLED: Air Management, Canister Purge, Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR), Electronic Spark Timing (EST), Fuel Control, Idle Air Control (IAC), Transmission Converter Clutch (TCC) or Shift Light, Electric Fuel Pump, Air Conditioning, Engine Cooling Fan, Diagnostics ("Check Engine" Light)

para_6e_p346_0.text:
Refer to the General Motors Maintenance Schedule in Section "0B" of the Chassis Service Manual for the maintenance service that should be performed to retain emission control performance.

The vehicle drive wheels always should be blocked and parking brake firmly set while checking the system.

This section of the Chassis Service Manual has been developed to describe the function and operation of the electronic engine control system that controls the driveability and emissions of the vehicle. Emphasis is placed on the diagnosis and repair of problems related to the system.

Section "6E" provides general diagnostic procedures, use, wiring and terminal repair procedures, and descriptions of special tools. It then leads into Sections "6E2". Section "6E2" deals with engine control systems using throttle body injection for fuel delivery.

Sections "6E2" is divided into three major sub-sections dealing with diagnosis and repair. They can be summarized as follows.

• Component Locations
• Wiring Diagrams
• ECM Terminal End View and Terminal Definitions
• Diagnostic Circuit Check. This must be the first step of any diagnostic procedure.
• Diagnostic Trouble Code Charts with facing pages containing circuit diagrams, circuit operation information, and helpful diagnostic information.

• This sub-section assists in diagnosis of intermittent problems or problems which don't result in the storing of diagnostic trouble codes. It is arranged by symptoms of poor driveability and emissions and lists possible causes of the problems.

• Component and Circuit Descriptions
• On-car Service
• Functional Checks/Diagnostic Charts
