para_6e2_p426_0.text: Definition: Engine delivers less than expected power. Little or no increase in speed when accelerator pedal is pushed down part way.

proc_6e2_p426_0.text:
LACK OF POWER, SLUGGISH, OR SPONGY - Checks
1. Compare customer's car to similar unit. Verify the problem actually exists.
2. Perform careful visual check as described at the start of Section "B".
3. Remove air cleaner assembly and check for filter being clogged, or air inlet tube being restricted.
4. CHECK: Visual check of vacuum hoses for damage or loosely connected.
5. CHECK: Engine ignition timing.
6. CHECK: Loose, damaged, or defective spark plug wire(s).
7. CHECK: Fouled spark plugs, mechanical problems, oil consumption, etc.
8. CHECK: Engine overheating.
9. CHECK: Worn distributor terminal.
10. CHECK: Electronic spark advance system for proper operation. See Section "C4".
11. Vacuum leak at intake manifold.
12. Slipping clutch (if equipped).
13. Fuel pump pressure. See Section "C2".
14. EFI system for proper operation. See Section "C2".
15. Low cylinder compression.
16. EGR valve operation. See Section "C7".
17. Brake system, dragging brakes, stuck brakes, etc.
18. Accelerator cable play wrong.
19. Poor performance of manifold air temperature sensor signal.
20. Poor performance of coolant temperature sensor signal.
21. Poor performance of Throttle Switch (M/T) or Throttle Position Sensor (A/T) signal.
22. Faulty ECM. See Section "C1".

para_6e2_p426_1.text: Definition: Engine makes sharp metallic knocks that change with throttle position. Sounds like popcorn popping.

proc_6e2_p426_1.text:
DETONATION / SPARK KNOCK - Checks
1. Check for obvious problem such as: Engine overheating due to loose belts or restricted air flow through the radiator.
2. Check for obvious problem such as: Restricted coolant flow through radiator or engine.
3. CHECK: Ignition timing.
4. CHECK: Proper spark plug(s) and gap.
5. CHECK: Poor spark plug wire connections.
6. CHECK: EFI system. See Section "C2".
7. EGR valve operation. See Section "C7".
8. Fuel quality.
9. Excessive oil in combustion chamber.
10. PCV valve operation. See Section "C13".
11. Visual check for intake gasket leaks.
12. ESA system. See Section "C4".
13. Restricted Exhaust System.
14. Clean engine combustion chambers with top engine cleaner.
15. Follow instructions on the car.

para_6e2_p426_2.text: Definition: Engine idle unevenly, or RPM may vary (called hunting). If bad enough car may shake. Either condition may cause the car to stall if severe enough, engine idle speed incorrect.

proc_6e2_p426_2.text:
ROUGH, UNSTABLE, OR INCORRECT IDLE, STALLING - Checks
1. Perform a careful visual check as described at the start of Section "B".
2. Check accelerator cable for proper operation.
3. Visually check all vacuum hose connections.
4. CHECK: Worn distributor cap terminals.
5. CHECK: Ignition timing.
6. CHECK: Defective or damaged distributor cap.
7. CHECK: ESA system. See Section "C4".
8. Air cleaner assembly for clogged filter or restricted air inlet tubes.
9. Intake for leaks.
10. Faulty spark plugs or wrong spark plugs.
11. EFI system. See Section "C2".
12. Engine overheating.
13. Low compression.
14. EGR valve operation. See Section "C7".
15. PCV valve operation. See Section "C13".

EFI system. See Section "C2".
Engine overheating.
Low compression.
EGR valve operation. See Section "C7".
PCV valve operation. See Section "C13".

proc_6e2_p427_0.text:
POOR FUEL ECONOMY
1. Visually check vacuum lines for cracks, loose connections, or damage.
2. Check fuel delivery system of obvious leaks.
3. Verify the actual fuel mileage.
4. CHECK: Air cleaner filter to be clogged, or air inlet tube blocked. Ignition timing. Wrong spark plugs, or worn, damaged spark plugs. Poor fuel quality. Restricted exhaust. See CHART B-1 of this section. Transaxle operation, i.e.; slipping, not shifting, wrong shift points (M/T) clutch slipping.
5. Brakes not fully releasing.
6. Wrong or faulty thermostat.
7. Wrong tire size or improper tire pressure.
8. EGR valve operation. See Section "C7".
9. EFI system. See Section "C2".
10. PCV valve operation. See Section "C13".
11. ESA system. See Section "C4".
12. High idle.
13. Faulty Throttle Switch (M/T) or Throttle Position Sensor (A/T) signal.
14. Faulty MAT sensor signal.
15. Faulty coolant temperature sensor signal.
16. Faulty ECM.

proc_6e2_p427_1.text:
CUTS OUT, MISSES
1. Perform carefully visual check as described at the start of Section "B".
2. Check for misfiring cylinder, spark plugs, spark plug wires, coil connection, etc.
3. Perform compression check to verify if problem is mechanical and not electrical.
4. Check EGR valve for not properly scaling, stuck partly open.
5. Check ignition timing.
6. Check CAM timing.

proc_6e2_p427_2.text:
EXCESSIVE EXHAUST EMISSIONS OR ODORS
1. Perform careful visual and physical check as described at the beginning of Section "B".
2. Perform "Diagnostic Circuit Check."
3. CHECK: EGR valve not opening. Use CHART C-7. Vacuum leaks. Faulty coolant system and/or coolant fan operation. Use CHART C-12. Remove carbon with top engine cleaner. Follow instructions on can.
4. If the system is running RICH (block learn less than 118), refer to "Diagnostic Aids" on facing page of Code 45.
5. If the system is running LEAN (block learn greater than 138), refer to "Diagnostic Aids" on facing page of Code 44.
6. If emission test indicates excessive NOx, check for items which cause car to run lean or too hot.
7. If emission test indicates excessive HC and CO or exhaust has excessive odors, check for items which cause car to run RICH: Incorrect fuel pressure. Use CHART A-7. Fuel loading of evaporative vapor canister. Use CHART C-3. PCV valve plugging, sticking, or blocked PCV hose. Check for fuel in crankcase. Catalytic converter lead contamination (Look for removal of fuel filler neck restrictor.) Improper fuel cap installation. Faulty spark plugs, plug wires, or ignition components. See Section "6D".

• General description of components and sub-systems.

For locations of components, wiring diagrams, and ECM Terminal End View, refer to the front of the "A" Section.

para_6e2_p429_1.text:
The ECM is installed to the underside of the instrument panel on the driver's side.

The ECM is a precision unit consisting of a one chip microcomputer, A/D (Analog/Digital) converter, I/O (Input/Output) unit, ROM (Read Only Memory) and RAM (Random Access Memory). It is an essential part of the electronic control system, for its functions include not only such major functions as to control the fuel injector, ISC solenoid valve, fuel pump relay, etc. but also a self-diagnosis function and a fail-safe function as described in the following section.

The ECM diagnoses troubles which may occur in the system when the ignition switch is "ON" and the engine is running. It indicates the result by turning "ON" or flashing "Check Engine" light when a fault occurs in any of the following systems:
• Oxygen Sensor
• Coolant Temperature Sensor
• Throttle Switch (M/T model) or Throttle Position Sensor (A/T model)
• Manifold Air Temperature Sensor
• Map Sensor
• Ignition Signal
• Crank Angle Sensor
• Vehicle Speed Sensor
• EGR system
• CPU (Central Processing Unit) of ECM

fig_6e2_p429_0.text:
Figure C1-1 - ECM and Location
1: ECM
2: Instrument panel
3: Steering wheel

para_6e2_p430_0.text:
The ECM and "Check Engine" light operates as follows:
"Check Engine" light illuminates when the ignition switch is turned "ON" (but the engine is stopped) with the diagnosis switch terminal ungrounded regardless of the condition of EFI system. This is only to check the "Check Engine" light bulb and its circuit.
If the EFI system is free from any faults after the engine starts, the "Check Engine" light turns "OFF".
When the ECM detects a trouble that has occurred in the above areas, it turns the "Check Engine" light "ON" while the engine is running to warn the driver of the occurrence of a fault and at the same time it stores the trouble code in ECM back-up memory.
(The code is kept in memory even if the fault was only temporary and disappeared immediately. It is not erased unless the power to ECM is shut "OFF" for 20 seconds or longer.)
The ECM will indicate trouble codes that are stored in its memory by means of flashing of "Check Engine" light at the time of inspection (i.e. when diagnosis switch terminal is grounded and ignition switch is turned "ON").

note_6e2_p430_0.text: NOTE: When a trouble occurs in the above systems except EGR system, and disappears while the diagnosis switch terminal is ungrounded and the engine is running, the "Check Engine" light remains "ON" as long as the trouble exists but it goes "OFF" when the normal condition is restored. When it is the EGR system where a fault occurs, even if it is only a temporary one, the "Check Engine" light remains "ON" until the ignition switch is turned "OFF". Only ignition circuit trouble (Code No. 41) is not stored in back-up memory of the ECM. (In other words, even if ECM has detected a trouble in the ignition circuit, once the ignition switch is turned "OFF", code No. 41 will not be indicated even when the diagnosis switch terminal is grounded and ignition switch is turned "ON".) Therefore, to check the diagnostic code when the engine fails to start, crank the engine and then ground the diagnostic switch terminal with ignition switch "ON".

para_6e2_p430_1.text: When trouble has occurred in the EFI system and a fault is detected by the ECM, control over the injector, ISC solenoid valve and others is maintained on the basis of the calculated signals and/or back-up program prestored in the ECM. This function is called "fail-safe function". Thus, with this function, a certain level of engine performance is available even when some failure occurs and disability in running is avoided. The systems covered are as follows:

para_6e2_p430_2.text:
INFORMATION SENSORS

The MAP sensor signals the ECM as to pressure changes in the intake manifold. It consists of a semiconductor type pressure converting element which converts a pressure change into an electrical change and an electronic circuit which amplifies and corrects the electrical change. The ECM sends a 5-volt reference voltage to the MAP sensor. As the manifold pressure changes, the electrical resistance (Figure C1-3) of the sensor also changes. By monitoring the sensor output voltage, the ECM senses the manifold pressure (intake air volume).
The ECM uses the voltage signal from the MAP sensor as one of the signals to control fuel injector, ISC solenoid valve, shift-up indicator light and EGR VSV (if equipped).

The throttle switch, consisting of 2-contact points (idle switch and wide open switch) is connected to the throttle valve shaft on the throttle body, and detects the throttle valve opening.

fig_6e2_p431_1.text:
Figure C1-4 - Throttle Switch
1: TS
2: Idle switch
3: Wide open switch

para_6e2_p431_0.text:
The throttle opening in the idle state is detected by means of the idle switch, and the wide open state is detected by the wide open switch.

By monitoring their "ON"/"OFF" signals, ECM detects the throttle valve opening.

The ECM uses the "ON"/"OFF" signals from throttle switch as one of the signals to control fuel injector, ISC solenoid valve, shift light and EGR VSV (if equipped).

para_6e2_p431_1.text: Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) (A/T model only) (Figure C1-5)

para_6e2_p431_2.text:
The TPS, consisting of a contact point (idle switch) and a potentiometer, is connected to the throttle valve shaft on the throttle body, and detects the throttle valve opening.

The throttle opening in the idle state is detected by means of the contact point which turns "ON" in that state. But beyond that the full opening is detected by the potentiometer as follows.

fig_6e2_p431_2.text:
Figure C1-5 - Throttle Position Sensor
1: TPS
2: TPS adjusting holes
3: TPS screw holes
4: Turn TPS after fitting and align holes.

para_6e2_p431_3.text:
A 5-volt reference voltage is applied to the sensor from the ECM and as its brush moves over the resistance according to the throttle valve opening, the output voltage varies accordingly.

By monitoring the "ON"/"OFF" signal and sensor output voltage, ECM detects the throttle valve opening.

ECM uses the signal from the TPS as one of the signals to control (fuel injector, ISC solenoid valve and EGR VSV (if equipped).

Also, the ECM converts the voltage signal from the TPS into "ON"/"OFF" signal and sends it to A/T control module, where it is used as one of the signals to control the automatic transmission.

para_6e2_p431_4.text: Located at the side of air cleaner case, the MAT sensor constantly measures the temperature of the air entering the manifold and converts a change in the air temperature. The sensor is a thermistor, that is, as air temperature lowers, resistance increases and as it rises, resistance decreases. As air temperature of the intake varies, the ECM, by monitoring the voltage drop, adjusts the amount of fuel injection according to the air temperature.

para_6e2_p431_5.text:
Located at the side of throttle body, the CTS measures the temperature of the engine coolant. Like the air temperature sensor, the CTS is a thermistor.

That as coolant temperature lowers, resistance increases and as it rises, resistance decreases.

fig_6e2_p432_0.text:
Figure C1-6 - Coolant Temperature Sensor
1: ENGINE COOLANT TEMPERATURE SENSOR

para_6e2_p432_0.text: By monitoring the voltage drop of the coolant temperature sensor, the ECM detects the engine coolant temperature and that affects most systems under the control of the ECM.

fig_6e2_p432_2.text:
Figure C1-8 - Oxygen Sensor
1: Terminal post
2: Insulator
3: Zirconia element
4: Element cover
5: Atmosphere
6: Exhaust gases

para_6e2_p432_1.text:
The Oxygen (O2) sensor is located on the exhaust manifold to detect the concentration of oxygen in the exhaust gases. It consists of the zirconia element (with thin platinum surface coating) which generates electromotive force, lead wire which draws out the electromotive force and cover and housing which protect the zirconia element from damage.

The zirconia element, by its property, generates the electromotive force when a difference in oxygen concentration exists between its faces. As its temperature rises, the change of the electromotive force is amplified by catalytic reaction of the platinum. The oxygen sensor makes use of this property. Atmosphere is introduced into the oxygen sensor, the inside of the zirconia element is exposed to the atmosphere and outside to exhaust gases. Thus the difference in concentration between the inside and the outside of the zirconia element varies with the oxygen concentration in the exhaust gases.

The large concentration difference results in about 1V of the electromotive force and small difference results in about 1 volt. To put in other words, if the amount of oxygen in the exhaust gases is less (air-fuel mixture is richer than the stoichiometric mixture), about 1 volt of electromotive force is generated and if more (air-fuel mixture is leaner than the stoichiometric mixture), almost none is generated.

In this way, the oxygen sensor detects whether the oxygen concentration is high or low (or the mixture is leaner or richer than the stoichiometric mixture).

The Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) consisting of the lead switch and magnet is built in the speedometer. As the magnet turns with the speedometer cable, its magnetic force causes the lead switch to turn "ON" and "OFF". Such "ON"/"OFF" frequency increases or decreases in proportion with the car speed and is sent to the ECM as pulse signals.

The crank angle sensor located in the distributor consists of the signal generator (pick-up coil and magnet) and signal rotor.
As the signal rotor turns, AC voltage is generated in the pick-up coil which varies in a pulsatory way as shown in Figure C1-10. This pulse signal (3 pulses/revolution) is sent to the ECM where it is used to calculate the engine speed and also as one of the signals to control various devices.

fig_6e2_p433_0.text:
Figure C1-9 - Vehicle Speed Sensor
1: SPEED SENSOR
2: MAGNET
3: SPEEDOMETER ASSEMBLY

fig_6e2_p433_1.text:
Figure C1-10 - Crank Angle Sensor
1: Pick up coil
2: Magnet
3: Signal rotor
4: Air gap

para_6e2_p433_0.text:
This signal is sent from the engine starter circuit. Receiving it, the ECM detects that the engine is cranking and uses it as one of the signals to control the fuel injector and fuel pump relay.

This signal is sent from A/T control module as a battery voltage signal only when A/T is in "R", "D", "2" or "L" range. Thus, the ECM judges whether A/T is in one of the above ranges or in "P" or "N", and uses it as one of the signals to control the fuel injector and ISC solenoid valve.

This signal is sent from the illumination light circuit. It is used to reduce intensity of the shift-up indicator light when the illumination light is "ON".

This signal is sent from the A/C control module. When the A/C clutch is energized the A/C control module will pull a signal line low that is in series with the A/C vacuum switching valve solenoid. The ECM uses this signal to adjust air/fuel mixture for the lean condition the A/C VSV solenoid creates. The vehicles engine speed will be increased slightly to prevent stalling.

The fuel injector is driven by its solenoid coil based upon the ECM output signal.

There is some delay called "Ineffective Injection Time", where no fuel is provided, between the ECM signal and valve action.

Since ineffective injection time depends on the battery voltage, the ECM takes voltage information to compensate for fuel injection time.

proc_6e2_p434_0.text:
Precautions In Identifying Diagnostic Code
1. Before identifying diagnostic codes indicated by the "Check Engine" light, don't disconnect connectors from the ECM, battery cables, ECM ground wire harness, main fuse or 15A fuse for the tail light circuit. Such disconnection will erase in ECM trouble code(s) in memory.
2. If abnormality or malfunction lies in two or more areas, "Check Engine" light indicates applicable codes three times each. And flashing of these codes is repeated as long as the diagnosis terminal is grounded (spare fuse is connected) and ignition switch is "ON".
3. Note of diagnostic code indicated first.
4. Intermittent troubles - There are cases where the "Check Engine" light indicates a diagnostic code representing a fault which occurred only temporarily and has gone. In such case, it may occur that good parts are replaced unnecessarily. To prevent such an error, be sure to follow instructions given below when checking by using the "Diagnostic Flow Chart".
- When trouble can be identified, that is, it is not an intermittent one:
  Check sensor (actuator), wires and each connection and if they are all in good condition.
- When fault can not be identified but "Check Engine" light indicates a trouble code:
  Diagnose fault by using that code No. and if sensor (actuator), wires and each connection are all in good condition, erase diagnostic code in ECM memory. Then conduct a test run and check what "Check Engine" light indicates. Only when it indicates trouble code again, is the fault present.
  If it indicates no trouble code but normal Code No. 12, it means that an intermittent trouble did occur and has gone. In this case, check wires and connections carefully again.

In such case, it may occur that good parts are replaced unnecessarily. To prevent such an error, be sure to follow instructions given below when checking by using the "Diagnostic Flow Chart".
- When trouble can be identified, that is, it is not an intermittent one:
  Check sensor (actuator), wires and each connection and if they are all in good condition.
- When fault can not be identified but "Check Engine" light indicates a trouble code:
  Diagnose fault by using that code No. and if sensor (actuator), wires and each connection are all in good condition, erase diagnostic code in ECM memory. Then conduct a test run and check what "Check Engine" light indicates. Only when it indicates trouble code again, is the fault present.
  If it indicates no trouble code but normal Code No. 12, it means that an intermittent trouble did occur and has gone. In this case, check wires and connections carefully again.

proc_6e2_p434_1.text:
Notes on System Circuit Inspection
1. Intermittent troubles - Most intermittent problems are caused by faulty electrical connections or wiring. Perform careful check of suspect circuits for:
- Poor mating of connector halves, or terminals not fully seated in connector body (backed out).
- Improperly formed or damaged terminals. All connector terminals in problem circuit should be carefully reformed to increase contact tension.
- Poor terminal to wire connection.
2. Never connect any tester (voltmeter, ohmmeter, or whatever) to the ECM when its connector is disconnected. Attempt to do it may cause damage to the ECM.
3. Never connect an ohmmeter to the ECM with its connector connected to it. Attempt to do it may cause damage to ECM and sensors.
4. Be sure to use a voltmeter whose minimum resistance is more than one 1MΩ per 1 volt. Any other voltmeter should not be used because accurate measurements are not obtained. (Use J 34029-A or equivalent.)
5. When checking voltage at each terminal of the connector which is connected to ECM, be sure to connect negative probe to body ground and using a jumper wire, connect ECM case to body ground. Applying probes of voltmeter improperly may cause the sensor or ECM to be shorted and damaged.
steps:

- Poor mating of connector halves, or terminals not fully seated in connector body (backed out).
- Improperly formed or damaged terminals. All connector terminals in problem circuit should be carefully reformed to increase contact tension.
- Poor terminal to wire connection.
Never connect any tester (voltmeter, ohmmeter, or whatever) to the ECM when its connector is disconnected. Attempt to do it may cause damage to the ECM.
Never connect an ohmmeter to the ECM with its connector connected to it. Attempt to do it may cause damage to ECM and sensors.

Be sure to use a voltmeter whose minimum resistance is more than one 1MΩ per 1 volt. Any other voltmeter should not be used because accurate measurements are not obtained. (Use J 34029-A or equivalent.)

When checking voltage at each terminal of the connector which is connected to ECM, be sure to connect negative probe to body ground and using a jumper wire, connect ECM case to body ground. Applying probes of voltmeter improperly may cause the sensor or ECM to be shorted and damaged.

para_6e2_p434_0.text: There are two diagnosis switch terminals; one included in the junction/fuel block and the other in the monitor connector in the engine compartment. When either diagnosis switch terminal is grounded, a diagnosis signal is fed to the ECM which then outputs a self-diagnosis code and at the same time fixes the "ON" time of ISC solenoid valve constant.

para_6e2_p435_0.text:
The test switch terminal is included in the monitor connector. When this terminal is grounded, the ECM sets the ignition timing to base timing.
When both test switch terminal and diagnosis switch terminal are not grounded, ECM outputs A/F duty through the A/F duty check terminal.

ISC SOLENOID VALVE

note_6e2_p435_0.text:
NOTE: • When disconnecting and connecting connectors, make sure to turn ignition switch "OFF".
• When checking connection of terminals, check the male and female half for excessive opening and both for locking (looseness), corrosion, dust, etc.
• When connecting a probe of ohmmeter, voltmeter, etc. to connector terminal, be sure to connect it from wire harness side of connector.
• When connecting meter probe from terminal side of connector because it can't be connected from harness side, use extra care not to bend male terminal of connector or force its female terminal open. In case of a connector as shown in Figure C1-12, connect probe as shown to avoid opening female terminal. Never connect probe where male terminal is supposed to fit.
• (See Figure C1-13) Before measuring voltage at each terminal, check to make sure that battery voltage is 11 volts or higher. Such terminal voltage check at low battery voltage will lead to erroneous diagnosis.

para_6e2_p435_1.text: Service of the ECM consists of complete replacement of the ECM. There are no serviceable parts, such as a PROM or Mem-Cal to replace inside this ECM. If the ECM is determined to be faulty, it is then to be replaced as a complete assembly.

impo_6e2_p435_0.text: IMPORTANT: When replacing the production ECM with a service ECM (controller), it is important to transfer the broadcast code and production ECM number to the service ECM label. Please do not record on the ECM cover. This will allow positive identification of the ECM parts throughout the service life of the vehicle.

para_6e2_p438_0.text:
The function of the fuel metering system is to deliver the correct amount of fuel to the engine under all operating conditions. Fuel is delivered by the Throttle Body Injection (TBI) unit, which is controlled by the Electronic Control Module (ECM), based on several important engine operation parameters. These parameters include engine speed, manifold pressure, engine coolant temperature, throttle position, and exhaust oxygen content.

The fuel metering system consists of the air/fuel delivery system and the electronic control system. This section covers the air/fuel delivery system. For information on the "Electronic Control System", refer to Section "C1" in this manual.

The main components of this system are the fuel tank, fuel pump, fuel filter, throttle body (including fuel injector, fuel pressure regulator and air valve), fuel feed line, fuel return line, air cleaner and ISC solenoid valve.

The fuel in the fuel tank is pumped up by the fuel pump, filtered by the fuel filter and fed under pressure to the injector installed in the throttle body. Fuel pressure applied to the fuel injector (the fuel pressure in the fuel feed line) is always kept a certain amount higher than the pressure in the intake manifold by the fuel pressure regulator. The fuel is injected into the throttle body in conic dispersion when the injector opens according to the injection signal from ECM. The fuel pressure that is relieved by the pressure regulator is returned to the fuel tank through the fuel return line. The injected fuel is mixed with the air which has been filtered through the air cleaner in the throttle body. The air/fuel mixture is drawn through clearance between throttle valve and bore and idle bypass passage into the intake manifold. Then the intake manifold distributes the air/fuel mixture to each combustion chamber.

When the engine is cold, the air is drawn through air valve bypassing the throttle valve into the intake manifold.

When ISC solenoid valve opens according to the signal from ECM, the air is drawn through a hose bypassing the throttle valve into the intake manifold.

For the structure and operation of the fuel tank and filter, refer to ENGINE FUEL (SECTION 6C).

para_6e2_p439_0.text:
In this system, the ECM controls the "ON" time (length of pulse) and timing of the fuel injection from the fuel injector into the throttle body according to the signals from the various sensors so that suitable air/fuel mixture is supplied to the engine in each driving condition.

There are two types of injection timing. One is "synchronous injection" in which injection is synchronous with the ignition signal (or signal from crank angle sensor) and the other is "asynchronous injection" in which injection takes place independently of the ignition signal (or signal from crank angle sensor).

The factors to determine the injection time are the basic injection time which is calculated on the basis of the engine speed and the intake manifold pressure (amount of the intake air) and various compensations which are determined according to the signals from various sensors that detect the state of the engine and driving conditions.

fig_6e2_p439_1.text:
Figure C2-2 - Synchronous Injection
P.S.: POWER STROKE
E.S.: EXHAUST STROKE
I.S.: INTAKE STROKE
C.S.: COMPRESSION STROKE

para_6e2_p439_1.text: Normally, the injector injects fuel at every ignition signal (or at every signal from crank angle sensor). But when the engine coolant temperature is low immediately after its start, the injection time for one ignition cycle is divided into cycles and injection takes place accordingly.

fig_6e2_p440_0.text:
Figure C2-3 - Asynchronous Injection
P.S.: Power Stroke
I.S.: Intake Stroke
E.S.: Exhaust Stroke
C.S.: Compression Stroke

para_6e2_p440_0.text:
When the throttle valve is opened from its idle position, the injector injects fuel in addition to synchronous injection independently of the ignition signal (or signal from the crank angle sensor).

In order to improve starting performance, enriching compensation at start is carried out. For a certain time after the engine is started, air/fuel mixture enriching compensation is made so as to stabilize the engine speed. The amount of compensation varies depending on the engine coolant temperature.

When the engine is cold, enriching compensation is made to ensure good driveability until the engine coolant temperature reaches the specified level. The amount to enrich the air/fuel mixture is decreased as the temperature rises.

To ensure smooth acceleration, enriching compensation is carried out when the intake manifold pressure varies by more than a specified amount during acceleration. The amount of compensation is determined according to the engine coolant temperature and how much the intake manifold pressure varies.

fig_6e2_p440_1.text:
Figure C2-4 - Parameter Diagram for Fuel Injection Control System
CRANK ANGLE SENSOR: ENGINE SPEED
MAP: INTAKE MANIFOLD PRESSURE
MAT: INTAKE AIR TEMPERATURE
CTS: ENGINE COOLING WATER TEMPERATURE
ENGINE STARTER CIRCUIT: ENGINE START SIGNAL
OXYGEN SENSOR: O2 CONCENTRATION
TS OR TPS: THROTTLE VALVE OPENING
SPEED SENSOR: CAR SPEED
BATTERY: BATTERY VOLTAGE
A/T CONTROL MODULE (A/T MODEL ONLY): "R", "D", "2" OR "L" RANGE SIGNAL

para_6e2_p441_0.text:
To provide maximum power during high load driving conditions, the air/fuel mixture is made richer to ensure that all the available air is used during combustion.

To obtain a proper air/fuel mixture ratio during deceleration, leaning compensation is carried out when the intake manifold pressure varies by more than a specified amount during deceleration.

A power voltage drop delays the mechanical operation of the injector. Then the actual injection time becomes shorter for the time that electricity is supplied to the injector. To compensate this, the electricity supply time is made longer when the voltage is lower.

The air/fuel ratio may vary due to such factors as variation in each engine itself and aging.
To compensate such variation, feed back compensation is used and base air/fuel mixture ratio is adjusted to a proper level for feed back compensation.

Fuel injection stops (with operation of the injector prevented) when decelerating (i.e. when the throttle valve is at idle position and the engine speed is high), so that unburned gas will not be exhausted and it starts again when above conditions are not met.
The fuel injection also stops when the engine speed exceeds 7,000 rpm to prevent over-run which affects the engine adversely and it starts again when the engine speed reduces to less than 6,800 rpm.

para_6e2_p441_1.text:
It is necessary to keep the air/fuel mixture close to the theoretical air/fuel ratio (14.7) to obtain efficient performance of the 3-way catalyst and high clarification rate of CO, HC and NOx in the exhaust gas. For that purpose, ECM operates as follows. It first compares the signal from the oxygen sensor with a specified reference voltage and if the signal is higher, it detects that the air/fuel ratio is richer than the theoretical air/fuel ratio and reduces fuel. On the other hand, if the signal is lower, it detects that the air/fuel ratio is leaner and increases fuel. By repeating these operations, it adjusts the air/fuel ratio closer to the theoretical air/fuel ratio.
1) When oxygen concentration in the exhaust gas is low, that is, when the air/fuel ratio is smaller than the theoretical air/fuel ratio (fuel is richer), electromotive force of the oxygen sensor increases and a rich signal is sent to ECM.
2) Upon receipt of the rich signal, ECM decreases the amount of fuel injection, which causes oxygen concentration in the exhaust gas to increase and electromotive force of the oxygen sensor to decrease. Then a lean signal is sent to ECM.
3) As ECM increases the amount of fuel injection according to the lean signal, oxygen concentration in the exhaust gas decreases and the condition is back to the situation described in step 1.
This control process, however, will not take place under any of the following conditions:
● At engine start and when fuel injection is increased after engine start
● When engine coolant temperature is low
● When highly loaded and fuel injection is increased
● At fuel cut-off
● When oxygen sensor is cold
● When engine is running at high speed (higher than about 4000 rpm).

para_6e2_p441_2.text:
Engine idle speed is controlled by the ECM through the Idle Speed Control (ISC) solenoid valve which allows more/less air to bypass the throttle valve. The reasons for idle speed control are:
● To keep the engine idle speed as specified at all times. The engine idle speed can vary due to the following reasons:
  - Load applied to engine (when electric load is applied, automatic transmission is shifted to "R", "D", "2" or "L" range, air-conditioner is turned "ON," etc.)
  - Variation in atmospheric pressure
  - Change in engine itself with passage of time
  - Other factors causing idle speed to change
● To improve starting performance of engine
● To compensate air/fuel mixture ratio when decelerating (dash-pot effect)
The ISC solenoid valve opens the bypass air passage when it is turned "ON" by ECM and closes it when turned "OFF."
ECM detects the engine condition by using signals from various sensors and switches and while repeating "ON" and "OFF" cycle of ISC solenoid valve at a certain rate (12 times a second), it controls bypass air flow by increasing and decreasing its "ON" time within a cycle.

fig_6e2_p442_0.text:
Figure C2-5 - Fuel Pump
1: CHECK VALVE
2: HOUSING
3: MAGNET
4: MAGNET SPRING
5: IMPELLER
6: ARMATURE
7: BEARING
8: BRUSH
9: BRUSH SPRING

para_6e2_p442_0.text:
While the engine is cranking, the ECM keeps the ISC solenoid valve "ON" so as to obtain better start of the engine. After the engine has started, it reduces "ON" time gradually to maintain the idle speed as specified.

When the accelerator pedal is depressed (throttle valve is at other than idle), the ECM keeps the ISC solenoid valve "ON." When decelerating, on the other hand, it reduces its "ON" time gradually (thereby reducing the bypass air flow gradually) to adjust air/fuel mixture to an optimum ratio for combustion.

When the car is at a stop, the throttle valve is at the idle position and the engine is running, ECM controls the bypass air flow by increasing or decreasing "ON" time of the ISC solenoid valve so that the engine speed is kept at a specified idle speed.

With an air-conditioner equipped car, when the air-conditioner is "ON," a certain amount of the bypass air is supplied by the air-conditioner Vacuum Switching Valve (VSV) independently of the ISC valve bypass air circuit. The VSV bypass air is used for fine control of engine idle speed.

impo_6e2_p442_0.text: IMPORTANT: The vacuum switching valve automatically adjusts the engine idle speed to specification by means of ECM. If such specified idle speed is not available, it is necessary to adjust the basic idle speed with the idle speed adjusting screw. See "Idle Speed/A/C VSV Adjustment Procedure" in this section.

para_6e2_p442_1.text: The ECM controlled electric fuel pump is located in the fuel tank, and consists of an armature, a magnet, an impeller, a brush set, a check valve, etc.

para_6e2_p443_0.text:
ECM controls "ON"/"OFF" operation of the fuel pump by turning it "ON" via the fuel pump relay under any of the following conditions.
• For 2 seconds after ignition switch "ON."
• While cranking engine (while engine start signal is received by the ECM).
• Crank angle sensor signal is received by the ECM.
When power is supplied to the fuel pump, the motor in the pump runs and so does the impeller. This causes a pressure difference to occur between both sides of the impeller as there are many grooves around it. Then the fuel is drawn through the inlet port, and with its pressure increased it is discharged through the outlet port. The fuel pump also has a check valve to keep some pressure in the fuel feed line even when the fuel pump is stopped.

para_6e2_p443_1.text:
The throttle body consists of the main bore, air and/or fuel passage, vacuum passage (for pressure sensor, ignition timing vacuum advancer, evaporative emission control system and EGR system), air induction passage and the following parts.
• Fuel injector which injects fuel according to the signal from the ECM.
• Fuel pressure regulator which maintains the fuel pressure to the injector a certain amount higher than the pressure in the intake manifold.
• Throttle valve which is interlocked with the accelerator pedal and controls the amount of the air/fuel mixture drawn into the combustion chamber.
• Air valve which supplies the bypass air when engine is cold.
• Idle speed adjusting screw which controls the amount of bypass air to adjust engine idle speed.
• TS (M/T model) or TPS (A/T model) which detects the throttle valve opening and sends a signal to the ECM.

The fuel injector is an electromagnetic type injection nozzle which injects fuel in the throttle body bore according to the signal from the ECM.

fig_6e2_p443_0.text:
Figure C2-7 - Fuel Injector Cross-Section
1: O-RING (LARGE)
2: COIL
3: PLUNGER
4: NEEDLE VALVE
5: O-RING (SMALL)
6: FILTER

para_6e2_p443_2.text:
When the solenoid coil of the injector is energized by the ECM, it becomes an electromagnet and attracts the plunger. At the same time, the needle valve which is incorporated with the plunger opens and the injector which is under the fuel pressure injects fuel in conic dispersion. As the stroke of the needle valve of the injector is set constant, the amount of fuel injected at one time is determined by the length of time during which the solenoid coil is energized (injection time).

The fuel pressure regulator is diaphragm-operated relief valve consisting of diaphragm, spring and valve. It keeps the fuel pressure applied to the injector 1.8 kg/cm2 (180 kPa, 25.6 psi) higher than that in the intake manifold at all times.

fig_6e2_p444_0.text:
Figure C2-8 - Pressure Regulator Cross-Section
1: CHAMBER "A"
2: CHAMBER "B"
3: SPRING
4: DIAPHRAGM
5: VALVE
6: FROM FUEL PUMP
7: TO FUEL TANK
8: INTAKE MANIFOLD PRESSURE (VACUUM)

para_6e2_p444_0.text: The pressure applied to the chamber "A" of fuel pressure regulator is intake manifold pressure and that to the chamber "B" is fuel pressure (Figure C2-8). When the fuel pressure rises more than 1.8 kg/cm² (180 kPa, 25.6 psi) higher than the intake manifold pressure, the fuel pushes the valve in the regulator open and excess fuel returns to the fuel tank via the return line.

fig_6e2_p444_1.text:
Figure C2-9 - Opening Air Valve
1: Throttle body
2: Thermo wax
3: Springs
4: Valve

6: To intake pipe

para_6e2_p444_1.text:
The air valve consists of thermo-wax, springs and a valve. When the engine is cold (or engine coolant is lower than about 60°C (140°F)), the thermo-wax contracts. In this state, the valve opens by the spring force, allowing the air to be drawn into the intake manifold. Thus the amount of intake air increases even when the throttle valve is at the idle position and the engine speed rises to the fast idle state which is higher than the idle speed.

As the engine is warmed up, the thermo-wax expands gradually, then the piston pushes down the valve gradually, and the amount of air passing through the air valve decreases and so does the engine speed. When the engine coolant temperature reaches about 60°C (140°F), the valve is fully closed and the engine speed is back to the normal idle speed.

The ISC solenoid valve opens and closes the air bypass passage according to the signal from ECM. When it opens, the air is supplied to the intake manifold.

impo_6e2_p444_1.text: IMPORTANT: Before starting engine place transaxle gear selector in Park (Neutral for M/T) and set parking brake and block drive wheels.

proc_6e2_p445_0.text:
IDLE SPEED CHECK AND ADJUSTMENT
1. Check accelerator cable for free movement and excessive play, cable play should be within specification. If out of specification adjust (Figure C2-11):
   a. Loosen accelerator able lock nut.
   b. Adjust cable play to .12 - .20 in. (3-5 mm).
2. Inspect lead wires and hoses of EFI and engine emissions control systems for being connected securely.
3. Check accelerator cable for proper play.
4. Check ignition timing, and adjust if necessary.
5. Turn "OFF" all accessories (A/C, wipers, heater, lights, etc.).
6. Make sure air cleaner is installed correctly.
7. Remove cover on idle speed adjusting screw.
8. Start engine and allow it to reach normal operating temperature idle (accessories "OFF").
9. Connect a tachometer.
10. Install a spare fuse to diagnosis switch terminal in fuse block, see Figure C2-12.
11. Check idle speed. It should be:
   • 700 ± 50 rpm A/T Metro LSI only)
   • 850 ± 50 rpm A/T
   • 750 ± 50 rpm M/T
   If idle speed is okay, proceed to steps 13 through 16.
12. If necessary, adjust idle speed to correct idle speed by turning idle speed adjusting screw "IN" to increase or "OUT" to decrease idle speed. See Figure C2-13.
13. Remove spare fuse from diagnosis switch terminal (Figure C2-12).
14. Disconnect tachometer.
15. Install idle speed screw cover.
16. Install air cleaner assembly and related hoses.

proc_6e2_p446_0.text:
IDLE SPEED / A/C VSV ADJUSTMENT
1. Remove air cleaner assembly.
2. Start engine and allow it to reach normal operating temperature (with A/C "ON").
3. Connect a tachometer.
4. Install a spare fuse in diagnosis switch terminal (Figure C2-12).
5. Check idle speed, adjust as necessary by adjusting the A/C vacuum switch valve idle screw.
   • 850 ± 50 rpm A/T
   • 900 ± 50 rpm M/T
6. Remove spare fuse from diagnosis switch terminal.
7. Disconnect tachometer.
8. Install air cleaner assembly.
9. Check idle speed. Adjust as necessary to reach specification stated in step "5".

7. Disconnect tachometer.
8. Install air cleaner assembly.
9. Check idle speed. Adjust as necessary to reach specification stated in step "5".

caut_6e2_p446_0.text: CAUTION: To reduce the risk of fire and personal injury, it is necessary to relieve the fuel system pressure before servicing the fuel system.

proc_6e2_p446_1.text:
FUEL PRESSURE RELIEF PROCEDURE
1. Loosen fuel filler cap to relieve tank vapor pressure.
2. Place transaxle gear shift lever in Neutral or Park for A/T models. Set parking brake and block drive wheels.
3. Remove main fuse block cover and engine coolant reservoir from its bracket.
4. Remove screws holding main fuse block.
5. Remove coupler holding fuel pump relay and disconnect. Fuel pump relay, see Figure C2-15.
6. Start engine and run until it stalls. Crank engine for 3 seconds to insure there is no fuel left in lines.
7. After completing repair. Reverse steps 2-4.
steps: 1. Loosen fuel filler cap to relieve tank vapor pressure.
2. Place transaxle gear shift lever in Neutral or Park for A/T models. Set parking brake and block drive wheels.
3. Remove main fuse block cover and engine coolant reservoir from its bracket.
4. Remove screws holding main fuse block.
5. Remove coupler holding fuel pump relay and disconnect. Fuel pump relay, see Figure C2-15.
6. Start engine and run until it stalls. Crank engine for 3 seconds to insure there is no fuel left in lines.
7. After completing repair. Reverse steps 2-4.

caut_6e2_p446_1.text: CAUTION: After relieving system pressure, a small amount of fuel may be released when servicing fuel lines or connections. In order to reduce the chance of personal injury, cover fuel line fittings with a shop towel before disconnecting, to catch any fuel that may leak out. Place the towel in an approved container when disconnect is completed.

para_6e2_p446_0.text: A fuel system pressure test is a part of several diagnostic charts and symptoms checks. To perform this test, follow this procedure:

proc_6e2_p446_2.text:
FUEL SYSTEM PRESSURE TEST
1. Relieve fuel pressure using the procedure in this section.
2. Disconnect negative battery cable.
3. Remove air cleaner assembly.
4. Disconnect fuel feed hose from throttle body.
5. Install fuel pressure gauge (J 34730-1), and T-Fitting (J 34730-75) between fuel feed line and throttle body. Clamp securely to ensure that no leaks occur. See Figure C2-16.
6. Start engine, and idle at normal operating temperature.
7. Check fuel pressure as follows:
   Idling - 90-140 kPa (13-20 psi)
   Engine "OFF", Ignition "ON"
      158-210 kPa (23-30.5 psi)
   Engine "OFF", Ignition "ON"
      (Pressure reduces as time passes)
   If pressure is not within specification, refer to CHART A-5 or A-7.
8. Relieve fuel pressure using the procedure in this section.
9. Disconnect fuel pressure gauge assembly.
10. Connect fuel feed line to throttle body.
11. Install air cleaner assembly.
12. Start engine and check for fuel leaks.
steps: 1. Relieve fuel pressure using the procedure in this section.
2. Disconnect negative battery cable.
3. Remove air cleaner assembly.
4. Disconnect fuel feed hose from throttle body.
5. Install fuel pressure gauge (J 34730-1), and T-Fitting (J 34730-75) between fuel feed line and throttle body. Clamp securely to ensure that no leaks occur. See Figure C2-16.
6. Start engine, and idle at normal operating temperature.

7. Check fuel pressure as follows:
   Idling - 90-140 kPa (13-20 psi)
   Engine "OFF", Ignition "ON"
      158-210 kPa (23-30.5 psi)
   Engine "OFF", Ignition "ON"
      (Pressure reduces as time passes)
   If pressure is not within specification, refer to CHART A-5 or A-7.
8. Relieve fuel pressure using the procedure in this section.
9. Disconnect fuel pressure gauge assembly.
10. Connect fuel feed line to throttle body.
11. Install air cleaner assembly.
12. Start engine and check for fuel leaks.

para_6e2_p447_0.text: All TBI component parts, with the exception of those noted below, should be cleaned in a cold immersion cleaner such as Carbon X (X-55) or equivalent.

noti_6e2_p447_0.text: NOTICE: The Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Idle Air Control (IAC) valve, pressure regulator diaphragm assembly, fuel injector or other components containing rubber should not be placed in a solvent or cleaner bath. A chemical reaction will cause these parts to swell, harden, or distort. Do not soak the throttle body with the above parts attached. If the throttle body assembly requires cleaning, soaking time in the cleaner should be kept to a minimum. Some models have hidden throttle shaft dust seals that could lose their effectiveness by extended soaking.

proc_6e2_p447_1.text:
THROTTLE BODY INJECTION UNIT SERVICE
1. Clean all metal parts thoroughly and blow dry with shop air. Be sure that all fuel and air passages are free of dirt or burrs.
2. Inspect mating casting surfaces for damage that could affect gasket sealing.
3. Check that throttle valve lever moves freely and smoothly.
steps: 1. Clean all metal parts thoroughly and blow dry with shop air. Be sure that all fuel and air passages are free of dirt or burrs.
2. Inspect mating casting surfaces for damage that could affect gasket sealing.
3. Check that throttle valve lever moves freely and smoothly.

noti_6e2_p450_0.text: NOTICE: Use care to not submerse throttle body in water to prevent possible damage due to corrosion. Only the air valve should be submerged in the water.

proc_6e2_p450_4.text:
Inspect (Figure C2-24)
1. Remove throttle body assembly as previously described in this section.
2. Separate the throttle body halves.
3. Remove coolant temperature sensor.
4. Immerse air valve in water.
5. Check that the valve closes as the water temperature rises, should fully close at 60°C (140°F). If it does not, replace the valve.
6. Install coolant temperature sensor.
7. Install upper and lower throttle body section, using a new gasket. Tighten attaching screws to 3.5 N·m (31 lb. in.).
8. Install throttle body with a new gasket. Tighten attaching bolts to 23 N·m (17 lb. ft.).

tbl_6e2_p454_0.text:
GENERAL DESCRIPTION: C3-1
PURPOSE: C3-1
EVAPORATIVE EMISSION CONTROL SYSTEM: C3-1
RESULTS OF INCORRECT OPERATION: C3-1
ON-VEHICLE SERVICE: C3-2
CHARCOAL CANISTER REPLACEMENT: C3-2
CANISTER HOSES: C3-2
CANISTER COMPONENT CHECK: C3-2
CANISTER VALVE CHECK: C3-2
BI-METAL VACUUM SWITCHING VALVE (BVSV) CHECK: C3-3
BVSV REPLACEMENT: C3-3
2-WAY CHECK VALVE CHECK: C3-3
PARTS INFORMATION: C3-3

para_6e2_p454_0.text:
The basic Evaporative Emission Control System (EECS) used on all vehicles is the charcoal canister storage method. This method transfers fuel vapor from the fuel tank to an activated carbon (charcoal) storage device (canister) to hold the vapors when the vehicle is not operating. When the engine is running, the fuel vapor is purged from the carbon element by intake air flow and consumed in the normal combustion process.

An evaporative emission control system is used to prevent emission of fuel vapor.

The vapor generated in the fuel tank while driving or the engine at a stop passes through a 2-way check valve and enters the charcoal canister where the charcoal absorbs and stores the fuel vapor.

Only when the following conditions are all satisfied, ported vacuum is applied to canister purge control valve and the valve opens.
• Engine is running.
• Engine coolant temperature is high. (Engine is warmed up.)
• Throttle valve opens larger than idle position.

As a result, fuel vapor in the canister is sucked into intake manifold through purge control valve and purge line.

In this state, the canister is purged or cleaned by air drawn through the filter at the bottom of the canister.

When engine coolant water temperature is low, the vacuum signal line communicates with atmosphere through Bimetal Vacuum Switching Valve (BVSV).

Therefore, ported vacuum is not applied to purge control valve, and the valve is closed. In this state, the canister is not purged.

The 2-way check valve is provided to keep the pressure in the fuel tank constant. When the pressure in the fuel tank becomes positive and reaches its specified value, it opens the valve to let the vapor flow into the charcoal canister. On the other hand, when the pressure in the fuel tank becomes negative and reaches its specified value, it opens the valve to let the air flow into the fuel tank.

para_6e2_p454_1.text:
Poor idle, stalling and poor driveability can be caused by:
- Inoperative purge solenoid
- Damaged canister
- Hoses split, cracked and, or not connected to the proper tubes.

Evidence of fuel loss or fuel vapor odor can be caused by:
- Liquid fuel leaking from fuel lines, or fuel pump
- Cracked or damaged canister
- Disconnected, misrouted, kinked, deteriorated or damaged vapor hoses, or control hoses.

If the solenoid is open, the canister can purge to the intake manifold at all times. This can allow extra fuel at idle or during warm-up, which can cause rough or unstable idle, or too rich operation.

If the solenoid is closed the canister can become over loaded resulting odor.

para_6e2_p457_0.text:
This car uses two types of ignition systems: one is a conventional type solid state ignition system which is used for Metro LS Model M/T model and A/T model and the other is an Electronic Spark Advance (ESA) system which is used for the METRO model.

The basic components of the ignition system are ignition coil, distributor and spark plugs and those of the distributor are signal generator (signal rotor and pickup coil), igniter, rotor, vacuum advancer and centrifugal advancer.

When the distributor shaft rotates, the magnetic flux passing through the pickup coil varies due to the change in air gap between the pickup coil and the signal rotor. As a result, an alternating current voltage is induced in the pickup coil.

The voltage induced, turns the igniter "ON" and "OFF" which in turn, switches the ignition coil primary current "ON" and "OFF." Thus, high voltage is induced into the secondary winding of ignition coil and ignition voltage is supplied to the spark plugs.

The spark advance is produced by the vacuum advance which operates based on the engine vacuum and centrifugal advance.

In the ESA system, the ECM is programmed for the best ignition timing under every engine condition. After receiving signals indicating the engine status (e.g. engine revolutions, manifold vacuum, coolant temperature, etc.) it selects the most suitable ignition timing from its memory. The ECM controls a driver to ground within the ECM. This causes the coils primary circuit to be low or "OFF", thus high voltage is induced in the secondary windings of the coil. Voltage is then supplied to the spark plugs. Thus ignition timing is controlled to yield the best engine performance.

para_6e2_p463_0.text: SECTION C7 - EXHAUST GAS RECIRCULATION (EGR) SYSTEM (FOR ALL CALIFORNIA EMISSIONS AND ONLY ESA MODEL FEDERAL EMISSIONS SYSTEMS)

tbl_6e2_p463_0.text:
GENERAL DESCRIPTION: C7-1
OPERATION: C7-1
EGR VALVE CHECK: C7-1
EGR MODULATOR CHECK: C7-2
EGR VACUUM SWITCH VALVE SOLENOID CHECK: C7-3
EGR VALVE REPLACEMENT: C7-3
EGR MODULATOR REPLACEMENT: C7-3
EGR VACUUM SWITCHING VALVE REPLACEMENT: C7-3
PARTS INFORMATION: C7-3

para_6e2_p463_1.text:
The EGR System controls the formation of NOx (Oxides of Nitrogen) Emissions by recirculating the exhaust gases into the combustion chamber. This is done through the intake manifold.

The EGR is controlled by the ECM through an EGR vacuum switching valve and modulator. Vacuum is allowed to the EGR Valve at acceleration and shut "OFF" upon deceleration or constant engine speed.

The diaphragm mounted in the EGR modulator is operated by back pressure of the exhaust gas to open and close the valve. By this opening and closing action of the valve, the EGR modulator controls the vacuum transmitted to the EGR valve.

Under a low load condition such as low speed driving, the exhaust pressure is low. In this state, the diaphragm in the EGR modulator is pushed down by the spring force and the modulator valve opens to allow the air into the vacuum passage from the outside.

As a result, the vacuum transmitted to the EGR valve becomes smaller and so does the opening of the EGR valve.

Thus, less amount of exhaust gas is recirculated to the intake manifold.

Under a high load condition such as high speed driving, on the other hand, the exhaust pressure is high. By the high exhaust pressure, the diaphragm in the modulator is pushed up and closes its valve. As the air does not enter the vacuum passage in this state, the vacuum transmitted to the EGR valve grows larger and so does the opening of the EGR valve.

Thus, larger amount of exhaust gas is recirculated to the intake manifold.

Under any one of the following conditions, ECM closes the vacuum passage of VSV. In this state, as the vacuum is not transmitted to the EGR valve, it remains closed.
• When engine coolant temperature is low.
• When throttle valve is at idle position.
• When engine is running under high load.
• When intake manifold pressure is low.

Other than the above, EGR valve opens and closes in accordance with the EGR modulator operation.

proc_6e2_p463_0.text:
EGR VALVE CHECK
1. Engine operating a normal temperature 60°C (120°F).
2. Place finger on EGR valve diaphragm and accelerate engine, diaphragm should move.
3. If diaphragm doesn't move see CHART C-7.
4. Disconnect vacuum hose from EGR valve.
5. Apply 10" inches of vacuum using a vacuum pump to valve with finger on diaphragm. It should move and engine may stall.
6. If valve passes the above test use Code Chart 51. If valve fails, replace valve.

proc_6e2_p465_0.text:
EGR Vacuum Switch Valve Solenoid Check
1. Disconnect vacuum hoses (2) from solenoid.
2. Disconnect electrical connector with ignition "OFF".
3. Using DVM check resistance between two terminals.
4. You should have 33 - 39 ohms of resistance. If resistance is within specification proceed to step 5. If not, replace solenoid.
5. Blow into tube "A", air should exhaust through filter, not tube "B" (Figure C7-4).
6. Reconnect electrical connector.
7. Ignition "ON", ground diagnosis switch terminal.
8. Blow air into tube "A", air should exhaust from tube "B" now (Figure C7-4). If VSV solenoid fails the check, replace VSV.

para_6e2_p466_1.text:
The purpose of the shift light is to provide a display which indicates the optimum fuel economy point for upshifting the manual transaxle based on engine speed and load. The display lamp is located in the instrument panel and is activated by the ECM when:
• Vehicle speed is higher than 3 mph (5 km/h)
• Both Idle Switch and WOT Switch are "OFF".
• Engine speed is greater than 1500 RPM.
• Engine speed will vary with intake manifold pressure and engine coolant temperature.
The shift light will dim slightly when the headlights and/or parking lights are turned "ON". This is to give better visibility.

The shift light circuit can be checked using CHART C-8.

See Section "8A" to repair wiring problem.

fig_6e2_p467_0.text:
CHART C-8 MANUAL TRANSAXLE (M/T) SHIFT LIGHT CHECK 1.0L (VIN 6) "M" CARLINE (TBI)
SHIFT LIGHT: Shift light lamp symbol connected between ignition switch and LT BLUE wire
LT BLUE: Light blue wire connecting shift light to ECM shift light driver
SHIFT LIGHT DRIVER: ECM internal driver that grounds the LT BLU wire to turn on shift light
B2 FOR METRO: ECM connector pin B2 used for Metro
A8 FOR METRO LSI: ECM connector pin A8 used for Metro LSI

para_6e2_p467_0.text:
The shift light indicates the best transaxle shift point for maximum fuel economy. The light is controlled by the ECM and is turned "ON" by grounding the LT BLU wire from the light to the ECM.

If the LT BLU wire is grounded the lamp will be "ON" all the time. If the circuit is open the lamp will not be "ON" when commanded by the ECM.

proc_6e2_p467_0.text:
Numbers below refer to circled numbers on the diagnostic chart.
1. This should not turn the shift light "ON". If it does, the LT BLU wire is shorted to ground or the ECM is faulty.
2. This checks the shift light circuit up to the ECM connector.

tbl_6e2_p469_0.text:
GENERAL DESCRIPTION: C13-1
OPERATION: C13-1
RESULTS OF INCORRECT OPERATION: C13-1
DIAGNOSIS: C13-2
FUNCTIONAL CHECK OF PCV VALVE: C13-2
ON-VEHICLE SERVICE: C13-2
PARTS INFORMATION: C13-2

para_6e2_p469_0.text:
The compressed combustion gas which passes by the piston into the crankcase is known as blow-by gas. This gas contains large amounts of CO and HC. The PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) system (Figure C13-1) is provided to prevent the blow-by-gas from being emitted into atmosphere and it operates as follows.

When the vacuum in the intake manifold is low (throttle valve open), the PCV valve is wide open due to its spring force. Thus a large amount of the blow-by-gas is drawn into the intake manifold.

On the other hand, when the vacuum in the manifold is high, the PCV valve opening is limited due to the high vacuum. Thus the amount of the blow-by-gas drawn into the intake manifold is small.

fig_6e2_p469_0.text:
Figure C13-1 - PCV System Operation
1: INTAKE MANIFOLD
2: CYLINDER HEAD COVER
3: PCV VALVE
4: THROTTLE BODY
5: AIR CLEANER
6: BLOW-BY GAS
7: FRESH AIR
8: BLOW-BY GAS AND FRESH AIR MIXTURE

para_6e2_p469_1.text:
The primary control is through the PCV valve (Figure C13-2) which meters the flow at a rate depending on manifold vacuum.

To maintain idle quality, the PCV valve restricts the flow when intake manifold vacuum is high. If abnormal operating conditions arise, the system is designed to allow excessive amounts of blow-by gases to back flow through the crankcase vent tube into the air cleaner to be consumed by normal combustion.

A plugged valve or hose may cause:
• rough idle
• stalling or slow idle speed
• oil leaks
• oil in air cleaner
• sludge in engine

A leaking valve or hose would cause:
• rough idle
• stalling
• high idle speed

fig_6e2_p469_1.text:
Figure C13-2 - PCV Valve Cross Section
1: CHECK VALVE

proc_6e2_p470_0.text:
1. Remove PCV valve from intake manifold.
2. Run the engine at idle.
3. Place your thumb over end of valve to check for vacuum. If there is no vacuum at valve, check for plugged hoses or manifold port, or PCV valve. Replace plugged or deteriorated hoses.
4. Turn "OFF" the engine and remove PCV valve. Shake valve and listen for the rattle of needle inside the valve. If valve does not rattle, replace valve.
steps: Remove PCV valve from intake manifold.
Run the engine at idle.
Place your thumb over end of valve to check for vacuum. If there is no vacuum at valve, check for plugged hoses or manifold port, or PCV valve. Replace plugged or deteriorated hoses.
Turn "OFF" the engine and remove PCV valve. Shake valve and listen for the rattle of needle inside the valve. If valve does not rattle, replace valve.

para_6e2_p470_0.text:
With this system, any blow-by in excess of the system capacity (from a badly-worn engine, sustained heavy load, etc.) is exhausted into the air cleaner and is drawn into the engine.

Proper operation of the PCV system is dependent upon a sealed engine. If oil sludging or dilution is noted, and the PCV system is functioning properly, check engine for possible cause and correct to ensure that system will function as intended.

fig_6e2_p470_0.text:
Figure C13-3 - PCV Location
1: PCV VALVE

noti_6f_p474_0.text: NOTICE: ALL NEW GENERAL MOTORS VEHICLES ARE CERTIFIED BY THE UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY AS CONFORMING TO THE REQUIREMENTS OF THE REGULATIONS FOR THE CONTROL OF AIR POLLUTION FROM NEW MOTOR VEHICLES FACTORY STANDARDS. IN MOST CASES, THESE ADJUSTMENTS POINTS EITHER HAVE BEEN PERMANENTLY SEALED AND/OR MADE INACCESSIBLE TO PREVENT FACTORY PROCEDURE FOR TEMPORARILY REMOVING PLUGS, CAPS, ETC. FOR PURPOSES OF SERVICING, THE PRODUCT MUST BE STRICTLY FOLLOWED AND, WHEREVER PRACTICABLE, RETURNED TO THE ORIGINAL INTENT OF THE DESIGN.

para_6f_p474_0.text:
The exhaust system consists of an exhaust manifold, an exhaust center pipe, a muffler, and a seal and gaskets, etc., and the exhaust center pipe has the catalytic converter.

The catalytic converter is an emission control device added to the exhaust system to lower the levels of Hydrocarbon (HC), Carbon Monoxide (CO), and Oxides of Nitrogen (NOX) pollutants in the exhaust gas.

The catalyst in the catalytic converter is of a "Three-way" type. THE CATALTYIC CONVERTER REQUIRES THE USE OF UNLEADED FUEL ONLY.

caut_6f_p474_1.text: CAUTION: To avoid the danger of being burned, do not touch the exhaust system when the system is hot. Any service on the exhaust system should be performed when the system is cool.

para_6f_p474_1.text: Every 12 months or 7,500 miles (12,000 km), and when the car is raised for other service, check the exhaust system as follows:

proc_6f_p474_0.text:
Inspect
1. Rubber mountings for damage, deterioration, and out of position.
2. The exhaust system for leaks, loose connections, dents, and damages. For specified torques on loosened nut or bolts, refer to "On Vehicle Service" in this section.
3. Check the nearby body areas for damaged, missing, or mispositioned parts, open seams, holes, loose connections, or other defects which could permit exhaust fumes to seep into the car.
4. Make sure that the exhaust system components have enough clearance from the under body to avoid overheating and possible damage to the passenger compartment carpet.
5. Any defects should be fixed at once.
steps: Rubber mountings for damage, deterioration, and out of position.
The exhaust system for leaks, loose connections, dents, and damages. For specified torques on loosened nut or bolts, refer to "On Vehicle Service" in this section.
Check the nearby body areas for damaged, missing, or mispositioned parts, open seams, holes, loose connections, or other defects which could permit exhaust fumes to seep into the car.
Make sure that the exhaust system components have enough clearance from the under body to avoid overheating and possible damage to the passenger compartment carpet.
Any defects should be fixed at once.

tbl_6f_p475_0.text:
CONDITION: Exhaust restriction
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Restricted muffler No.1 or muffler No. 2.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Restricted catalytic converter.
  - CORRECTION: Replace, and determine cause of malfunction (i.e., engine misfire, over rich fuel mixture, etc.).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Restricted exhaust manifold.
  - CORRECTION: Remove and clear blockage, or replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Loose or broken element in catalytic converter.
  - CORRECTION: Replace, and determine cause of malfunction (i.e., engine misfire, over rich fuel mixture, etc.).
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Loose or broken muffler baffles.
  - CORRECTION: Replace as necessary.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Bent or kinked exhaust pipes.
  - CORRECTION: Replace as necessary.

tbl_6f_p476_0.text:
Condition: Exhaust leakage or noise
  - Possible Cause: Leaking pipes or components.
  - Correction: Replace damaged components or pipes.
  - Possible Cause: Loose connections.
  - Correction: Realign and tighten connections.
  - Possible Cause: Leaking flange gaskets.
  - Correction: Replace.
  - Possible Cause: Leaking exhaust manifold or gasket.
  - Correction: Tighten manifold and/or replace gasket.
  - Possible Cause: Loose or broken element in catalytic converter.
  - Correction: Replace, and determine cause of malfunction (i.e., engine misfire, over rich fuel mixture, etc.).
  - Possible Cause: Loose or broken muffler No. 1 or muffler No. 2.
  - Correction: Replace muffler No. 1 or muffler No. 2 as necessary.
  - Possible Cause: Exhaust components rubbing on vehicle chassis.
  - Correction: Realign and/or replace damaged components.

noti_6f_p476_0.text: NOTICE: If either muffler one or two needs to be replaced, replace the whole assembly.

para_6f_p476_0.text:
For removal and installation procedures of exhaust manifold and center pipe, refer to ENGINE MECHANICAL (Section 6A).

Be sure to hoist vehicle whenever the muffler assembly, center pipe, or any part used to mount or connect them is being replaced.

noti_6f_p476_1.text: NOTICE: As muffler center pipe has the catalytic converter in it, it should not be exposed to any impulse. Be careful not to drop it or hit it against something.

proc_6f_p476_0.text:
When exhaust manifold is removed
1. Inspect: Gaskets and seal for deterioration or damage. Replace them as necessary.
2. Tighten: Bolts and nuts to specified torques.
steps: Inspect: Gaskets and seal for deterioration or damage. Replace them as necessary.

para_7a_p477_0.text:
The automatic transaxle consists of the hydraulic torque converter, electronically controlled 3-speed automatic transaxle, countershaft and differential.

The transaxle consists of 2 planetary gears, 2 disc clutches, 1 band brake, 1 disc brake and 1 one way clutch. Its operation is controlled by selecting a position from 6 positions ("P," "R," "N," "D," "2" and "L") manually by means of the selector lever mounted on the compartment floor.

In the "D" range, the gear ratios are changed from 1st to 2nd and 2nd to 3rd automatically by electronic control. In the "2" position, the transaxle automatically changes the gear ratios from 1st to 2nd by the same electronic controls.

The automatic transaxle brake interlock system is designed to prevent the selector lever from being shifted out of the "P" position unless the ignition switch is turned to "ON" and the brake pedal is depressed. The system also prevents the ignition key from being removed unless the selector lever is in "P."

When the selector lever is in any position other than "P," the lock lever prevents the ignition key cylinder from being turned to the "LOCK" position which keeps the steering unlocked. When the selector lever is shifted to "P," the lock lever is released, which allows the ignition key to be removed and the steering to lock.

The selector lever is held in the "P" position unless the ignition switch is turned to "ON" and the brake pedal is depressed. When both of these conditions are satisfied, an electronic solenoid retracts the lock plate and the selector lever is allowed to move from the "P" position.

The selector assembly is also equipped with a manual release device. Pulling the release knob will release the selector lever without having the ignition switch in the "ON" position or depressing the brake pedal.

fig_7a_p477_0.text:
Figure 7A-1 Oil Level Gage and Oil Filler Tube
1: OIL LEVEL GAGE
2: RADIATOR HOSE
3: OIL FILLER TUBE

proc_7a_p478_0.text:
Figures 7A-1 and 7A-2
1. Place vehicle on level surface.
2. Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
3. With selector lever in "P" position, start engine. Do not race engine.
4. Run engine at idle and move selector lever through each range and return to the "P" position.
5. With engine running at idle, remove oil level gage from filler and wipe off.
6. Reinsert oil level gage into oil filler tube to its original position.
7. Remove the gage and check the oil level. The level should be between the "FULL HOT" and "LOW HOT" marks. If the level is below the "LOW HOT" mark, add fluid to bring the level to the "FULL HOT" mark. Bringing the fluid level from "LOW HOT" mark to "FULL HOT" mark requires 0.3 liters (0.70/0.58 US/Imp pt) of fluid. Use DEXTRON II or equivalent automatic transmission fluid.
steps: Place vehicle on level surface.
Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
With selector lever in "P" position, start engine. Do not race engine.
Run engine at idle and move selector lever through each range and return to the "P" position.
With engine running at idle, remove oil level gage from filler and wipe off.
Reinsert oil level gage into oil filler tube to its original position.

Remove the gage and check the oil level. The level should be between the "FULL HOT" and "LOW HOT" marks. If the level is below the "LOW HOT" mark, add fluid to bring the level to the "FULL HOT" mark. Bringing the fluid level from "LOW HOT" mark to "FULL HOT" mark requires 0.3 liters (0.70/0.58 US/Imp pt) of fluid. Use DEXTRON II or equivalent automatic transmission fluid.

noti_7a_p478_0.text: NOTICE: Do not overfill. Overfilling may cause foaming and loss of fluid through the vent. Transaxle slippage and mechanical failure may result.

fig_7a_p478_0.text:
Figure 7A-2 Fluid Level Marks
1: OIL LEVEL GAUGE
2: "FULL HOT" MARK
3: "LOW HOT" MARK
4: "FULL COLD" MARK
5: "LOW COLD" MARK

proc_7a_p478_1.text:
Figures 7A-1 and 7A-2
1. Place vehicle on level surface.
2. Apply parking brake and block wheels.
3. With selector level in "P" position, start engine and run it at idle for 5 minutes. Do not race engine.
4. Move selector lever through each range and place the lever in "P" position again.
5. With engine running at idle, check fluid level on oil level gage. Fluid level should be between "FULL COLD" and "LOW COLD" marks on the oil level gage.
6. If level is below "LOW COLD" mark, add fluid to bring the level between "LOW COLD" and "FULL COLD" marks. Use DEXTRON II or equivalent automatic transmission fluid. Do not overfill.
7. The above COLD level check is strictly temporary. Therefore, as a final check, check fluid level for proper level through HOT level check at normal operating temperature. Refer to "Checking Transaxle Fluid (Transaxle at Normal Operating Temperature)" earlier in this section.
steps: Place vehicle on level surface.
Apply parking brake and block wheels.
With selector level in "P" position, start engine and run it at idle for 5 minutes. Do not race engine.
Move selector lever through each range and place the lever in "P" position again.
With engine running at idle, check fluid level on oil level gage. Fluid level should be between "FULL COLD" and "LOW COLD" marks on the oil level gage.
If level is below "LOW COLD" mark, add fluid to bring the level between "LOW COLD" and "FULL COLD" marks. Use DEXTRON II or equivalent automatic transmission fluid. Do not overfill.

The above COLD level check is strictly temporary. Therefore, as a final check, check fluid level for proper level through HOT level check at normal operating temperature. Refer to "Checking Transaxle Fluid (Transaxle at Normal Operating Temperature)" earlier in this section.

para_7a_p478_0.text: Under normal driving conditions, change transaxle fluid every 100,000 km (160,000 miles). Change fluid every 15,000 miles (25,000 km) if the vehicle is usually driven under one or more of the following conditions which are considered severe transaxle service.

proc_7a_p478_2.text:
1. In heavy city traffic where outside temperature regularly reaches 32° C (90° F).
2. In very hilly or mountainous areas.
3. Commercial use, such as taxi, police vehicle or delivery service.
steps: In heavy city traffic where outside temperature regularly reaches 32° C (90° F).
In very hilly or mountainous areas.
Commercial use, such as taxi, police vehicle or delivery service.

proc_7a_p478_3.text:
Fluid Change Procedure
- Raise and suitably support vehicle. Refer to GENERAL INFORMATION (SEC.0A).
- With transaxle cool, remove drain plug and drain fluid.
- Install drain plug gasket and drain plug to oil pan, and tighten to 23 N·m (16.5 lb.ft.). Lower vehicle.
- Remove oil level gage from oil filler tube and add 1.5 liters (3.16/2.64 US/Imp. pt) of new fluid through oil filler tube. Use DEXTRON II or equivalent transmission fluid.
When refilling transaxle with fluid after it is overhauled, add about 3.5 liters (about 3.7/3.1 US/Imp. qt) assuming that torque converter is reused.
5. Check fluid level with transaxle at room temperature and at normal operating temperature. Refer to "Checking Fluid Level" earlier in this section.
steps: Raise and suitably support vehicle. Refer to GENERAL INFORMATION (SEC.0A).
With transaxle cool, remove drain plug and drain fluid.
Install drain plug gasket and drain plug to oil pan, and tighten to 23 N·m (16.5 lb.ft.). Lower vehicle.

Remove oil level gage from oil filler tube and add 1.5 liters (3.16/2.64 US/Imp. pt) of new fluid through oil filler tube. Use DEXTRON II or equivalent transmission fluid.
When refilling transaxle with fluid after it is overhauled, add about 3.5 liters (about 3.7/3.1 US/Imp. qt) assuming that torque converter is reused.
5. Check fluid level with transaxle at room temperature and at normal operating temperature. Refer to "Checking Fluid Level" earlier in this section.

para_7a_p479_0.text:
When filling dry torque converter and transaxle assembly with fluid, add about 4.9 liters (about 5.2/4.3 US/Imp.qt) of DEXTRON II transaxle fluid to the transaxle.

Check the transaxle (including oil hoses) for fluid leakage each time the vehicle is on a hoist. Inspect the oil hoses (inlet and outlet) for loose connections, deterioration or damage. If there is deterioration or damage, replace hoses.

para_7a_p479_1.text: In a major transaxle failure, where particles of metal have been carried with the oil throughout the units of the transaxle, it will be necessary to flush out the oil cooler and connecting lines. To flush the oil cooler and lines, use the following procedure:

proc_7a_p479_0.text:
COOLING LINE FLUSHING
1. Disconnect both cooler lines from the transaxle.
2. Place a hose over the end of the cooler inlet line (from the bottom of the cooler) and insert the hose into an empty container.
3. Flush clean oleum solvent or equivalent through the return line (from the top of the cooler) using an oil suction gun until clean solvent comes out of the hose. This will "back flush" the cooler.
4. Remove the hose from the inlet cooler line and place it on the return line.
5. Flush clean oleum solvent or equivalent through the inlet line until clean solvent comes out of the return line. Remove remaining solvent from cooler with compressed air applied to the return line and flush with transmission fluid.
6. Reconnect oil cooler lines.
steps: 1. Disconnect both cooler lines from the transaxle.
2. Place a hose over the end of the cooler inlet line (from the bottom of the cooler) and insert the hose into an empty container.
3. Flush clean oleum solvent or equivalent through the return line (from the top of the cooler) using an oil suction gun until clean solvent comes out of the hose. This will "back flush" the cooler.
4. Remove the hose from the inlet cooler line and place it on the return line.

5. Flush clean oleum solvent or equivalent through the inlet line until clean solvent comes out of the return line. Remove remaining solvent from cooler with compressed air applied to the return line and flush with transmission fluid.
6. Reconnect oil cooler lines.

para_7a_p479_2.text: If the transaxle oil cooler, located in the radiator assembly, has developed a leak allowing engine coolant to enter the transaxle, use the following procedure:

proc_7a_p479_1.text:
ENGINE COOLANT IN TRANSAXLE
1. Remove transaxle from vehicle. Refer to "Transaxle Assembly" later in this section.
2. Disassemble transaxle and replace all rubber type seals. (The coolant will attack the seal material causing leakage.) Refer to AUTOMATIC TRANSAXLE (SEC.7A1).
3. Replace the composition-faced clutch plate assemblies. (The facing material may become separated from the steel portion.)
4. Replace the nylon washers, speedometers gears and governor gear. (The nylon can swell and become damaged.)
5. Flush the converter, including T.C.C. types.
6. Thoroughly clean and rebuild transaxle, using new gaskets and oil filter.
7. Flush the cooler lines after the transaxle cooler has been properly repaired or replaced.
steps: 1. Remove transaxle from vehicle. Refer to "Transaxle Assembly" later in this section.
2. Disassemble transaxle and replace all rubber type seals. (The coolant will attack the seal material causing leakage.) Refer to AUTOMATIC TRANSAXLE (SEC.7A1).
3. Replace the composition-faced clutch plate assemblies. (The facing material may become separated from the steel portion.)
4. Replace the nylon washers, speedometers gears and governor gear. (The nylon can swell and become damaged.)
5. Flush the converter, including T.C.C. types.
6. Thoroughly clean and rebuild transaxle, using new gaskets and oil filter.
7. Flush the cooler lines after the transaxle cooler has been properly repaired or replaced.

impo_7a_p490_0.text:
IMPORTANT: Upon completion of neutral safety switch installation, perform the following steps to ensure proper operation.
a. Apply parking brake and block wheels.
b. With selector lever shifted to "P" position, turn ignition switch to "START" and check that the starter motor operates.
c. Shift selector lever from "P" to "N" range, turn ignition switch to "START" and check that the starter motor operates.
d. Shift selector lever from "N" to "L" and then back to "N" position, turn ignition switch to "START" and check that the starter motor operates.
e. Shift selector lever from "N" to "P" range and check starter motor for operation as in step d.
f. Check to make sure that in any other range than "P" and "N," starter motor does not operate when ignition switch is turned to "START."
g. Turn ignition switch "ON" and shift selector lever to "R" position and check backup lamp operation.
