proc_7a_p491_1.text:
Remove or Disconnect
- 1a. Apply grease around the cup at center of torque converter (Figure 7A-37).
- 1b. Measure the distance A shown in Figure 7A-37. The distance should be more than 21.3 mm (0.85 in.). If it is less than 21.4 mm (0.85 in.), the torque converter is improperly installed. Remove the torque converter and reinstall it correctly.
2. When installing transaxle, guide the right drive axle into differential side gear as the transaxle is being raised.
3. After inserting the inboard joints of (RH) and (LH) drive axles into differential side gears, push the inboard joints into side gears until the snap rings on drive axles engage side gears. Refer to DRIVE AXLE & AXLE SHAFT (SEC.4D).
4. After connecting oil pressure control cable to accelerator cable, check oil pressure control cable play and adjust if necessary. Refer to "Oil Pressure Control Cable" earlier in this section.
5. Install select cable. Refer to "Select Cable" procedure earlier in this section.
6. Refill transaxle and check fluid level. Refer to "Fluid Change" procedure earlier in this section.

para_7a1_p493_0.text:
The automatic transaxle consists of the hydraulic torque converter, electronically controlled 3-speed automatic transaxle, countershaft and differential.

The transaxle consists of 2 planetary gears, 2 disc clutches, 1 band brake, 1 disc brake and 1 one-way clutch. Its operation is controlled by selecting a position from 6 positions ("P", "R", "N", "D", "2" and "L") manually by means of the selector lever installed in the passenger compartment.

In the "D" or "2" range, the gear ratio is changed for the 1st, 2nd or 3rd speed ("D" range only) automatically by electronic control.

Figure 7A1-1 Automatic Transaxle Construction

2: TORQUE CONVERTER

11: ONE-WAY CLUTCH
12: FORWARD CLUTCH
13: COUNTER SHAFT
14: DIRECT CLUTCH
15: OIL PUMP
16: DIFFERENTIAL

para_7a1_p495_0.text:
The torque converter is a 3-element hydraulic type and consists of the pump, turbine and stator in a unit incapable of disassembly. The pump is mounted to the crankshaft, the turbine to the input shaft and the stator to the transaxle case by way of the one-way clutch.

The torque converter, which increases torque when starting, accelerating and up-hill driving, functions as a fluid clutch while driving at a constant speed.

fig_7a1_p495_0.text:
Figure 7A1-2 Torque Converter Cross Section
1: PUMP
2: TURBINE
3: STATOR
4: ONE-WAY CLUTCH

para_7a1_p496_0.text:
In this unit, the sun gear is mounted in its center and engaged with it are 4 pinion gears supported by a carrier. Then, the outer ring gear is engaged with them. Depending on gear combinations, revolution is changed in speed or direction.
Among the units which operate in connection with the planetary gear unit, there are a direct clutch, a second brake, a forward clutch, a one-way clutch and a 1st-reverse brake.

fig_7a1_p496_0.text:
Figure 7A1-3 Planetary Gear Set Cross Section
1: INPUT SHAFT
2: SECOND BRAKE BAND
3: DIRECT CLUTCH
4: FORWARD CLUTCH
5: FRONT PLANETARY GEAR
6: SUN GEAR
7: FRONT RING GEAR
8: ONE-WAY CLUTCH
9: REAR PLANETARY GEAR
10: REAR RING GEAR
11: 1ST REVERSE BRAKE
12: OUTPUT SHAFT

para_7a1_p497_0.text: The direct clutch connects the input shaft and the sun gear (common to both front and rear planetary gear units). It operates at the 3rd gear in the “D” range and in the “R” range. At the same time, the forward clutch operates in the former and the 1st-reverse brake in the later.

fig_7a1_p497_0.text:
Figure 7A1-4 Direct Clutch Cross Section
1: INPUT SHAFT
2: DIRECT CLUTCH
3: SUN GEAR
4: OIL PRESSURE
5: FORWARD CLUTCH
6: 1ST REVERSE BRAKE

para_7a1_p498_0.text:
The forward clutch connects the input shaft and ring gear of the front planetary gear. It operates in the ranges other than “P”, “R” and “N”. The units which operate simultaneously with the forward clutch are as follows:
• One-way clutch at the first gear in “D” or “2” range.
• Second brake at the 2nd gear in “D” or “2” range.
• Direct clutch at the 3rd gear in “D” range.
• 1st-reverse brake at the 1st gear in “L” range.

fig_7a1_p498_0.text:
Figure 7A1-5 Forward Clutch Cross Section

para_7a1_p499_0.text: The second brake is of a band type. It holds the sun gear of the front and rear planetary gear units stationary. It operates at the 2nd gear in "D" or "2" range simultaneously with the forward clutch.

fig_7a1_p499_0.text:

para_7a1_p500_0.text: The 1st-reverse brake holds the rear planetary carrier stationary. It operates in “L” or “R” range. At the same time, the forward clutch operates in “L” range and the direct clutch in “R” range.

fig_7a1_p500_0.text:

para_7a1_p500_1.text: The sprag type one-way clutch is used between the rear planetary carrier and the transmission case. It consists of a one-way clutch race, sprags and a retainer, and the one-way clutch race is mounted to the transaxle case. When the rear planetary carrier is in motion to turn to the arrow mark side at the first gear in “D” or “2” range, the sprags stick between carrier and race, and prevent the planetary carrier from turning reversely (to arrow mark side).

fig_7a1_p500_1.text:
Figure 7A1-7 First and Reverse Brake Cross Section and One-Way Clutch
1: SPRAG
2: ONE-WAY CLUTCH RACE (FIXED TO THE TRANS. CASE)
3: REAR PLANETARY CARRIER

para_7a1_p501_0.text:
As oil pressure is applied to the forward clutch, the input shaft and the front planetary ring gear are engaged. This allows the clockwise revolution of the input shaft to be transmitted directly to the ring gear and then through the front planetary carrier to the output shaft.
On the other hand, the sun gear which is engaged with the pinion gears of the front planetary gear turns counterclockwise. As this revolution is transmitted to the pinions of the rear planetary gear, the rear planetary carrier would also turn counterclockwise. However, being held by the one-way clutch, it cannot turn. Consequently, the pinions turn clockwise on their axes and this causes the ring gear of the rear planetary gear and output shaft to turn clockwise.

fig_7a1_p501_0.text:
Figure 7A1-8 Power Flow of 1st Gear in "D" or "2" Range (1) and (2)
1: FORWARD CLUTCH
2: ONE-WAY CLUTCH
3: INPUT SHAFT
4: OUTPUT SHAFT
5: COUNTER SHAFT
6: DIFFERENTIAL
7: TORQUE CONVERTER

fig_7a1_p501_1.text:
1: FORWARD CLUTCH
2: ONE-WAY CLUTCH
3: INPUT SHAFT
4: OUTPUT SHAFT
5: COUNTER SHAFT
6: FRONT PLANETARY RING GEAR
7: REAR PLANETARY RING GEAR
8: PINION OF REAR PLANETARY GEAR
9: SUN GEAR

para_7a1_p502_0.text: As oil pressure is applied to the forward clutch, the input shaft and the ring gear of the front planetary gear are engaged. This allows the revolution of the input shaft to be transmitted directly to the ring gear, causing the front planetary carrier to turn clockwise. In this state, the sun gear, being engaged with the pinions of the front planetary gear, would turn counterclockwise. However, the second brake is in operation and prevents it from turning. As a result, the pinions of the front planetary gear turn around the sun gear. Therefore, the front planetary ring gear and then the output shaft turn clockwise.

fig_7a1_p502_1.text:
Figure 7A1-9 Power Flow of 2nd Gear in "D" or "2" Range (1) and (2)
1: FORWARD CLUTCH
2: SECOND BRAKE
3: INPUT SHAFT
4: FRONT PLANETARY RING GEAR
5: SUN GEAR
6: REAR PLANETARY RING GEAR
7: OUTPUT SHAFT

para_7a1_p503_0.text: As oil pressure is applied to the forward clutch and the direct clutch, the input shaft is engaged with the ring gear and the sun gear of the front planetary gear. In other words, the input shaft is directly connected to the planetary gear unit itself. As a result, the pinions of the planetary gear are locked and thus the input shaft revolution is transmitted directly to the output shaft.

fig_7a1_p503_0.text:
Figure 7A1-10 Power Flow of 3rd Gear in "D" Range (1) and (2)
1: FORWARD CLUTCH
2: DIRECT CLUTCH

fig_7a1_p503_1.text:
1: FORWARD CLUTCH
2: DIRECT CLUTCH
3: INPUT SHAFT
4: FRONT PLANETARY RING GEAR
5: SUN GEAR
6: REAR PLANETARY RING GEAR
7: OUTPUT SHAFT

para_7a1_p504_0.text: The torque flow from the engine is the same as at the 1st gear in “D” or “2” range, but in this range, the 1st-reverse brake is put into operation to prevent the rear planetary carrier from clockwise revolution which occurs when engine brake is applied. In other words, when driving in “D” or “2” range, the counterclockwise revolution of the rear planetary carrier is stopped by means of the one-way clutch, but when the engine brake is applied the torque (clockwise) from the output shaft acts to prevent the one-way clutch from operating and the rear planetary carrier idles. This puts the 1st-reverse brake into operation so as to hold the rear planetary carrier stationary. Thus an engine braking force is provided.

fig_7a1_p504_1.text:
Figure 7A1-11 "L" Range Power Flow (1) and (2)
1: 1ST-REVERSE BRAKE
2: FORWARD CLUTCH
3: REAR PLANETARY RING GEAR
4: OUTPUT SHAFT
5: ONE-WAY CLUTCH
6: FRONT PLANETARY RING GEAR
7: SUN GEAR
8: INPUT SHAFT

para_7a1_p505_0.text: As oil pressure is applied to the direct clutch, the input shaft is engaged with the sun gear of the planetary gear. This allows the torque to be transmitted directly from the input shaft to the sun gear. On the other hand, the 1st-reverse brake is also at work and therefore the rear planetary carrier is held stationary. In this state, the pinions do not turn around the sun gear but turn around counterclockwise on their axes and cause the ring gear to turn counterclockwise. As the output shaft is spline fitted to the rear planetary ring gear, it also turns counterclockwise, thereby the car moves rearward.

fig_7a1_p505_1.text:
1: DIRECT CLUTCH
2: 1ST-REVERSE BRAKE
3: INPUT SHAFT
4: SUN GEAR
5: REAR PLANETARY RING GEAR
6: OUTPUT SHAFT
7: FRONT PLANETARY RING GEAR

para_7a1_p506_0.text: As the forward clutch and direct clutch are released, the engine torque is not transmitted from the input shaft to the output shaft. In “P” range, the parking lock pawl is engaged with the parking lock gear which is incorporated with the drive gear of the output shaft and thus the front wheels are mechanically locked.

para_7a1_p507_0.text: The oil pressure control system fundamentally consists of three parts: (1) oil pressure producing system which consists of an oil pump to produce oil pressure and regulator valve to regulate the pressure, (2) oil circuit to feed oil into the torque converter, the oil cooler and the transmission components to be lubricated, and (3) control system to shift the gear of the planetary gear unit by acting on each clutch and brake.

para_7a1_p508_0.text:
The valve body is found in the oil pan and has valves to control oil pressure to the various clutches and bands. In the valve body, oil passages connect the valves.

The oil pump is of the internal gear type. It feeds oil to the torque converter, lubricates each part of the transmission and delivers oil pressure to operate each clutch and brake solenoid. The oil pump is driven by the converter hub.

The manual valve is directly connected with the selector lever by a cable. It opens and closes the oil passage mechanically so as to form respective oil pressure circuits for "L", "2", "D", "N", "R", or "P" range according to the selector lever movement.

As the throttle valve is linked with the accelerator pedal by cable, it produces the throttle pressure corresponding to the extent the accelerator pedal is depressed which is directly related to the engine output.

When the accelerator pedal is depressed, the throttle cam pushes the shift plug which then compresses two springs to move the throttle valve. Thus the line pressure passage is opened and the throttle pressure is produced. The throttle pressure is also applied to the back of the throttle valve to push back the valve. The throttle pressure is determined by the spring pressure of the springs (position of the shift plug) and applied to the primary and secondary regulator valves to regulate the line pressure.

para_7a1_p509_0.text:
This valve regulates the oil pressure to the torque converter and lubricating oil pressure to each part of the transmission by means of the throttle pressure and spring.

When in "L" range, this valve controls the line pressure acting on the 1st-reverse brake to reduce a shock.

This valve is provided to keep the oil pressure in the torque converter constant.

This valve carries out the gear shift between 1st and 2nd gears. When the 2nd brake solenoid operates, the line pressure is applied to the shift valve and the valve moves to allow the line pressure to be applied to the 2nd brake and thus the transmission is shifted into 2nd gear.

In "L" or "R" range, the oil pressure is applied to the 1st-reverse brake when the 2nd brake solenoid operates.

para_7a1_p510_1.text:
This valve carries out the gear shift between 2nd and 3rd gears. When the direct clutch solenoid operates, the line pressure is applied to the shift valve and the valve moves to allow the line pressure to be applied to the direct clutch and thus the transmission is shifted from the 2nd gear to the 3rd gear.

When the oil pressure to the solenoid is relieved, the shift valve returns by means of the spring force and the transmission is shifted to the 2nd gear.

para_7a1_p510_2.text: This serves to reduce a shock in each gear shift. There are two accumulators, one for the forward clutch and the other for the 2nd brake.

para_7a1_p510_3.text:
These solenoids turn ON and OFF by the electric signals from the controller (computer). They serve to control the gear shift by operating the 1 - 2 and 2 - 3 shift valves. Direct clutch solenoid operates the 2 - 3 shift valve, and 2nd brake solenoid operates the 1 - 2
shift valve. When the solenoid valve is ON, the valve moves up to relieve the line pressure and when OFF, the valve moves down and therefore the line pressure is applied to the shift valve.

para_7a1_p511_0.text:
The oil cooler of dual pipe type is built in the lower tank of the engine cooling radiator and cools the automatic transaxle fluid.

GEAR SHIFT CONTROL SYSTEM

The controller controls the 2nd brake solenoid and the direct clutch solenoid by sending electric signals to them so as to attain automatic gear shift between the 1st and 2nd gears, and the 2nd and 3rd gears.

The controller sensed parameters are the throttle position sensor, neutral safety switch, and speed sensor. The switch and sensors sense the carburetor throttle valve opening, selector lever's position and vehicle speed, and send these signals to the controller. Then, the controller opens and closes the valves of the above solenoids according to these signals. The controller is located at the left corner inside the instrument main panel.

The throttle position sensor consisting of an idle switch and a potentiometer is connected to the throttle valve shaft.

Idle signal (ON signal when idling) and throttle valve opening signal (output voltage) are transmitted from TPS to ECM as voltage signal. The signal is converted to ON/OFF informations in ECM, and they are sent to controller through three lines.

Being linked with the selector lever, this switch changes selector lever positions into electric signals and sends them to the controller. The contact points of this switch for "P" and "N" ranges are also connected with the starter motor circuit. When the selector lever is shifted to the "P" or "N" position, the contact points for "P" or "N" range are connected and cause the starter motor to operate by turning the starter switch "ON". When the selector lever is in any other position than "P" and "N", the switch remains "OFF" and therefore the starter motor cannot be operated, that is, the engine cannot be started. Also, as its contact point for "R" range is connected with the backup light circuit and the selector lever is shifted to "R" range, the contact point contacts to light the backup lights.

These solenoids are mounted on the valve body. They are turned ON and OFF by the signals from the controller and actuate each shift valve (1 - 2 and 2 - 3 valves) so as to control transmission gear shift.

The 2nd brake solenoid operates 1 - 2 shift valve, and direct clutch solenoid does 2 - 3 shift valve.

para_7a1_p511_1.text: The speed sensor consists of a magnetic core with magnet and coil. It is mounted on transmission case with 0.6 mm (0.024 in) air gap between the core end and countershaft gear tooth. While the countershaft rotates, magnetic flux is cut by gear tooth. Thus a pulse is generated in the sensor coil according to the speed. And then, the pulse is transmitted to control module as speed signal.

para_7a1_p512_0.text:
When the engine is started and the oil pump starts to operate, oil in the oil pan passes thru the oil pump, is regulated by the primary regulator valve and sent to the torque converter. Also, the oil which is further regulated by the secondary regulator valve lubricates each part of the transmission.

In this range, the oil pressure is not applied to any clutch or brake as the line pressure from the regulator valve is stopped by means of the manual valve.

para_7a1_p513_0.text:
The line pressure which has passed through the manual valve is applied directly to the forward clutch. As this causes the clutch to be engaged, the 1st gear is shifted. The line pressure is also applied to the accumulator to reduce shocks at the time of clutch engagement.

As the direct clutch and the 2nd brake solenoids receive the electric signal from the controller, that is, they are turned ON, they relieve the line pressure. Therefore, neither the 1-2 nor 2-3 shift valve operates due to no oil pressure.

para_7a1_p514_0.text: As the car speed is accelerated at the 1st gear and reaches the 2nd gear shift point, the 2nd brake solenoid turns OFF and closes the relief circuit. This causes line pressure ⑱ to act on the 1-2 shift valve. Then the valve moves to allow the line pressure ⑱ to be applied to the 2nd brake and the 2nd gear is attained. At this time, the line pressure ⑱ is also applied to the accumulator to reduce a shock occurring when the 2nd brake works.

para_7a1_p515_0.text:
As the car speed is further accelerated in the 2nd gear and reaches the 3rd gear shift point, the direct clutch solenoid turns OFF and closes the relief circuit. This causes the line pressure ⑱ to be applied to the 2-3 shift valve. Then the oil pressure is applied to the direct clutch to make it engaged. At the same time, the the oil pressure acts, as a back pressure, on the 2nd brake piston. Thus the 2nd brake piston is pushed back, the 2nd brake is released and the 3rd gear is attained.
In this state, as the 2nd brake solenoid is OFF, the 1-2 shift valve is at work and opens the oil passage to the 2nd brake but the 2nd brake is balanced with the above mentioned back pressure and the return spring force pushes back the piston to prevent the 2nd brake from operating.

In this state, as the 2nd brake solenoid is OFF because it is not electrically charged from the controller, the line pressure ⓟ is applied to the 1-2 shift valve. Then the line pressure is applied to the 1st-reverse brake. Consequently the 1st gear is attained.

para_7a1_p517_0.text: The line pressure ①, after passing thru the manual valve, comes out as the line pressures ⑤ and ⑥. In this state, as the 2nd brake solenoid is OFF, the line pressure ⑤ is applied to the 1-2 shift valve and the line pressure ⑥ is applied to the 1st-reverse brake after passing through the B₂ Control valve. On the other hand, the line pressure ⑥ is applied to the direct clutch via the 2-3 shift valve, thus resulting in the reverse gear. Also, a part of the line pressure ⑥ acts on the primary regulator valve to regulate the line pressure ①.

para_7a1_p520_0.text: Before performing systematic trouble-shooting described in this section, make sure to check each of the following:

proc_7a1_p520_0.text:
Pre-Check Items
1. Engine coolant temperature is at normal operating temperature.
2. Engine idle speed is 800 – 900 rpm.
3. Transaxle fluid level is between "FULL HOT" and "LOW HOT" on oil level gauge at normal operating temperature of transaxle fluid.
4. Accelerator cable, oil pressure control cable and select cable are adjusted properly.
5. Electric circuit of gear shift control system is free from break, coupler disconnection and poor contact.
steps: Engine coolant temperature is at normal operating temperature.
Engine idle speed is 800 – 900 rpm.
Transaxle fluid level is between "FULL HOT" and "LOW HOT" on oil level gauge at normal operating temperature of transaxle fluid.
Accelerator cable, oil pressure control cable and select cable are adjusted properly.
Electric circuit of gear shift control system is free from break, coupler disconnection and poor contact.

fig_7a1_p520_0.text:
Figure 7A1-41 Diagnosis Chart (A)
Gear change failure: Starting point of diagnosis flowchart
Disconnect solenoid wire coupler and perform Manual Road Test: First diagnostic step
Is upshift from 1st to 2nd possible? – No: 1–2 shift valve sticks; 2nd brake solenoid sticks
Is upshift from 1st to 2nd possible? – Yes: Proceed to next question
Is upshift from 2nd to 3rd possible? – No: 2–3 shift valve sticks; Direct clutch solenoid sticks
Is upshift from 2nd to 3rd possible? – Yes: Gear shift control system defective

fig_7a1_p521_0.text:
Figure 7A1-42 Diagnosis Chart (B)
No starting or slipping in any drive range: Entry point: symptom of no starting or slipping in any drive range
Is engine rpm at stall normal?: Decision: check engine rpm at stall. Yes → Transaxle failure. No → branch to sub-conditions
Too high in any range: Sub-condition branch 1: engine rpm too high in any range → Is line pressure proper? Yes → Transaxle failure. No → Oil pump defective / Regulator valve operation failure / Throttle valve operation failure / Accelerator cable maladjusted
Too high only in R range: Sub-condition branch 2: engine rpm too high only in R range → Does engine rpm at stall become normal when coupler of solenoid wire is disconnected? Yes → 1st-Reverse brake worn. No → Is line pressure proper? Yes → Direct clutch worn. No → Oil leak in direct clutch oil circuit / Oil leak in R range oil circuit
Too high only in D range: Sub-condition branch 3: engine rpm too high only in D range → Does engine rpm at stall become normal when coupler of solenoid wire is disconnected? Yes → One-way clutch operation failure. No → Is line pressure proper? Yes → Forward clutch worn. No → Oil leak in forward clutch oil circuit / Oil leak in D range oil circuit
Too low in any range: Sub-condition branch 4: engine rpm too low in any range → Poor engine power / Torque converter defective

fig_7a1_p522_0.text:
Figure 7A1-43 Diagnosis Chart (C)
root: Too large shock at range selection or gear change
decision_1: Is line pressure proper?
no_branch: • Regulator valve defective
• Throttle valve defective
• Accelerator cable maladjusted
yes_branch_1: Too large shock when selecting "R" range from "N" range → • 1st-Reverse brake worn
• 1st-Reverse brake oil circuit check ball operation failure
yes_branch_2: Too large shock at 2nd to 3rd gear change → • Direct clutch worn
• Direct clutch oil circuit check ball operation failure
yes_branch_3: Too large shock at 1st to 2nd gear change → • 2nd brake accumulator defective
• 2nd brake worn
• 2nd brake oil circuit check ball operation failure
yes_branch_4: Too large shock when selecting "D" range from "N" range → • Forward clutch accumulator operation failure
• Forward clutch worn
• Forward clutch oil circuit check ball operation failure

para_7a1_p523_0.text:
This test is used to check the oil pressure system for operation by measuring the oil pressure line. Make sure to perform the test only when transaxle fluid is at normal operating temperature. Also, check for the following before the test.
• Transaxle fluid level is between "FULL HOT" and "LOW HOT" marks on oil level gauge (at normal operating temperature of fluid).
• Transaxle is free of oil leakage.
Test procedure is as follows.

proc_7a1_p523_0.text:
LINE PRESSURE TEST Procedure
1. With the engine off, remove plug as shown in Figure 7A1-44 and connect oil pressure gage to the threaded hole.
2. Install tachometer.
3. Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
4. With selector lever shifted to "P" position, start the engine.
5. Depress brake pedal fully.
6. Shift selector lever to "D" range and check oil pressure with engine running at 800-900 rpm and at 2,200-2,700 rpm respectively.
7. Repeat the same check as in step 6 with selector lever shifted to "R" range.
8. If the line pressure is within the specifications found in Figure 7A1-45, the oil pressure system is in good condition.
9. Possible causes for out-of-specification line pressure can be found in Figure 7A1-46. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.
steps: With the engine off, remove plug as shown in Figure 7A1-44 and connect oil pressure gage to the threaded hole.
Install tachometer.
Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
With selector lever shifted to "P" position, start the engine.
Depress brake pedal fully.
Shift selector lever to "D" range and check oil pressure with engine running at 800-900 rpm and at 2,200-2,700 rpm respectively.
Repeat the same check as in step 6 with selector lever shifted to "R" range.
If the line pressure is within the specifications found in Figure 7A1-45, the oil pressure system is in good condition.
Possible causes for out-of-specification line pressure can be found in Figure 7A1-46. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.

noti_7a1_p523_1.text: NOTICE: Be sure to perform the test only when transaxle fluid is at normal operating temperature. Don't run engine at stall for more than 5 seconds continuously, for oil temperature may rise excessively high.

para_7a1_p523_1.text: This test is to check the overall performance of the automatic transaxle and engine by measuring stall speed at "D" and "R" ranges.

proc_7a1_p523_1.text:
STALL TEST - Test Procedure
1. Install tachometer.
2. Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
3. Start engine with selector lever shifted to "P" range.
4. Depress brake pedal.
5. Shift selector lever to "D" range and depress accelerator pedal fully while watching tachometer. Read quickly the engine rpm when it has become constant (stall speed). Release accelerator pedal immediately after stall speed is checked.
6. In the same way, check stall speed at "R" range.
7. Stall speed should be within 2,200-2,700 rpm.
8. Possible causes for out-of-specification stall speed are shown in Figure 7A1-47. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.
steps: Install tachometer.
Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
Start engine with selector lever shifted to "P" range.
Depress brake pedal.

Shift selector lever to "D" range and depress accelerator pedal fully while watching tachometer. Read quickly the engine rpm when it has become constant (stall speed). Release accelerator pedal immediately after stall speed is checked.
In the same way, check stall speed at "R" range.
Stall speed should be within 2,200-2,700 rpm.
Possible causes for out-of-specification stall speed are shown in Figure 7A1-47. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.

para_7a1_p523_2.text: This test is to check if upshifts and downshifts take place at specified speeds while actually driving the vehicle.

proc_7a1_p523_2.text:
ROAD TEST - Test Procedure
- Warm up engine.
- With engine running at idle, shift selector lever to "D" range.
- Accelerate vehicle speed by depressing accelerator pedal very little.
- Check if upshift takes place from 1st to 2nd at 14 km/h (9 mile/h) and from 2nd to 3rd at 33 km/h (21 mile/h).
- Stop vehicle.
- Accelerate by depressing accelerator pedal fully. Then, check if upshift takes place from 1st to 2nd at 52 km/h (32 mile/h) and from 2nd to 3rd at 97 km/h (60 mile/h).
- Stop vehicle again.
8. Start vehicle and keep it running at 30 km/h (18 mile/h) and then release accelerator pedal all the way back. 1 or 2 seconds later, depress accelerator pedal fully and check if downshift from 2nd to 1st takes place.
9. Keep vehicle running at 75 km/h (47 mile/h) and in the same way as in step 10, check if downshift from 3rd to 2nd takes place.
10. If upshift or downshift fails to take place at each specified speed in the road test, possible causes for such failure are shown in Figure 7A1-49. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.
steps: Warm up engine.
With engine running at idle, shift selector lever to "D" range.
Accelerate vehicle speed by depressing accelerator pedal very little.
Check if upshift takes place from 1st to 2nd at 14 km/h (9 mile/h) and from 2nd to 3rd at 33 km/h (21 mile/h).
Stop vehicle.
Accelerate by depressing accelerator pedal fully. Then, check if upshift takes place from 1st to 2nd at 52 km/h (32 mile/h) and from 2nd to 3rd at 97 km/h (60 mile/h).
Stop vehicle again.

8. Start vehicle and keep it running at 30 km/h (18 mile/h) and then release accelerator pedal all the way back. 1 or 2 seconds later, depress accelerator pedal fully and check if downshift from 2nd to 1st takes place.
9. Keep vehicle running at 75 km/h (47 mile/h) and in the same way as in step 10, check if downshift from 3rd to 2nd takes place.

10. If upshift or downshift fails to take place at each specified speed in the road test, possible causes for such failure are shown in Figure 7A1-49. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.

para_7a1_p524_0.text: This test checks the gears being used in "L", "2" or "D" range when driven with unoperated gear shift control system.

proc_7a1_p524_0.text:
Test Procedure
1. With selector lever in "P" range, start engine and warm it up.
2. After warming up engine, disconnect the coupler of solenoid wire as shown in Figure 7A1-49.
3. With selector lever in "L" range, start the vehicle and accelerate to 30 km/h (19 mile/h). Check in this state that 1st gear is being used.
4. At 30 km/h (19 mile/h), shift selector lever to "2" range and accelerate to 60 km/h (37 mile/h). Check in this state that 2nd gear is being used.
5. At 60 km/h (37 mile/h), shift selector lever to "D" range and check that 3rd gear is used when speed is higher than 60 km/h (37 mile/h).
6. After above checks, stop vehicle, turn off engine and connect solenoid wire coupler.
steps: 1. With selector lever in "P" range, start engine and warm it up.
2. After warming up engine, disconnect the coupler of solenoid wire as shown in Figure 7A1-49.
3. With selector lever in "L" range, start the vehicle and accelerate to 30 km/h (19 mile/h). Check in this state that 1st gear is being used.
4. At 30 km/h (19 mile/h), shift selector lever to "2" range and accelerate to 60 km/h (37 mile/h). Check in this state that 2nd gear is being used.
5. At 60 km/h (37 mile/h), shift selector lever to "D" range and check that 3rd gear is used when speed is higher than 60 km/h (37 mile/h).
6. After above checks, stop vehicle, turn off engine and connect solenoid wire coupler.

3. If engine brake fails to operate in the above tests, possible causes for such failure can be found in Figure 7A1-50. Check each part which is suspected to be the cause.

Test Procedure
1. Stop the vehicle on a slope, shift selector lever to "P" range and at the same time apply parking brake.
2. After stopping engine, release parking brake lever gradually and check to see that vehicle remains stationary.

para_7a1_p525_0.text:
Before performing the systematic trouble shooting described in this section, make sure to check each of the following.
1. Electric circuit of gearshift control system is free from a break, coupler disconnection and poor contact.
2. Each hose of vacuum switches is securely connected.

proc_7a1_p525_2.text:
GEAR SHIFT CONTROL SYSTEM
1. Turn ignition switch "OFF".
2. After turning ignition switch "OFF", disconnect controller coupler.

para_7a1_p525_1.text: Place selector lever in "P", "R", "N", "D", "2" or "L" range and in each range check for continuity between the terminals of the disconnected controller coupler (on wiring harness side) respectively, see Figure 7A1-68.

proc_7a1_p525_3.text:
Neutral Safety Switch Unit Check
1. Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.
2. Shift selector lever as described in the following steps. With selector lever in "P" or "N" range, depending on each step, turn starter switch "ON" and check that the starter motor operates.
a. Shift selector lever to "P" range and turn ignition switch "ON".
b. Shift selector lever from "P" to "N" and turn ignition switch "ON".
c. Shift selector lever from "N" to "L", then back to "N" again and turn ignition switch "ON".
d. Shift selector lever from "N" back to "P" and turn ignition switch "ON".
3. If found faulty in any one of a, b, c or d checks in the above step, 2 nuts of manual shift lever may possibly be loose. Retighten both nuts and then perform checks in step 2 again.
4. After checks in step 2, shift selector lever to "D", "2", "L" and "R" one after another. In each of these ranges, turn ignition switch "ON" and check that this doesn't cause starter motor to operate.
steps: Apply parking brake and block vehicle wheels.

Shift selector lever as described in the following steps. With selector lever in "P" or "N" range, depending on each step, turn starter switch "ON" and check that the starter motor operates.
a. Shift selector lever to "P" range and turn ignition switch "ON".
b. Shift selector lever from "P" to "N" and turn ignition switch "ON".
c. Shift selector lever from "N" to "L", then back to "N" again and turn ignition switch "ON".
d. Shift selector lever from "N" back to "P" and turn ignition switch "ON".
If found faulty in any one of a, b, c or d checks in the above step, 2 nuts of manual shift lever may possibly be loose. Retighten both nuts and then perform checks in step 2 again.
After checks in step 2, shift selector lever to "D", "2", "L" and "R" one after another. In each of these ranges, turn ignition switch "ON" and check that this doesn't cause starter motor to operate.

proc_7a1_p527_0.text:
Trouble Shooting: Car Does Not Move Backward in "R" Range
1. Perform "A-4" check. If Not OK: Neutral safety switch defective.
2. If OK, perform solenoid check "C". If Not OK: Direct clutch solenoid or 2nd brake solenoid defective.
3. If OK: Controller defective.
5. If failure is not corrected even after retightening 2 nuts as described in step 3 or found faulty in step 4, loosen neutral safety switch mounting bolt and then, with selector lever shifted to "N" range, move neutral safety switch by hand as shown in Figure 7A1-67. Stop the neutral safety switch at the position where a "Click" is heard from the switch, and then, retighten the mounting bolt. Be sure to perform checks in steps 2 and 4 after retightening mounting bolt.
6. If faulty condition still exists even after adjusting neutral safety switch position as described in step 5, there may possibly be a poor contact within neutral safety switch.

proc_7a1_p531_1.text:
Solenoid Unit Check
1. Drain fluid from transaxle.
2. Remove transaxle oil pan.
3. Disconnect solenoid couplers at each solenoid.
4. Connect ohmmeter between solenoid terminal and solenoid body and measure solenoid resistance. If about 13 ohms resistance exists, solenoid is in good condition.
5. Connect positive terminal of 12V battery to solenoid terminal and negative terminal to solenoid body. Check that a "click" of valve operation is heard from solenoid when connection is made (Figure 7A1-63).
6. If found faulty in step 4 or 5, replace solenoid.
7. After checking, connect coupler to solenoid.
8. After installing oil pan, refill transaxle.

If found faulty in step 4 or 5, replace solenoid.

After installing oil pan, refill transaxle.

proc_7a1_p532_0.text:
"C" CHECK PROCEDURE
1. Direct clutch solenoid and 2nd brake solenoid check.
2. Disconnect solenoid wire coupler under battery.
3. Is a click heard from direct clutch solenoid when 12 voltage is applied to red wire terminal of coupler on transaxle side? If No: Direct clutch solenoid defective. If Yes: proceed.
4. Is a click heard from 2nd brake solenoid when 12 voltage is applied to yellow wire terminal of coupler? If No: 2nd brake solenoid defective. If Yes: Direct clutch and 2nd brake solenoids in good condition.

noti_7a1_p536_0.text: NOTICE: Before overhauling the transaxle, clean it thoroughly to protect its inner parts against dust and dirt during overhaul. When placing the removed transaxle on a work table, be sure not to damage the outside parts of the transaxle. Use special care for gasket removal. Gasket must be removed completely and gasket surface must not be damaged.

para_7a1_p543_0.text: Figure 7A1-108 shows the component groups. The following procedures describe disassembly, assembly, checking and adjustment procedures of each component group shown.

para_7a1_p543_1.text: Note that parts of the same types are used among some component groups. For example, discs, clutch plates and seals used for different component groups are of the same shapes. If more than two component groups are disassembled at the same time, it may occur that these parts of the same shape but for different component groups are mixed and wrong parts are used for reassembly. Therefore, if it becomes necessary to overhaul more than two component groups, work on one component group at a time. Should it be necessary to overhaul more than two groups at the same time for some reason, make sure to keep parts for each component group separated during disassembly.

proc_7a1_p544_0.text:
Cleaning Instructions
1. Before reassembly, be sure to clean all disassembled parts and apply air into fluid passages and holes to clean them.
2. Use automatic transaxle fluid or kerosene as cleaning solvent.
steps: Before reassembly, be sure to clean all disassembled parts and apply air into fluid passages and holes to clean them.
Use automatic transaxle fluid or kerosene as cleaning solvent.

caut_7a1_p544_0.text: CAUTION: When using compressed air to dry parts, keep your face away to avoid being sprayed with solvent.

proc_7a1_p544_1.text:
Assembly Instructions
1. New brake discs, clutch discs and brake bands that are to be used for replacement must be soaked in automatic transaxle fluid for at least two (2) hours before assembly.
2. Use special care when handling each part component group so as not to damage them.
3. Tighten each bolt and nut to their specified torque.
4. Before installing, be sure to apply automatic transmission fluid to all sliding, rolling and thrusting surfaces of all component parts. Also after installation, make sure to check each part for proper operation.
5. After each gear installation, check to make sure that gear rotates smoothly.
6. When handling bearings, use care to prevent damage to bearings. Apply Automatic Transmission Fluid to all bearings before installation.
7. All gaskets and rubber "O" rings should be replaced.
8. Make sure that snap ring ends are not lined with one of cutouts and are installed in groove correctly.
9. Check thrust bearings and races for wear or damage.
10. When installing bearings, do not apply force to ball or roller. When installing bearing, do not apply excessive force.
11. Be sure to make proper selection of direct and forward clutch flanges, input shaft bearing race and 2nd brake rod and to adjust each to specification.
12. When installing seal ring, be careful so that it is not expanded excessively, extruded or caught.
13. Apply Automatic Transmission Fluid to all "O" ring.

Make sure that snap ring ends are not lined with one of cutouts and are installed in groove correctly.

Be sure to make proper selection of direct and forward clutch flanges, input shaft bearing race and 2nd brake rod and to adjust each to specification.
When installing seal ring, be careful so that it is not expanded excessively, extruded or caught.
Apply Automatic Transmission Fluid to all "O" ring.

Inspect
- Pump body oil seal. Check for wear, damage or cracks. Replace oil seal if necessary.
- Body clearance of driven gear. Push driven gear to one side of body. Using a feeler gauge, measure clearance between driven gear and body. If the clearance exceeds the limit, replace driven gear.
3. Tip clearance of both drive and driven gears. Measure (radial) clearance between gear teeth and crescent. If the clearance exceeds the limit, replace the gear.
4. Side clearance of both gears. Using a steel straightedge (J-34673) and a feeler gauge, measure the side clearance between drive/driven gear and pump body.

para_7a1_p546_0.text: Check direct clutch clearance before disassembly. To check the clearance, measure the height between snap ring and clutch flange by using a Vernier as shown in Figure 7A1-117. If the height is within the specification, it means that the clutch clearance is within the specification. If the height is out of specification, replace clutch discs or plate with new ones.

proc_7a1_p546_1.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Clutch plate snap ring.
2. Clutch flange, discs and plates.
3. Clutch plate snap ring. Compress piston return springs and remove snap ring. Place J-23327 (Clutch Spring Compressor) on the return spring seat and compress springs with a press. Then using a screwdriver, remove snap ring.
4. Spring seat and return spring assembly.
5. Direct clutch piston. Apply compressed air through drum oil hole to remove piston. If piston does not pop out, lift out the piston with needle nose pliers.
6. Inner seal from drum.
7. Outer seal from piston.

3. Piston into drum. Be careful so that seals ("O" ring) do not get twisted or caught.
4. Clutch return spring assembly.
5. Spring seat.
6. Snap ring. Compress return springs and install spring seat snap ring in groove. Place J-23327 and J-25018-A (Clutch Spring Compressor and Adapter) on spring seat and compress springs

para_7a1_p549_0.text: Check forward clutch clearance before disassembly. To check the clearance, measure the height between the snap ring and clutch flange by using A Vernier as shown in Figure 7A1-127. If the height is within the specification, it means that the clutch clearance is within specification. If the height is out of specification, replace clutch discs or plates with new ones.

proc_7a1_p549_2.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Clutch plate snap ring.
2. Flange, discs and plates.
3. Spring seat snap ring. Compress piston return springs and remove snap ring. Place J-23327 and J-25018-A (Clutch Spring Compressor and Adapter) on the spring seat and compress spring with a press, and then, remove snap ring using a screwdriver. Do not push down return spring more than necessary.
4. Spring seat and springs.
5. Forward clutch piston. Apply compressed air through input shaft oil hole to remove piston. If piston does not pop out, lift it out with needle nose pliers.
6. Inner and outer seals ("O" rings) from piston.

2. Flange, discs and plates.

4. Spring seat and springs.
5. Forward clutch piston. Apply compressed air through input shaft oil hole to remove piston. If piston does not pop out, lift it out with needle nose pliers.
6. Inner and outer seals ("O" rings) from piston.

1. Check valve (ball) for free movement in clutch piston.
2. Check valve for leakage by using low pressure air. If found faulty, replace clutch piston.

proc_7a1_p549_4.text:

1. New inner and outer seals ("O" rings) to clutch piston. Apply Automatic Transmission Fluid to seals and fit to piston.
2. Piston into input shaft drum. Use care so that seals do not get twisted or caught.
3. Eighteen (18) piston return springs and spring seat.
4. Spring seat snap ring. Compress return springs and install snap rings in groove by using a screwdriver. Place J-23327 and J-25018-A (Clutch Spring Compressor and Adapter) on spring seat and compress springs with a press (Figure 7A1-129).
5. Install discs, plates and flange. Install them in following order.
   a. Plate
   b. Clutch disc
   c. Plate
   d. Clutch disc
   e. Plate
   f. Clutch disc
   g. Flange
6. Clutch plate snap ring.
7. After installing clutch plate snap ring, measure the height between snap ring and clutch flange as previously outlined. See Figure 7A1-127 for checking forward clutch clearance.    • Thickness 3.00mm (0.118 in.)
   • Thickness 3.37mm (0.132 in.)

para_7a1_p550_1.text: Important Steps in Disassembly and Reassembly of Valve Bodies

proc_7a1_p550_0.text:
Important Steps in Disassembly and Reassembly of Valve Bodies
1. When disassembling valve body, be sure to keep each valve together with its corresponding spring.
2. When removing upper valve body from lower one, be careful not to drop four (4) steel check balls shown in Figure 7A1-134. When assembling, install these four (4) steel balls at the
3. positions in upper valve body as shown in Figure 7A1-134.
3. Replace each gasket with a new one. Make sure that the new gasket is the same as the old one before installation.
4. When installing each valve in the valve body, use special care for proper installing direction.
5. Several throttle valve rings are used at the throttle valve in the upper valve body. Be sure to install the same number of throttle valve rings as those used before disassembly. See Figure 7A1-132 for the throttle valve ring.
6. When installing lower valve body cover and gasket to lower valve body, tighten the lower valve body bolts to specification.
● 4-6 N·m (3-4 ft. lbs.)
7. Tighten throttle valve cam bolt to specification.
● 6-9 N·m (4.5-6.5 ft. lbs.)
8. When installing upper valve body to lower one, install sixteen (16) upper valve body bolts as follows.
a. Install two (2) reamer bolts (positioning bolts) at the positions shown as "D" in Figure 7A1-135. Do not tighten these bolts to specification at this time. Finger-tight only.
b. Install other fourteen (14) bolts as shown in Figure 7A1-135.
c. Tighten four (4) bolts, shown as 2, to specification.
d. Tighten eight (8) bolts, shown as 3, to specification.
e. Tighten two (2) bolts, shown as 4, and two (2) reamer bolts, shown as 1, to specification.

proc_7a1_p554_0.text:
Disassembly
1. Remove the side bearings using puller J-28509-A and plug J-34851 (Figures 7A1-144 and 7A1-145).
2. Remove the speedometer drive gear using puller J-28509-A and plug J-34851.
3. Remove the roll pin retaining the pinion shaft (Figure 7A1-146).
4. Remove the pinion shaft, pinion gears and side gears from the case.
5. Remove the ring gear retaining bolts and then remove ring gears.

proc_7a1_p554_1.text:
Disassembly
1. Install the side washers and then the side gears.
2. Install the pinion washers and pinion gears.
3. Install the pinion gear shaft.
4. Measure the backlash of the side pinions and side gears with a feeler gage as shown in Figure 7A1-147. If the backlash exceeds the specified value, adjust it by varying the thickness of the side gear washers. Refer to Figure 7A1-148 for available side gear washer sizes.
5. Install the pinion shaft roll pin.
6. Install the ring gear and eight (8) retaining bolts as shown in Figure 7A1-149. Torque bolts to specification.
7. Install the speedometer gear as shown in Figure 7A1-150.
8. Install the RH and LH side bearings as shown in Figure 7A1-51.
Install the bearings with the seal side facing the differential case.

proc_7a1_p554_2.text:
General Assembly Instructions
- Automatic transaxle consists of highly precise parts. As even a flaw in a small part may cause oil leakage or decrease in function, check each part carefully before installation.
- Be sure to torque each bolt and nut to specification.
3. Before assembling new clutch discs and brake band, soak them in automatic transmission fluid for at least two (2) hours.
4. Apply automatic transmission fluid on sliding or rotating surfaces of the parts before assembly.
5. Do not use adhesive cements on gaskets and similar parts.
6. When assembling transaxle, be sure to use new gaskets and "O" rings.
7. Clean all parts with compressed air.
8. Be sure to install thrust bearings and races in correct direction and position as shown in Figure 7A1-152.

Be sure to torque each bolt and nut to specification.

5. Do not use adhesive cements on gaskets and similar parts.
6. When assembling transaxle, be sure to use new gaskets and "O" rings.
7. Clean all parts with compressed air.
8. Be sure to install thrust bearings and races in correct direction and position as shown in Figure 7A1-152.

proc_7a1_p555_0.text:
Install or Connect
1. Manual shift shaft and parking lock pawl.
   a. Install lower washer and parking lock rod to manual shift shaft.
   b. Manual shift shaft into transaxle case, and then the manual detent spring. Torque bolt and nut of manual detent spring to 8 - 12 N·m (6.0 - 8.5 lb.ft.).
2. Use care when installing so that manual shift shaft will not damage oil seal lip when passing through it.
3. c. Shift shaft upper washer and the manual shift lever to manual shift shaft. Tighten two (2) nuts (Upper and lower nuts) as follows. See Figure 7A1-156.
  • Tighten lower nut to 27 - 33 N·m (20 - 23.5 ft.lb.).
  • Tighten upper nut to 27 - 33 N·m (20 - 23.5 ft.lb.).
4. d. After tightening nuts, check manual shift shaft for smooth rotation.
5. e. Restrictor pin and snap ring to parking lock pawl sleeve and then install to case.
6. f. Parking lock pawl.
  • Shift manual shift lever to a position other than "parking" position.
  • Install parking lock pawl.
  • Install lock pawl shaft and lock pawl spring. Check to make sure that parking lock pawl moves smoothly by moving manual shift lever.

para_7a1_p562_0.text: 17. One-way clutch (Figure 7A1-176). Place the one-way clutch on the rear planetary gear assembly while turning planetary gear assembly clockwise by hand, insert one-way clutch to correct position.

noti_7a1_p562_0.text:
NOTICE: One-way clutch must be installed in a certain direction. Check for its proper installation as follows (Figure 7A1-177).

After installing one-way clutch to the rear planetary gear, try turning rear planetary gear clockwise by hand. If it turns clockwise smoothly (freely), that proves the proper installation of the one-way clutch. However, if it doesn't turn clockwise (locked) and turns counterclockwise smoothly, it means that the one-way clutch is installed in the wrong direction. In such a case, remove one-way clutch, reinstall it in opposite direction and carry out the above described check again to make sure that the planetary gear turns smoothly only clockwise.

29. Direct clutch washer on the direct clutch. Apply grease to the washer and install it on the direct clutch with its grooved face outward.
30. Direct clutch on the input shaft. Align teeth of the direct clutch discs and then install direct clutch on the input shaft. After installing direct clutch, check it for proper installation by moving it up and down lightly by hand. If the direct clutch makes a clear sound like a "Click" when moved up and down, it is installed in place. But if no sound or a "Click" is heard, it is possible that direct clutch is not installed correctly. In such a case, remove direct clutch and reinstall.
31. Ring gear races and bearing. Apply grease to the ring gear races and bearing. Install ring gear race (OD: 35.8 mm, 1.41 in.) on the ring gear with its flange side down. Install the other ring gear race (OD: 37.9 mm, 1.49 in.) and bearing. See Figure 7A1-159 for proper installation.
32. Input shaft and forward/direct clutch assembly. Hold the input shaft with direct clutch installed by hand and while turning it back and forth, insert it into the case. Align teeth of the forward clutch discs before installation. When installing the input shaft, be careful so that its bearing and race will not fall off. Be careful not to damage the output shaft seal. After installing input shaft, check it for proper installation by moving it up and down lightly by hand. If input shaft makes clear sound like a "Click" when moved up and down, it is installed in place. But if it doesn't, that means it is not installed in proper position. In such a case, remove the assembly and reinstall.

para_7b_p579_0.text:
Figures 1 and 2

The transmission provides five forward speeds and one reverse speed by means of three synchronizers and three shafts: input shaft, countershaft in constant mesh with input shaft, and a reverse shaft which uses a sliding idler gear arrangement (Figures 1 and 2).

The low speed synchronizer is mounted on the counter shaft and will engage with either the counter shaft first gear or second gear. The high speed synchronizer is mounted on input shaft and will engage with either the input shaft third gear or fourth gear.

The fifth speed synchronizer is mounted on input shaft and is engaged with the input shaft fifth gear.

The countershaft turns the ring gear and differential assembly, thereby turning the front drive axles which are attached to the front wheels.

For servicing, it is necessary to use genuine sealant or its equivalent on mating surfaces of transmission case which is made of aluminum. The case fastening bolts must be tightened to specified torque by means of torque wrench. It is also important that all parts are thoroughly cleaned with cleaning fluid and air dried before reassembling.

Further, care must be taken to adjust preload of counter shaft taper roller bearings.

Figure 3

Movement of the gear shift control lever is transmitted to the gear shift shaft through gear shift control shaft, and gear shift/select shaft through the gear shift arm and shift yoke. The gear shift/select shaft actuates the shift/select lever which actuates each shift fork shaft and shift fork to shift transaxle into the desired gear. The gear shift interlock plate is provided to prevent the transaxle from engaging into two gears while shifting.

para_7b_p579_1.text:
Figures 4 through 8

The fifth gear/reverse gear shift cam along with the cam guide return spring and fifth to reverse interlock guide bolt are provided to prevent the gear shift lever from being directly shifted from fifth gear to reverse gear.

proc_7b_p579_0.text:
5th and Reverse Gear Shift Cam Operation
1. When the gear shift lever is shifted to "fifth" gear, the fifth and reverse gear shift cam turns clockwise with gear shift/select shaft. It is pushed up by the cam guide return spring to the position where the interlock guide bolt prevents the cam from being shifted directly from "fifth gear to reverse" (Figure 5).
2. Shifting from "fifth" to "neutral" is available and further shift to reverse is interrupted. However, when shift/select shaft is moved to neutral of "third" - "fourth" position, the shift limit cam mechanism is cancelled (Figure 6).
3. The selected position in neutral of "fifth" - "REV" is shown in (Figure 7). The shift/select shaft moves up to allow shift/select lever to shift in "fifth" or "reverse" position. The shift cam remains in position because of interference from the guide bolt (Figure 7).
4. Now the shifter is ready to be shifted to "reverse" without interference of the shift cam (Figure 8).
steps: When the gear shift lever is shifted to "fifth" gear, the fifth and reverse gear shift cam turns clockwise with gear shift/select shaft. It is pushed up by the cam guide return spring to the position where the interlock guide bolt prevents the cam from being shifted directly from "fifth gear to reverse" (Figure 5).

Shifting from "fifth" to "neutral" is available and further shift to reverse is interrupted. However, when shift/select shaft is moved to neutral of "third" - "fourth" position, the shift limit cam mechanism is cancelled (Figure 6).

The selected position in neutral of "fifth" - "REV" is shown in (Figure 7). The shift/select shaft moves up to allow shift/select lever to shift in "fifth" or "reverse" position. The shift cam remains in position because of interference from the guide bolt (Figure 7).
Now the shifter is ready to be shifted to "reverse" without interference of the shift cam (Figure 8).

proc_7b_p586_1.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Hoist vehicle.
2. Gear shift control shaft from gear shift control lever by removing control shaft bolt and nut (Figure 14).
3. Four guide plate bolts and loosen extension rod nut at transaxle end then move extension rod to the side (Figure 14).
4. Lower vehicle.
5. Two screws from shift control lever cover (if equipped with cover).
6. Four control lever housing nuts and push shift control lever boot(s) into floor hole. Take out shift control lever assembly from under the floor.
7. Hold control lever knob with a soft jawed vise, loosen its screw and remove control lever (Figure 15).
8. Two housing nuts, then disassemble control lever (Figure 15).

proc_7b_p586_2.text:

1. Assembly control lever and install two housing nuts (Figure 16).
2. Control lever and its screw. If necessary, use soft jawed vise to hold control lever knob.
3. Shift control lever assembly into floor, and pull shift control lever boot(s) up from the floor and install four control lever housing bolts and nuts.
4. Two screws into shift control lever cover (if equipped with cover).
5. Raise vehicle.
6. Reposition extension rod and tighten extension rod nut.
7. Four guide plate bolts.

proc_7b_p588_1.text:
Install or Connect
1. New oil seal into driven gear case with spring side facing down after using J37764, first apply grease #1052169. Special tool J37764 may be used for installation.
2. Driven gear into driver gear case applying grease #1052169.
3. Spring pin by supporting flat portion of driver gear case with a wood block.
4. Case assembly to transaxle applying grease #1052196 to O-ring, then install bolt.
5. Cable, case clip, and boot.

proc_7b_p605_0.text:
Install or Connect
- Differential gear and measure thrust play of differential gear as follows (Figure 75).

Left Side
• Hold differential assembly with a soft jawed vise and apply measuring tip of dial gage to top surface of gear.
• Using two screwdrivers, move gear up and down and read movement of dial gage pointer. Thrust play should be between 0.05 - 0.33 mm (0.002 -0.013 in.).

Right side
• Using a similer procedure to the above, set dial gage tip to gear shoulder.
• Move gear up and down by hand and read dial gage. Thrust play should be between 0.05 - 0.33 mm (0.002 - 0.013 in.).
- Drive in spring pin from right side till it is flush with differential case surface.
- Left bearing using J34851 and hammer.
- Speedometer drive gear, support differential assembly to allow LH. bearing to float and then press-fit RH. bearing using J29036 with J8092 and hammer (Figure 76).
5. Ring gear while holding differential assembly with soft jawed vise, install eight final gear bolts (Figure 77).

caut_7c_p617_0.text: CAUTION: When servicing clutch parts, do not create dust by grinding or sanding clutch disc or by cleaning parts with a dry brush or with compressed air. (A water dampened cloth – NOT SOAKED – should be used). The clutch disc contains asbesto fibers which can become airborne if dust is created during servicing. Breathing dust containing asbesto fibers may cause serious bodily harm.

Clutch Start Switch: 7C-3
Clutch Cable: 7C-4

para_7c_p617_0.text:
The clutch is a diaphragm-spring clutch of a dry single disc type. The diaphragm spring is of a tapering-finger type, which is a solid ring in the outer diameter part, with a series of tapering fingers pointing inward.

The disc, carrying four torsional coil springs, is positioned on the transaxle input shaft with a serration fit.

The clutch cover is secured to the flywheel, and carries the diaphragm spring in such a way that the peripheral edge part of the spring pushes on the pressure plate against the flywheel (with the disc in between), when the clutch release bearing is held back.

This is the engaged condition of the clutch.

Depressing the clutch pedal causes the release bearing to advance and pushes on the tips of the tapering fingers of the diaphgram spring.

When this happens, the diaphragm spring pulls the pressure plate away from the flywheel, thereby interrupting the flow of drive from flywheel through clutch disc to transaxle input shaft.

The clutch start switch is mounted above clutch pedal. This switch is in the electric circuit of starter motor, and operates according to movement of clutch pedal. When starting engine, the clutch pedal should be fully depressed in order to turn on this switch.

If the clutch pedal is released or is not depressed all the way, this switch is "OFF" and the starter motor will not run even if the ignition key is in the "START" position. Refer to Figure 5 for the clutch start switch.

tbl_7c_p618_0.text:
Condition: Slipping
  - Possible Cause: Improper clutch pedal free travel.
  - Correction: Adjust free travel.
  - Possible Cause: Worn or oily clutch disc facing.
  - Correction: Replace disc.
  - Possible Cause: Warped disc or pressure plate.
  - Correction: Replace disc or clutch cover.
  - Possible Cause: Weak diaphragm spring.
  - Correction: Replace clutch cover.

tbl_7c_p618_1.text:
Condition: Grabbing (Chattering)
  - Possible Cause: Worn or oily clutch disc facing.
  - Correction: Replace disc.
  - Possible Cause: Warped disc or pressure plate.
  - Correction: Replace clutch cover or disc.
  - Possible Cause: Loose engine mounts.
  - Correction: Tighten or replace mounts.
  - Possible Cause: Weak torsion springs.
  - Correction: Replace disc.

tbl_7c_p618_2.text:
Condition: Noisy
  - Possible Cause: Worn release bearing.
  - Correction: Replace release bearing.
  - Possible Cause: Dry clutch cable.
  - Correction: Replace or grease.

proc_7c_p619_1.text:
CLUTCH START SWITCH (FIGURE 5 AND 6)
Clutch Switch Adjustment (continued)
- Apply parking brake firmly and place gear shift lever in neutral position.
2. Disconnect lead wire at switch.
3. Loosen the lock nut and screw the switch out.
4. Depress clutch pedal all the way to the floor, and then return it back 15 to 30 mm (0.6-1.2 in.) along its travel from the floor.
5. Connect ohmmeter to the switch, and slowly screw the switch in until it is "ON". Hold the switch at this position, and tighten the lock nut to 13 N'm (10 Ft. Lbs.)
6. Connect the lead wire.

5. Connect ohmmeter to the switch, and slowly screw the switch in until it is "ON". Hold the switch at this position, and tighten the lock nut to 13 N'm (10 Ft. Lbs.)
6. Connect the lead wire.

Removal
1. Disconnect negative cable at the battery.
2. Remove clutch cable joint nut. Disconnect cable from the release arm.
3. Remove the clutch cable bracket retaining bolts and remove the bracket from the cable.
4. Remove cable retaining bolts at cowl and disconnect at pedal.
5. Remove the clutch cable from the vehicle.

2. Remove clutch cable joint nut. Disconnect cable from the release arm.
3. Remove the clutch cable bracket retaining bolts and remove the bracket from the cable.
4. Remove cable retaining bolts at cowl and disconnect at pedal.
5. Remove the clutch cable from the vehicle.

4. Connect the clutch cable to the release lever and install the joint nut on the cable.
5. Adjust the clutch cable as previously outlined under Clutch Pedal Free Travel.
6. Connect the negative cable at the battery.

Removal
1. Remove the tranaxle as outlined under Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
2. Install Tool J37761 into the pilot bearing to support the clutch assembly during removal. Hold flywheel stationary using J35271.
3. Loosen the clutch cover to flywheel attaching bolts evenly one turn at a time until spring pressure is released, then remove the bolts. Remove the clutch cover, clutch disc and Tool J37761 from the flywheel.

proc_7c_p621_0.text:
Inspection
1. Clean the flywheel mating surfaces of all oil, grease, metal deposits, etc. Inspect the flywheel for cracks, heat checking or other defects. Replace or repair as required.
2. Check the wear on the facings of the clutch disc by measuring the depth of each rivet head depression. If the depth is found to have reached the service limit at any of the holes, replace the clutch disc assembly.
3. Check the diaphragm spring and pressure plate for wear or damage. If the spring or plate is excessively worn, or damaged, replace the clutch cover assembly. Do not disassemble the clutch cover assembly. If defective, replace the assembly.
4. Check the pilot bearing for smooth operation. If the bearing does not spin freely, replace it.
steps: Clean the flywheel mating surfaces of all oil, grease, metal deposits, etc. Inspect the flywheel for cracks, heat checking or other defects. Replace or repair as required.

Check the wear on the facings of the clutch disc by measuring the depth of each rivet head depression. If the depth is found to have reached the service limit at any of the holes, replace the clutch disc assembly.

Check the diaphragm spring and pressure plate for wear or damage. If the spring or plate is excessively worn, or damaged, replace the clutch cover assembly. Do not disassemble the clutch cover assembly. If defective, replace the assembly.
Check the pilot bearing for smooth operation. If the bearing does not spin freely, replace it.

proc_7c_p621_1.text:
Installation
- Position the clutch disc and clutch cover in relative installed position and support them with alignment tool J35271 or a drive gear. The clutch disc is installed with the damper springs to the transmission.
- Install the clutch cover bolts and tighten down evenly and gradually until tight to avoid possible clutch distortion. Torque bolts to specifications.
- Remove Tool J35271.
- Lightly lubricate the splines and pilot bearing surface of the input shaft with grease #1051344 or equivalent (Figure 11).
- Lightly lubricate the release bearing with grease #1051344 or equivalent (Figure 12).
6. Install the transaxle as outlined in Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
7. Adjust the clutch cable as previously outlined under Clutch Pedal Free Travel.
steps: Position the clutch disc and clutch cover in relative installed position and support them with alignment tool J35271 or a drive gear. The clutch disc is installed with the damper springs to the transmission.
Install the clutch cover bolts and tighten down evenly and gradually until tight to avoid possible clutch distortion. Torque bolts to specifications.
Remove Tool J35271.
Lightly lubricate the splines and pilot bearing surface of the input shaft with grease #1051344 or equivalent (Figure 11).
Lightly lubricate the release bearing with grease #1051344 or equivalent (Figure 12).
6. Install the transaxle as outlined in Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
7. Adjust the clutch cable as previously outlined under Clutch Pedal Free Travel.

proc_7c_p623_0.text:
Replacement
1. Remove the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
2. Disconnect the return spring from the shaft fork.
3. Remove the clutch release bearing from the transaxle. Inspect the release bearing for rough rotation, wear or damage. If any of the above conditions are found, replace the bearing.
4. Lightly lubricate the release bearing with grease #1051344 or equivalent (Figure 16).
5. Install the clutch release bearing.
6. Connect the return spring to the shaft fork.
7. Install the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
steps: 1. Remove the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
2. Disconnect the return spring from the shaft fork.
3. Remove the clutch release bearing from the transaxle. Inspect the release bearing for rough rotation, wear or damage. If any of the above conditions are found, replace the bearing.
4. Lightly lubricate the release bearing with grease #1051344 or equivalent (Figure 16).
5. Install the clutch release bearing.
6. Connect the return spring to the shaft fork.
7. Install the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).

noti_7c_p623_0.text: NOTICE: Do not place bearing in degreaser or damage to the seals may result.

fig_7c_p623_3.text:
Figure 17 Removing No. 1 Bushing and Shaft Seal
1: RELEASE SHAFT ARM
2: RELEASE SHAFT

proc_7c_p623_2.text:
Replacement (Figures 17 - 24)
1. Remove the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
2. Disconnect the return spring from the shaft fork.
3. Remove the clutch release bearing.
4. Remove the clutch release arm.
5. Remove the release shaft seal and #2 bushing using Tool J-37973 (Figure 17).
6. Remove the release shaft and return spring from the transaxle.
7. Remove the #1 bushing from the transaxle using Tool J37158 (Figure 19).
8. Inspect the transaxle retainer for wear (Figure 20). If the retainer is excessively worn or damaged, replace the right case.
8. Install the #1 bushing into the transaxle (Figure 21). After installing, apply grease to the I.D. of the bushing.
9. Install the release shaft and return spring.
10. Install the #2 bushing into the transaxle using Tool J37973 (Figure 22). Before installing, apply grease to the I.D. of the bushing.
11. Install the shaft seal. Before installing, apply grease to the lip portion of the seal. After installation of the seal, stake the transaxle case against the seal at two points (Figure 23).
12. Slide the shaft seal cover onto the release shaft.
13. Install the clutch release arm. Align punch mark on release arm with punch mark on release shaft (Figure 24).
14. Install the clutch release bearing. Before installing, lubricate the bearing with grease (Figure 16).
15. Hook the return spring end to the release shaft fork.
16. Install the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
17. Adjust the clutch as previously outlined in this section.
steps: 1. Remove the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
2. Disconnect the return spring from the shaft fork.
3. Remove the clutch release bearing.
4. Remove the clutch release arm.
5. Remove the release shaft seal and #2 bushing using Tool J-37973 (Figure 17).
6. Remove the release shaft and return spring from the transaxle.
7. Remove the #1 bushing from the transaxle using Tool J37158 (Figure 19).
Inspect the transaxle retainer for wear (Figure 20). If the retainer is excessively worn or damaged, replace the right case.
8. Install the #1 bushing into the transaxle (Figure 21). After installing, apply grease to the I.D. of the bushing.
9. Install the release shaft and return spring.
10. Install the #2 bushing into the transaxle using Tool J37973 (Figure 22). Before installing, apply grease to the I.D. of the bushing.
11. Install the shaft seal. Before installing, apply grease to the lip portion of the seal. After installation of the seal, stake the transaxle case against the seal at two points (Figure 23).
12. Slide the shaft seal cover onto the release shaft.
13. Install the clutch release arm. Align punch mark on release arm with punch mark on release shaft (Figure 24).
14. Install the clutch release bearing. Before installing, lubricate the bearing with grease (Figure 16).
15. Hook the return spring end to the release shaft fork.
16. Install the transaxle as outlined in the Manual Transaxle (Section 7B).
17. Adjust the clutch as previously outlined in this section.

para_8_p629_0.text:
The chassis electrical components of this vehicle are designed to operate on 12 volts power supplied by the battery. The electrical system utilizes negative ground polarity.

All chassis low voltage wires are insulated. The insulation is color coded for identification of individual chassis circuits.

para_8_p629_1.text: The main fuseblock is located on the left hand fender apron in the engine compartment. The junction/fuseblock is located under the instrument panel on the left hand side. The designation of each main fuse is shown in Figure 1. The designation and location of each junction/fuseblock fuse is shown in Figure 2.

caut_8_p629_0.text:
CAUTION: • When replacing a fuse, be sure to use one having the correct amperage rating.
• Before replacing a fuse, turn OFF all switches, including the ignition switch.

para_8_p634_0.text: The fuel gage circuit consists of the fuel gage and the sending unit in the tank (Figure 11). Current flowing through the gage coil is varied to control the movement of the gage pointer. When the tank is full, the sending unit resistance is decreased allowing more current flow into the gage coil causing the pointer to move to the "F" (FULL) position (Figure 11).

proc_8_p634_0.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Negative battery cable.
2. Rear seat.
3. Electrical leads from sending unit (yellow/red).
steps: 1. Negative battery cable.
2. Rear seat.
3. Electrical leads from sending unit (yellow/red).

proc_8_p634_1.text:
Inspect (Figures 12 and 13)
1. Connect negative battery cable.
2. Use a test bulb to ground the lead wire (Figure 12).
3. Turn the ignition switch "ON." The bulb should light and the gage pointer should fluctuate after a few seconds. If not, remove the fuel gage to check it.
4. Measure the gage coil for "F" to "E" resistance (Figure 13).
5. If fuel gage does not meet specifications, replace gage.
6. Disconnect negative battery cable.
steps: Connect negative battery cable.
Use a test bulb to ground the lead wire (Figure 12).
Turn the ignition switch "ON." The bulb should light and the gage pointer should fluctuate after a few seconds. If not, remove the fuel gage to check it.
Measure the gage coil for "F" to "E" resistance (Figure 13).
If fuel gage does not meet specifications, replace gage.
Disconnect negative battery cable.

tbl_8_p635_0.text:
Condition: Fuel gage inoperative.
  - Possible Cause: Gage fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse, check for short.
  - Possible Cause: Fuel gage faulty.
  - Correction: Check gage.
  - Possible Cause: Sending unit faulty.
  - Correction: Check sending unit.
  - Possible Cause: Wiring or faulty ground.
  - Correction: Repair.

tbl_8_p635_1.text:
Condition: Water temperature gage inoperative.
  - Possible Cause: Gage fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse, check for short.
  - Possible Cause: Water temperature gage faulty.
  - Correction: Check gage.
  - Possible Cause: Water temperature sending unit faulty.
  - Correction: Check sending unit.
  - Possible Cause: Wiring or faulty ground.
  - Correction: Repair.

tbl_8_p635_2.text:
Condition: Brake warning lamp (parking brake lamp) inoperative.
  - Possible Cause: Lamp fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse, check for short.
  - Possible Cause: Bulb burnt out.
  - Correction: Replace bulb.
  - Possible Cause: Brake fluid level switch faulty.
  - Correction: Check warning switch.
  - Possible Cause: Wiring or faulty ground.
  - Correction: Repair.

tbl_8_p635_3.text:
Condition: Seat belt warning lamp/buzzer inoperative.
  - Possible Cause: Lamp fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse, check for short.
  - Possible Cause: Bulb burned out.
  - Correction: Replace bulb.
  - Possible Cause: Buzzer defective.
  - Correction: Replace buzzer.
  - Possible Cause: Wiring or faulty ground.
  - Correction: Repair.

tbl_8_p635_4.text:
Condition: Ignition key and light switch warning buzzer inoperative.
  - Possible Cause: Buzzer fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse, check for short.
  - Possible Cause: Buzzer faulty.
  - Correction: Replace buzzer.
  - Possible Cause: Wiring or faulty ground.
  - Correction: Repair.

tbl_8_p635_5.text:
Condition: Shift-up indicator inoperative. (Manual Transmission equipped vehicles only).
  - Possible Cause: Indicator lamp fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse and check for short.
  - Possible Cause: Bulb burnt out.
  - Correction: Replace bulb.
  - Possible Cause: Speedometer faulty.
  - Correction: Check speedometer.

para_8_p637_1.text:
The water temperature gage circuit consists of the temperature gage installed in the cluster and the sending unit installed in the intake manifold.

The sending unit shows different resistance values depending on the coolant temperature. This causes the current flowing through the temperature gage coil to change, controlling the gage pointer. That is, when the coolant temperature increases, the sending unit resistance decreases with more current flowing through the gage coil, raising the gage pointer upward from the "C" position (Figure 16).

proc_8_p637_0.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Negative battery cable.
2. Electrical leads from sending unit (yellow/white).
steps: 1. Negative battery cable.
2. Electrical leads from sending unit (yellow/white).

proc_8_p637_1.text:
Inspect (Figures 17 and 18)
1. Connect negative battery cable.
2. Use a bulb in position to ground the above wire (Figure 17).
3. Turn the ignition switch ON. Confirm that the bulb is ON with the gage pointer fluctuating several seconds thereafter. If the pointer is faulty, remove the temperature gage to check it.
4. Measure the resistance between the temperature U/W lead wire terminal (A) and (B) (Figure 18).
5. If the temperature gage does not meet specifications, replace gage.
6. Disconnect negative battery cable.
steps: Connect negative battery cable.
Use a bulb in position to ground the above wire (Figure 17).
Turn the ignition switch ON. Confirm that the bulb is ON with the gage pointer fluctuating several seconds thereafter. If the pointer is faulty, remove the temperature gage to check it.
Measure the resistance between the temperature U/W lead wire terminal (A) and (B) (Figure 18).
If the temperature gage does not meet specifications, replace gage.
Disconnect negative battery cable.
