1. Electrical leads to sending unit.
2. Negative battery cable.
steps: 1. Electrical leads to sending unit.
2. Negative battery cable.

para_8_p637_2.text:
Warm up the sending unit (Figure 19). Its resistance should decrease with an increase in
temperature. The temperature and resistance relationship can be plotted in a graph (Figure 20).

para_8_p638_0.text: The oil pressure light circuit consists of the oil pressure switch installed in the cylinder block and the light (warning light) inside the cluster. The oil pressure switch is open when oil pressure is produced.

proc_8_p638_0.text:
Oil Pressure Switch
1. Oil pressure switch for proper continuity (Figure 22).
2. If check results are not satisfactory, the switch must be replaced.
steps: 1. Oil pressure switch for proper continuity (Figure 22).
2. If check results are not satisfactory, the switch must be replaced.

para_8_p638_1.text:
The brake fluid level warning light circuit consists of a brake fluid level switch installed in the master cylinder reservoir, a brake fluid level warning light in the gage cluster, a diode, and a check relay (Figure 23).

Also, this circuit includes a parking brake switch which warns that the parking brake is applied (Figure 23).

proc_8_p638_1.text:
Operation of Warning Light
1. When the engine is stopped, the warning lamp comes on if the ignition switch is turned ON and the parking brake is applied.
2. For bulb check, the warning lamp comes on briefly during engine starting regardless of the brake fluid level position and the parking brake operation because the contacts of the check relay are closed.
3. After the engine is started, release the parking brake. If the lamp goes off, the brake fluid level is adequate.
steps: 1. When the engine is stopped, the warning lamp comes on if the ignition switch is turned ON and the parking brake is applied.

2. For bulb check, the warning lamp comes on briefly during engine starting regardless of the brake fluid level position and the parking brake operation because the contacts of the check relay are closed.
3. After the engine is started, release the parking brake. If the lamp goes off, the brake fluid level is adequate.

para_8_p638_2.text: 1. Use an ohmmeter to measure the switch for resistance and continuity. If found defective, replace the switch (Figure 24).

para_8_p638_3.text:
The seat belt warning light buzzer circuit is a system designed to light and sound the buzzer respectively for several seconds, urging the driver to wear his seat belt. When ignition switch is turned ON, the warning light comes ON for several seconds whether the seat belt is fastened or not. After several seconds, the light goes OFF. When ignition switch is turned ON with the seat belt unfastened, the warning buzzer sounds for several seconds.

When the warning light/buzzer does not operate, use the Circuit Diagram as a reference to check the bulb, buzzer, wiring, etc. (Figure 25).

proc_8_p638_2.text:
Inspect (Figures 25 and 26)
1. Connect negative terminal of the battery to buzzer terminals (1) and (7), and the positive terminal of the battery to buzzer terminals (4) and (5) (Figure 26).
2. Check that the buzzer emits a buzzing sound for 4 to 8 seconds and stops. If check result is not satisfactory, the buzzer must be replaced.
steps: 1. Connect negative terminal of the battery to buzzer terminals (1) and (7), and the positive terminal of the battery to buzzer terminals (4) and (5) (Figure 26).
2. Check that the buzzer emits a buzzing sound for 4 to 8 seconds and stops. If check result is not satisfactory, the buzzer must be replaced.

para_8_p638_4.text:
The ignition key warning buzzer circuit is a system designed to sound the buzzer if the driver leaves the car with the ignition switch key inserted in place. That is, when the left front door is opened with the ignition switch key in "ACC" or "OFF" position, the warning buzzer sounds.

If the ignition switch key warning buzzer does not sound, use the Wiring Diagram as reference to check the buzzer, wiring, etc. (Figure 27).

proc_8_p638_3.text:
Inspect (Figures 27 and 28)
1. Connect negative terminal of the battery to buzzer terminal (6) and (7), and the positive terminal of the battery to buzzer terminal (4) (Figure 28).
2. Buzzer should emit a continuous buzzing sound. If check result is not satisfactory, the buzzer must be replaced.
steps: 1. Connect negative terminal of the battery to buzzer terminal (6) and (7), and the positive terminal of the battery to buzzer terminal (4) (Figure 28).
2. Buzzer should emit a continuous buzzing sound. If check result is not satisfactory, the buzzer must be replaced.

para_8_p638_5.text:
The light switch warning buzzer circuit is a system designed to sound the buzzer if the driver stops the engine with the light switch (headlamps and/or parking lamp) turned ON. That is, when the ignition key is turned from "ON" position to "ACC" or "OFF" position with the light switch turned ON, the warning buzzer sounds (Figure 29).

If the light switch warning buzzer does not sound, use the Wiring Diagram as reference to check the buzzer, switch, wiring, etc. (Figure 29).

proc_8_p638_4.text:
Inspect (Figures 29 and 30)
1. Connect the positive terminal of the battery to buzzer terminals (2) and (5) and the negative terminal of the battery to buzzer terminal (7). Then disconnect connection to buzzer terminal (5) (Figure 30).
2. The buzzer should emit a continuous buzzing sound. If check result is not satisfactory, the buzzer must be replaced.
steps: 1. Connect the positive terminal of the battery to buzzer terminals (2) and (5) and the negative terminal of the battery to buzzer terminal (7). Then disconnect connection to buzzer terminal (5) (Figure 30).
2. The buzzer should emit a continuous buzzing sound. If check result is not satisfactory, the buzzer must be replaced.

BODY AND CHASSIS ELECTRICAL

  - Possible Cause: Lamp burnt out.
  - Correction: Replace lamp.
  - Possible Cause: Fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse.
  - Possible Cause: Faulty socket, wiring or grounding.

tbl_8_p657_1.text:
BODY AND CHASSIS ELECTRICAL
Condition: Headlamps do not light
  - Possible Cause: Fuse blown.
  - Correction: Replace fuse and check.
  - Possible Cause: Light/Hazard flasher or Turn Signal/Dimmer switch faulty.
  - Correction: Check switches.
  - Possible Cause: Faulty wiring or grounding.
  - Correction: Repair as necessary.

tbl_8_p658_0.text:
BODY AND CHASSIS ELECTRICAL
CONDITION: One beam (HI or LO) only does not light
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Light/Hazard flasher or Turn Signal/Dimmer switch faulty.
  - CORRECTION: Check switch.

proc_8_p658_0.text:
Inspect
1. Check the Light/Hazard flasher and Turn Signal/Dimmer switches for each terminal-to-terminal continuity (See section "SWITCHES").
2. The headlamps are grounded below the left and right dash side panels, and at the junction/fuseblock (Figure 36).
steps: Check the Light/Hazard flasher and Turn Signal/Dimmer switches for each terminal-to-terminal continuity (See section "SWITCHES").

para_8_p658_0.text: Before adjusting the headlamp beams, adjust air pressures of all tires to the specified value. Move the vehicle up and down by hand to settle its suspension. Then move it over a flat surface. There are available a variety of headlamp beams setting methods (e.g., the screen method using a focusing tester, etc.). However, the method described here does not use such a tester.

proc_8_p658_1.text:
Adjusting Headlamp Beams
1. Beam Alignment

Unless otherwise required by local regulations, align the headlamp beams according to the following procedure:
• Place a vehicle 7.6m (25 ft.) from a blank wall. Check to see if the hot spot (high intensity zone) of each main (high) beam axis falls as illustrated. The beam alignment should be carried out with a driver (68kg, 150 kg, 150 lb.) aboard. (Figures 37 and 38).
2. Headlamp Adjustment

There are two screws which can be used for adjustment. Use these screws to adjust the headlamp position for the vertical and horizontal alignment of each beam (Figure 39).
steps:
Beam Alignment

Unless otherwise required by local regulations, align the headlamp beams according to the following procedure:
• Place a vehicle 7.6m (25 ft.) from a blank wall. Check to see if the hot spot (high intensity zone) of each main (high) beam axis falls as illustrated. The beam alignment should be carried out with a driver (68kg, 150 kg, 150 lb.) aboard. (Figures 37 and 38).

Headlamp Adjustment

There are two screws which can be used for adjustment. Use these screws to adjust the headlamp position for the vertical and horizontal alignment of each beam (Figure 39).

proc_8_p658_2.text:

1. Check turn signal/dimmer switch and light/hazard flasher for each terminal-to-terminal continuity (See section "SWITCHES") (Figure 40).
2. Turn signal bulbs should be grounded at each side of front fender apron (Figure 41).

tbl_8_p659_0.text:
Inspect (Figures 40 and 41)
CONDITION: Flash rate high or one side only flashes.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Faulty ground.
  - CORRECTION: Repair.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Wrong Bulb.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: One of the light bulbs burnt out on the right or left side of the front or rear of the vehicle.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective flasher (turn and hazard).
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Open circuit or high resistance existing between the turn signal/dimmer switch and/or light/hazard flasher switch and the lights on one side.
  - CORRECTION: Repair.

tbl_8_p659_1.text:
Inspect (Figures 40 and 41)
CONDITION: No flashing.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Blown fuse on turn signal-and-hazard warning circuit.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Open circuit or high resistance existing between battery and switch.
  - CORRECTION: Repair.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Open circuit or high resistance existing between switch and relay.
  - CORRECTION: Repair.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective switch.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective relay.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.

tbl_8_p659_2.text:
Inspect (Figures 40 and 41)
CONDITION: Flash rate low.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Supply voltage too low.
  - CORRECTION: Recharge battery.
  - POSSIBLE CAUSE: Defective relay.
  - CORRECTION: Replace.

para_8_p667_0.text:
The windshield wipers are of the two or three speed (intermittent) type. The windshield washers are equipped with a separate type washer pump. The windshield washer fluid reservoir is located near the front of the left front fender. There are washer pumps for the front and rear (optional) wiper systems.

The circuit is designed so that when the wiper switch is turned OFF the wiper blades automatically return to their specified level park positions. When the wiper switch is turned ON with the ignition switch ON, current is supplied continuously from the battery (via lead Y/Bl - switch - lead Bl or Bl/R) to the motor, rotating the motor to move the blades (Figure 49).

The gear-and-linkage mechanism, which converts the rotary movement of the motor to the wiping movement of the blades, has a cam on the shaft of its output gear.

The cam serves to connect contact P0 to the contact P1. Although the cam thus serves to make and break the circuits containing the contacts P0, P1, and P2, the wiper motor makes its rotation completely independent of this cam rotation. When the wiper switch is turned OFF (the switch is set to the OFF position) with the blades positioned at the park positions, current supply is cut, causing the motor to stop with the blades staying in the same positions.

Even when the wiper switch if turned OFF (the switch is set to the OFF position) with the blades positioned other than at the park positions, current is still supplied, though intermittently, through a different path from the battery (via lead Y/Bl - contact P2 - contact P0 - lead Bl/W - switch (OFF position) - lead Bl or Bl/R) to the motor.

Therefore, the motor can still rotate supplied with this intermittent current, causing the blades to return to the park positions.

As soon as the blades have returned to their park positions, the cam connects contact P0 to contact P1, causing current to be shunted around the motor.

When current is no longer supplied to the motor, a counter electromotive force is generated in the motor armature, causing a current to flow through the motor-and-shunt circuit so that the motor is stopped with the blades staying at the specified level park positions.

para_8_p667_1.text: When the wiper switch is set to the intermittent position with the ignition switch ON (the condenser is charged at this time), current from the battery flows through the Y/Bl wire, generates magnetic force in the coil in the relay and causes the contacts in the relay to close. Then current is transmitted in the sequence of Y/Bl, Bl/W, wiper switch, and Bl causing the wiper motor to rotate (meanwhile, the condenser discharges). By the time the wiper motor makes one rotation and the cam in the motor comes to the park position P1 the condenser in the relay has finished discharging (no magnetic force in the coil in the relay). Then the contacts in the relay open and the wiper arm stop in the park position. They remain that way until the condenser is fully charged. As soon as the condenser begins discharging after being fully charged, magnetic force generated in the the relay causes the contacts to close. As described above, intermittent operation of the wiper motor is controlled by charging and discharging of the condenser (Figure 49).

proc_8_p674_0.text:
Checking wire grid damage
1. Turn the ignition switch ON.
2. Turn the defogger switch ON.
3. Use a voltmeter to check the voltage at the center of each heat wire (Figure 61).
steps: Turn the ignition switch ON.
Turn the defogger switch ON.
Use a voltmeter to check the voltage at the center of each heat wire (Figure 61).

para_8_p674_1.text: If the voltage obtained is 10V, the wire is damaged between its center and positive end. If the voltage is zero, the wire is damaged between its center and ground (Figure 62).

proc_8_p674_1.text:
Locating grid damage
1. Touch the voltmeter positive (+) lead to the heat wire positive terminal end.
2. Touch the voltmeter negative lead with a foil strip to the heater grid positive terminal end, then move it along the wire to the negative terminal end.
3. The place which causes the voltmeter to fluctuate from zero to several volts is point where there is damage.

Touch the voltmeter negative lead with a foil strip to the heater grid positive terminal end, then move it along the wire to the negative terminal end.
The place which causes the voltmeter to fluctuate from zero to several volts is point where there is damage.

When repairing the defogger circuit, use the following procedures (Figure 64).
1. Apply masking tape at both the upper and lower sides of grid wire to be repaired.
2. Apply commercially-available repair agent with a fine-tip brush.
3. Two to three minutes later, remove the masking tape previously applied.
4. Allow 24 hours drying times before operating the defogger again; wire with heat gun.

para_9a_p684_0.text:
All factory equipped sound systems have ungrounded speakers. Installing add-on tape players, CB's or other units which use the car speakers may damage sound system.

FM broadcasts are the "line of sight" from station antenna to receiving antenna. The range is often limited to 25 miles (40 km) or less for steady reception. Tall buildings or hills may cause flutter or noise which is not the fault of the radio; select a stronger station for clear sound.

All factory equipped sound systems with an FM stereo radio will switch to stereo operation whenever an FM stereo broadcast is being received, and an indicator in the dial will light. "left" and "right". Stereo sound is noticeably more realistic to the ear.

The antenna is located in the left front pillar. To extend it, pull the knob up. Antenna must be kept clean for quality performance.

para_9a_p686_1.text: The following operation is common to both radios (Figure 1).

proc_9a_p686_0.text:
To Operate The Radio
1. Turn Radio On (PWR) - Press "PWR" button; press again to turn radio off. (Radio also goes off with ignition key.)
2. Adjust Volume (VOL) - Press "VOL" (up button) to increase volume; press "VOL" (down button) to decrease volume.
3. Select AM or FM - Press "AM-FM" button to select desired band ("AM" or "FM" will be displayed depending on band choice).
4. Select Stations - Use one of the four methods of tuning: Seek - Press "SEEK" button to automatically search for the next listenable station. Scan - Press "SCAN" button to sample each station for a few seconds; press again to stop scanning (If radio is in "SCAN" mode, the word "SCAN" will be displayed). Manual Tuning - Press and hold "TUNE" with arrow pointing up or down. Release as desired frequency is approached.
5. Setting Favorite Stations - Locate a favorite station by using the "SEEK", "SCAN", or up-down tuning buttons. Then press the "SET" button; within five seconds press one of the five station buttons (The word "SET" plus station frequency will be displayed). The radio will now return to the station frequency whenever that station button is depressed.
6. Setting Time of Day: To set hours - Press and hold "SET" button and at the same time, press and hold "SEEK" button until correct hour appears. To set minutes - Press and hold "SET" button and at the same time, press and hold "SCAN" button until correct minute appears (Time can also be set with tape playing).
7. To Display Time with Ignition Off - Press "RCL" button or "PROG/RCL" button if you have radio with tape player.
8. To Display Frequency - If radio is turned on and time is being displayed, press "RCL" button (PROG/RCL on radio with tape player), to display frequency.
9. To Receive Stereo - The radio will automatically switch to stereo when tuned to an FM station broadcasting stereo, and the stereo indicator light, "ST" will be displayed.
10. Speaker Balance (BAL) - Slide balance control, "BAL", towards left to adjust sound balance to left speakers or right to adjust sound balance to right speakers.
11. Speaker Front - Rear (FADE) - Slide fade control, "FADE", towards left to adjust sound balance to rear of car or towards right to adjust sound balance to front of car.
12. Tone Settings - Treble and Bass: Treble - Slide "TREB" control toward left to decrease treble. Slide "TREB" control toward right to increase treble ("TREB" control set at center position balances treble response). Bass - Slide "BASS" control toward right to increase bass ("BASS" control set at center position balances bass response).

Select Stations - Use one of the four methods of tuning: Seek - Press "SEEK" button to automatically search for the next listenable station. Setting Favorite Stations - Locate a favorite station by using the "SEEK", "SCAN", or up-down tuning buttons. The radio will now return to the station frequency whenever that station button is depressed.

Setting Time of Day: To set hours - Press and hold "SET" button and at the same time, press and hold "SEEK" button until correct hour appears. To Display Time with Ignition Off - Press "RCL" button or "PROG/RCL" button if you have radio with tape player.
To Display Frequency - If radio is turned on and time is being displayed, press "RCL" button (PROG/RCL on radio with tape player), to display frequency.
To Receive Stereo - The radio will automatically switch to stereo when tuned to an FM station broadcasting stereo, and the stereo indicator light, "ST" will be displayed.

noti_9a_p686_2.text: NOTICE: When radio is turned off, the last volume setting is stored in memory. When turned back on, it will return to that level.

noti_9a_p686_3.text: NOTICE: The last station heard on each will be stored in memory. When switching back to that band, it will automatically return.

noti_9a_p686_4.text: NOTICE: A total of ten stations can be preset (five AM and five FM).

noti_9a_p686_5.text: NOTICE: Slide balance control to center position to equalize sound balance between right and left speakers.

noti_9a_p686_6.text: NOTICE: Slide fade control to center position to equalize sound balance between front and rear speakers.

proc_9a_p686_1.text:
To Operate Tape Player (If Equipped)
1. Inserting Cassette Tape - Insert tape into door marked "AUTO REVERSE" with exposed edge of tape to the right.
2. Reversing Sides - Press "PROG/RCL" button to play other side of tape.
3. Auto-Reverse Feature - When end-of-tape is reached, the unit will automatically reverse direction and play other side of the tape as indicated by the lighted arrow.
4. Fast Forward (FF) - Press "FF" button to activate; depress "EJT" button half way to stop fast motion and return to playing speed.
5. Fast Reverse (REW) - Press "REW" button to activate; depress "EJT" button half way to stop fast motion and return to playing speed.
6. To Eject Tape (EJT) and Return to "Radio" - Firmly press "EJT" button.
steps: Inserting Cassette Tape - Insert tape into door marked "AUTO REVERSE" with exposed edge of tape to the right.
Reversing Sides - Press "PROG/RCL" button to play other side of tape.
Auto-Reverse Feature - When end-of-tape is reached, the unit will automatically reverse direction and play other side of the tape as indicated by the lighted arrow.
Fast Forward (FF) - Press "FF" button to activate; depress "EJT" button half way to stop fast motion and return to playing speed.
Fast Reverse (REW) - Press "REW" button to activate; depress "EJT" button half way to stop fast motion and return to playing speed.
To Eject Tape (EJT) and Return to "Radio" - Firmly press "EJT" button.

noti_9a_p686_7.text: NOTICE: A lighted arrow will be displayed. When the arrow points up, the top side of the tape (as inserted) is playing. When the arrow points down, the bottom side of the tape is playing.

noti_9a_p687_0.text: NOTICE: Power button must be "ON". When tape is ejected, the radio becomes operative. When the ignition is turned off, internal pressure on the tape is automatically removed so that no damage will occur.

para_9a_p687_0.text:
Optimum performance can be maintained by cleaning the internal tape head, capstan, and pinch roller periodically (approx. each 100 hours of operation). This can be done by inserting a nonabrasive cleaning cassette in place of the music tape.

Store cassettes away from extreme heat or direct sunlight. Protect the open ends from dirt or damage; store them in their original cases or other protective cases.

For best results, 120 minute tapes are not recommended.

When leaving the car, cassettes may be left in the tape player if the deck is in the "auto reverse" type (tapes are either automatically ejected or internally protected).

para_10_p689_0.text:
One key is used for ignition, door, instrument panel compartment and trunk lock cylinders. The keys are cut on both edges to make them reversible (Figures 1 and 2).

Key identification is obtained from the five character key code stamped on the key code tag. From this key code, the key code cutting combination can be determined from a code list (available to owners of key cutting equipment from suppliers).

If key codes are not available from records or tags, the key code can be obtained from the right hand door lock cylinder (if lock has not been replaced). Lock cylinders supplied by the factory as service parts are unmarked.

If the original key is available, the key code cutting combination can be determined by laying the key on the diagram shown.

proc_10_p689_0.text:
CUTTING KEYS
1. Determine special code from the code list or the key code diagram.
2. Cut a blank key to the proper level for eight tumbler positions.
3. Check key operation in the lock cylinder.
steps: Determine special code from the code list or the key code diagram.
Cut a blank key to the proper level for eight tumbler positions.
Check key operation in the lock cylinder.

para_10_p689_1.text:
Lock cylinders are available from service parts as a complete assembly with key. Tumblers are not available from service parts. If repairs to the tumbler are needed, replace it with a new lock cylinder and key.

There are no internal parts available for lock cylinders, so there are no assembling and coding procedures.

para_10_p690_0.text: The body side moldings that are attached to the body with adhesive tape. To ensure a good molding replacement (new or old moldings), the panel surface should be warm (21°C to 32°C or 70°F to 90°F), clean and free of any wax or oily film. Two methods are listed to attach loose molding ends or completely removed moldings.

proc_10_p690_0.text:
Molding End Loose
1. Wash affected area with soap and water and wipe dry. Wipe panel and adhesive side of molding with a clean rag using oil-free naphtha or alcohol. If molding has pulled loose from adhesive backing (tape remains on body panel), do not remove tape from body. Clean back of molding and tape on body with oil-free naphtha or alcohol and proceed with step 3.
2. If needed, apply a length of masking tape as a molding guide. A straightedge may also be used in most cases.
3. Apply adhesive to back of molding and press in place. If Loctite 414 adhesive (#1052283) or equivalent is used, apply constant pressure to molding for 30 seconds or until a firm bond has been made.
steps: Wash affected area with soap and water and wipe dry. Wipe panel and adhesive side of molding with a clean rag using oil-free naphtha or alcohol. If molding has pulled loose from adhesive backing (tape remains on body panel), do not remove tape from body. Clean back of molding and tape on body with oil-free naphtha or alcohol and proceed with step 3.
If needed, apply a length of masking tape as a molding guide. A straightedge may also be used in most cases.
Apply adhesive to back of molding and press in place. If Loctite 414 adhesive (#1052283) or equivalent is used, apply constant pressure to molding for 30 seconds or until a firm bond has been made.

proc_10_p690_1.text:
Molding Completely Removed
1. Wash affected panel area with soap and water and wipe dry. Remove all traces of adhesive from body panel and back of molding using oil-free naphtha or alcohol.
2. Mark proper position of molding with a length of masking tape. Use adjacent moldings as a guide, if applicable (Figure 3).
3. If body is below 21°C (70°F) due to shop temperature or outside temperature, warm body panel with heat gun while proceeding with step 4.
4. Apply a double-coated acrylic foam tape such as 3M Super Automotive Attachment Tape (#06380, which has a white backing; or #06379, which has a black backing) or equivalent to the molding.
5. Align molding to tape guideline and press firmly in place.
steps: Wash affected panel area with soap and water and wipe dry. Remove all traces of adhesive from body panel and back of molding using oil-free naphtha or alcohol.
Mark proper position of molding with a length of masking tape. Use adjacent moldings as a guide, if applicable (Figure 3).
If body is below 21°C (70°F) due to shop temperature or outside temperature, warm body panel with heat gun while proceeding with step 4.
Apply a double-coated acrylic foam tape such as 3M Super Automotive Attachment Tape (#06380, which has a white backing; or #06379, which has a black backing) or equivalent to the molding.
Align molding to tape guideline and press firmly in place.

para_10_p690_1.text:
GM vehicles are designed to operate under normal environmental conditions. The design criteria for sealing materials and components takes into consideration the sealing forces required to withstand the natural elements. These specifications do not, and cannot, take into consideration all artificial conditions (i.e., high pressure car washes).

The water test procedure has been correlated to the natural elements and will determine the ability of a car to perform under normal operating conditions.

Repairing body water leaks is a problem of proper testing, diagnosis, and repair through adjustment of misaligned components and/or application of proven repair materials. The first step in water leak diagnosis is finding the conditions under which the leak occurs (for example, leak noticed only when parked on an inclined drive or water in spare tire compartment).

If the general leak area can be found, the exact entry point can be quickly isolated by use of a localized test such as a water hose or air hose. If the leak source is not obvious, the generalized testing method using water test equipment such as the water test stands shown in Figure 4 should be used. It may be necessary to remove some interior trim panels or components to locate and confirm repairs.

para_10_p690_2.text:
Specifications for construction of the water test stand are shown in Figure 5.

If the specified water pressure of 155 kPa (22 psi) cannot be obtained because of a local situation, both test stands may be moved toward body until water spray overlap can be obtained.

fig_10_p690_0.text:
Figure 3 Adhesive Body Side Molding Repair
1: TAPE GUIDELINE

fig_10_p690_1.text: Figure 4 Watertest Stands Positioned For Front End Watertest

impo_10_p691_0.text: IMPORTANT: Pinpoint the leak area before any repair is made. Random repair may only temporarily restrict water entry and make future diagnosis and repair more difficult.

para_10_p691_0.text: Continue localized testing in the same general area to confirm that all leaks have been located.

proc_10_p691_0.text:
WATER HOSE TEST
1. Have an assistant inside the car to detect the actual leak point (Figures 6 and 7).
2. Use unrestricted water flow (no nozzle).
3. Begin at base of suspected leak area and move upward slowly.
steps: Have an assistant inside the car to detect the actual leak point (Figures 6 and 7).
Use unrestricted water flow (no nozzle).
Begin at base of suspected leak area and move upward slowly.

proc_10_p691_1.text:
AIR HOSE TEST
1. Apply bubble solution (liquid soap) to suspected area (Figures 8 and 9).
2. Apply air pressure with an air hose from inside the car. Do not exceed 205 kPa (30 psi).
3. Observe for outside bubbles at suspected leak area.
steps: Apply bubble solution (liquid soap) to suspected area (Figures 8 and 9).
Apply air pressure with an air hose from inside the car. Do not exceed 205 kPa (30 psi).
Observe for outside bubbles at suspected leak area.

para_10_p691_1.text:
To locate the exact leak point or to repair the leak, it may be necessary to remove some interior trim panels or components. For waterleak repair procedure on stationary glass, see Section 10-2 (Figure 10).

After completion of any water leak repair, the general area should be retested using the water test stand. Do not use air hose or water hose to test repaired areas as the repair material may dislodge under abnormal pressure.

As rust proof treatment, steel sheets are given corrosion resistance on the interior and/or exterior. These corrosion resistant steel sheet materials are called one or two-side galvanized steel sheets. It is for the sake of rust protection that these materials are selected and given a variety of treatments as shown below.

proc_10_p691_2.text:
AIR HOSE TEST
1. Steel sheets are treated with cathodic electro-primer which is excellent in corrosion resistance.
2. Rust proof wax coatings are applied to the door and side sill insides where moisture is liable to stay.
3. Vinyl coating is applied to the body underside and wheel housing inside.
4. Chip resistant material is applied to the side sill, front skirt and rear skirt outside bottom areas to protect the painted surface from the damage due to flying stones.
5. Sealer is applied to the door hem, engine compartment steel sheet-to-steel joint, and the like portions to prevent water penetration resulting in rust occurence.
steps: Steel sheets are treated with cathodic electro-primer which is excellent in corrosion resistance.
Rust proof wax coatings are applied to the door and side sill insides where moisture is liable to stay.
Vinyl coating is applied to the body underside and wheel housing inside.
Chip resistant material is applied to the side sill, front skirt and rear skirt outside bottom areas to protect the painted surface from the damage due to flying stones.
Sealer is applied to the door hem, engine compartment steel sheet-to-steel joint, and the like portions to prevent water penetration resulting in rust occurence.

para_10_p692_0.text: All the metal panels are coated with metal conditioners and primer coating during vehicle production. Following the repair and/or replacement parts installation, every accessible bare metal surface should be cleaned and coated with rustproof primer.

para_10_p693_0.text:
Perform this operation prior to the application of sealer, rustproof wax coating, and chip resistant material.

Sealer is applied to the specific joints of a vehicle during production. The sealer is intended to prevent dust from entering the vehicle and serves also as an anti-corrosive barrier. The sealer is applied to the door and hood hem areas and between panels. Correct and reseal the originally-sealed joints if damaged. Reseal the attaching joints of a new replacement panel and reseal the hem area of a replacement door or hood.

Use a quality sealer to seal the flanged joints, overlap joints, and seams. The sealer must have flexible characteristics and paintablity after it is applied to repair areas.

For the sealer to fill open joints, use caulking material. Select a sealer in conformance with the place and purpose of a specific use. Observe the manufacturer's label-stated instructions when using the sealer.

In many cases, repaired places require color painting. When this is required, follow the ordinary techniques specified for the finish preparation, color painting, and undercoating build-up.

Rustproof wax, a penetrative compound, is applied to the metal-to-metal surfaces (door and side sill insides) where it is difficult to use ordinary undercoating material for coating. Therefore, when selecting the rustproof wax, it should be the penetrative type.

During the undercoating (vinyl coating) application, care should be taken that sealer is not applied to the engine-related parts and shock absorber mounting or rotating parts. Following the undercoating, make sure that body drain holes are kept open.

The sequence of the application steps of the anti-corrosive materials are as follows:

proc_10_p693_0.text:
Anti-Corrosive Material Application Sequence
1. Clean and prepare the metal surface.
2. Apply primer.
3. Apply sealer (all joints sealed originally).
4. Apply color in areas where color is required such as hem flanges, exposed joints, and under body components.
5. Apply anti-corrosive compound (penetrative wax).
6. Apply chip-resistant material.
7. Apply undercoating (rustproof material).
steps: Clean and prepare the metal surface.
Apply primer.
Apply sealer (all joints sealed originally).
Apply color in areas where color is required such as hem flanges, exposed joints, and under body components.
Apply anti-corrosive compound (penetrative wax).
Apply chip-resistant material.
Apply undercoating (rustproof material).

para_10_p693_1.text:
When the welding or heating operation causes the original galvanization or other anti-corrosive materials to be burnt, the interior and underbody panel surfaces must be cleaned.

Removal of residues of the burning should be carried out carefully when the relevant place has box-type construction or has a shape which limits the access to the interior surfaces. In general, the following method can be used satisfactorily for the removal of those residues.

caut_10_p693_0.text: CAUTION: Standard shop practices, particularly eye protection, should be followed during these operations to avoid personal injury.

para_10_p693_2.text:
Scraping can be used where access is possible. If a standard putty knife or scraper will not fit into the affected area, consider fabricating a small flexible scraper from a narrow piece of sheet metal.

A jet of compressed air will remove most residue and could be most effective in limited-access areas. Eye protection is absolutely necessary in an operation of this type.

Sandblasting is most effective and should be used when the equipment is available and access to the area is good. Sandblasting is an excellent method for clean-up and preparation of open joints, underbody components, and hem flange areas.

Wire brushing (power and by hand).

Sandpaper and steel wool can be used when access is good.

para_10_p693_3.text:
Paintable plastic parts are ABS plastic parts. ABS plastic is used for the front grille and headlamp cover.

Rigid or hard ABS plastic needs no primer coating.

General acrylic lacquers can be painted properly over the hard ABS plastic in terms of adherence.

proc_10_p693_1.text:
Painting ABS Plastic Parts
1. Use cleaning solvent to wash paint finish.
2. Apply conventional acrylic color lacquer to the part surface.
3. Follow the lacquer directions for the required drying time. (As for the drying temperature, a range 60°F to 70°F is proper.)
steps: Use cleaning solvent to wash paint finish.
Apply conventional acrylic color lacquer to the part surface.
Follow the lacquer directions for the required drying time. (As for the drying temperature, a range 60°F to 70°F is proper.)

para_10_p693_4.text: Plastic parts employ not only ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) plastic but also polypropylene, vinyl, or the like plastic. Burning test method to identify the ABS plastic is described below.

proc_10_p693_2.text:
ABS Plastic Identification Burning Test
1. Use a sharp blade to cut off a plastic sliver from the part at its hidden backside.
2. Hold sliver with pincers and set it on fire.
3. Carefully observe the condition of the burning plastic.
4. ABS plastic raises distinguishable black smoke while burning with its residue suspended in air temporarily.
5. Polypropyrene raises no readily distinguishable smoke while burning.
steps: Use a sharp blade to cut off a plastic sliver from the part at its hidden backside.
Hold sliver with pincers and set it on fire.
Carefully observe the condition of the burning plastic.
ABS plastic raises distinguishable black smoke while burning with its residue suspended in air temporarily.
Polypropyrene raises no readily distinguishable smoke while burning.

para_10_p693_5.text: Metal service replacement parts (or assemblies) are coated with electro-deposition primer. For proper adhesion of a paint, the following finish process (refinish steps) becomes necessary.

proc_10_p693_3.text:
Metal Replacement Parts Finishing Steps
- Use wax or grease-removing solvent to clean the part.
- Use a wet or dry sandpaper (#400) to polish the panel lightly. Clean the part again.
3. If factory-applied primer coating is cut through to the bare metal, apply metal conditioner to the bare metal exposed to open air. As for the method of use of the metal conditioner, follow the directions on the container.
4. Allow primer-surfacer to dry completely before starting sandpaper polishing. As for the drying time, follow the directions advised on the primer-surface container.
5. Use a wet or dry sandpaper (#400) and water to polish the panel lightly.
6. Wash the part again.
7. Apply color coating to the part.
8. Different paints demand different drying methods. Hence, follow the directions advised on the pertinent paint container.
9. When the lacquer coating (quick-drying paint coating) is applied, dry the coated surface and polish it with compound.
10. In the case of the lacquer coating, wax should not be applied to the coated surface until the surface has dried completely (approximately two months).
steps: Use wax or grease-removing solvent to clean the part.
Use a wet or dry sandpaper (#400) to polish the panel lightly. Clean the part again.

3. If factory-applied primer coating is cut through to the bare metal, apply metal conditioner to the bare metal exposed to open air. As for the method of use of the metal conditioner, follow the directions on the container.
4. Allow primer-surfacer to dry completely before starting sandpaper polishing. As for the drying time, follow the directions advised on the primer-surface container.
5. Use a wet or dry sandpaper (#400) and water to polish the panel lightly.
6. Wash the part again.
7. Apply color coating to the part.
8. Different paints demand different drying methods. Hence, follow the directions advised on the pertinent paint container.
9. When the lacquer coating (quick-drying paint coating) is applied, dry the coated surface and polish it with compound.
10. In the case of the lacquer coating, wax should not be applied to the coated surface until the surface has dried completely (approximately two months).

proc_10_p694_0.text:
Metal Replacement Parts Finishing Steps
1. Use a proper wire brush, adhesive or liquid rust removing agent to remove rust. As for the method of use, follow the directions advised for the respective materials.
2. If necessary, wash the parts with detergent, rinse, and dry them.
3. Before installing the exterior body parts, apply anti-corrosive compound to all the cleaned surfaces of the exterior body parts. Also, apply anti-corrosive compound to the inner surfaces of the exterior body parts to be installed.
steps: 1. Use a proper wire brush, adhesive or liquid rust removing agent to remove rust. As for the method of use, follow the directions advised for the respective materials.
2. If necessary, wash the parts with detergent, rinse, and dry them.

3. Before installing the exterior body parts, apply anti-corrosive compound to all the cleaned surfaces of the exterior body parts. Also, apply anti-corrosive compound to the inner surfaces of the exterior body parts to be installed.

para_10_p694_0.text:
Paintable plastic interior trim components can be divided into three general types:
• Polypropylene Plastic
• ABS Plastic
• Vinyl Plastic

It is important for a painter to be able to identify each plastic in order to paint it satisfactorily. Painting of complete soft seat cushion and seatback trim cover assemblies of vinyl construction is not approved by the factory. Excluding the soft seat cushion and seatback trim cover assemblies, the plastic used most widely on the interior of bodies is polypropylene.

The purpose of the following tests is to determine the identity of a given plastic so that proper paint procedures and materials can be used.

proc_10_p694_1.text:
INTERIOR PLASTIC TRIM PARTS FINISHING
1. From a hidden backside portion of the part, remove a sliver of plastic with a sharp blade.
2. While holding the sliver of plastic with tweezers or laying it on a clean noncombustible surface, ignite the plastic.
3. Observe the burning plastic closely:
   a. Polypropylene burns with no readily visible smoke.
   b. ABS plastic burns with a readily visible black smoke residue which hangs temporarily in the air.
steps: 1. From a hidden backside portion of the part, remove a sliver of plastic with a sharp blade.
2. While holding the sliver of plastic with tweezers or laying it on a clean noncombustible surface, ignite the plastic.

3. Observe the burning plastic closely:
   a. Polypropylene burns with no readily visible smoke.
   b. ABS plastic burns with a readily visible black smoke residue which hangs temporarily in the air.

proc_10_p694_2.text:
INTERIOR PLASTIC TRIM PARTS FINISHING
1. Heat a copper wire in a suitable flame such as provided by a propane or equivalent torch until the wire glows red.
2. Touch the heated wire to the backside or hidden surface of the part being tested in a manner so as to retain some of the plastic on the wire.
3. Return the wire (and retained plastic) to the flame and observe for a green, turquoise blue flame. A flame in this color range indicates that the plastic being tested is vinyl.
4. If black smoke residue, which hangs temporarily in the air, is readily visible when wire (with retained plastic residue) is returned to the flame, the part is made of flexible (soft) ABS plastic material.
steps: 1. Heat a copper wire in a suitable flame such as provided by a propane or equivalent torch until the wire glows red.
2. Touch the heated wire to the backside or hidden surface of the part being tested in a manner so as to retain some of the plastic on the wire.
3. Return the wire (and retained plastic) to the flame and observe for a green, turquoise blue flame. A flame in this color range indicates that the plastic being tested is vinyl.

4. If black smoke residue, which hangs temporarily in the air, is readily visible when wire (with retained plastic residue) is returned to the flame, the part is made of flexible (soft) ABS plastic material.

para_10_p694_1.text: The system for painting polypropylene parts involves the use of a special primer. Since polypropylene plastic is hard, it can be color coated after prime with conventional interior acrylic lacquer.

noti_10_p694_0.text: NOTICE: Service part must be primed with a coating of special polypropylene primer according to factory recommendations. Failure to use the required primer as directed will result in color coat lifting and/or peeling problems. Use Polypropylene Primer, part #1052364, or equivalent.

proc_10_p694_3.text:
PAINTING POLYPROPYLENE PLASTIC PARTS
1. Wash part with a solvent such as Acryli-Clean, Pre-Kleano, Prep-Sol, or equivalent. Follow label directions.
2. Apply a thin, wet coat of polypropylene primer according to label directions. Wetness of primer is determined by observing gloss reflection of spray application in adequate lighting. Be sure primer application includes all edges. Allow primer to flash dry one minute minimum and ten minutes maximum.
3. During the above flash time period (1 to 10 minutes), apply conventional interior acrylic lacquer color as required and allow to dry before installing part. Application of color during above flash time range promotes best adhesion of color coats.
steps: 1. Wash part with a solvent such as Acryli-Clean, Pre-Kleano, Prep-Sol, or equivalent. Follow label directions.

2. Apply a thin, wet coat of polypropylene primer according to label directions. Wetness of primer is determined by observing gloss reflection of spray application in adequate lighting. Be sure primer application includes all edges. Allow primer to flash dry one minute minimum and ten minutes maximum.

3. During the above flash time period (1 to 10 minutes), apply conventional interior acrylic lacquer color as required and allow to dry before installing part. Application of color during above flash time range promotes best adhesion of color coats.

para_10_p695_0.text: Rigid or hard ABS plastic requires no primer. Conventional interior acrylic lacquers adhere satisfactorily to hard ABS plastics.

proc_10_p695_0.text:
Painting Rigid or Hard ABS Plastic Parts
1. Wash part with a solvent such as Acryli-Clean, Pre-Kleano, Prep-Sol, or equivalent.
2. Apply conventional interior acrylic lacquer color according to trim combination (see paint supplier color chart for trim and color code). Apply only enough color for proper hiding to avoid washout of "grain" effect.
3. Allow to dry following label directions and then install part.
steps: Wash part with a solvent such as Acryli-Clean, Pre-Kleano, Prep-Sol, or equivalent.

Apply conventional interior acrylic lacquer color according to trim combination (see paint supplier color chart for trim and color code). Apply only enough color for proper hiding to avoid washout of "grain" effect.
Allow to dry following label directions and then install part.

para_10_p695_1.text:
The outer cover material of flexible instrument panel cover assemblies is made mostly of ABS plastic modified with PVC or vinyl. The same is true of many padded door trim assemblies. The soft cushion padding under ABS covers is urethane foam plastic.

The most widely used flexible vinyls (polyvinyl chloride) are coated fabrics as used in seat trim, some door trim assemblies, headlinings, and sunshades. Most head restraints are covered with flexible vinyls. Examples of hard vinyls are door and front seatback assist handles, coat hooks, and exterior molding inserts.

The paint system for vinyl and flexible ABS plastic involves the use of interior vinyl color and a clear vinyl top coat. No primer or primer-sealer is required.

proc_10_p695_1.text:
Painting Vinyl and Flexible (Soft) ABS Plastic Parts
1. Wash part with a vinyl cleaning and preparation solvent, such as Vinyl Prep, Vinyl Prep Conditioner, or equivalent. Wipe off cleaner while still wet with clean, lint-free cloth.
2. As soon as the surface has been wiped dry, apply interior vinyl color in wet coats. Allow flash time between coats. Follow label directions. Use proper vinyl color as shown by interior trim code combination. Apply only enough color for proper hiding to avoid washout of grain effect.
3. Before color flashes completely, apply one wet double coat of vinyl clear top coat. Use top coat with appropriate gloss level to match adjacent similar components. The clear coat is necessary to control the gloss requirement and to prevent cracking (rubbing-off) of the color coat after drying.
4. Allow to dry according to label directions before installing part.
steps: Wash part with a vinyl cleaning and preparation solvent, such as Vinyl Prep, Vinyl Prep Conditioner, or equivalent. Wipe off cleaner while still wet with clean, lint-free cloth.

As soon as the surface has been wiped dry, apply interior vinyl color in wet coats. Allow flash time between coats. Follow label directions. Use proper vinyl color as shown by interior trim code combination. Apply only enough color for proper hiding to avoid washout of grain effect.

Before color flashes completely, apply one wet double coat of vinyl clear top coat. Use top coat with appropriate gloss level to match adjacent similar components. The clear coat is necessary to control the gloss requirement and to prevent cracking (rubbing-off) of the color coat after drying.
Allow to dry according to label directions before installing part.

para_10_p695_2.text:
Interior colors are color keyed to trim code combination numbers located on the body number plate or service parts identification label.

Conventional interior acrylic lacquer colors are designed for use only on hard trim parts, such as:
1. Steel parts (primer and/or sealer required on new service parts).
2. Hard ABS plastic (no primer necessary).
3. Hard polypropylene plastic (special primer required).

Each major paint supplier provides an interior color chart which identifies the stock number, color name, gloss factor, and trim code combination number for each conventional interior color.

Vinyl interior colors are designed for soft trim parts such as instrument panel cover assemblies, door trim assemblies, and head restraints. These colors require a final top coat. Other trim parts require a degree of gloss to match similar adjacent parts. Use interior vinyl colors and clear vinyl finishes such as Ditzler Vinyl Spray Colors, American Jetway UR-1 Vynicolor, or equivalents.

para_10_p696_0.text:
Minor glass scratches and abrasions on the outside surface of the glass can be removed or reduced using the methods described in this section.

There are 2 basic types of auto glass: laminated safety plate (used in all windshields) and solid tempered safety plate (used in all side and back windows).

A major concern in the removal of scratches from glass is the chance of causing double vision in areas of occupant's vision. For this reason, removal of scratches or abrasions on a windshield in the occupant's line of vision is more limited than in other areas. Distortion is most apt to result when trying to remove deep scratches. Scratch removal must be performed with care.

• Low speed (600-1300 RPM) rotary polisher (Skil Model No. 570 or equivalent).
• Wool felt rotary-type polishing pad, about 75 mm (3 in.) in diameter and 50 mm (2 in.) thick.
• Powdered cerium oxide (No. 14 Rareox or equivalent) mixed with water as the abrasive compound. Follow manufacturer's directions when using any type of polishing compound.
• Wide mouth container to hold the polish.

noti_10_p696_0.text: NOTICE: This operation must not be used on the inside of rear window glass which contains heating elements that will be damaged.

proc_10_p696_0.text:
Removal of Minor Scratches and Abrasions
1. Mix 2 parts of polishing compound (No. 14 Rareox or equivalent) with 1 part water to obtain a creamy mixture.
2. Stir mixture to maintain a creamy texture. Powdered cerium oxide is hard to mix with water and tends to separate.
3. Draw a circle around scratches on opposite side of glass with a wax marking pencil or crayon. Draw other lines directly behind scratches to serve as guides in locating scratch during polishing.
4. Use masking paper where needed to catch drippings or spattered polish.
5. Dip felt pad attached to polisher into mixture several times to ensure that pad is well saturated.
6. Using moderate, but steady pressure, hold pad flat against scratched area of glass and, with a feathering-out motion, polish affected area (Figure 1). Avoid heavy pressure. It does not speed up operation and may cause overheating of glass.
7. Cover enough area around scratch with a feathering-out motion to eliminate a bull's-eye. Do not hold tool in one spot or operate tool on the glass any longer than 30 to 45 seconds at a time. If glass becomes hot to touch, let it air cool before proceeding further. Cooling with cold water may crack heated glass.
8. Dip pad into mixture frequently to ensure that wheel and glass are always wet during polishing operation. A dry pad causes too much heat to build up.
9. After removing scratch or abrasion, wash glass with water and wipe body clean of any polish.
10. Clean polishing pad.

Stir mixture to maintain a creamy texture. Powdered cerium oxide is hard to mix with water and tends to separate.

Use masking paper where needed to catch drippings or spattered polish.

If glass becomes hot to touch, let it air cool before proceeding further. Cooling with cold water may crack heated glass.

para_10_p696_1.text: The upper reveal molding for the windshield is a vinyl trim that fills the cavity between the body and glass edge. The reveal molding is hand pressed into place and retained by urethane adhesive.

proc_10_p696_1.text:
Upper Windshield Molding - Remove or Disconnect
1. With flat bladed tool, pry off top and side windshield molding.
2. Grasp molding by hand and slowly pull away from body.
3. Apply clear primer from urethane kit (#96360667) to lower surface of molding.
4. Apply urethane to cavity between body and glass.
5. Starting from the center, hand press molding into place. Tape may be applied to help retain molding.

para_10_p697_1.text:
To replace a windshield or back glass installed with urethane requires either partial or complete replacement of the adhesive. Partial replacement of material is known as the short method. Complete replacement is known as the extended method.

The short method can be used when original adhesive left on window opening pinch weld flanges after glass removal can serve as a base for new glass. This method would apply in cases of cracked windshield or removal of windows that are still intact. The amount of adhesive left in the window opening can be controlled during glass removal.

The extended method is to be used when the original adhesive left in the window opening after glass removal cannot serve as a base for new glass. This method would be used in cases needing metal work or paint repair in the opening. In these cases, original material is removed and replaced with new material during window replacement.

para_10_p697_2.text:
Adhesive kit #9636067 (urethane adhesive) or equivalent contains some of the items needed to replace a urethane adhesive using the short method, or any adhesive installed glass, using the extended method.

• Solvent for cleaning edge of glass (preferably alcohol).
• Household cartridge-type caulking gun.
• Commercial type razor knife (for cutting around edge of glass).
• Eyeleteer.
• Piano wire.
• Rubber sucker grip.

para_10_p697_3.text: The front windshield is installed by using a special type of urethane adhesive in conjunction with a primer. When replacing a window, it is important to use an adhesive which provides sufficient adhesion strength and to follow the proper procedure (Figure 1).

noti_10_p697_0.text: NOTICE: Each adhesive has its own drying and setting time and must be handled and used according to specific procedures. Failure to follow these procedures could result in a poor glass-to-body bond. Be sure to read the adhesive instructions very carefully and observe each step of the procedure very closely.

proc_10_p697_4.text:

- Rearview mirror, sun visors and front pillar trims.
- Warm front section of roof lining. Then remove front side rib of roof lining from between front glass and body (Figure 2).
- Drill hole with eyeleteer through adhesive and slip piano wire through it (Figure 3).
- Cut adhesive all around front glass with piano wire (2 technicians required) (Figures 4 and 5).
- Windshield.
10. Using a knife, smooth remaining adhesive around body side so that it is uniform in thickness-- 1-2 mm (Figure 6).
11. If glass is to be re-used, remove adhesive from glass; use care not to damage the primer coated surface (Figure 7).

proc_10_p698_0.text:
Install or Connect
1. Interior trim.
2. Clean: Windshield edge using a cleaning solvent. Let dry for 10 minutes. Molding surface "A" with a clean cloth (Figure 8).
2. Spacers to lower side of windshield.
3. Upper molding to glass. Warming molding for 1/2-hour at 35° C (95° F) will facilitate work.
4. Windshield to body. Position glass so that clearance between upper end of glass and body is about 6 mm (0.236-inch) and clearances between each side of glass and body are even. Using a wax pencil, mark mating points on glass and body. Remove glass.
5. Primer to body surface where windshield is to be adhered using a new brush (Figure 10).
6. Side moldings to glass.
7. Primer to edge of windshield using same new brush.
8. Primer for molding (Urethane) to surface "A" using a new brush (Figure 8).
9. Adhesive to glass (Figure 18). Apply adhesive starting from bottom side of glass. Be careful not to damage primer. Height of adhesive applied to lower side should be higher than that of other 3 sides. Press glass against body quickly after adhesive is applied. Use of rubber sucker grips is helpful to hold and carry glass after adhesive is applied. Peel remaining paper from rubber dam.
10. Windshield to body using rubber sucker grips. Align glass to body using mating marks drawn earlier.

para_10_p698_0.text: Vehicle for water leaks by running water from hose over windshield. If leakage is found, dry window and fill leaky point with adhesive. If water still leaks at this point, remove glass and repeat installation procedure.

impo_10_p698_2.text: IMPORTANT: Do not use high pressure water. Do not apply compressed air to parts with adhesive while drying. Do not use infrared lamp or similar device for drying. Sudden closing of door before adhesive is completely set may cause glass to loosen or come off. If door is opened or closed before adhesive is completely set, make sure to open all door glasses and use proper care. If molding is not securely in place, hold it down with tape until adhesive is completely set. Each adhesive has its own setting time. Refer to manufacturer's instructions for setting time and any precautions to be taken before adhesive is set. Refrain from driving vehicle until adhesive is completely set to ensure proper adhesion.

para_10_p703_0.text: Unlike the three door model, the five door model's quarter windows do not open. The procedure for removal of the quarter window is very similar to the procedure for windshield or rear glass removal. The quarter window is bonded to the body using a special type of urethane adhesive in conjunction with a primer. When replacing a window, it is important to use an adhesive which provides sufficient adhesion strength and to follow the proper procedures.

noti_10_p703_1.text: NOTICE: Each adhesive has its own drying and setting time and must be handled and used according to specific procedures. Failure to follow these procedures could result in poor glass-to-body bond. Be sure to read the adhesive instructions carefully and observe each step of the procedure very closely.

proc_10_p703_1.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Molding around quarter window with knife.
2. Use tape to cover body area around window opening to prevent potential damage.
3. Punch a hole through adhesive with eyeleteer and then slip piano wire through hole (Figure 26).
4. Cut adhesive all around quarter glass with piano wire (Figure 27).
5. Quarter window.
6. Use a knife to smooth the remaining adhesive on body to a uniform thickness all around, about 2-3 mm (Figure 28).
7. Where rubber dam is to be placed, remove adhesive until painted surface is clean.
8. When re-using glass, remove adhesive from glass surface, taking care not to damage primer coating (Figure 39).

para_10_p705_0.text: Where accessible, water leaks can be corrected without removing and reinstalling the glass. This method applies only to urethane installed glass and the use of adhesive furnished in kit #9636067 or equivalent.

proc_10_p705_0.text:
WATER LEAK CORRECTION
1. Remove reveal moldings in area of leak. In some cases, it may become necessary to remove garnish moldings or trim strip lace to locate source of leak.
2. Mark location of leak(s). Carefully push outward on glass in area of leak to determine extent of leak. This operation should be performed while water is being applied to leak area. Mark extent of leak area.
3. Clean: From outside body, clean any dirt or foreign material from leak area with water, then dry area with air hose.
3. Using a sharp knife, trim off uneven edge of adhesive material at leak point and 75 mm (3 in.) to 100 mm (4 in.) on both sides of leak point or beyond limits of leak area.
4. Prime affected area, as shown in operation B with black primer supplied in kit. Agitate primer prior to use. Allow primer to dry five minutes.
5. Apply adhesive material at leak point and 75 mm (3 in.) to 100 mm (4 in.) on both sides of leak point or beyond leak point.
6. Immediately after performing step 5, use a flat stick or other suitable flat-bladed tool to work adhesive material well into leak point and into joint of original material and body to effect watertight seal along entire length of material application.
7. Using warm or hot water, spray test to ensure that leak has been corrected. Do not run heavy stream of water directly on freshly applied adhesive.
8. Replace all previously removed parts.

2. Mark location of leak(s). Carefully push outward on glass in area of leak to determine extent of leak. This operation should be performed while water is being applied to leak area. Mark extent of leak area.

3. Using a sharp knife, trim off uneven edge of adhesive material at leak point and 75 mm (3 in.) to 100 mm (4 in.) on both sides of leak point or beyond limits of leak area.
4. Prime affected area, as shown in operation B with black primer supplied in kit. Agitate primer prior to use. Allow primer to dry five minutes.
5. Apply adhesive material at leak point and 75 mm (3 in.) to 100 mm (4 in.) on both sides of leak point or beyond leak point.

6. Immediately after performing step 5, use a flat stick or other suitable flat-bladed tool to work adhesive material well into leak point and into joint of original material and body to effect watertight seal along entire length of material application.
7. Using warm or hot water, spray test to ensure that leak has been corrected. Do not run heavy stream of water directly on freshly applied adhesive.
8. Replace all previously removed parts.

para_10_p706_0.text:
The optional rear window defogger system consists of a rear glass that has a number of horizontal ceramic silver compound element lines and 2 vertical buss bars on the inside surface of the rear glass. The feed wire or terminal is soldered to the buss bar on the side. The ground wire or terminal is soldered on the other side.

The system operates on 12 volts. Under some conditions, heat from the glass may not be detected by finger touch. The length of time required to remove interior fog from the back glass will vary with such conditions as car speed, outside glass temperature, atmospheric pressure and number of passengers.

para_10_p708_0.text:
To locate inoperative grid lines, start engine and turn on the rear window defogger system. Ground test lamp lead and lightly touch the other prod to each grid line.

If test lamp bulb shows full brilliance at both ends of grid lines, check for loose ground wire contact to body metal.

Test lamp brilliance will decrease as prod is moved further away from feed buss bar.

All grid lines must be tested in at least 2 places to eliminate the possibility of bridging a break. For best results, contact each grid line a few millimeters (inches) on either side of the glass centerline. If an abnormal light reading is apparent on a specific grid line, place test lamp prod on that grid and move prod until light goes out. This will indicate a break in the continuity of the grid line.

proc_10_p708_0.text:
Inspect
1. Rear window defogger grid lines.
2. Mark grid line breaks on outside of glass with a wax pencil or crayon.

para_10_p708_2.text: Grid lines area to be repaired. Buff with steel wool and wipe clean using cloth dampened with alcohol. Buff and clean about 6 mm (1/4 in.) beyond each side of break in grid line.

proc_10_p708_1.text:
Remove or Disconnect
1. Grid line repair decal or 2 strips of tape positioned above and below repair area.
   • Repair decal or tape must be used to control width of repair area.
   • If decal is used, be sure the die-cut metering slot is the same width as the grid line.
2. Remove the cap (separate from the container of grid repair material).
   • Mix hardener and silver plastic thoroughly.
   • If hardener has crystalized, immerse packet in hot water until the hardener reliquifies.
3. At room temperature, apply grid repair material to repair area using a small wood stick or spatula.
4. Carefully remove the decal or tape.

   • Hold heat gun nozzle 25 mm (1 in.) from surface.
   • A minimum temperature of 149° C (300° F) is required.

para_10_p709_0.text: The rear defogger buss bar lead wire or terminal can be reattached by resoldering, using a solder containing 3% silver and a rosin flux paste.

proc_10_p709_1.text:
Braided Lead Wire Repair
1. Before soldering the buss bar, repair area should be buffed with fine steel wool.
2. Apply the paste-type rosin flux in small quantities to the wire lead and buss bar repair area using a brush.
3. The soldering iron tip should be coated with solder beforehand. Use only enough heat to melt the solder and only enough solder to ensure a complete repair.
4. Do not overheat the wire when resoldering to the buss bar.

tbl_10_p710_0.text:
GENERAL BODY CONSTRUCTION: 10-3-1
Alignment Checking: 10-3-1
Floor Pan Insulator: 10-3-1
Floor Carpets: 10-3-3
Underbody Dimensions: 10-3-3

para_10_p710_0.text:
Information in this section pertains to unitized body construction which incorporates integral front and rear frame side rails.

Consideration must be given when checking underbody dimensions. The engine, front suspension lower control arms and rack and pinion steering mount assembly are supported by an engine cradle which is bolted to the body through rubber insulators at four locations. In addition, two rubber insulated struts secure the engine to the upper rail of the engine compartment front panel which is a component of the body structure.

Mounting provisions for the front suspension system are shared by chassis components (suspension lower control arms and engine cradle) and body components (engine compartment side panels). The suspension strut towers must be dimensionally correct in relation to the remainder of the underbody in order to maintain specified suspension strut and caster/camber angles.

Since the individual underbody components also contribute directly to the overall strength of the body, it is essential that proper welding techniques be observed during service repair operations. The underbody components should be properly sealed and rustproofed whenever body repair operations destroy or damage the original sealing and rustproofing. When rustproofing critical underbody components, it is essential to use a quality type of air dry primer such as corrosion-resistant chromate or equivalent material. Combination type primer-surfaces are not recommended.

para_10_p710_1.text:
An accurate method of determining the alignment of the underbody utilizes a measuring tram gage. The tram gage set required to perform the recommended measuring checks must include a vertical pointer capable of reaching 914 mm (36").

Two types of measurements can be made with a tram gage: direct point-to-point measurements and measurements calculated on a horizontal plane (datum line) parallel to the underbody. In the latter case, the height or vertical pointers must be set as specified for each point to be measured.

Point-to-point measurements are generally taken on front structure steering and suspension components and simply require the vertical pointers to be equally set. In some cases point-to-point measurements may be directly taken with a tape measure or appropriate measuring tool (Figures 1 and 3).

Dimensions-to-gage holes are measured to the leading edge or center of the holes and flush to adjacent surface metal. The alphabetically identified points of measurement and metric-to-English dimensional conversion data are given later in this section (Figures 4 and 5).

Floor pan insulators have been designed for higher floor pan temperatures that result from the use of the catalytic converter in the exhaust system. Therefore, when servicing a car in the field, it is essential that any insulators that may have been disturbed or removed be reinstalled in the original sequence and location. Also, if it becomes necessary to replace an insulator, the material specified for that particular location on the floor pan must be used.

All of the above materials must meet Motor Vehicle Safety Standard No. 302 for flammability.

When servicing or replacing interior insulators, the following instructions must be observed:

proc_10_p710_0.text:
Floor Pan Insulator Service Instructions
1. Insulators must be installed in the original position and sequence. Pieces should be butted together in order to avoid gapping or overlapping.
2. If it is necessary to replace an insulator, the specific material must be used.
3. Use original part to determine the amount of replacement material required and as a template for cutting and fitting the new piece to the floor pan.
4. When installing insulator, do not enlarge cutouts or holes that are used for the attachment of interior components such as seats or seat belts.
5. Cross body harness for interior components such as power seats, lap belt warning light and alarm or rear speakers must be routed over the floor pan insulators in the original location and clipped in place.
6. Spray-on deadeners and trim adhesives should not be applied to the top of the floor pan at the area directly over the catalytic converter or muffler(s).
steps: Insulators must be installed in the original position and sequence. Pieces should be butted together in order to avoid gapping or overlapping.
If it is necessary to replace an insulator, the specific material must be used.
Use original part to determine the amount of replacement material required and as a template for cutting and fitting the new piece to the floor pan.
When installing insulator, do not enlarge cutouts or holes that are used for the attachment of interior components such as seats or seat belts.

Cross body harness for interior components such as power seats, lap belt warning light and alarm or rear speakers must be routed over the floor pan insulators in the original location and clipped in place.
Spray-on deadeners and trim adhesives should not be applied to the top of the floor pan at the area directly over the catalytic converter or muffler(s).

para_10_p712_1.text: Each underbody component affects strength of the car itself as well as wheel alignment (toe-in, camber, caster). It is essential, therefore, to check underbody carefully and perform welding properly. If necessary, do correction or replacement. When damage is found in sealing or rustproof treatment, it is mandatory to correct it properly. For sealing and rustproof treatment, refer to ANTI-CORROSION TREATMENT section.

Adjust
1. Adjust latch striker so that the striker shaft lines up with the center groove of the door lock assembly (Figure 3).
2. To adjust the fit in a vertical direction, use shims under the striker as shown (Figures 3 and 4).
3. Rubber cushions are located on each side of the back door base. They are screw-adjustable and can be used to help set door closure (Figure 5).

IMPORTANT: Do not disassemble support as its cylinder is gas-filled.

When discarding an old support, wrap it in a vinyl bag as shown. Use a 2 - 3 mm (0.08 - 0.12 in.) drill to make a hole, through bag into support (Figure 8).

When installing a new lift support, be very careful not to scratch its surface, and never allow dirt or oil to contact its surface.

para_10_p737_0.text:
The back door weatherstrip is a one-piece, continuous rubber strip that fits over the back door opening flange. To remove, simply pull off and cut. To install, prop door open and disconnect one end of lift support mechanisms (the body attachment side is quicker, as there is no trim which requires removal). Slip the new weatherstrip around the detached supports and onto the flange. Reattach supports and check for water leakage.

para_10_p743_1.text:
The rear seat covers are similar to the front seat covers. To remove the seat back cover: Remove seat back from vehicle and loosen trim cover from all sides.

The rear seat cushion is more involved. After removing the cushion from the vehicle, disconnect C-rings at lower perimeter of seat cushion. The tip area of the cover is bonded to the foam pad; this glued section measures 4.9x15 in. Take care when removing this cover not to pull off any of the foam with the dried adhesive. When replacing covers, make sure the fit is as tight as possible.

noti_10_p744_0.text: NOTICE: When fasteners are removed, always reinstall them at the same location from which they were removed. If a fastener needs to be replaced, use the correct part number fastener for that application. If the correct part number fastener is not available, a fastener of equal size and strength (or stronger) may be used. Fasteners that are not reused, and those requiring thread locking compound, will be called out. The correct torque value must be used when installing fasteners that require it. If the above conditions are not followed, parts or system damage could result.

tbl_10_p744_0.text:
General Description: 10-10-1
Automatic (Passive) Front Safety Belts: 10-10-1
Rear Safety Belts: 10-10-1
Diagnosis: 10-10-3
On-Vehicle Service: 10-10-3
Specifications: 10-10-10
Special Tools: 10-10-11

para_10_p744_0.text:
The automatic (passive) front safety belts are automatically set into place when the driver and/or passenger sits in a front seat and closes the door. They are automatically released when the door is opened. Thus neither buckling nor unbuckling is required.

The front safety belts have a system to cancel the lock function of the retractor, allowing the door to be opened smoothly. When a door is opened, both the door latch switch and the door switch close. When these switches are closed, the control module relay is energized and battery voltage is applied to the retractor solenoid.

Three lamps are provided with the automatic front safety belts. The driver's and passenger's safety belt reminder lamps are found on either side of the steering column. They warn the driver or passenger that their safety belt cannot provide proper restraint due to the belt being unbuckled or system failure. The door warning lamp is located in the instrument panel cluster and alerts the driver or passenger that a door is not closed tightly.

caut_10_p744_0.text:
CAUTION: The automatic safety belt may fail to provide proper restraint if, after the belt has been buckled and the door has been completely closed, any of the following conditions exist:
• Warning lamp does not operate at all.
• Warning lamp remains on or flashing.
Driving vehicle with the safety belt in an ineffective state may cause personal injury to the driver or passenger.

para_10_p744_1.text: The following switches are incorporated into the automatic safety belt system:

proc_10_p744_0.text:
AUTOMATIC (PASSIVE) FRONT SAFETY BELTS
1. A safety belt switch is located in each safety belt buckle.
2. Door switches are located in the lower rear door jambs.
3. Door latch switches are installed in each door latch.
4. The warning lamp check switch is built into the ignition switch.
steps: A safety belt switch is located in each safety belt buckle.
Door switches are located in the lower rear door jambs.
Door latch switches are installed in each door latch.
The warning lamp check switch is built into the ignition switch.

para_10_p744_2.text: The shoulder harnesses found in the rear seat are equipped with Emergency and Auto Locking Retractors (E-ALR). The E-ALR system will lock the safety belt webbing under the following conditions:

proc_10_p744_1.text:
REAR SAFETY BELTS
1. When the belt is pulled rapidly from the retractor.
2. When the vehicle is accelerating or decelerating.
3. When the vehicle is inclined.
4. If the webbing is pulled all the way out and then allowed to retract. At this time the system will not allow the webbing to be pulled out any further until it is retracted fully.
steps: When the belt is pulled rapidly from the retractor.
When the vehicle is accelerating or decelerating.
When the vehicle is inclined.
If the webbing is pulled all the way out and then allowed to retract. At this time the system will not allow the webbing to be pulled out any further until it is retracted fully.

impo_10_p744_0.text: IMPORTANT: Keep sharp objects and potentially damaging objects away from belts.
